problem_statement.Title: Inconsistent permissions for uploaded files when FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS is left as None (TemporaryUploadedFile vs MemoryUploadedFile)
problem_statement.Description: Files saved by FileSystemStorage can end up with different filesystem permission bits depending on the upload handler and the global FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS setting. When settings.FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS is None (the current default), FileSystemStorage skips a final chmod; files moved from temporary files can therefore retain the temporary file's mode while files created via the streaming-write path are created with mode 0o666 masked by the process umask, producing inconsistent final permissions.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Default setting is None: django/conf/global_settings.py sets FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS = None, encoding the assumption that no default permission should be enforced for uploaded files. (location: django/conf/global_settings.py, line ~305–307)

2) Storage defers to the setting and conditionally skips chmod: FileSystemStorage.file_permissions_mode resolves to settings.FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS when the instance attribute is unset, and FileSystemStorage._save only calls os.chmod(full_path, self.file_permissions_mode) when file_permissions_mode is not None. Thus, when the setting is None the final chmod step is skipped. (location: django/core/files/storage.py, property file_permissions_mode lines ~216–218; _save lines ~254–255 and ~283–284)

3) Temporary-upload path preserves tempfile mode: TemporaryUploadedFile constructs its tempfile via django.core.files.temp.NamedTemporaryFile (which on non-Windows platforms is an alias for the stdlib tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile). The move operation used to place the temp file into its final location (django.core.files.move.file_move_safe) prefers os.rename (which preserves the source file's permission bits) and its fallback calls shutil.copystat (which copies permission bits). As a result, destination files can inherit the temporary file's permission bits unless a subsequent chmod is applied. (locations: django/core/files/uploadedfile.py line ~61; django/core/files/temp.py lines ~71–72; django/core/files/move.py lines ~48–49 and ~70–71)

4) Streaming-write path creates files with mode 0o666 masked by umask: The streaming-write save path uses os.open(full_path, FileSystemStorage.OS_OPEN_FLAGS, 0o666) before writing chunks; the current process umask is applied to the created file's mode. Therefore streaming-write saves and temp-file moves can start with different modes by construction. (location: django/core/files/storage.py, _save line ~260)

5) Tests reflect the current default: tests/test_utils/tests.py asserts that default_storage.file_permissions_mode is None, reflecting the repository's current default. Changing the default setting will affect tests that assert the current None default. (location: tests/test_utils/tests.py, test_override_file_upload_permissions lines ~1096–1104)

6) Platform/runtime behavior and verification gap: behavior of tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile (e.g., typical mode values such as 0o600 on some Unix systems), platform-specific NamedTemporaryFile wrapper behavior on Windows, special filesystem semantics (CIFS, network filesystems), and umask interactions are runtime/platform concerns and are not validated by repository code inspection alone. These were not verified here by running tests on multiple platforms or filesystems. (locations: django/core/files/temp.py, django/core/files/uploadedfile.py; investigation gap: runtime/platform validation not performed)
StepsToReproduce: Use a Django project with default settings (no FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS set).
Configure default file storage to FileSystemStorage and ensure MEDIA_ROOT points to a filesystem you can inspect.
Start the dev server or use a test view that accepts file uploads.
Upload a small file (so the streaming-write path / in-memory handler is used). After upload, inspect the saved file permissions (e.g., ls -l). Note the mode (commonly 0o644 when umask masks 0o666).

Upload a larger file (so TemporaryUploadedFile + NamedTemporaryFile is used, leading to file_move_safe os.rename or copy path). After upload, inspect the saved file permissions. Observe that the mode can be different (commonly a more-restrictive mode such as 0o600 on some Unix/Python configurations).
Compare the two results: note the inconsistent permissions between small and large uploads when FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS is left as None.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Uploaded files should have consistent, predictable filesystem permissions independent of which upload handler (in-memory streaming vs temporary-file + rename/copy) was used. For example, in a typical configuration files saved to disk by the storage backend should present a stable final permission (user-visible example: 0o644) regardless of upload size/handler.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: With settings.FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS = None, FileSystemStorage skips the final chmod step. Files moved from temporary files can therefore retain the temporary file's mode (a restrictive mode is commonly observed on some Unix/Python configurations), while files created by the streaming-write path are created via os.open(..., 0o666) and end up with 0o666 masked by the process umask (commonly 0o644). This causes inconsistent final file modes depending on upload size/handler.

hints_text:
I think you're talking about ef70af77ec53160d5ffa060c1bdf5ed93322d84f (#28540). I guess the question is whether or not that documentation should be duplicated elsewhere.
Thank you Tim, this is precisely what I was looking for! I can only see one issue with the current docs (if you excuse me for bothering you with such minor details). ​The documentation for the FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS setting reads: If this isn’t given or is None, you’ll get operating-system dependent behavior. On most platforms, temporary files will have a mode of 0o600, and files saved from memory will be saved using the system’s standard umask. As I would understand this text, only temporary files get a mode of 0o600. I would then ask myself: "Why should I care about temporary files, they should be gone anyway after the file is uploaded?" and skip setting FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS. What is important but is not properly conveyed to the user is that not only temporary files themselves, but also the actual files which end up in the media folder get permissions of 0o600. Currently a developer can only discover this either by careful reading of the Deployment checklist page (manage.py check --deploy does not seem to check FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS) or by hitting the inconsistent permissions accidentally (like I did). I propose to unify the docs for FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS on the Settings page and the Deployment checklist page like this: ​https://gist.github.com/earshinov/0340f741189a14d4fd10e3e902203ad6/revisions#diff-14151589d5408f8b64b7e0e580770f0e Pros: It makes more clear that one gets different permissions for the *uploaded* files. It makes the docs more unified and thus easier to synchronously change in the future if/when required. I recognize that my edits might seem too minor and insignificant to be worth the hassle of editing the docs, committing, re-publishing them etc., but still I hope you will find them useful enough to be integrated into the official docs.
Now that I think about, maybe Django could provide # <Commentary about inconsistent permissions when this setting is omitted> FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSINS=0o600 in the ​default project settings so that developers don't miss it? 600 seems a reasonable default, particularly because people would get 600 anyway (at least on some operating systems) when the TemporaryFileUploadHandler is engaged.
Since this has come up again, I've suggested on django-developers (​https://groups.google.com/d/topic/django-developers/h9XbQAPv5-I/discussion) that we adjust the FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSION default to 0o644 (This was the conclusion I eventually came to from the discussion on #28540.) Lets see what people say there.
Thus far, no great objections on the mailing list to adjusting the FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSION default. Thus I'm going to rename this and Accept on that basis. A PR would need to: Adjust the default. Add a Breaking Change note to releases/2.2.txt (on the assumption we can get it in for then.) — This should include a set to None to restore previous behaviour' type comment. Adjust the references in the settings docs and deployment checklist. Make sure any other references are adjusted.
Replying to Carlton Gibson: Thus far, no great objections on the mailing list to adjusting the FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSION default. Thus I'm going to rename this and Accept on that basis. Thank you! Hopefully, this change will prevent confusion and unpleasant surprises for Django users in the future.
Hello everyone, I would like to work on this. But before that there are few important questions: There is a related setting called FILE_UPLOAD_DIRECTORY_PERMISSIONS. Its document says that This value mirrors the functionality and caveats of the FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS setting. Shall we also change its default from None to 0o644(Please suggest if something different should be provided for directories) and update its document as well? Since 2.2 pre-release branch is now in feature freeze state, Shall we move the change to 3.0 version? On a side note, some tests must be refactored for new values for both of these settings. I think that's alright.
That note is referring to that non-leaf directories are created using the process umask. (See ​`makedirs()` docs.) This is similar to FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS, when not using the temporary file upload handler. The underlying issue here is the inconsistency in file permissions, depending on the file size, when using the default settings that Django provides. There is no such inconsistency with directory permissions. As such changes should not be needed to FILE_UPLOAD_DIRECTORY_PERMISSIONS. (Any issues there would need to be addressed under a separate ticket.)
Replying to Carlton Gibson: I see and understand the issue better now. Thanks for the clarification. I'll make the changes as you have suggested in your previous comment. Only question remaining is about introducing this change in 3.0 version. Shall we move it to 3.0 release?
Shall we move it to 3.0 release? Yes please.

test_patch:
diff --git a/tests/model_fields/test_filepathfield.py b/tests/model_fields/test_filepathfield.py
new file mode 100644
--- /dev/null
+++ b/tests/model_fields/test_filepathfield.py
@@ -0,0 +1,22 @@
+import os
+
+from django.db.models import FilePathField
+from django.test import SimpleTestCase
+
+
+class FilePathFieldTests(SimpleTestCase):
+    def test_path(self):
+        path = os.path.dirname(__file__)
+        field = FilePathField(path=path)
+        self.assertEqual(field.path, path)
+        self.assertEqual(field.formfield().path, path)
+
+    def test_callable_path(self):
+        path = os.path.dirname(__file__)
+
+        def generate_path():
+            return path
+
+        field = FilePathField(path=generate_path)
+        self.assertEqual(field.path(), path)
+        self.assertEqual(field.formfield().path, path)

problem_statement.Title: FilePathField.path does not accept callables — forms.FilePathField assumes concrete path and is incompatible with callable paths
problem_statement.Description: When a FilePathField is given a callable for its path (intended to compute a machine-specific directory at runtime), the forms.FilePathField constructor treats path as a filesystem string and immediately uses it with os.walk/os.scandir. The model FilePathField preserves the original path value (including callables) into deconstructed kwargs for migrations and forwards that value into the form; this creates a mismatch between model serialization and form consumption.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) django/forms/fields.py::FilePathField.__init__ assumes the path argument is a concrete filesystem path string and uses it directly with os.walk(self.path) and os.scandir(self.path) (forms/fields.py lines ~1079–1116). There is no branch that checks for or evaluates a callable path before these filesystem operations.

2) django/db/models/fields/__init__.py::FilePathField.deconstruct records the field's self.path into kwargs (kwargs['path'] = self.path) when self.path != '' (models/fields/__init__.py lines ~1688–1692). django/db/models/fields/__init__.py::FilePathField.formfield forwards that same self.path through to the form layer ('path': self.path) (models/fields/__init__.py lines ~1710–1718). This means a callable assigned at the model level will be preserved and handed to forms.FilePathField as-is.

3) There is an established precedent for allowing callables on model field arguments: django/db/models/fields/files.py::FileField.generate_filename handles callable upload_to via a callable check ("if callable(self.upload_to): filename = self.upload_to(instance, filename)") (models/fields/files.py lines ~295–304). This demonstrates an existing, consistent pattern for callable-handling that FilePathField does not mirror on the form side.

4) Migration serialization constraints: django/db/models/fields/__init__.py::Field.deconstruct documents that migration arguments may include top-level classes and top-level functions and that such callables will be referenced by import path (models/fields/__init__.py docstring lines ~413–419). Practically, this means only importable (top-level) callables will have stable migration representations; lambdas or nested functions are not guaranteed to be serializable into migrations.

Overall root pattern: model FilePathField preserves callables for path at deconstruction time and forwards them to the form, while forms.FilePathField.__init__ expects a concrete path string and immediately performs filesystem iteration on that value. This mismatch is the underlying cause of the reported behavior.
StepsToReproduce: Define a top-level callable that returns a filesystem path, for example in models.py:
def local_files_path():
return os.path.join(settings.LOCAL_FILE_DIR, 'example_dir')
Declare a model field using that callable as the path: file = models.FilePathField(path=local_files_path)

Instantiate a form or otherwise cause the form-side to be constructed (e.g., call MyModel._meta.get_field('file').formfield() or render a ModelForm that includes the FilePathField). Observe that forms.FilePathField.__init__ attempts to use os.walk / os.scandir on the callable value and raises a TypeError or similar because the callable is not a filesystem path string.

Note: passing path=os.path.join(settings.LOCAL_FILE_DIR, 'example_dir') (i.e., evaluating the path expression at import time) yields a plain string, which will be serialized into migrations as the concrete path. That outcome is a result of the model code evaluating the expression at import time rather than Django evaluating a callable during migration generation.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Developers can assign a top-level importable, no-argument callable to models.FilePathField.path and have that callable preserved by model deconstruction for migrations. Form construction (forms.FilePathField) should accept the model field's path value as provided by formfield() without failing; when a callable is passed through, the form layer should be able to work with the representation produced/serialized by the model layer (i.e., not error when given a callable that can be serialized into migrations).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The forms.FilePathField constructor uses self.path directly with os.walk/os.scandir and does not check for or evaluate callables. As a result, instantiating the form field with a callable path value will attempt to iterate the callable and raise a TypeError or similar. Separately, developers who evaluated a path expression at import time (path = os.path.join(...)) will find a concrete, machine-specific string embedded in migrations because the model field received a string value during class definition.

hints_text:
So, to clarify, what exactly is the bug/feature proposal/issue here? The way I see it, you're supposed to use os.path.join() and LOCAL_FILE_DIR to define a relative path. It's sort of like how we use BASE_DIR to define relative paths in a lot of other places. Could you please clarify a bit more as to what the issue is so as to make it easier to test and patch?
Replying to Hemanth V. Alluri: So, to clarify, what exactly is the bug/feature proposal/issue here? The way I see it, you're supposed to use os.path.join() and LOCAL_FILE_DIR to define a relative path. It's sort of like how we use BASE_DIR to define relative paths in a lot of other places. Could you please clarify a bit more as to what the issue is so as to make it easier to test and patch? LOCAL_FILE_DIR doesn't have to be the same on another machine, and in this case it isn't the same on the production server. So the os.path.join() will generate a different path on my local machine compared to the server. When i ran ./manage.py makemigrations the Migration had the path resolved "hardcoded" to my local path, which will not work when applying that path on the production server. This will also happen when using the BASE_DIR setting as the path of your FilePathField, seeing as that's based on the location of your project folder, which will almost always be on a different location. My suggestion would be to let makemigrations not resolve the path and instead keep the os.path.join(), which I have now done manually. More importantly would be to retain the LOCAL_FILE_DIR setting in the migration.
Replying to Sebastiaan Arendsen: Replying to Hemanth V. Alluri: So, to clarify, what exactly is the bug/feature proposal/issue here? The way I see it, you're supposed to use os.path.join() and LOCAL_FILE_DIR to define a relative path. It's sort of like how we use BASE_DIR to define relative paths in a lot of other places. Could you please clarify a bit more as to what the issue is so as to make it easier to test and patch? LOCAL_FILE_DIR doesn't have to be the same on another machine, and in this case it isn't the same on the production server. So the os.path.join() will generate a different path on my local machine compared to the server. When i ran ./manage.py makemigrations the Migration had the path resolved "hardcoded" to my local path, which will not work when applying that path on the production server. This will also happen when using the BASE_DIR setting as the path of your FilePathField, seeing as that's based on the location of your project folder, which will almost always be on a different location. My suggestion would be to let makemigrations not resolve the path and instead keep the os.path.join(), which I have now done manually. More importantly would be to retain the LOCAL_FILE_DIR setting in the migration. Please look at this ticket: https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/6896 I think that something like what sandychapman suggested about an extra flag would be cool if the design decision was approved and if there were no restrictions in the implementation for such a change to be made. But that's up to the developers who have had more experience with the project to decide, not me.
This seems a reasonable use-case: allow FilePathField to vary path by environment. The trouble with os.path.join(...) is that it will always be interpreted at import time, when the class definition is loaded. (The (...) say, ...and call this....) The way to defer that would be to all path to accept a callable, similarly to how FileField's upload_to takes a callable. It should be enough to evaluate the callable in FilePathField.__init__(). Experimenting with generating a migration looks good. (The operation gives path the fully qualified import path of the specified callable, just as with upload_to.) I'm going to tentatively mark this as Easy Pickings: it should be simple enough.
Replying to Nicolas Noé: Hi Nicolas, Are you still working on this ticket?
Sorry, I forgot about it. I'll try to solve this real soon (or release the ticket if I can't find time for it).
​PR
Can I work on this ticket ?
Sure, sorry for blocking the ticket while I was too busy...
I think that Nicolas Noe's solution, ​PR, was correct. The model field can accept a callable as it is currently implemented. If you pass it a callable for the path argument it will correctly use that fully qualified function import path in the migration. The problem is when you go to actually instantiate a FilePathField instance, the FilePathField form does some type checking and gives you one of these TypeError: scandir: path should be string, bytes, os.PathLike or None, not function This can be avoided by evaluating the path function first thing in the field form __init__ function, as in the pull request. Then everything seems to work fine.
Hi, If I only change self.path in forms/fields.py, right after __init__ I get this error: File "/home/hpfn/Documentos/Programacao/python/testes/.venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/django/forms/fields.py", line 1106, in __init__ self.choices.append((f, f.replace(path, "", 1))) TypeError: replace() argument 1 must be str, not function The 'path' param is used a few lines after. There is one more time. Line 1106 can be wrong. If I put in models/fields/__init__.py - after super(): if callable(self.path): self.path = self.path() I can run 'python manage.py runserver'

problem_statement.Title: SQLCompiler incorrectly removes ORDER BY clauses when expressions are multiline RawSQL
problem_statement.Description: When ORDER BY expressions are provided as multiline RawSQL (i.e., the compiled SQL contains newline characters), SQLCompiler's deduplication logic can incorrectly treat distinct expressions as duplicates and drop one of them from the generated ORDER BY clause.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Fragile regex for extracting expression vs ordering token — django/db/models/sql/compiler.py::SQLCompiler.__init__ defines self.ordering_parts = re.compile(r'(.*)\s(ASC|DESC)(.*)') which uses '.' (dot) without DOTALL. Because dot does not match newline characters, the regex's group(1) can be truncated when the compiled SQL contains embedded newlines (multiline expressions).

2) Truncated extraction used as deduplication key in get_order_by — django/db/models/sql/compiler.py::SQLCompiler.get_order_by calls without_ordering = self.ordering_parts.search(sql).group(1) and then uses params_hash = make_hashable(params) and seen membership on (without_ordering, params_hash). The deduplication formula in code is therefore: dedup_key = (self.ordering_parts.search(sql).group(1), make_hashable(params)). If group(1) is truncated for multiline SQL, distinct expressions that share the same trailing line will produce identical dedup_key values and one will be dropped.

3) Same truncated extraction used in extra-select membership test — django/db/models/sql/compiler.py::SQLCompiler.get_extra_select also calls without_ordering = self.ordering_parts.search(sql).group(1) and compares the tuple (without_ordering, params) against select-side tuples (sql, params) produced by get_select. The extraction assumption (single-line expression before ASC/DESC) is the same in both places and both locations participate in comparison logic.

4) RawSQL preserves user-supplied newlines — django/db/models/expressions.py::RawSQL.as_sql returns ('(%s)' % self.sql, self.params), i.e. the raw SQL string (self.sql) is included verbatim inside parentheses. Multiline SQL supplied to RawSQL is therefore passed to the compiler unchanged and can contain embedded newlines that trigger the regex truncation.

5) OrderBy tokens assumed exact — django/db/models/expressions.py::OrderBy.template uses '%(expression)s %(ordering)s' and OrderBy.as_sql sets ordering to 'ASC' or 'DESC'. The ordering_parts regex depends on those exact tokens; the regex extraction is therefore coupled to the emitted ordering tokens and whitespace pattern.
StepsToReproduce: Define RawSQL instances containing multiline SQL expressions (including newline characters) that differ earlier in the expression but share identical final lines. Example: three RawSQL CASE expressions where the last line is the same (e.g., 'else null end').
Call queryset.order_by(...) with those RawSQL expressions, e.g. MyModel.objects.all().order_by(RawSQL(multiline_case_1).desc(), RawSQL(multiline_case_2).asc(), RawSQL(multiline_case_3).desc()).
Force Django to compile the SQL (for example, iterate the queryset or print(queryset.query)).
Observe the compiled SQL ORDER BY: one of the provided ordering expressions is missing (the middle one in the example).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: All ORDER BY expressions supplied by the user are preserved in the compiled SQL in the same order and with the requested ASC/DESC, regardless of whether the expressions are multiline or contain identical trailing lines.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: One of the ORDER BY clauses supplied as a multiline RawSQL expression is missing from the compiled SQL ORDER BY clause; distinct ordering expressions can be dropped when they contain identical trailing lines (a missing ordering term is observable from the generated SQL).

hints_text:
Is there a reason you can't use ​conditional expressions, e.g. something like: MyModel.objects.annotate( custom_order=Case( When(...), ) ).order_by('custom_order') I'm thinking that would avoid fiddly ordering_parts regular expression. If there's some shortcoming to that approach, it might be easier to address that. Allowing the ordering optimization stuff to handle arbitrary RawSQL may be difficult.
Is there a reason you can't use ​conditional expressions No, but I didn't knew about the issue, and writing raw sqls is sometimes faster (not in this case ;) I'm really happy having possibility to mix raw sqls with object queries. Next time I'll use expressions, for sure. Allowing the ordering optimization stuff to handle arbitrary RawSQL may be difficult. Personally I'd like to skip RawSQL clauses in the block which is responsible for finding duplicates. If someone is using raw sqls, he knows the best what he is doing, IMO. And it is quite strange if Django removes silently part of your SQL. This is very confusing. And please note that printing a Query instance was generating incomplete sql, but while checking Query.order_by manually, the return value was containing all clauses. I thought that just printing was affected, but our QA dept told me the truth ;) I know there is no effective way to compare similarity of two raw clauses. This may be hard for expression objects, too, but you have a possibility to implement some __eq__ magic (instead of comparation of generated sqls). Unfortunately I don't know why duplicates detection was implemented, so it's hard to tell how to improve this part.
Patches welcome, I suppose.

problem_statement.Title: Merging 3+ Media objects can produce spurious MediaOrderConflictWarning and wrong JS order
problem_statement.Description: Combining Media from three or more widgets can trigger a MediaOrderConflictWarning even when there is a valid global ordering, and can produce an unintuitive final JS order that depends on the sequence of pairwise merges rather than the true partial-order constraints implied by each widget's Media lists.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) django/forms/widgets.py:Media.merge: merge(list_1, list_2) is implemented as a pairwise, stateful insertion algorithm. It copies list_1, iterates over list_2 in reverse, inserts missing elements at last_insert_index, updates last_insert_index when an element already exists, and warns when an existing element's index > last_insert_index. This behavior encodes the assumption that sequential/pairwise merges produce a correct global order; in practice it imposes sequence-dependent ordering constraints and can generate spurious MediaOrderConflictWarning and wrong final orderings. (See merge implementation: reversed(list_2), last_insert_index, warnings.warn with the two items printed.)

2) django/forms/widgets.py:Media._js: the _js property merges all collected contributor lists by iteratively calling Media.merge(js, next_list) in the order contributors were added (js = self._js_lists[0]; for obj in self._js_lists[1:]: js = merge(js, obj)). Because merge is sequence-dependent, the order in which contributor lists appear in _js_lists determines the constraints that are enforced and can create spurious inversions.

3) django/forms/widgets.py:Media._css: the _css property similarly merges CSS contributor dicts pairwise across self._css_lists. For each medium it calls self.merge(existing_list, next_list) in sequence, which mirrors the same sequence-dependent assumption for CSS assets and can produce symmetric spurious constraints when multiple contributors provide the same medium.

4) django/forms/widgets.py:Media.__add__ and media collection semantics: Media.__add__ concatenates contributor lists into combined._js_lists and combined._css_lists (combined._js_lists = self._js_lists + other._js_lists), preserving the order of contributors. That collection behavior is correct as a way to record contributors, but the downstream pairwise merging in _js/_css interprets those collected lists in a sequence-dependent way and thus lets addition order impose ordering constraints.

5) Callers that build Media via repeated addition (sequence-dependent collection): The following sites build combined Media by repeated addition and therefore feed the sequence-dependent collected lists into Media._js/_css: django/forms/forms.py:BaseForm.media (media = Media(); for field in self.fields.values(): media = media + field.widget.media), django/forms/widgets.py:MultiWidget._get_media (media = Media(); for w in self.widgets: media = media + w.media), and other internal callers that use media accumulation such as django/contrib/admin/helpers.py:AdminForm.media and code paths in django/contrib/admin/options.py that do media = self.media + adminForm.media and then add inline formset media. These call sites preserve contributor sequence (via __add__) and rely on the merging behavior performed later.

6) Tests and behavioral contract surface: tests/forms_tests/tests/test_media.py contains unit tests that verify merge behavior and document the expected three-way merge properties (e.g. test_merge, test_merge_warning, test_merge_js_three_way, test_merge_css_three_way). The existing algorithm produces warnings and final orderings that differ from the intended/expected three-way merge semantics expressed in those tests; the tests are the contract that any corrected merging logic must satisfy.

Note: The above findings are all of the same root-cause pattern: the merge algorithm and the sequential/pairwise application of it to collected contributor lists produce sequence-dependent constraints. All locations that collect Media contributor lists and rely on the pairwise merge are equally affected.
StepsToReproduce: Define the widgets and form from the example: ColorPicker with class Media: js = ['color-picker.js'], SimpleTextWidget with Media.js = ['text-editor.js'], FancyTextWidget with Media.js = ['text-editor.js', 'text-editor-extras.js', 'color-picker.js'], and a form MyForm having fields background_color = forms.CharField(widget=ColorPicker()), intro = forms.CharField(widget=SimpleTextWidget()), body = forms.CharField(widget=FancyTextWidget()).
Instantiate the form and access its media in a Python shell or runtime: evaluate MyForm().media (or render a form that references form.media).
Observe that a MediaOrderConflictWarning is emitted and that the returned Media.js ordering is Media(js=['text-editor-extras.js', 'color-picker.js', 'text-editor.js']) in this example.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Compute a global ordering of the JS and CSS assets that respects the partial-order constraints implied by each widget's Media lists (for the example: text-editor.js, text-editor-extras.js, color-picker.js) and emit MediaOrderConflictWarning only when the combined constraints are genuinely contradictory (i.e., a cycle). The computed ordering should not depend on the sequence in which Media objects were added together.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Accessing MyForm().media emits a MediaOrderConflictWarning identifying an inversion (text-editor-extras.js versus text-editor.js) and returns Media(js=['text-editor-extras.js', 'color-picker.js', 'text-editor.js']). The warning and final ordering are sequence-dependent and reflect spurious constraints introduced by pairwise incremental merging.

hints_text:
As a tentative fix, I propose that media objects should explicitly distinguish between cases where we do / don't care about ordering, notionally something like: class FancyTextWidget(forms.Widget): class Media: js = { ('text-editor.js', 'text-editor-extras.js'), # tuple = order is important 'color-picker.js' # set = order is unimportant } (although using a set for this is problematic due to the need for contents to be hashable), and the result of adding two media objects should be a "don't care" so that we aren't introducing dependencies where the original objects didn't have them. We would then defer assembling them into a flat list until the final render call. I haven't worked out the rest of the algorithm yet, but I'm willing to dig further if this sounds like a sensible plan of attack...
Are you testing with the fix from #30153?
Yes, testing against current master (b39bd0aa6d5667d6bbcf7d349a1035c676e3f972).
So ​https://github.com/django/django/commit/959d0c078a1c903cd1e4850932be77c4f0d2294d (the fix for #30153) didn't make this case worse, it just didn't improve on it. The problem is actually the same I encountered, with the same unintuitive error message too. There is still a way to produce a conflicting order but it's harder to trigger in the administration interface now but unfortunately still easy. Also, going back to the state of things pre 2.0 was already discussed previously and rejected. Here's a failing test and and an idea to make this particular test pass: Merge the JS sublists starting from the longest list and continuing with shorter lists. The CSS case is missing yet. The right thing to do would be (against ​worse is better) to add some sort of dependency resolution solver with backtracking but that's surely a bad idea for many other reasons. The change makes some old tests fail (I only took a closer look at test_merge_js_three_way and in this case the failure is fine -- custom_widget.js is allowed to appear before jquery.js.) diff --git a/django/forms/widgets.py b/django/forms/widgets.py index 02aa32b207..d85c409152 100644 --- a/django/forms/widgets.py +++ b/django/forms/widgets.py @@ -70,9 +70,15 @@ class Media: @property def _js(self): - js = self._js_lists[0] + sorted_by_length = list(sorted( + filter(None, self._js_lists), + key=lambda lst: -len(lst), + )) + if not sorted_by_length: + return [] + js = sorted_by_length[0] # filter(None, ...) avoids calling merge() with empty lists. - for obj in filter(None, self._js_lists[1:]): + for obj in filter(None, sorted_by_length[1:]): js = self.merge(js, obj) return js diff --git a/tests/forms_tests/tests/test_media.py b/tests/forms_tests/tests/test_media.py index 8cb484a15e..9d17ad403b 100644 --- a/tests/forms_tests/tests/test_media.py +++ b/tests/forms_tests/tests/test_media.py @@ -571,3 +571,12 @@ class FormsMediaTestCase(SimpleTestCase): # was never specified. merged = widget3 + form1 + form2 self.assertEqual(merged._css, {'screen': ['a.css', 'b.css'], 'all': ['c.css']}) + + def test_merge_js_some_more(self): + widget1 = Media(js=['color-picker.js']) + widget2 = Media(js=['text-editor.js']) + widget3 = Media(js=['text-editor.js', 'text-editor-extras.js', 'color-picker.js']) + + merged = widget1 + widget2 + widget3 + + self.assertEqual(merged._js, ['text-editor.js', 'text-editor-extras.js', 'color-picker.js'])
Thinking some more: sorted() is more likely to break existing code because people probably haven't listed all dependencies in their js attributes now. Yes, that's not what they should have done, but breaking peoples' projects sucks and I don't really want to do that (even if introducing sorted() might be the least disruptive and at the same time most correct change) wanting to handle the jquery, widget1, noConflict and jquery, widget2, noConflict case has introduced an unexpected amount of complexity introducing a complex solving framework will have a really bad impact on runtime and will introduce even more complexity and is out of the question to me I'm happy to help fixing this but right now I only see bad and worse choices.
I don't think sorting by length is the way to go - it would be trivial to make the test fail again by extending the first list with unrelated items. It might be a good real-world heuristic for finding a solution more often, but that's just trading a reproducible bug for an unpredictable one. (I'm not sure I'd trust it as a heuristic either: we've encountered this issue on Wagtail CMS, where we're making extensive use of form media on hierarchical form structures, and so those media definitions will tend to bubble up several layers to reach the top level. At that point, there's no way of knowing whether the longer list is the one with more complex dependencies, or just one that collected more unrelated files on the way up the tree...) I'll do some more thinking on this. My hunch is that even if it does end up being a travelling-salesman-type problem, it's unlikely to be run on a large enough data set for performance to be an issue.
I don't think sorting by length is the way to go - it would be trivial to make the test fail again by extending the first list with unrelated items. It might be a good real-world heuristic for finding a solution more often, but that's just trading a reproducible bug for an unpredictable one. Well yes, if the ColorPicker itself would have a longer list of JS files it depends on then it would fail too. If, on the other hand, it wasn't a ColorPicker widget but a ColorPicker formset or form the initially declared lists would still be preserved and sorting the lists by length would give the correct result. Since #30153 the initially declared lists (or tuples) are preserved so maybe you have many JS and CSS declarations but as long as they are unrelated there will not be many long sublists. I'm obviously happy though if you're willing to spend the time finding a robust solution to this problem. (For the record: Personally I was happy with the state of things pre-2.0 too... and For the record 2: I'm also using custom widgets and inlines in feincms3/django-content-editor. It's really surprising to me that we didn't stumble on this earlier since we're always working on the latest Django version or even on pre-release versions if at all possible)
Hi there, I'm the dude who implemented the warning. I am not so sure this is a bug. Let's try tackle this step by step. The new merging algorithm that was introduced in version 2 is an improvement. It is the most accurate way to merge two sorted lists. It's not the simplest way, but has been reviewed plenty times. The warning is another story. It is independent from the algorithm. It merely tells you that the a certain order could not be maintained. We figured back than, that this would be a good idea. It warns a developer about a potential issue, but does not raise an exception. With that in mind, the correct way to deal with the issue described right now, is to ignore the warning. BUT, that doesn't mean that you don't have a valid point. There are implicit and explicit orders. Not all assets require ordering and (random) orders that only exist because of Media merging don't matter at all. This brings me back to a point that I have [previously made](https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/30153#comment:6). It would make sense to store the original lists, which is now the case on master, and only raise if the order violates the original list. The current implementation on master could also be improved by removing duplicates. Anyways, I would considers those changes improvements, but not bug fixes. I didn't have time yet to look into this. But I do have some time this weekend. If you want I can take another look into this and propose a solution that solves this issue. Best -Joe
"Ignore the warning" doesn't work here - the order-fixing has broken the dependency between text-editor.js and text-editor-extras.js. I can (reluctantly) accept an implementation that produces false warnings, and I can accept that a genuine dependency loop might produce undefined behaviour, but the combination of the two - breaking the ordering as a result of seeing a loop that isn't there - is definitely a bug. (To be clear, I'm not suggesting that the 2.x implementation is a step backwards from not doing order checking at all - but it does introduce a new failure case, and that's what I'm keen to fix.)
To summarise: Even with the new strategy in #30153 of holding on to the un-merged lists as long as possible, the final merging is still done by adding one list at a time. The intermediate results are lists, which are assumed to be order-critical; this means the intermediate results have additional constraints that are not present in the original lists, causing it to see conflicts where there aren't any. Additionally, we should try to preserve the original sequence of files as much as possible, to avoid unnecessarily breaking user code that hasn't fully specified its dependencies and is relying on the 1.x behaviour. I think we need to approach this as a graph problem (which I realise might sound like overkill, but I'd rather start with something formally correct and optimise later as necessary): a conflict occurs whenever the dependency graph is cyclic. #30153 is a useful step towards this, as it ensures we have the accurate dependency graph up until the point where we need to assemble the final list. I suggest we replace Media.merge with a new method that accepts any number of lists (using *args if we want to preserve the existing method signature for backwards compatibility). This would work as follows: Iterate over all items in all sub-lists, building a dependency graph (where a dependency is any item that immediately precedes it within a sub-list) and a de-duplicated list containing all items indexed in the order they are first encountered Starting from the first item in the de-duplicated list, backtrack through the dependency graph, following the lowest-indexed dependency each time until we reach an item with no dependencies. While backtracking, maintain a stack of visited items. If we encounter an item already on the stack, this is a dependency loop; throw a MediaOrderConflictWarning and break out of the backtracking loop Output the resulting item, then remove it from the dependency graph and the de-duplicated list If the 'visited items' stack is non-empty, pop the last item off it and repeat the backtracking step from there. Otherwise, repeat the backtracking step starting from the next item in the de-duplicated list Repeat until no items remain
This sounds correct. I'm not sure it's right though. It does sound awfully complex for what there is to gain. Maintaining this down the road will not get easier. Finding, explaining and understanding the fix for #30153 did already cost a lot of time which could also have been invested elsewhere. If I manually assign widget3's JS lists (see https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/30179#comment:5) then everything just works and the final result is correct: # widget3 = Media(js=['text-editor.js', 'text-editor-extras.js', 'color-picker.js']) widget3 = Media() widget3._js_lists = [['text-editor.js', 'text-editor-extras.js'], ['color-picker.js']] So what you proposed first (https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/30179#comment:1) might just work fine and would be good enough (tm). Something like ​https://github.com/django/django/blob/543fc97407a932613d283c1e0bb47616cf8782e3/django/forms/widgets.py#L52 # Instead of self._js_lists = [js]: self._js_lists = list(js) if isinstance(js, set) else [js]
@Matthias: I think that solution will work, but only if: 1) we're going to insist that users always use this notation wherever a "non-dependency" exists - i.e. it is considered user error for the user to forget to put color-picker.js in its own sub-list 2) we have a very tight definition of what a dependency is - e.g. color-picker.js can't legally be a dependency of text-editor.js / text-editor-extras.js, because it exists on its own in ColorPicker's media - which also invalidates the [jquery, widget1, noconflict] + [jquery, widget2, noconflict] case (does noconflict depend on widget1 or not?) I suspect you only have to go slightly before the complexity of [jquery, widget1, noconflict] + [jquery, widget2, noconflict] before you start running into counter-examples again.
PR: ​https://github.com/django/django/pull/11010 I encountered another subtle bug along the way (which I suspect has existed since 1.x): #12879 calls for us to strip duplicates from the input lists, but in the current implementation the only de-duplication happens during Media.merge, so this never happens in the case of a single list. I've now extended the tests to cover this: ​https://github.com/django/django/pull/11010/files#diff-7fc04ae9019782c1884a0e97e96eda1eR154 . As a minor side effect of this extra de-duplication step, tuples get converted to lists more often, so I've had to fix up some existing tests accordingly - hopefully that's acceptable fall-out :-)
Matt, great work. I believe it is best to merge all lists at once and not sequentially as I did. Based on your work, I would suggest to simply use the algorithms implemented in Python. Therefore the whole merge function can be replaced with a simple one liner: import heapq from collections import OrderedDict def merge(*sublists): return list(OrderedDict.fromkeys(heapq.merge(*sublists))) # >>> merge([3],[1],[1,2],[2,3]) # [1, 2, 3]
It actually behaves different. I will continue to review your pull-request. As stated there, it would be helpful if there is some kind of resource to understand what strategy you implemented. For now I will try to review it without it.

problem_statement.Title: sqlmigrate prints BEGIN/COMMIT even when backend cannot roll back DDL
problem_statement.Description: The sqlmigrate management command prints transaction wrappers (BEGIN/COMMIT) around generated SQL whenever a migration is marked atomic. This can be misleading on database backends that do not support rolling back DDL, because the printed SQL implies the DDL would be executed inside a transaction when it would not be at runtime.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sqlmigrate uses migration.atomic alone to decide whether to show transaction wrappers. Location: django/core/management/commands/sqlmigrate.py: Command.handle sets self.output_transaction = migration.atomic (line 59). This encodes the assumption that migration.atomic is sufficient to indicate transactional DDL for display purposes.

2) BaseCommand.execute unconditionally wraps command output with connection.ops.start_transaction_sql()/connection.ops.end_transaction_sql() when self.output_transaction is True. Location: django/core/management/base.py: BaseCommand.execute (lines 366-372). This means the subclass-controlled boolean self.output_transaction is the sole determinant of whether BEGIN/COMMIT text is printed.

3) Runtime atomic execution is computed differently (conjunction of migration.atomic and the backend feature). Location & formula: django/db/backends/base/schema.py: BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor.__init__ sets self.atomic_migration = self.connection.features.can_rollback_ddl and atomic (line 97). MigrationExecutor.collect_sql passes atomic=migration.atomic to the schema editor (django/db/migrations/executor.py: line 221). The correct runtime relationship used by the schema editor is: atomic_migration = connection.features.can_rollback_ddl AND migration.atomic.

4) The default backend feature for rolling back DDL is False, so many backends will not support transactional DDL by default. Location & value: django/db/backends/base/features.py: BaseDatabaseFeatures.can_rollback_ddl = False (line 167). Some backends (e.g., PostgreSQL, SQLite) override this to True (django/db/backends/postgresql/features.py: can_rollback_ddl = True; django/db/backends/sqlite3/features.py: can_rollback_ddl = True).

5) Test coverage gap: existing tests verify that non-atomic migrations do not show transaction wrappers by using a non-atomic migration module, but there is no test that verifies the case where migration.atomic is True and connection.features.can_rollback_ddl is False. Location: tests/migrations/test_commands.py contains test_sqlmigrate_for_non_atomic_migration (lines ~606-618) but no test for atomic=True + can_rollback_ddl=False.

6) Related commands do not follow the same conditional logic for display: other management commands that output SQL set output_transaction = True at class-level and thus will always print wrappers, regardless of connection.features.can_rollback_ddl. Locations: django/core/management/commands/sqlflush.py (output_transaction = True, line 12) and django/core/management/commands/sqlsequencereset.py (output_transaction = True, line 8).
StepsToReproduce: Use a migration that is marked atomic (migration.atomic == True).
Ensure the current database backend does not support transactional DDL (connection.features.can_rollback_ddl == False). For testing, patch or mock connection.features.can_rollback_ddl to False.
Run the management command: ./manage.py sqlmigrate <app_label> <migration_name> (or call_command('sqlmigrate', ...)).
Observe that BEGIN and COMMIT lines are present around the generated SQL output even though the backend cannot roll back DDL.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When displaying SQL for a migration, BEGIN/COMMIT transaction wrappers are shown only when the displayed transaction would reflect actual transactional DDL at runtime — i.e., only when the migration is atomic and the current database backend supports rolling back DDL (migration.atomic is True AND connection.features.can_rollback_ddl is True).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: sqlmigrate displays BEGIN/COMMIT wrappers whenever migration.atomic is True, even on backends where connection.features.can_rollback_ddl is False, causing the printed SQL to imply transactional DDL where it would not occur at runtime.

problem_statement.Title: DurationField: user-facing invalid-format message shows wrong duration syntax
problem_statement.Description: The error message shown for models.DurationField reports an incorrect accepted-format string. Example: entering "14:00" is parsed as "00:14:00" (14 minutes) by the parser, but the model-level invalid-format message shown to users says the format is "[DD] [HH:[MM:]]ss[.uuuuuu]", which does not match the parser's actual requirements. The DurationField class also does not provide a default help_text for forms/admin to indicate the expected syntax.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect hardcoded human-facing format string in django/db/models/fields/__init__.py, class DurationField, attribute default_error_messages['invalid']. Current value (exact text found in code): "'%(value)s' value has an invalid format. It must be in [DD] [HH:[MM:]]ss[.uuuuuu] format." (django/db/models/fields/__init__.py, lines 1588–1591). The hardcoded string is inconsistent with parsing/serialization behavior and is the direct source of the misleading message observed by users.

2) The authoritative parser behavior (django/utils/dateparse.py: standard_duration_re / parse_duration) requires seconds and allows minutes optionally; hours are only matched when the input is in HH:MM:SS form. Evidence in the regex: the seconds group is mandatory (?P<seconds>\d+) (line 35), minutes are optional (?:(?P<minutes>\d+):)? (line 34), and the hours group is guarded by a lookahead ((?:(?P<hours>\d+):)(?=\d+:\d+))? so hours only match when followed by two colon-separated groups (line 33). This parser behavior is the source-of-truth for accepted input formats (django/utils/dateparse.py, lines 29–37 and parse_duration usage lines 133–147).

3) The serializer/formatter (django/utils/duration.py: duration_string) emits strings as "{:02d}:{:02d}:{:02d}" with optional leading days and optional ".uuuuuu" for microseconds (lines 22–26). That output always includes seconds and is consistent with the parser's requirement that seconds are present; this alignment confirms the fault is the human-facing message rather than parsing/serialization.

4) DurationField does not supply a default user-facing help_text describing the accepted format. The DurationField class does not override __init__ and therefore uses Field.__init__ default help_text (empty string); there is no default help_text specific to DurationField in the inspected code (django/db/models/fields/__init__.py: DurationField class, lines 1580–1638).

5) Forms-level DurationField (django/forms/fields.py: DurationField) uses a generic invalid message ('Enter a valid duration.') and delegates parsing to parse_duration (lines 469–495). While the forms field correctly delegates to the authoritative parser, it does not expose the detailed format string to users; the inconsistent model-level message is therefore the main misleading string at model validation time.

6) Tests that would detect this wording mismatch are not present in the inspected test module for field error messages: tests/validation/test_error_messages.py contains many checks for other temporal field messages (DateField, DateTimeField, TimeField) but does not assert DurationField's invalid-format message or form help_text (tests/validation/test_error_messages.py, lines 1–91). This increases the risk of the incorrect message persisting undetected.
StepsToReproduce: Create a model with a DurationField (or use an existing model with a DurationField).
In a ModelForm or the admin, enter the string "14:00" into the duration field and submit/save.

Observe the parsed/stored value (the parser interprets "14:00" as 00:14:00 i.e., 14 minutes) and the validation error/help text shown to the user (the model invalid-format message displays "[DD] [HH:[MM:]]ss[.uuuuuu]").
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: User-visible validation messages and any default help_text for DurationField should correctly describe the syntax actually accepted by the parser/serializer — i.e., that seconds are mandatory, minutes are optional, and hours are optional except when the input is provided as HH:MM:SS. The format presented to users (error message / help text) should match the parser/serializer behavior, for example as the bracketed form: "[DD] [[HH:]MM:]ss[.uuuuuu]".
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The model-level invalid-format message for DurationField currently states "[DD] [HH:[MM:]]ss[.uuuuuu]", which is inconsistent with the parser/serializer behavior (seconds mandatory, minutes optional, hours only for HH:MM:SS). The forms-level DurationField uses a generic 'Enter a valid duration.' message and DurationField provides no default help_text, so users do not receive a correct, specific format hint. Example: entering "14:00" is parsed as "00:14:00" (14 minutes), but the model error text implies a different nesting of optional components.

problem_statement.Title: UsernameValidator allows trailing newline in usernames
problem_statement.Description: ASCIIUsernameValidator and UnicodeUsernameValidator accept usernames that end with a trailing newline because their class-level regex patterns use ^...$ anchors which, in Python, allow a match before a final newline.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. Incorrect end-of-string assumption in ASCIIUsernameValidator. Location: django/contrib/auth/validators.py — ASCIIUsernameValidator.regex is set to r'^[\w.@+-]+$'. Rationale: the pattern uses ^ and $ but $ can match immediately before a final '\n', so the validator's pattern does not enforce an absolute end-of-string in the presence of a trailing newline.

2. Incorrect end-of-string assumption repeated in UnicodeUsernameValidator. Location: django/contrib/auth/validators.py — UnicodeUsernameValidator.regex is set to r'^[\w.@+-]+$'. Rationale: same as (1); the pattern allows inputs that end with a newline.

3. Operational context that makes the anchors critical: RegexValidator applies the compiled regex via a search operation. Location: django/core/validators.py::RegexValidator (__init__ and __call__). Rationale: RegexValidator compiles regexes lazily and evaluates them with self.regex.search(str(value)), so the pattern itself must encode whole-string constraints; an internal $ anchor that can match before a final newline is insufficient to guarantee rejection of trailing-newline inputs.

4. Project convention and precedent for absolute anchors. Location: django/core/validators.py examples (e.g. URLValidator, integer_validator, slug_re and EmailValidator regexes) show use of \Z or equivalent to avoid $ newline behavior. Rationale: the codebase contains other validators that use anchors which do not treat a final newline as a match position, indicating the required matching semantics for strict full-string validation.

5. Missing test coverage for this edge case. Location: tests/auth_tests/test_validators.py — UsernameValidatorsTests exercises allowed/disallowed characters but contains no test asserting that usernames ending with '\n' are rejected. Rationale: absence of test coverage for trailing-newline inputs allows this behavior to persist unnoticed.

6. Operational impact because validators are used as defaults and are deconstructible. Location: django/contrib/auth/validators.py (classes are @deconstructible) and django/contrib/auth/models.py (AbstractUser.username_validator = UnicodeUsernameValidator()). Rationale: the validator instances are used by the User model and are deconstructible for migrations/serialization, so the validators' default behavior is surfaced in model validation and may affect migrations/serialized state.
StepsToReproduce: Instantiate UnicodeUsernameValidator() and call it with the value 'joe\n'. Observe whether a ValidationError is raised.
Instantiate ASCIIUsernameValidator() and call it with the value 'glenn\n'. Observe whether a ValidationError is raised.
Attempt to create or save a User (or another model field using the username validator) with username set to 'alice\n' and observe whether model validation allows it.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Usernames that end with a newline character are rejected by the username validators and produce a validation error when validating or saving user data.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Username validators accept strings that end with a newline (for example, 'joe\n' is considered valid), allowing creation or validation of usernames with trailing newlines.

problem_statement.Title: HttpResponse mishandles memoryview/bytearray and returns repr bytes instead of underlying data
problem_statement.Description: When a memoryview or bytearray is passed into HttpResponse (for example HttpResponse(memoryview(b"My Content"))), the response body becomes the object's repr encoded as bytes (b'<memory at 0x...>' or b"bytearray(...)") instead of the underlying binary data (b'My Content'). This typically occurs when database adapters (e.g. some PostgreSQL drivers) return binary fields as memoryview objects.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) django/http/response.py::HttpResponseBase.make_bytes — incorrect assumption: non-bytes, non-str values are treated as arbitrary objects and are stringified and encoded. The method's fallback is str(value).encode(self.charset), which causes memoryview or bytearray objects to be serialized to their Python repr when converted to response bytes (see make_bytes: fallback -> str(value).encode(...)).

2) django/http/response.py::HttpResponse.content (setter) — incorrect assumption: any object with __iter__ (except bytes and str) should be treated as an iterable of content chunks. The setter detects iterability via hasattr(value, '__iter__') and excludes only (bytes, str), causing memoryview and bytearray (both iterable) to be iterated and their elements to be joined as if they were chunk sequences (see content.setter: if hasattr(value, '__iter__') and not isinstance(value, (bytes, str)): content = b''.join(self.make_bytes(chunk) for chunk in value)).

3) Coupled behavior — both locations share the same root cause pattern (misclassification of bytes-like/buffer-like objects). Because make_bytes stringifies unexpected types and content.setter may pre-iterate buffer-like objects, both assumptions together cause the observed incorrect output. Either behavior alone can produce incorrect results for memoryview/bytearray inputs.

4) StreamingHttpResponse semantics (related edge case) — StreamingHttpResponse._set_streaming_content stores iter(value) and streaming_content maps make_bytes over that iterator. If a user passes a bare memoryview as streaming_content, iter(memoryview) yields element-wise items (integers/bytes), so the streaming response will process those items rather than treating the memoryview as a single bytes-like chunk. If the iterator yields memoryview objects (e.g., [memoryview(b'...')]), the chunk items would be memoryviews and treated according to make_bytes. This is a related API/semantics edge case (see StreamingHttpResponse._set_streaming_content and streaming_content property).

5) Test coverage gap — there are tests covering memoryview handling in utilities (force_bytes) and in certain model fields, but there are no unit tests in the repository exercising HttpResponse/StreamingHttpResponse constructed directly with memoryview or bytearray inputs (constructor, write/writelines, streaming_content as a bare memoryview). The lack of targeted tests allowed this behavior to go unnoticed by response-specific tests.
StepsToReproduce: from django.http import HttpResponse
response = HttpResponse("My Content")
inspect response.content  # shows b'My Content'
response = HttpResponse(b"My Content")
inspect response.content  # shows b'My Content'
response = HttpResponse(memoryview(b"My Content"))
inspect response.content  # shows b'<memory at 0x...>' (incorrect)
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When given bytes-like inputs (bytes, bytearray, memoryview), HttpResponse.content should contain the underlying binary data. Example: HttpResponse(memoryview(b"My Content")).content == b"My Content". Similarly, HttpResponse(bytearray(b"...")).content should equal the underlying bytes. For streaming responses, an iterable that yields memoryview objects should produce their underlying bytes when assembled; the behavior when a bare memoryview is passed as streaming_content is an API semantic to be clarified.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: HttpResponse(memoryview(b"My Content")).content returns the memoryview object's repr encoded as bytes (e.g. b"<memory at 0x7fcc47ab2648>") instead of b"My Content". Memoryview/bytearray are either stringified by make_bytes or iterated and joined by content.setter, producing incorrect output.

hints_text:
I guess HttpResponseBase.make_bytes ​could be adapted to deal with memoryview objects by casting them to bytes. In all cases simply wrapping the memoryview in bytes works as a workaround HttpResponse(bytes(model.binary_field)).
The fact make_bytes would still use force_bytes if da56e1bac6449daef9aeab8d076d2594d9fd5b44 didn't refactor it and that d680a3f4477056c69629b0421db4bb254b8c69d0 added memoryview support to force_bytes strengthen my assumption that make_bytes should be adjusted as well.
I'll try to work on this.

problem_statement.Title: Collector.delete fast-path does not clear in-memory instance PK after delete
problem_statement.Description: Collector.delete contains a fast-path for deleting a single model instance with no dependencies that issues the database DELETE and returns before performing the per-instance in-memory cleanup. As a result, the in-memory instance retains its old primary key (pk) after calling .delete().
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Fast-path skips per-instance cleanup: The single-object fast-path in Collector.delete issues the SQL DELETE and returns without performing the in-memory cleanup that clears instance primary key attributes. Location: django/db/models/deletion.py :: Collector.delete (fast-path branch). Evidence: the code calls sql.DeleteQuery(model).delete_batch([instance.pk], self.using) inside transaction.mark_for_rollback_on_error() and immediately returns (see deletion.py lines 278–280), and does not call setattr(instance, model._meta.pk.attname, None) in that branch.

2) Non-fast-path performs explicit PK clearing: The normal delete path performs a final update of collected instances that sets per-instance PK attributes to None via setattr(instance, model._meta.pk.attname, None). Location: django/db/models/deletion.py :: Collector.delete (final cleanup loop). Evidence: the final cleanup loop sets the pk attr to None for each instance in self.data (see deletion.py lines 324–326).

3) Call sites rely on Collector.delete for post-delete in-memory state: Model.delete and QuerySet.delete invoke Collector.collect(...) and then return collector.delete(), and do not clear instance.pk themselves. Location(s): django/db/models/base.py :: Model.delete (lines 917–919) and django/db/models/query.py :: QuerySet.delete (lines 698–700). This means the omitted cleanup in the fast-path results in caller-visible instances retaining their old pk.

4) fast_deletes is a separate code path that was not fully audited here: Collector.fast_deletes collects queryset-like objects that are deleted via qs._raw_delete(...) during Collector.delete (deletion.py lines 290–293). Those objects are not part of self.data and so are not affected by the final self.data cleanup loop; the implications for in-memory state of caller-held instances related to fast_deletes were not fully audited in the investigation.

5) Observed return encoding at fast-path: The fast-path returns a tuple (count, {model._meta.label: count}) (deletion.py line 280). This is an observed behavior of the fast-path branch (an encoded return shape) and is part of the current implementation at that location.

6) Investigation gaps (audit notes): The prior investigation did not (a) fully audit interactions between the fast-path and Collector.fast_deletes to confirm any additional in-memory cleanup needs, (b) fully verify semantics with pre_delete/post_delete signal handlers in all edge cases, and (c) provide an attached unit test demonstrating the in-memory pk becomes None (or not) after invoking the fast-path .delete(). These gaps affect confidence in the broader scope but do not change the primary diagnosis above.
StepsToReproduce: Define or use a Django model that has no related/dependent objects.
Create and save an instance of that model, e.g. obj = MyModel.objects.create(...).
Call obj.delete().
Inspect the in-memory instance's primary key, e.g. print(obj.pk) or assert obj.pk is None.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: After calling .delete() on a model instance with no dependencies, the instance's in-memory primary key (pk) should be cleared (None) to reflect that the database row no longer exists.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: After calling .delete() on a model instance with no dependencies using the fast-path, the in-memory instance's pk still holds the previous value (not None) even though the database row was deleted.

Regression test.
I have attached a simple fix which mimics what ​https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/db/models/deletion.py#L324-L326 does for multiple objects. I am not sure if we need ​https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/db/models/deletion.py#L320-L323 (the block above) because I think field_updates is only ever filled if the objects are not fast-deletable -- ie ​https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/db/models/deletion.py#L224 is not called due to the can_fast_delete check at the beginning of the collect function. That said, if we want to be extra "safe" we can just move lines 320 - 326 into an extra function and call that from the old and new location (though I do not think it is needed).

+                ).update(content_type=new_content_type)
+        except IntegrityError:
+            old = '{}_{}'.format(old_content_type.app_label, old_content_type.model)
+            new = '{}_{}'.format(new_content_type.app_label, new_content_type.model)
+            sys.stdout.write(style.WARNING(WARNING.format(old=old, new=new, query=permissions_query)))

problem_statement.Title: auth.0011_update_proxy_permissions migration fails with IntegrityError when target permission already exists
problem_statement.Description: When applying Django auth migration 0011_update_proxy_permissions, the migration can raise an IntegrityError because it attempts to change Permission.content_type in bulk and thereby produce a duplicate (content_type, codename) pair that violates the Permission model's uniqueness constraint.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) The migration function update_proxy_model_permissions in django/contrib/auth/migrations/0011_update_proxy_permissions.py performs a bulk update of Permission.content_type via Permission.objects.filter(...).update(content_type=new_content_type). This encodes an incorrect assumption that bulk-updating the content_type cannot produce duplicate (content_type, codename) pairs. (location: django/contrib/auth/migrations/0011_update_proxy_permissions.py, lines 27–30)

2) The Permission model enforces uniqueness on (content_type, codename) via Meta.unique_together = (('content_type', 'codename'),). The database enforces this invariant and will reject any operation that results in two Permission rows with the same (content_type, codename). (location: django/contrib/auth/models.py, line 69)

3) If a Permission with the target (new_content_type, codename) already exists prior to running the migration, the bulk update will attempt to change the old Permission's content_type to new_content_type and thereby create a duplicate row, causing a database IntegrityError. The original error reported is an IntegrityError for duplicate key on (content_type_id, codename) (e.g. Key (content_type_id, codename)=(12, add_agency) already exists). (evidence: original stacktrace; migration file lines 27–30)

4) The migration uses the same update function for both applying and unapplying the migration: revert_proxy_model_permissions calls update_proxy_model_permissions(apps, schema_editor, reverse=True). Therefore the same bulk-update assumption affects both forward and reverse migration paths. (location: django/contrib/auth/migrations/0011_update_proxy_permissions.py, lines 33–38)

5) The test coverage in tests/auth_tests/test_migrations.py sets up permission rows pointing at the concrete model and exercises the code path where the bulk update is safe; the tests do not create a pre-existing conflicting Permission at the proxy content_type and therefore do not exercise the duplicate-collision scenario. This gap in test coverage means the unsafe bulk-update assumption was not caught by the existing tests. (location: tests/auth_tests/test_migrations.py, e.g. setUp() creating permissions with content_type=self.concrete_content_type)

6) Any handling of colliding permissions is constrained by many-to-many relationships between User/Group and Permission (User.user_permissions and Group.permissions are ManyToManyFields). These M2M relations imply through-table uniqueness semantics for (user_id, permission_id) and (group_id, permission_id) and must be accounted for by any operation that merges or replaces Permission rows. The code locations that illustrate these relations are in django/contrib/auth/models.py where the ManyToManyFields to Permission are defined. (location: django/contrib/auth/models.py, lines 109–113 and 224–231)
StepsToReproduce: Have a Django project containing the auth and contenttypes apps and a migration state before auth.0011_update_proxy_permissions has been applied.

Ensure there are two ContentType rows for a model: the concrete model content_type and the proxy model content_type (for example, when a model has been recreated as a proxy or when proxy content types already exist).

Create or ensure there exists a Permission with codename X for the proxy content_type (the migration's target content_type), and at least one Permission with the same codename X for the concrete content_type (the migration's source content_type).
Run python manage.py migrate (or python manage.py migrate auth 0011).
Observe an IntegrityError raised for duplicate key on the unique (content_type, codename) constraint (e.g. Key (content_type_id, codename)=(12, add_agency) already exists).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The auth.0011_update_proxy_permissions migration completes without raising an IntegrityError. If a permission with the target (content_type, codename) already exists, the migration does not result in duplicate (content_type, codename) rows and preserves the intended user/group permission assignments (so users/groups retain the correct permissions after the migration).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Running the migration raises a django.db.utils.IntegrityError: duplicate key value violates unique constraint on auth_permission unique index (unique on (content_type_id, codename)). Example: Key (content_type_id, codename)=(12, add_agency) already exists. The migration triggers this by performing a bulk update of Permission.content_type that can produce duplicate (content_type, codename) pairs.

hints_text:
Please provide a sample project or enough details to reproduce the issue.
Same problem for me. If a Permission exists already with the new content_type and permission name, IntegrityError is raised since it violates the unique_key constraint on permission model i.e. content_type_id_code_name
To get into the situation where you already have permissions with the content type you should be able to do the following: Start on Django <2.2 Create a model called 'TestModel' Migrate Delete the model called 'TestModel' Add a new proxy model called 'TestModel' Migrate Update to Django >=2.2 Migrate We think this is what happened in our case where we found this issue (​https://sentry.thalia.nu/share/issue/68be0f8c32764dec97855b3cbb3d8b55/). We have a proxy model with the same name that a previous non-proxy model once had. This changed during a refactor and the permissions + content type for the original model still exist. Our solution will probably be removing the existing permissions from the table, but that's really only a workaround.
Reproduced with steps from comment. It's probably regression in 181fb60159e54d442d3610f4afba6f066a6dac05.
What happens when creating a regular model, deleting it and creating a new proxy model: Create model 'RegularThenProxyModel' +----------------------------------+---------------------------+-----------------------+ | name | codename | model | +----------------------------------+---------------------------+-----------------------+ | Can add regular then proxy model | add_regularthenproxymodel | regularthenproxymodel | +----------------------------------+---------------------------+-----------------------+ Migrate Delete the model called 'RegularThenProxyModel' Add a new proxy model called 'RegularThenProxyModel' +----------------------------------+---------------------------+-----------------------+ | name | codename | model | +----------------------------------+---------------------------+-----------------------+ | Can add concrete model | add_concretemodel | concretemodel | | Can add regular then proxy model | add_regularthenproxymodel | concretemodel | | Can add regular then proxy model | add_regularthenproxymodel | regularthenproxymodel | +----------------------------------+---------------------------+-----------------------+ What happens when creating a proxy model right away: Create a proxy model 'RegularThenProxyModel' +----------------------------------+---------------------------+---------------+ | name | codename | model | +----------------------------------+---------------------------+---------------+ | Can add concrete model | add_concretemodel | concretemodel | | Can add regular then proxy model | add_regularthenproxymodel | concretemodel | +----------------------------------+---------------------------+---------------+ As you can see, the problem here is that permissions are not cleaned up, so we are left with an existing | Can add regular then proxy model | add_regularthenproxymodel | regularthenproxymodel | row. When the 2.2 migration is applied, it tries to create that exact same row, hence the IntegrityError. Unfortunately, there is no remove_stale_permission management command like the one for ContentType. So I think we can do one of the following: Show a nice error message to let the user delete the conflicting migration OR Re-use the existing permission I think 1. is much safer as it will force users to use a new permission and assign it accordingly to users/groups. Edit: I revised my initial comment after reproducing the error in my environment.
It's also possible to get this kind of integrity error on the auth.0011 migration if another app is migrated first causing the auth post_migrations hook to run. The auth post migrations hook runs django.contrib.auth.management.create_permissions, which writes the new form of the auth_permission records to the table. Then when the auth.0011 migration runs it tries to update things to the values that were just written. To reproduce this behavior: pip install Django==2.1.7 Create an app, let's call it app, with two models, TestModel(models.Model) and ProxyModel(TestModel) the second one with proxy=True python manage.py makemigrations python manage.py migrate pip install Django==2.2 Add another model to app python manage.py makemigrations migrate the app only, python manage.py migrate app. This does not run the auth migrations, but does run the auth post_migrations hook Note that new records have been added to auth_permission python manage.py migrate, this causes an integrity error when the auth.0011 migration tries to update records that are the same as the ones already added in step 8. This has the same exception as this bug report, I don't know if it's considered a different bug, or the same one.
Yes it is the same issue. My recommendation to let the users figure it out with a helpful message still stands even if it may sound a bit painful, because: It prevents data loss (we don't do an automatic delete/create of permissions) It prevents security oversights (we don't re-use an existing permission) It shouldn't happen for most use cases Again, I would love to hear some feedback or other alternatives.
I won’t have time to work on this for the next 2 weeks so I’m de-assigning myself. I’ll pick it up again if nobody does and I’m available to discuss feedback/suggestions.
I'll make a patch for this. I'll see about raising a suitable warning from the migration but we already warn in the release notes for this to audit permissions: my initial thought was that re-using the permission would be OK. (I see Arthur's comment. Other thoughts?)
Being my first contribution I wanted to be super (super) careful with security concerns, but given the existing warning in the release notes for auditing prior to update, I agree that re-using the permission feels pretty safe and would remove overhead for people running into this scenario. Thanks for taking this on Carlton, I'd be happy to review.
