problem_statement.Title: Field.contribute_to_class unconditionally overwrites user-defined get_<field>_display (Django 2.2+)
problem_statement.Description: Model-defined overrides of get_<field>_display() (for example get_foo_bar_display) are ignored in the examined Django 2.2+ code. During model class creation generated accessor methods are installed onto the model class after user-defined methods are attached, and those generated methods replace any existing attributes with the same name.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) django/db/models/fields/__init__.py :: Field.contribute_to_class — the method unconditionally assigns a generated accessor using setattr(cls, 'get_%s_display' % self.name, partialmethod(cls._get_FIELD_display, field=self)) when a field has choices. This encodes the assumption that it is always safe to overwrite any existing attribute named get_<field>_display on the model class, which is not true given the model-class construction ordering. (See fields/__init__.py: lines that call setattr for 'get_%s_display'.)

2) django/db/models/fields/__init__.py :: DateField.contribute_to_class — the method unconditionally assigns generated accessors using setattr(cls, 'get_next_by_%s' % self.name, ...) and setattr(cls, 'get_previous_by_%s' % self.name, ...) when appropriate. This encodes the assumption that it is always safe to overwrite any existing get_next_by_<field> and get_previous_by_<field> methods on the model class. (See fields/__init__.py: lines that call setattr for 'get_next_by_%s' and 'get_previous_by_%s'.)

3) django/db/models/base.py :: ModelBase.__new__ / add_to_class — the metaclass separates attributes that implement contribute_to_class from normal attributes and passes non-contributable attributes (including user-defined methods declared in the model body) into type.__new__ so they become part of the class object first; contributable attributes (field instances) are added afterward via add_to_class which calls value.contribute_to_class(cls, name). Because contribute_to_class for Field and DateField performs unconditional setattr after user methods already exist on the class, user-defined methods named get_<field>_display, get_next_by_<field>, or get_previous_by_<field> can be replaced.

4) Relationship between generated accessors and existing attributes — the generated accessor names correspond exactly to the field name (e.g., get_{field.name}_display, get_next_by_{field.name}, get_previous_by_{field.name}), and the code as written installs those names unconditionally. Given the metaclass ordering, this means the generated accessors will replace any user-defined attributes with the same names; the correct diagnostic relationship is that generated accessors for a field name will collide with any pre-existing attribute of the same name on the model class.
StepsToReproduce: Create and save an instance: FooBar.objects.create(foo_bar=1) or instantiate in the shell: FooBar(foo_bar=1).
Call print(str(instance)) or evaluate instance.__str__() / instance.get_foo_bar_display() and observe the returned value.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When a model defines a method named get_<field>_display (e.g. get_foo_bar_display), calls to that method (including calls made from __str__ or other model code) should invoke the user-defined method and return its result (e.g. "something").
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: In the examined Django 2.2+ code, a user-defined get_<field>_display method is overwritten at class-creation time by a generated accessor installed by Field.contribute_to_class; the generated accessor is called instead and returns the field's choice label (e.g. 'foo' or 'bar') rather than the user method's result.

hints_text:
Thanks for this report. Regression in a68ea231012434b522ce45c513d84add516afa60. Reproduced at 54a7b021125d23a248e70ba17bf8b10bc8619234.
OK, I have a lead on this. Not at all happy about how it looks at first pass, but I'll a proof of concept PR together for it tomorrow AM.
I don't think it should be marked as blocker since it looks like it was never supported, because it depends on the order of attrs passed in ModelBase.__new__(). So on Django 2.1 and Python 3.7: In [1]: import django ...: django.VERSION In [2]: from django.db import models ...: ...: class FooBar(models.Model): ...: def get_foo_bar_display(self): ...: return "something" ...: ...: foo_bar = models.CharField("foo", choices=[(1, 'foo'), (2, 'bar')]) ...: ...: def __str__(self): ...: return self.get_foo_bar_display() # This returns 'foo' or 'bar' in 2.2, but 'something' in 2.1 ...: ...: class Meta: ...: app_label = 'test' ...: ...: FooBar(foo_bar=1) Out[2]: <FooBar: foo> Before ​Python 3.6 the order of attrs wasn't defined at all.
Sergey, an example from the ticket description works for me with Django 2.1 and Python 3.6, 3.7 and 3.8.
In [2]: import django ...: django.VERSION Out[2]: (2, 1, 13, 'final', 0) In [3]: import sys ...: sys.version Out[3]: '3.5.7 (default, Oct 17 2019, 07:04:41) \n[GCC 8.3.0]' In [4]: from django.db import models ...: ...: class FooBar(models.Model): ...: foo_bar = models.CharField("foo", choices=[(1, 'foo'), (2, 'bar')]) ...: ...: def __str__(self): ...: return self.get_foo_bar_display() # This returns 'foo' or 'bar' in 2.2, but 'something' in 2.1 ...: ...: def get_foo_bar_display(self): ...: return "something" ...: ...: class Meta: ...: app_label = 'test' ...: ...: FooBar(foo_bar=1) Out[4]: <FooBar: foo>
OK, so there is a behaviour change here, but Sergey is correct that it does depend on attr order, so it's hard to say that this can be said to ever have been thought of as supported, with the exact example provided. This example produces the opposite result on 2.1 (even on >=PY36): def test_overriding_display_backwards(self): class FooBar2(models.Model): def get_foo_bar_display(self): return "something" foo_bar = models.CharField("foo", choices=[(1, 'foo'), (2, 'bar')]) f = FooBar2(foo_bar=1) # This returns 'foo' or 'bar' in both 2.2 and 2.1 self.assertEqual(f.get_foo_bar_display(), "foo") Because get_foo_bar_display() is defined before foo_bar is gets replaced in the the add_to_class() step. Semantically order shouldn't make a difference. Given that it does, I can't see that we're bound to maintain that behaviour. (There's a possible fix in Field.contribute_to_class() but implementing that just reverses the pass/fail behaviour depending on order...) Rather, the correct way to implement this on 2.2+ is: def test_overriding_display(self): class FooBar(models.Model): foo_bar = models.CharField("foo", choices=[(1, 'foo'), (2, 'bar')]) def _get_FIELD_display(self, field): if field.attname == 'foo_bar': return "something" return super()._get_FIELD_display(field) f = FooBar(foo_bar=1) self.assertEqual(f.get_foo_bar_display(), "something") This is stable for declaration order on version 2.2+. This approach requires overriding _get_FIELD_display() before declaring fields on 2.1, because otherwise Model._get_FIELD_display() is picked up during Field.contribute_to_class(). This ordering dependency is, ultimately, the same issue that was addressed in a68ea231012434b522ce45c513d84add516afa60, and the follow-up in #30254. The behaviour in 2.1 (and before) was incorrect. Yes, there's a behaviour change here but it's a bugfix, and all bugfixes are breaking changes if you're depending on the broken behaviour. I'm going to downgrade this from Release Blocker accordingly. I'll reclassify this as a Documentation issue and provide the working example, as overriding _get_FIELD_display() is a legitimate use-case I'd guess.
Replying to Carlton Gibson: (There's a possible fix in Field.contribute_to_class() but implementing that just reverses the pass/fail behaviour depending on order...) Doesn't this fix it? if not hasattr(cls, 'get_%s_display' % self.name): setattr(cls, 'get_%s_display' % self.name, partialmethod(cls._get_FIELD_display, field=self))

problem_statement.Title: admin_views.test_multidb fails with persistent per-alias SQLite test DBs: sqlite3.OperationalError: database is locked
problem_statement.Description: When using persistent per-alias SQLite test files (TEST['NAME']) together with --keepdb, tests that touch multiple database aliases can fail with sqlite3.OperationalError: database is locked because two distinct aliases can be grouped as the same underlying test database and both attempt concurrent writes to the same file.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. django/db/backends/sqlite3/creation.py :: DatabaseCreation.test_db_signature builds the test-database signature from connection.settings_dict['NAME'] (the configured/production NAME) and omits the computed test database name returned by self._get_test_db_name(), except for a special-case addition of the alias for in-memory URIs. Evidence: sqlite3/creation.py lines showing _get_test_db_name() uses TEST['NAME'] and test_db_signature constructs sig = [self.connection.settings_dict['NAME']] (sqlite3/creation.py lines 17-21 and 97-101).

2. django/test/utils.py :: get_unique_databases_and_mirrors and setup_databases group database aliases by connection.creation.test_db_signature() and create one test database per signature, configuring other aliases with the same signature as mirrors. Because the sqlite3 signature omits the computed test DB name, aliases configured with distinct TEST['NAME'] values can receive identical signatures and be incorrectly treated as mirrors. Evidence: test utils code that keys test_databases by connection.creation.test_db_signature() and then creates the DB once per signature (django/test/utils.py lines 270-275 and 154-185).

3. django/db/backends/base/creation.py :: BaseDatabaseCreation.test_db_signature includes self._get_test_db_name() in the signature. The sqlite3 implementation therefore deviates from the base-class pattern/expectation that the computed test DB name (which reflects TEST['NAME'] when present) is part of the test-database identity used to deduplicate test DB creation. Evidence: base class test_db_signature returns a tuple including self._get_test_db_name() (base/creation.py lines 285-297).

4. The practical consequence: when two database aliases are intended to use different persistent SQLite test files (different TEST['NAME'] values) but are grouped by an incorrect signature, both aliases can be configured to use the same physical SQLite file. Tests that perform writes across aliases during setup (e.g., setUpTestData) can then perform concurrent or overlapping writes against the same SQLite file and raise sqlite3.OperationalError: database is locked. Concrete repro path observed in tests/admin_views/test_multidb.MultiDatabaseTests.setUpTestData where create_superuser / model.save() are invoked for each alias. Evidence: tests/admin_views/test_multidb.py lines 33-44 and the original failure stack trace in the bug report showing the error during User.save() in setUpTestData.

5. Correct relationship that is missing from the sqlite3 signature (diagnostic statement): the test database identity used to deduplicate/test-creation decisions must reflect the computed test database name that will actually be created/used by the test runner (i.e., the value returned by _get_test_db_name(), which honors TEST['NAME'] and special memory URIs). The in-memory special-case (distinct in-memory DB URIs per alias) must continue to be handled so in-memory test DBs remain distinct as appropriate.
StepsToReproduce: Configure two sqlite DATABASES aliases (e.g. 'default' and 'other') and set distinct persistent test file names: TEST['NAME'] = 'test_default.sqlite3' and TEST['NAME'] = 'test_other.sqlite3'.
Run the test runner for the failing test with keepdb enabled and no parallelism: ./tests/runtests.py admin_views.test_multidb -v 3 --keepdb --parallel 1

Observe the test setup failing during class-level setup (setUpTestData) with sqlite3.OperationalError: database is locked raised during model.save() (create_superuser path in the failure stack trace).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When separate DATABASES aliases are configured with distinct persistent TEST['NAME'] SQLite files and --keepdb is used, the test runner should create/use separate test database files per alias (or otherwise preserve the intended per-alias test-database identity), so tests that write to each alias do not conflict and no SQLite "database is locked" errors occur during test setup.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Aliases configured with different persistent TEST['NAME'] were grouped by the test runner as the same test database (treated as mirrors). Two aliases ended up pointing to the same SQLite file and concurrent writes during test setup produced sqlite3.OperationalError: database is locked (stack trace shows failure during User.save in setUpTestData).

problem_statement.Title: makemigrations serializes nested (inner) classes as top-level, dropping outer-class qualifier
problem_statement.Description: When a Field subclass or enum class is defined as an inner (nested) class and used on a Model, manage.py makemigrations produces migration code that refers to the nested class as if it were a top-level class in the module (for example, test1.models.State or test1.models.Inner) instead of preserving the outer-class qualifier (for example, test1.models.Thing.State or test1.models.Outer.Inner). The generated migration file can also contain import statements that treat a class-qualified path segment as if it were a module.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) TypeSerializer.builds the type path using the class's __name__, dropping nesting qualifiers.
   - Location: django/db/migrations/serializer.py :: TypeSerializer.serialize (lines ~258–273).
   - Concrete behavior: the serializer returns "%s.%s" % (module, self.value.__name__) for non-builtin types, so a nested class with __qualname__ "Outer.Inner" becomes module + "Inner" instead of module + "Outer.Inner".

2) DeconstructableSerializer._serialize_path assumes the left-hand side of path.rsplit('.', 1) is an importable module and emits an import for it.
   - Location: django/db/migrations/serializer.py :: DeconstructableSerializer._serialize_path (lines ~92–100).
   - Concrete behavior: the method does module, name = path.rsplit('.', 1) and then unconditionally uses imports = {"import %s" % module} (except for a django.db.models special-case). If path is produced with a qualname that contains dots (e.g. "test1.models.Thing.State"), the code treats "test1.models.Thing" as a module and emits "import test1.models.Thing".

3) Field.deconstruct produces paths that include the class __qualname__, so qualname-containing paths are expected input to the serializer.
   - Location: django/db/models/fields/__init__.py :: Field.deconstruct (lines ~490–501).
   - Concrete behavior: deconstruct builds path = "%s.%s" % (self.__class__.__module__, self.__class__.__qualname__), which yields strings of the form "module.Outer.Inner" for nested classes.

4) serializer_factory routes type objects to TypeSerializer before the deconstruct handler, so classes passed directly are serialized by the logic in (1).
   - Location: django/db/migrations/serializer.py :: serializer_factory (lines ~314–333).
   - Concrete behavior: the factory checks isinstance(value, type) and returns TypeSerializer(value) prior to checking for a deconstruct() method.

5) Other serializers demonstrate the intended handling of nested qualnames and guards for local (non-importable) qualnames; TypeSerializer's behavior deviates from this precedent.
   - Locations: django/db/migrations/serializer.py :: EnumSerializer.serialize (lines ~119–126) and FunctionTypeSerializer.serialize (lines ~141–156).
   - Concrete behavior: these serializers use __qualname__ and check for local/non-importable indicators (e.g. '<' in qualname), whereas TypeSerializer uses __name__ and lacks such guards.

Together, these behaviors lead to two observable symptoms in generated migrations: the outer-class qualifier is dropped from nested class references, and migration import statements based on the naive rsplit may reference non-module dotted names (e.g. "import test1.models.Thing").
StepsToReproduce: Run: python manage.py makemigrations
Open the generated migration file and inspect the field definition — the migration references test1.models.Inner instead of test1.models.Outer.Inner.

Alternate reproduction (enum nested in a model):
Create:
import enum
from enumfields import Enum, EnumField
class Thing(models.Model):
    @enum.unique
    class State(Enum):
        on = 'on'
        off = 'off'
    state = EnumField(enum=State)
Run: python manage.py makemigrations and inspect the generated migration; it will reference test1.models.State instead of test1.models.Thing.State.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Generated migration code should refer to nested classes with their full qualified name including the outer-class qualifier (for example, test1.models.Outer.Inner or test1.models.Thing.State). Import lines in the migration should import actual modules and not treat class-qualified segments as modules.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: makemigrations outputs migration code that drops the outer-class qualifier and references nested classes as if they were top-level (for example, test1.models.Inner or test1.models.State). The serializer may also emit import statements based on a naive rsplit that produce invalid imports like "import test1.models.Thing" when the dotted path contains a class qualname.

From tracing through the code, I believe the Enum is getting serialized using the django.db.migrations.serializer.TypeSerializer, which still uses the __name__ rather than __qualname__. problem_statement.Title: Optional named-capturing URL group with inner capture causes extra positional args (TypeError) in Django 3.0
problem_statement.Description: When a regex URL pattern defines an optional named group that contains inner (unnamed) capturing groups, resolving that pattern can produce unexpected positional arguments that are passed to the view, causing a TypeError (e.g. "modules() takes from 1 to 2 positional arguments but 3 were given"). This was observed after upgrading to Django 3.0.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) django/urls/resolvers.py :: RegexPattern.match — The method builds kwargs by filtering match.groupdict() to remove None values and then decides whether to suppress positional args based on whether that filtered kwargs dict is non-empty. Concretely, the code computes kwargs = {k: v for k, v in match.groupdict().items() if v is not None} and then args = () if kwargs else match.groups(). This treats "no matched named-group values" (all named-group matches are None) the same as "pattern has no named groups" and thus allows inner unnamed captures to be returned as positional args.

2) django/urls/resolvers.py :: URLPattern.resolve and URLResolver.resolve — These resolver methods accept the (args, kwargs) produced by pattern.match and rely on the emptiness of the merged kwargs dict to decide whether positional args should be used. URLPattern.resolve forwards the pattern.match() result into a ResolverMatch. URLResolver.resolve merges parent kwargs and default_kwargs with subpattern kwargs and then uses "if not sub_match_dict" to decide whether to prepend parent args to subpattern args; if match() returns positional args in cases where the pattern actually defines named groups, those positional args are propagated into the final ResolverMatch.

3) django/core/handlers/base.py :: BaseHandler._get_response — The handler unpacks the ResolverMatch and invokes the view as wrapped_callback(request, *callback_args, **callback_kwargs). Any stray positional args produced by the URL resolving process are forwarded directly as positional parameters to the view and can cause a TypeError when the view signature does not accept them.

4) django/urls/resolvers.py :: RoutePattern.match (evidence of intended semantics) — RoutePattern.match constructs kwargs from match.groupdict() (without filtering None) and always returns args == (), i.e. it suppresses positional groups when the pattern defines named groups. This demonstrates the intended relationship between pattern-level named groups and suppression of positional args.

5) Relationship/formula (diagnostic): The decision whether to suppress positional args should follow pattern-level presence of named groups (groupdict keys), not whether any named-group matches produced non-None values. Stated as a relationship: let groupdict = match.groupdict(); let kwargs = {k: v for k, v in groupdict.items() if v is not None}; then args should be empty when the pattern defines named groups (i.e., when groupdict is non-empty), rather than when kwargs is non-empty.

6) Additional impact/context: get_ns_resolver constructs RegexPattern instances for namespaced resolvers (so namespaced URLconf entries also exercise RegexPattern.match). Also, URLResolver.resolve merges self.default_kwargs into the dict used to decide positional-vs-keyword semantics, so default_kwargs present on parent resolvers affect the decision. These facts influence the set of code paths and configurations affected by the behavior described above.

7) Investigation gap: The analysis did not run the full Django test suite or perform an exhaustive search for third-party code that might depend on the previous behavior. There may be callers or external code that relied on the pre-change semantics; this was not exhaustively checked.
StepsToReproduce: Use Django 3.0.
In URLConf add a regex pattern with an optional named group containing inner captures, for example: re_path(r'^module/(?P<format>(html|json|xml))?/?$', views.modules, name='modules').
Define the view expecting only request and an optional format parameter, for example: def modules(request, format='html'): ... return render(...).
Request the URL that omits the optional group, e.g. GET /module/.
Observe a server error (TypeError) during view invocation indicating unexpected extra positional arguments.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When the optional named URL group does not match, the view is called without unexpected extra positional arguments. The optional parameter should be provided only via the view's default or as a keyword argument derived from named captures; positional arguments from inner unnamed captures should not be injected when the pattern defines named groups.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Resolving the pattern produces extra positional arguments derived from inner unnamed captures and the view is invoked like wrapped_callback(request, <unexpected_positional1>, ...), causing a TypeError (e.g. "modules() takes from 1 to 2 positional arguments but 3 were given").

It seems to work if you remove the extra parentheses: re_path(r'^module/(?P<format>html|json|xml)?/?$', views.modules, name='modules'), It seems Django is getting confused by the nested groups.

problem_statement.Title: get_<field>_display returns raw value for subclass-added choice tuples when choices are overridden on an abstract base
problem_statement.Description: When a concrete model inherits an abstract base that defines a Field with choices, and the concrete model (or its class-level choices variable) provides additional choice tuples, get_<field>_display() can return the raw stored value for the subclass-added tuples instead of the expected human-readable label. The behavior occurs because the generated get_<field>_display accessor may remain bound to a Field instance originating from the abstract base class and thus uses the base Field's choices set when resolving labels.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Field.contribute_to_class uses hasattr(cls, 'get_%s_display' % self.name) to decide whether to install the generated accessor. Location: django/db/models/fields/__init__.py:Field.contribute_to_class (see lines where the code checks self.choices is not None and then uses if not hasattr(cls, 'get_%s_display' % self.name)). Incorrect assumption: treating an inherited generated accessor as equivalent to an explicit user-defined override prevents installing a new accessor bound to the copied Field instance on subclasses.

2) The generated accessor is created as partialmethod(cls._get_FIELD_display, field=self), i.e. it is bound to the specific Field instance passed at contribute time. Location: django/db/models/fields/__init__.py:Field.contribute_to_class (the partialmethod call that sets 'get_%s_display' binds field=self). Incorrect assumption: the code relies on the accessor being or remaining bound to the correct Field instance for the concrete model, but binding occurs at installation time and does not automatically switch when a new Field instance is added to a subclass.

3) Model._get_FIELD_display resolves labels solely from the bound Field instance's flatchoices using the relationship: choices_dict = dict(make_hashable(field.flatchoices)); return force_str(choices_dict.get(make_hashable(value), value), strings_only=True). Location: django/db/models/base.py:Model._get_FIELD_display (implementation uses the supplied field.flatchoices to build the lookup mapping). Incorrect assumption: that the Field instance passed into _get_FIELD_display already reflects the concrete model's choices; when the accessor is bound to a different (parent) Field instance, subclass-specific tuples are not present in field.flatchoices and thus label lookup falls back to the raw value.

4) Fields declared on abstract bases are copied into concrete subclasses via copy.deepcopy(field) followed by new_class.add_to_class(field.name, new_field). Location: django/db/models/base.py:ModelBase.__new__ (the loop copying parent_fields into new_field and calling new_class.add_to_class). Interaction: add_to_class calls value.contribute_to_class for the copied Field instance, which is the point where a new accessor should be installed for the subclass Field. Incorrect assumption: that copying a Field and adding it to the subclass necessarily results in a get_<field>_display accessor that is bound to the new Field instance; the hasattr guard in Field.contribute_to_class can prevent rebinding, leaving the inherited accessor bound to the original Field instance from the abstract base.
StepsToReproduce: Define an abstract base model A with choices and a CharField using them, e.g. foo_choice = [("A","output1"),("B","output2")]; field_foo = models.CharField(..., choices=foo_choice); class Meta: abstract = True.

Define a concrete subclass B(A) that overrides foo_choice to add a new tuple, e.g. foo_choice = [("A","output1"),("B","output2"),("C","output3")]; and define field_foo = models.CharField(..., choices=foo_choice) on B.
Create and save an instance of B with field_foo = "C".
Call instance.get_field_foo_display() and observe the returned value.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: get_field_foo_display() returns the label corresponding to the concrete model's choices for the field (for the example above, calling on a B instance with value "C" returns "output3"). More generally, the generated get_<field>_display accessor should resolve labels using the Field.flatchoices of the Field instance defined on the concrete model.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: get_field_foo_display() returns the raw stored value (e.g. "C") for choice tuples that were added on the subclass, instead of returning the expected label (e.g. "output3").

​https://github.com/django/django/pull/12266
I think this ticket is very much related to the discussions on #30931. The line if not hasattr(cls, 'get_%s_display' % self.name) breaks the expected behaviour on model inheritance, which causing this bug. (see ​https://github.com/django/django/commit/2d38eb0ab9f78d68c083a5b78b1eca39027b279a#diff-bf776a3b8e5dbfac2432015825ef8afeR766) IMO there are three important points to discuss: 1- Obviously get_<field>_display() should work as expected with inheritance, so this line should be reverted/fixed: if not hasattr(cls, 'get_%s_display' % self.name) 2- I think developers should be able to override get_<field>_display() method on the model class: class Bar(models.Model): foo = models.CharField('foo', choices=[(0, 'foo')]) def get_foo_display(self): return 'something' b = Bar(foo=0) assert b.get_foo_display() == 'something' 3- I think Field should not check an attribute of model class and make a decision based on it. This check and set logic should be delegated to BaseModel with an abstraction to make it less magical and more clean. Maybe something like this: class ModelBase(type): .... def add_overridable_to_class(cls, name, value): // Set value only if the name is not already defined in the class itself (no `hasattr`) if name not in cls.__dict__: setattr(cls, name, value) class Field(RegisterLookupMixin): ... def contribute_to_class(self, cls, name, private_only=False): ... if self.choices is not None: cls.add_overridable_to_class('get_%s_display' % self.name, partialmethod(cls._get_FIELD_display, field=self))
Why would checking on fields class be a bad idea? If you are a field of a model that is not abstract but your parent is an abstract method, wouldn't you want to override your parent's method if you both have the same method?
Replying to George Popides: Why would checking on fields class be a bad idea? If you are a field of a model that is not abstract but your parent is an abstract method, wouldn't you want to override your parent's method if you both have the same method? Well it is not something I would prefer because it makes two classes tightly coupled to each other, which means it is hard to change one without touching to the other one and you always need to think about side effects of your change. Which eventually makes this two classes hard to test and makes the codebase hard to maintain. Your logic about overriding might/or might not be true. I would just execute this logic on ModelBase rather than Field.

problem_statement.Title: translation.E004 raised for sublanguage LANGUAGE_CODE (e.g. "de-at") even though base language exists in LANGUAGES
problem_statement.Description: When settings.LANGUAGE_CODE is a sublanguage tag (for example "de-at") and the corresponding base language ("de") is present in settings.LANGUAGES, Django's system check raises translation.E004 claiming LANGUAGE_CODE is not in LANGUAGES. This contradicts documented/runtime behavior that a base language should be used when a sublanguage is not provided.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Exact-membership requirement in the system check (django/core/checks/translation.py::check_language_settings_consistent): the check computes available_tags = {i for i, _ in settings.LANGUAGES} | {'en-us'} and returns E004 if settings.LANGUAGE_CODE not in available_tags. The check assumes LANGUAGE_CODE must be an exact entry in LANGUAGES.

2) The system check does not consult configured LANG_INFO fallbacks (django/conf/locale.__init__.py::LANG_INFO). LANG_INFO can declare explicit fallback alternatives for specific tags (e.g., Chinese variants), but the check ignores these and only uses literal entries from settings.LANGUAGES.

3) Divergence from runtime fallback algorithm (django/utils/translation/trans_real.py::get_supported_language_variant): at runtime, possible_lang_codes is built as [lang_code] + LANG_INFO[lang_code]['fallback'] (if present) + [generic_lang_code], and the runtime returns the first code that is both listed in supported languages and passes check_for_language(code) (which verifies translation files exist). The system check's membership-only test is narrower than the runtime's LANG_INFO and generic-base-based resolution.

4) Hardcoded special-case in the system check: the check explicitly unions {'en-us'} into available_tags, which is an exception treated as implicitly present regardless of settings.LANGUAGES.

5) Missing test coverage for the sublanguage-with-base scenario: tests/check_framework/test_translation.py contains assertions for E004 in obvious mismatch cases but does not include a test that LANGUAGE_CODE='de-at' (or similar sublanguage) is accepted when LANGUAGES includes 'de'.
StepsToReproduce: In a Django project settings.py set LANGUAGE_CODE = 'de-at'.
Set LANGUAGES to include the base language, for example LANGUAGES = [('de', 'German'), ('en', 'English')].
Run the system checks: python manage.py check (or start the server to trigger system checks).
Observe that Django raises a SystemCheckError listing (translation.E004) You have provided a value for the LANGUAGE_CODE setting that is not in the LANGUAGES setting.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: No system check error should be raised when LANGUAGE_CODE is a sublanguage (e.g. 'de-at') if the base language (e.g. 'de') is present in LANGUAGES (or an allowed documented fallback applies). Django should accept the configured LANGUAGE_CODE and proceed to use the base language as the effective language according to documented/runtime fallback rules.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Django raises SystemCheckError with (translation.E004) stating the configured LANGUAGE_CODE is not in LANGUAGES when LANGUAGE_CODE is a sublanguage like 'de-at', even though 'de' exists in LANGUAGES. (Example: on Django 3.0.2, LANGUAGE_CODE='de-at' triggers E004 while LANGUAGE_CODE='es-ar' works when 'es-ar' is explicitly provided.)

problem_statement.Title: Admin readonly display renders JSONField Python repr ({'foo': 'bar'}) instead of JSON ({"foo": "bar"})
problem_statement.Description: When a model JSONField is shown readonly in the Django admin, its Python value is rendered using the Python dict/list repr (single quotes) instead of valid JSON (double quotes). This appears in admin list and detail readonly displays.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) display_for_field has no special-case for models.JSONField: django/contrib/admin/utils.py::display_for_field implements specific formatting for many model field types but does not detect or serialize models.JSONField instances; when a model field is not specially handled the function delegates to display_for_value. (Location: django/contrib/admin/utils.py::display_for_field — values fall through to display_for_value.)

2) display_for_value falls back to Python repr for dicts: django/contrib/admin/utils.py::display_for_value formats lists/tuples by joining their str() representations but for dicts (and other non-handled object types) the final fallback is return str(value). For dict values this produces the Python representation with single quotes (e.g. {'foo': 'bar'}), not JSON (e.g. {"foo": "bar"}). (Location: django/contrib/admin/utils.py::display_for_value — final else -> str(value).)

3) models.JSONField defines and uses an encoder for canonical JSON serialization: django/db/models/fields/json.py::JSONField accepts encoder/decoder arguments (self.encoder/self.decoder) and uses json.dumps(value, cls=self.encoder) when preparing values for the database and for validation. The admin presentation code does not consult or reuse this encoder, so admin output is inconsistent with field serialization semantics. (Location: django/db/models/fields/json.py::JSONField.__init__ and JSONField.get_prep_value.)

4) forms.JSONField implements prepare_value and preserves InvalidJSONInput, but admin display does not exercise it: django/forms/fields.py::JSONField.prepare_value returns json.dumps(value, cls=self.encoder) for Python values and preserves InvalidJSONInput instances. The admin utils, however, operate on model Field instances and raw Python values via display_for_field/display_for_value and do not call a form field's prepare_value in the general case. Whether admin presentation should incorporate forms.JSONField.prepare_value (and thereby preserve InvalidJSONInput behavior) was not determined by the investigation. (Location: django/forms/fields.py::JSONField.prepare_value and bound_data/InvalidJSONInput; investigation gap: admin does not currently call this method.)

5) contrib.postgres JSONField variants share the same serialization expectations: django/contrib/postgres/fields/jsonb.py subclasses the builtin JSONField, so postgres-specific JSONField classes carry the same encoder/decoder semantics and are affected by the same admin-display mismatch. (Location: django/contrib/postgres/fields/jsonb.py::JSONField subclassing built-in JSONField.)

6) Admin call sites use display_for_field and will surface whichever string it produces: django/contrib/admin/helpers.py and django/contrib/admin/templatetags/admin_list.py both invoke display_for_field(value, field, empty_value_display) when rendering readonly fields in forms and list displays. Because display_for_field currently delegates to display_for_value for JSON-like Python objects, both list and detail readonly views will show Python repr rather than JSON. (Locations: django/contrib/admin/helpers.py and django/contrib/admin/templatetags/admin_list.py.)

7) Tests do not cover JSONField admin display formatting: tests for admin utils (tests/admin_utils/tests.py) include many display_for_field/display_for_value cases (None, numbers, dates, lists, etc.) but do not assert that models.JSONField values are rendered as JSON. The lack of explicit test coverage for JSONField presentation in the admin utilities permits this inconsistency to go undetected by existing tests. (Location: tests/admin_utils/tests.py — missing JSONField presentation assertions.)
StepsToReproduce: Define a model with a models.JSONField and save an instance with a value like {"foo": "bar"}.
Register the model in the Django admin (or ensure it's visible in the admin list/detail view).

View the model in the admin readonly display (either list view or detail form readonly field) and observe the displayed JSONField value; it appears as Python repr with single quotes (e.g. {'foo': 'bar'}) instead of JSON with double quotes.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: In admin readonly displays (list and detail), a JSONField value should be presented using a valid JSON serialization consistent with the field's serialization semantics (i.e. the same JSON encoding semantics the field uses for storage/validation), or otherwise in a clearly labeled and user-meaningful format. For example, a stored JSON object {"foo": "bar"} should be shown as {"foo": "bar"} in the admin UI.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Admin readonly displays render JSONField values using Python's dict/list repr (single quotes), e.g. {'foo': 'bar'}, rather than valid JSON (double-quoted keys/strings).

It might be worth waiting for #12990 to land before proceeding here as the patch will be able to simply rely of django.db.models.JSONField instance checks from that point.

problem_statement.Title: TransactionTestCase.serialized_rollback fails to restore DB snapshot due to missing transaction/constraint/forward-ref handling in BaseDatabaseCreation.deserialize_db_from_string
problem_statement.Description: When TransactionTestCase.serialized_rollback = True is used, restoring the serialized DB snapshot via BaseDatabaseCreation.deserialize_db_from_string can fail to restore referential integrity. The deserialization process currently saves objects sequentially without transactional/constraint/forward-reference handling, which can lead to IntegrityError or an inconsistent database state during fixture setup.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption: the serialized object order is sufficient to satisfy all foreign-key constraints. Location: django/db/backends/base/creation.py::BaseDatabaseCreation.serialize_db_to_string and django/core/serializers/__init__.py::sort_dependencies. Evidence: serialize_db_to_string uses serializers.sort_dependencies (creation.py lines 112–121). sort_dependencies only orders models with respect to natural-key dependencies (serializers/__init__.py lines 159–197) and does not produce a full FK topological ordering for arbitrary foreign-key edges.

2) Incorrect assumption: deserializing and calling obj.save() sequentially outside a transaction is safe. Location: django/db/backends/base/creation.py::BaseDatabaseCreation.deserialize_db_from_string. Evidence: current implementation iterates over serializers.deserialize and calls obj.save() directly (creation.py lines 128–130) and contains no transaction.atomic context.

3) Incorrect assumption: constraint checks need not be temporarily disabled during bulk restore. Location: django/db/backends/base/creation.py::BaseDatabaseCreation.deserialize_db_from_string. Evidence: deserialize_db_from_string does not use connection.constraint_checks_disabled(); by contrast, loaddata disables constraint checks during loading (django/core/management/commands/loaddata.py lines 111–116).

4) Incorrect assumption: forward references encountered during deserialization will not occur or can be ignored. Location: django/db/backends/base/creation.py::BaseDatabaseCreation.deserialize_db_from_string. Evidence: the deserialization framework supports handle_forward_references and produces DeserializedObject.deferred_fields which must be saved later via DeserializedObject.save_deferred_fields (django/core/serializers/base.py lines 195–249). deserialize_db_from_string does not request handle_forward_references nor collect/call save_deferred_fields.

5) Incorrect assumption: it is acceptable to skip final constraint verification after restore. Location: django/db/backends/base/creation.py::BaseDatabaseCreation.deserialize_db_from_string. Evidence: deserialize_db_from_string makes no call to connection.check_constraints(...); loaddata explicitly calls connection.check_constraints(table_names=...) after deferred saves (django/core/management/commands/loaddata.py lines 120–123).

6) Scope observation: no core backend overrides of deserialize_db_from_string were found, so the behavior is centralized. Location: django/db/backends/base/creation.py::BaseDatabaseCreation.deserialize_db_from_string and repo search. Evidence: search for deserialize_db_from_string found only the BaseDatabaseCreation implementation and the TransactionTestCase caller (creation.py and testcases.py), indicating a single-site change affects core backends.
StepsToReproduce: Define two models with a foreign-key relationship (e.g., ModelB has FK -> ModelA).
Create a TransactionTestCase subclass with serialized_rollback = True.
In setUpTestData or equivalent, create instances of both models so they are present in the serialized snapshot.

Run the test: during TransactionTestCase._fixture_setup, the stored JSON snapshot is deserialized via BaseDatabaseCreation.deserialize_db_from_string; if the serialized ordering places the FK-holder before the FK-target, the fixture setup can raise IntegrityError or result in missing/incorrectly restored references.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When serialized_rollback = True is used, the test framework restores the DB snapshot deterministically and user-visible relationships are intact: no IntegrityError occurs during fixture setup, all serialized objects are present, and foreign-key references point to the expected target objects.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: During fixture setup with serialized_rollback = True, deserializing the stored JSON snapshot can raise IntegrityError or leave referenced objects missing, resulting in an inconsistent database state for the test (user-visible failures during setup).

hints_text: I've run into a problem related to this one (just reported as #31051), so I ended up looking into this problem as well. The original report still seems accurate to me, with the proposed solution valid. I've been working on a fix and (most of the work), testcase for this problem. I'll do some more testing and provide a proper PR for this issue and #31051 soon. The testcase is not ideal yet (testing the testing framework is tricky), but I'll expand on that in the PR. Furthermore, I noticed that loaddata does not just wrap everything in a transaction, it also explicitly disables constraint checks inside the transaction: with connection.constraint_checks_disabled(): self.objs_with_deferred_fields = [] for fixture_label in fixture_labels: self.load_label(fixture_label) for obj in self.objs_with_deferred_fields: obj.save_deferred_fields(using=self.using) # Since we disabled constraint checks, we must manually check for # any invalid keys that might have been added table_names = [model._meta.db_table for model in self.models] try: connection.check_constraints(table_names=table_names) except Exception as e: e.args = ("Problem installing fixtures: %s" % e,) raise I had a closer look at how this works (since I understood that a transaction already implicitly disables constraint checks) and it turns out that MySQL/InnoDB is an exception and does *not* defer constraint checks to the end of the transaction, but instead needs extra handling (so constraint_checks_disabled() is a no-op on most database backends). See #3615.

problem_statement.Title: Inherited Parent.Meta.ordering = ['-pk'] is flipped to ASC when querying multi-table Child
problem_statement.Description: When a model (Parent) declares Meta.ordering = ['-pk'] and another model (Child) inherits from it (multi-table inheritance), querying Child produces an ORDER BY on the parent primary key ascending (ASC) instead of descending (DESC). The ordering sign is lost/flipped during SQL compilation.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) SQLCompiler.find_ordering_name forwards the outer resolved order into recursive resolution for related-model ordering strings (django/db/models/sql/compiler.py::SQLCompiler.find_ordering_name). Specifically, the function derives name and order via get_order_dir(name, default_order) and then calls self.find_ordering_name(item, opts, alias, order, already_seen) for related-model ordering items (compiler.py:706, 729-730). This means the inner resolution receives the outer "order" value as its default_order argument.

2) get_order_dir and ORDER_DIR make the interpretation of a leading '-' relative to the provided default (django/db/models/sql/query.py::get_order_dir and django/db/models/sql/constants.py::ORDER_DIR). ORDER_DIR maps 'ASC' -> ('ASC','DESC') and 'DESC' -> ('DESC','ASC') (constants.py:20-23). get_order_dir(field, default) uses ORDER_DIR[default] and returns the opposite direction when field starts with '-', so a '-' prefix is interpreted as the opposite of the provided default (query.py:2256-2267). Because find_ordering_name supplies the outer-derived order as the default to inner resolutions, an explicit '-' on an inner ordering string can be interpreted relative to that outer default and produce a flipped direction.

3) Parent Meta.ordering entries are propagated verbatim into the child class during model creation (django/db/models/base.py::ModelBase.__new__). When a child class does not define its own Meta.ordering, new_class._meta.ordering is assigned from base_meta.ordering (base.py:146-147). This causes Child._meta.ordering to contain the literal string(s) from Parent (e.g. '-pk') and triggers the compiler path that expands related-model ordering strings and exercises the recursive-default behavior described above.

4) Combined effect and intended semantics: the combination of (1) and (2) causes an explicit '-' that refers to a related model's ordering to be interpreted relative to an outer default and thus potentially reversed. The intended semantic is that the sign applied to an ordering name should determine the direction applied to the resolved ordering expressions for that name; resolving an inner model's ordering strings should not change meaning based on an outer order value in a way that flips an explicitly-specified '-' into the opposite direction. (This is a statement of the expected semantics; implementation details are not included here.)
StepsToReproduce:
Open Django shell and run:
>>> print(Child.objects.all().query)
Observe the generated SQL ORDER BY clause.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Querying Child should return rows ordered by primary key descending, matching Parent.Meta.ordering (i.e., SQL should include ORDER BY ... DESC on the parent primary key).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Querying Child generates SQL with ORDER BY on the parent primary key ascending. Example observed SQL: SELECT "myapp_parent"."id", "myapp_child"."parent_ptr_id" FROM "myapp_child" INNER JOIN "myapp_parent" ON ("myapp_child"."parent_ptr_id" = "myapp_parent"."id") ORDER BY "myapp_parent"."id" ASC

problem_statement.Title: Incorrect hint text suggests ForeignKey and symmetrical=False for recursive many-to-many through models
problem_statement.Description: When an intermediary (through) model for a ManyToManyField contains multiple ForeignKey fields producing ambiguity and no through_fields are set, Django's system check presents a misleading hint that recommends using ForeignKey("self", symmetrical=False, through="..."). The hint text inaccurately references ForeignKey and the 'symmetrical' option in that context and can mislead users.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Hardcoded incorrect hint literal in django/db/models/fields/related.py inside ManyToManyField._check_relationship_model (two occurrences). The method constructs the message:
   'If you want to create a recursive relationship, use ForeignKey("%s", symmetrical=False, through="%s").'
   These occurrences appear when the intermediate (through) model has multiple foreign keys to one side (check ids fields.E334 and fields.E335). The literal is formatted with RECURSIVE_RELATIONSHIP_CONSTANT and relationship_model_name (i.e. the string uses '%s' substitutions for the recursive constant and the through model name).

2) A unit test encodes the same incorrect hint, ensuring the incorrect wording is preserved in the test suite: tests/invalid_models_tests/test_relative_fields.py:test_ambiguous_relationship_model asserts the expected hint text 'If you want to create a recursive relationship, use ForeignKey("self", symmetrical=False, through="AmbiguousRelationship").'

3) Inspection of related runtime classes shows the incorrectness is textual/UX-only: django/db/models/fields/reverse_related.py:ManyToManyRel defines and stores attributes such as symmetrical and through on the many-to-many relationship object, and django/db/models/fields/related_descriptors.py uses rel.symmetrical in descriptor logic. Those components correctly treat 'symmetrical' and 'through' as ManyToMany-related attributes, indicating the problem is an inaccurate hint message rather than a relationship-handling logic error.

4) Repository search for the exact phrase and the substring 'symmetrical=False, through' found only the two occurrences in django/db/models/fields/related.py and the single test mentioned above. These three locations are the code sites encoding the incorrect guidance.

Investigation gap: I did not run the test suite or i18n string extraction locally; potential side effects in translation catalogs or CI behavior when modifying the literal were not validated here.
StepsToReproduce: Define an intermediary (through) model used by a ManyToManyField that contains multiple ForeignKey fields to the same model and do not set through_fields.
Attach that through model to a ManyToManyField on a model so Django performs model validation.
Run Django's system checks (for example, manage.py check) or otherwise load the models to trigger the model validation that produces the hint.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When the system-check identifies an ambiguous intermediary model, the user-facing hint should recommend the correct ManyToManyField usage (i.e., a suggestion that references ManyToManyField("<recursive-constant>", through="<relationship_model>") and not options that belong to ForeignKey).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The system-check currently produces the hint: 'If you want to create a recursive relationship, use ForeignKey("self", symmetrical=False, through="AmbiguousRelationship").' — it references ForeignKey and the 'symmetrical' option in a context where those belong to ManyToMany, which is misleading.

         primary key, and the query would be equivalent, the optimization

+        # Column names from JOINs to check collisions with aliases.
+        if allow_aliases:
+            column_names = set()
+            seen_models = set()
+            for join in list(self.alias_map.values())[1:]:  # Skip base table.

+                        for field in model._meta.local_concrete_fields

+                    seen_models.add(model)

             for alias, annotation in self.annotation_select.items():
@@ -1940,7 +1953,7 @@ def set_group_by(self, allow_aliases=True):

                         alias = None
                     group_by_cols = annotation.get_group_by_cols(alias=alias)

problem_statement.Title: GROUP BY emits quoted annotation alias for Subquery, causing ambiguous column error (Django 3.0)
problem_statement.Description: Annotating a queryset with a Subquery using an alias (for example 'status') and then grouping/values by that alias can cause the SQL compiler to emit the quoted alias ("status") in the GROUP BY clause. If a joined table also defines a column with the same name, the database reports an ambiguous column reference and the query fails.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Subquery provides an alias reference for group-by: Subquery.get_group_by_cols(alias) returns Ref(alias, self) when an alias is provided. Location: django/db/models/expressions.py:Subquery.get_group_by_cols (see get_group_by_cols: if alias: return [Ref(alias, self)]). 

2) Ref renders as a quoted identifier: Ref.as_sql compiles a Ref to connection.ops.quote_name(self.refs) (the quoted alias string) rather than emitting the underlying expression SQL. Location: django/db/models/expressions.py:Ref.as_sql (see as_sql: return connection.ops.quote_name(self.refs), []).

3) Query construction preserves the alias-ref in group_by: Query.set_group_by calls annotation.get_group_by_cols(alias=alias) and extends query.group_by with the returned items, which means a Subquery annotation with an alias becomes a Ref(alias, Subquery) entry in query.group_by when aliases are allowed. Location: django/db/models/sql/query.py:Query.set_group_by (calls annotation.get_group_by_cols(alias=alias)).

4) The SQL compiler compiles group_by entries generically: SQLCompiler.get_group_by compiles each group_by expression by calling self.compile(expr) without special-casing a Ref whose source is a Subquery. Because Ref.as_sql returns the quoted alias, this produces GROUP BY "alias" in the SQL. Location: django/db/models/sql/compiler.py:SQLCompiler.get_group_by (compiles expressions via self.compile(expr)).

Collectively: these behaviors cause a Ref(alias, Subquery) to be emitted as the quoted alias (e.g. "status") in the GROUP BY clause, producing an unqualified identifier that can be ambiguous if any table in the FROM/JOIN tree defines the same column name.
StepsToReproduce: Define models A, B, AB (through model with IntegerField status), and C (ForeignKey to A with IntegerField status) as in the original report.
On Django 3.0.x with PostgreSQL, run the sequence in the report:
ab_query = AB.objects.filter(a=OuterRef('pk'), b=1)
filter_conditions = Q(pk=1) | Q(ab_a__b=1)
query = A.objects.filter(filter_conditions).annotate(status=Subquery(ab_query.values('status')), c_count=Count('c'))
answer = query.values('status').annotate(total_count=Count('status'))
Evaluate/print answer (or run the SQL) and observe the ambiguous column error and the generated SQL showing GROUP BY "status".
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The generated SQL should include an unambiguous GROUP BY entry for the annotated Subquery (for example GROUP BY (SELECT ...)) or otherwise produce a GROUP BY entry that the database interprets as the intended subquery expression, so the grouped query executes and returns the expected grouped counts for the annotated 'status'.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: On Django 3.0.4 the compiler emits GROUP BY "status" and PostgreSQL raises django.db.utils.ProgrammingError: column reference "status" is ambiguous. The failing SQL contains GROUP BY "status" while an earlier Django version produced GROUP BY (SELECT ...) for the same construction.

problem_statement.Title: SafeExceptionReporterFilter.cleanse_setting does not recurse into iterables (lists/tuples), leaving nested secrets unmasked
problem_statement.Description: Sensitive values stored inside non-dict iterables (for example, lists or tuples containing dicts, or nested lists) are not inspected or masked by SafeExceptionReporterFilter.cleanse_setting. As a result, secrets inside those iterables appear unmasked in get_safe_settings() output and on debug pages.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) cleanse_setting only recurses into dict instances. Location: django/views/debug.py: SafeExceptionReporterFilter.cleanse_setting (elif isinstance(value, dict): ...). Effect: values that are lists, tuples, frozensets, or other iterables containing dicts are returned unchanged and inner dicts keep raw secret values.

2) Callable wrapping is applied only to the final 'cleansed' object. Location: django/views/debug.py: SafeExceptionReporterFilter.cleanse_setting (if callable(cleansed): cleansed = CallableSettingWrapper(cleansed)). Effect: callables nested inside iterables that are returned unchanged are not wrapped by CallableSettingWrapper.

3) Consumers rely on cleanse_setting to produce fully safe values. Location: django/views/debug.py: SafeExceptionReporterFilter.get_safe_settings and django/views/debug.py: ExceptionReporter.get_traceback_data (settings: self.filter.get_safe_settings()). Effect: any unmasked values produced by cleanse_setting are propagated to data rendered on debug pages and included in exception reports.

4) Test coverage does not include iterable-of-dicts cases. Location: tests/view_tests/tests/test_debug.py (e.g., test_cleanse_setting_recurses_in_dictionary tests dict-in-dict recursion but no tests for lists/tuples/frozenset-of-dicts or nested iterables). Effect: the missing test cases mean regressions or omissions for iterable traversal remain undetected.

5) Masking criteria/constants are not the cause. Location: django/views/debug.py: SafeExceptionReporterFilter.cleansed_substitute and SafeExceptionReporterFilter.hidden_settings (cleansed_substitute = '********************'; hidden_settings regex 'API|TOKEN|KEY|SECRET|PASS|SIGNATURE' with re.I). Effect: the masking pattern and substitute are present and applied when keys matched at the inspected locations; the root cause is traversal, not the masking constants.

6) Edge-case considerations observed in the investigation: dicts are unhashable (so sets of dicts are invalid), and nested container types (tuple, frozenset, generators) are potential edge cases. Location: investigation notes and test design. Effect: handling and tests for these container types must be considered when addressing traversal and callable-wrapping, but these are part of the diagnosis of where traversal is incomplete.
StepsToReproduce: In settings.py, define MY_SETTING similar to the example: a dict with keys 'foo','secret','token' and nested iterables, e.g.:

MY_SETTING = { 'foo': 'value', 'secret': 'value', 'token': 'value', 'something': [{'foo': 'value'}, {'secret': 'value'}, {'token': 'value'}], 'else': [[{'foo': 'value'}, {'secret': 'value'}, {'token': 'value'}], [{'foo': 'value'}, {'secret': 'value'}, {'token': 'value'}]] }
Start a Django project using the repository code that contains SafeExceptionReporterFilter.cleanse_setting as inspected.
From a Django shell or a view, call SafeExceptionReporterFilter().get_safe_settings() (or otherwise trigger a debug/exception report) and inspect get_safe_settings()['MY_SETTING'].
Observe that top-level keys matching hidden_settings (for example 'secret' or 'token') are masked, but entries with matching keys inside lists/tuples/nested iterables remain unmasked.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: All sensitive values (keys matching the hidden_settings pattern) are masked in the settings output regardless of whether they appear at top-level dicts, inside nested dicts, or inside iterable containers such as lists, tuples, frozensets, or nested lists. Callables found anywhere in settings should be represented consistently (i.e., wrapped the same way), regardless of whether they are nested inside iterables or appear at top-level.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Only dict instances are traversed and masked. Secrets contained inside lists/tuples/nested iterables (for example, dicts inside a list) remain unmasked and are visible in get_safe_settings() output; callables nested inside those iterables are not wrapped by CallableSettingWrapper.

problem_statement.Title: Migration crashes when deleting index_together that shares fields with unique_together
problem_statement.Description: Deleting an index_together (via migrations) that covers the same columns as an existing unique_together raises a ValueError and aborts the migration. Observed on Django 1.11.10; failure occurs in BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor._delete_composed_index.
problem_statement.RootCause:
The failure is caused by multiple incorrect assumptions in the base schema editor and related code paths that deal with composed indexes/constraints:

1) Single-constraint assumption when deleting composed indexes (django/db/backends/base/schema.py::BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor._delete_composed_index): the method computes the set of matching DB constraint names for the given column list via self._constraint_names(...), and unconditionally requires exactly one matching name, raising ValueError("Found wrong number (%s) of constraints...") when this is not the case. This assumption fails when both a DB unique constraint and a separate DB index exist for the same column set (e.g. a "_uniq" constraint and a separate "_idx" index), because _constraint_names can return multiple matching names.

2) Creation-side assumption when adding index_together (django/db/backends/base/schema.py::BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor.alter_index_together): the method creates an index for newly added index_together entries by calling self._create_index_sql(...) without checking whether an existing DB constraint or index already covers the same columns. When a unique constraint already exists on those columns, the existence of the unique-backed index and a separate index can lead to ambiguity and unexpected index recreation behavior.

3) Caller symmetry: both alter_index_together (deleting/creating index entries) and alter_unique_together (deleting/creating unique entries) use the same composed-index deletion logic (BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor._delete_composed_index). Therefore the single-name assumption affects both index_together deletions and unique_together deletions (the deletion is performed with different constraint flags: {'index': True} vs {'unique': True}).

4) Reliance on DB introspection infodicts for disambiguation (django/db/backends/base/schema.py::BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor._constraint_names): _constraint_names obtains constraints via connection.introspection.get_constraints(cursor, table) and compares returned infodicts' keys (e.g. infodict['columns'], infodict['unique'], infodict['index'], infodict['type']) to filter matches. The code assumes these keys are present and carry consistent semantics across database backends; this dependency was not exhaustively verified and represents a behaviour/compatibility risk across backends.

5) Backend delegation: some backends perform small pre-checks and then delegate to the base implementation (e.g. django/db/backends/mysql/schema.py::DatabaseSchemaEditor._delete_composed_index calls super()). Those delegations mean the base-code assumptions affect those backends as well.

Each of the above is an independent encoding of the same root cause pattern: an assumption that a single database object uniquely corresponds to a model-level composed (unique/index) definition, and insufficient handling of the situation where multiple DB objects (index vs unique-backed index) exist for the same set of columns. The code locations named above are the precise points where these assumptions are made.
StepsToReproduce: Create a Django model with two fields (e.g. a, b).
Add both (a, b) to Meta.unique_together and to Meta.index_together (or create equivalent migrations).
Apply migrations so the database has both the unique constraint and a separate index on (a, b).
Remove index_together from the model (keep unique_together) and run the migration that deletes the index_together.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The migration completes successfully. Removing index_together that covers columns already constrained by unique_together should not crash; the unique constraint remains in place. Moving an index declaration (for example, from index_together to Meta.indexes) should not cause unnecessary index drop-and-recreate cycles visible to the user.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Migration aborts with ValueError: "Found wrong number (2) of constraints for ..." raised in django/db/backends/base/schema.py::_delete_composed_index because two DB objects (a unique constraint and an index) match the same column set. The deletion fails and, when index declarations are moved, this can cause unexpected index recreation behavior.

problem_statement.Title: QuerySet.delete() returns inconsistent per-model dict when zero objects deleted
problem_statement.Description: QuerySet.delete() returns a tuple (total_deleted, per_model_dict). When no rows are deleted (total_deleted == 0), the per-model dict is inconsistent: sometimes it is {} and sometimes it contains model-label keys with value 0. The inconsistency depends on the internal delete path used.
problem_statement.RootCause:
Multiple locations in the ORM unconditionally record a per-model entry even when the number of affected rows is zero, while other code paths produce no per-model entry for zero-affected rows. The code locations and incorrect assumptions are:

1) django/db/models/deletion.py:Collector.collect (fast_deletes population)
   - Assumption: a queryset-like object that can be fast-deleted should be appended to self.fast_deletes for processing without checking if it matches any rows.
   - Relevant code: collect() does `if self.can_fast_delete(objs): self.fast_deletes.append(objs); return` (lines 240–242), and appends related-model sub-querysets into self.fast_deletes (line 314).

2) django/db/models/deletion.py:Collector.delete (fast_deletes loop)
   - Assumption: counts from fast-delete query executions should always be recorded into the per-model counter.
   - Relevant code: the fast-delete loop does `count = qs._raw_delete(using=self.using)` followed by `deleted_counter[qs.model._meta.label] += count` (lines 409–412). If a fast-delete queryset matches no rows, qs._raw_delete() may return 0, and the Counter entry for that model label is added with value 0.

3) django/db/models/deletion.py:Collector.delete (per-model delete loop)
   - Assumption: counts from per-model DeleteQuery.delete_batch should always be recorded into the per-model counter.
   - Relevant code: the per-model delete loop does `count = query.delete_batch(pk_list, self.using)` followed by `deleted_counter[model._meta.label] += count` (lines 425–429). If delete_batch returns 0, the Counter entry for that model label is added with value 0.

4) django/db/models/deletion.py:Collector.delete (single-instance fast-delete early return)
   - Assumption: the single-instance fast-path can return a per-model dict unconditionally.
   - Relevant code: when there is a single model and a single instance, the fast-path does `count = sql.DeleteQuery(model).delete_batch([instance.pk], self.using)` and `return count, {model._meta.label: count}` (lines 392–398). If count == 0, this returns a dict containing the model label mapped to 0.

5) django/db/models/query.py:QuerySet.delete (consumer forwarding)
   - Assumption: callers of Collector.delete should receive its dict result unchanged.
   - Relevant code: QuerySet.delete constructs a Collector, calls collector.collect(del_query), then `deleted, _rows_count = collector.delete()` and `return deleted, _rows_count` (lines 734–740). QuerySet.delete does not normalize or filter the per-model dict returned by Collector.delete.

6) tests/delete/tests.py:test_fast_delete_empty_no_update_can_self_select (test dependency)
   - Observation: an existing unit test asserts that an empty fast-delete returns (0, {'delete.User': 0}) (lines 694–698). This test encodes and depends on the current behavior of producing zero-valued per-model entries for empty fast-delete querysets.

Resulting pattern: the root cause is a consistent code pattern where counts (including zero) are recorded into the deleted_counter structure and sometimes reported to callers, while other code paths do not create per-model entries when nothing is deleted. The code paths that create zero-valued per-model entries are the fast_deletes loop, the per-model delete loop, and the single-instance early return described above (all in django/db/models/deletion.py).
StepsToReproduce: Run a fast-delete queryset that matches no rows (e.g., a model that is eligible for fast-delete): MyFastModel.objects.filter(pk=nonexistent_pk).delete(). Observe the return value; it may be (0, {'app.MyFastModel': 0}).

Run a delete on a model that goes through the per-instance/non-fast-delete path and matches no rows (e.g., a model with cascading related deletes): MyRelatedModel.objects.filter(pk=nonexistent_pk).delete(). Observe the return value; it may be (0, {}).

Compare the two results; one path produced an empty per-model dict {}, the other produced a dict with the model label mapped to 0. Reproduce the two behaviors using the tests in tests/delete/tests.py (see test_fast_delete_empty_no_update_can_self_select).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When total_deleted == 0 (no rows affected), the API should be consistent across delete paths: callers should receive the same-shaped per-model dict regardless of whether the deletion was performed via fast-delete or per-instance paths (i.e. the per-model dict should not differ depending on internal delete implementation).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: When no rows are deleted, QuerySet.delete() sometimes returns (0, {}) and sometimes returns (0, {'app.Model': 0}) depending on internal delete paths: fast-delete paths and the single-instance fast-delete shortcut produce per-model dict entries with zero counts, while other delete paths produce an empty dict.

hints_text: I guess we could adapt the code not to include any key if the count is zero in the second case.

problem_statement.Title: makemigrations does not validate UniqueConstraint.fields exist (no models.E012; AddConstraint missing FK deps)
problem_statement.Description: When a model adds a UniqueConstraint referring to non-existent fields, makemigrations and the system checks do not validate the constraint field names (no models.E012). This differs from unique_together, which is validated. As a result, invalid constraints can be emitted by makemigrations and AddConstraint operations may be created without recorded FK dependency tuples; missing-field errors occur later when SQL is generated or migrations are applied.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Model checks do not validate constraint field names. Location: django/db/models/base.py::Model._check_constraints. Evidence: _check_constraints performs DB-feature-related warnings for constraints but does not call cls._check_local_fields for fields referenced by UniqueConstraint instances. The existence check that would raise models.E012 (cls._check_local_fields) is implemented in the same file but is not invoked for UniqueConstraint.fields.

2) MigrationAutodetector detects added constraints but does not validate their fields or compute FK dependencies when emitting AddConstraint. Location: django/db/migrations/autodetector.py::MigrationAutodetector.create_altered_constraints and ::generate_added_constraints. Evidence: create_altered_constraints computes added constraints via list-difference and stores them; generate_added_constraints emits operations.AddConstraint(...) via add_operation without invoking get_field() on constraint.fields or extending dependencies via _get_dependencies_for_foreign_key.

3) UniqueConstraint looks up fields at SQL generation time, causing missing-field errors to be raised late. Location: django/db/models/constraints.py::UniqueConstraint.constraint_sql and ::create_sql. Evidence: these methods call model._meta.get_field(field_name) for each field in self.fields while producing SQL, so a FieldDoesNotExist would be raised during SQL creation/apply rather than earlier during checks/autodetection.

4) AddConstraint.state_forwards appends constraints into model state without validating fields itself. Location: django/db/migrations/operations/models.py::AddConstraint.state_forwards. Evidence: state_forwards inserts the constraint into model_state.options['constraints'] and calls state.reload_model(..., delay=True) without performing explicit validation of constraint.fields.

5) unique_together handling demonstrates a different/validated pattern: MigrationAutodetector._generate_altered_foo_together calls get_field() for each field and collects FK dependencies via _get_dependencies_for_foreign_key. Location: django/db/migrations/autodetector.py::_generate_altered_foo_together. Evidence: that method uses self.new_apps.get_model(...)._meta.get_field(field_name) and extends a dependencies list via self._get_dependencies_for_foreign_key(field) when appropriate.

6) Exact dependency tuple format used by the autodetector for foreign-key-related dependencies. Location: django/db/migrations/autodetector.py::MigrationAutodetector._get_dependencies_for_foreign_key. Evidence: this helper returns dependency tuples with the explicit format (app_label, object_name, None, True) and may append a second tuple for an explicit through model when present (i.e., (through_app_label, through_object_name, None, True)).
StepsToReproduce: Create a Django project and app with a model that defines a UniqueConstraint referencing a non-existent field, e.g. UniqueConstraint(fields=['missing_field'], name='uq_bad').
Run python manage.py makemigrations for the app.
Observe that makemigrations completes without raising models.E012 or failing on the missing field.
(Optional) Replace the UniqueConstraint with equivalent unique_together=['missing_field'] and run python manage.py makemigrations to observe models.E012 being raised for the missing field.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: makemigrations and the system checks should validate that every field named in a UniqueConstraint exists on the model and surface the error as models.E012 (consistent with unique_together behavior). When a constraint references ForeignKey fields, generated migration operations that represent the constraint should include any relevant migration dependency tuples so migrations record ordering dependencies before applying the constraint.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: makemigrations does not validate UniqueConstraint.fields; no models.E012 is raised for constraints that name non-existent fields. AddConstraint operations are emitted without recorded FK dependency tuples, and missing-field errors are raised later when constraint SQL is generated or when applying migrations.

problem_statement.Title: QuerySet.distinct() silently ignored after combinator (union/intersection/difference)
problem_statement.Description: Calling QuerySet.distinct() after a combinator (.union/.intersection/.difference or combined QuerySets) does not apply DISTINCT/DISTINCT ON to the generated SQL and does not raise; the call is silently ignored.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) QuerySet.distinct() omits the combined-query guard and therefore permits callers to set distinct_fields on a Query that already represents a combined query. Location: django/db/models/query.py :: QuerySet.distinct (lines 1137–1145) — distinct() simply calls obj.query.add_distinct_fields(*field_names) without calling self._not_support_combined_queries('distinct').

2) The low-level Query API allows distinct state to be set regardless of combinator state. Location: django/db/models/sql/query.py :: Query.add_distinct_fields (lines 1840–1846) — add_distinct_fields unconditionally sets self.distinct_fields = field_names and self.distinct = True with no check for self.combinator.

3) The SQL compiler applies DISTINCT only on the non-combinator code path and ignores distinct state when a combinator is present. Location: django/db/models/sql/compiler.py :: SQLCompiler.as_sql and get_combinator_sql (as_sql: lines 498–521; get_combinator_sql: lines 429–482) — if self.query.combinator is set, as_sql calls get_combinator_sql(...) and does not call get_distinct() or apply DISTINCT/DISTINCT ON; the distinct-handling code is only reached on the non-combinator branch.

4) The combination of (1)-(3) produces an observable invariant violation: the public API and lower-level query API allow a Query to have both query.combinator != None and distinct_fields set, but the compiler never applies those distinct fields when compiling combinator queries. This results in a silent no-op (distinct is ignored) rather than an error or an applied DISTINCT in SQL.

5) Investigative note: a repository search found no other internal callers of Query.add_distinct_fields() besides QuerySet.distinct and the method definition itself (search results: django/db/models/query.py line 1144 and django/db/models/sql/query.py line 1840). That reduces the surface for internal bypasses, but direct runtime manipulation of sql.Query instances by third-party code could still create the invalid state; this was not exhaustively ruled out.
StepsToReproduce: Create test data with duplicate values (e.g., multiple Sample objects with the same name).
qs = Sample.objects.filter(user=self.user)
qs1 = qs.filter(name='Dub').annotate(rank=Value(0, IntegerField()))
qs2 = qs.filter(name='Sam1').annotate(rank=Value(1, IntegerField()))
qs = qs1.union(qs2)
qs = qs.order_by('name').distinct('name')
Observe that duplicates are not removed and the call to distinct('name') has no effect (e.g., qs.count() is larger than expected).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When a user calls QuerySet.distinct() after a combinator, the API should not silently ignore the request. The user-visible outcome should be either that DISTINCT/DISTINCT ON is applied to the final result set or that a clear error is raised indicating that DISTINCT is not supported after combined queries (e.g., a NotSupportedError).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Calling QuerySet.distinct() after a combinator records distinct fields on the Query object (distinct_fields is set) but the SQL generated for the combined query does not include DISTINCT/DISTINCT ON (the compiler emits the combinator SQL such as SELECT ... UNION SELECT ...). The distinct() call is silently ignored and duplicates remain.

hints_text: distinct() is not supported but doesn't raise an error yet. As ​​per the documentation, "only LIMIT, OFFSET, COUNT(*), ORDER BY, and specifying columns (i.e. slicing, count(), order_by(), and values()/values_list()) are allowed on the resulting QuerySet.". Follow up to #27995.
