problem_statement.Title: ASGIStaticFilesHandler: TypeError 'NoneType' object is not callable' when serving static files over ASGI
problem_statement.Description: When using django.contrib.staticfiles.handlers.ASGIStaticFilesHandler under ASGI (for example, with daphne), requests for static files raise a TypeError because the ASGI request path attempts to await an uninitialized middleware chain or otherwise lacks an async response path.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) ASGIStaticFilesHandler.__init__ (django/contrib/staticfiles/handlers.py): ASGIStaticFilesHandler.__init__ sets self.application and self.base_url but does not call super().__init__(). Because ASGIHandler.__init__ (django/core/handlers/asgi.py) calls load_middleware(is_async=True), omitting super().__init__() prevents the BaseHandler initialization sequence (including middleware loading) from running and leaves BaseHandler._middleware_chain at its default value of None.

2) StaticFilesHandlerMixin.load_middleware signature and behavior (django/contrib/staticfiles/handlers.py): StaticFilesHandlerMixin defines a no-argument load_middleware(self) that is a no-op (pass). BaseHandler/ASGIHandler expect load_middleware(is_async=...) to be callable during initialization (django/core/handlers/base.py and django/core/handlers/asgi.py). Invoking ASGIHandler.__init__ would call load_middleware(is_async=True); the current mixin signature is incompatible with that call and would raise a TypeError if reached.

3) Missing async response path in StaticFilesHandlerMixin (django/contrib/staticfiles/handlers.py): The mixin implements only a synchronous get_response(self, request) and does not provide a get_response_async. ASGIHandler.__call__ obtains the response via await self.get_response_async(request) (django/core/handlers/asgi.py). With _middleware_chain left unset (None) and no get_response_async override provided by the mixin, awaiting self._middleware_chain(request) results in TypeError: 'NoneType' object is not callable.

4) BaseHandler expectations for middleware/async path (django/core/handlers/base.py): BaseHandler._middleware_chain is initialized to None at class level, and BaseHandler.get_response_async awaits self._middleware_chain(request) and appends request.close to response._resource_closers. The ASGI entrypoint (ASGIHandler.__call__) relies on either a properly-initialized middleware chain or an async response method; the current mixin/ASGI wrapper combination does not satisfy either expectation, producing the observed failure.
StepsToReproduce: Configure Django to use django.contrib.staticfiles.handlers.ASGIStaticFilesHandler as the ASGI application (or otherwise instantiate and expose ASGIStaticFilesHandler via an ASGI server).
Start an ASGI server (example: daphne) pointing at that ASGI application.
Make an HTTP GET request for a static asset (for example, GET /static/<existing-file>).

Observe the server raises TypeError: 'NoneType' object is not callable with a traceback showing ASGIHandler.__call__ invoking await self.get_response_async(request) and failing when _middleware_chain is None.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Static file requests served via the ASGI handler complete successfully and return a valid HTTP response (for example, 200 FileResponse) without raising internal TypeError exceptions; request resource cleanup (such as closing request-related resources) is performed after the response is sent.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: ASGI requests for static files cause the ASGI application to raise TypeError: 'NoneType' object is not callable. The traceback shows django/core/handlers/asgi.py calling await self.get_response_async(request) which in turn attempts to await self._middleware_chain(request) where _middleware_chain is None, and the file is not served.

--- a/django/utils/text.py
+++ b/django/utils/text.py
@@ -393,17 +393,18 @@ def unescape_string_literal(s):

 def slugify(value, allow_unicode=False):
     """
-    Convert to ASCII if 'allow_unicode' is False. Convert spaces to hyphens.
-    Remove characters that aren't alphanumerics, underscores, or hyphens.
-    Convert to lowercase. Also strip leading and trailing whitespace.
+    Convert to ASCII if 'allow_unicode' is False. Convert spaces or repeated
+    dashes to single dashes. Remove characters that aren't alphanumerics,
+    underscores, or hyphens. Convert to lowercase. Also strip leading and
+    trailing whitespace, dashes, and underscores.
     """
     value = str(value)
     if allow_unicode:
         value = unicodedata.normalize('NFKC', value)
     else:
         value = unicodedata.normalize('NFKD', value).encode('ascii', 'ignore').decode('ascii')
-    value = re.sub(r'[^\w\s-]', '', value.lower()).strip()
-    return re.sub(r'[-\s]+', '-', value)
+    value = re.sub(r'[^\w\s-]', '', value.lower())
+    return re.sub(r'[-\s]+', '-', value).strip('-_')
 
 
 def camel_case_to_spaces(value):

problem_statement.Title: django.utils.text.slugify preserves leading underscores and trailing hyphens
problem_statement.Description: slugify(value) in django/utils/text.py leaves leading '_' characters and trailing '-' characters in the returned slug (e.g. "___This is a test ---" -> "___this-is-a-test-").
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) django/utils/text.py::slugify(value, allow_unicode=False) encodes an assumption that removing disallowed characters with re.sub(r"[^\w\s-]", "", value.lower()) and then collapsing hyphens/whitespace with re.sub(r"[-\s]+", "-", value) (plus .strip() which removes only whitespace) is sufficient to produce a clean slug. The implementation sequence is: normalization, value = re.sub(r"[^\w\s-]", "", value.lower()).strip(), then return re.sub(r"[-\s]+", "-", value). This sequence leaves certain separator characters at the ends of the result.

2) Regex-character-class assumption in django/utils/text.py::slugify: the regex r"[^\w\s-]" permits underscore because in Python "\w" includes underscore. As a result, leading underscore characters from the input survive the first substitution and are present in the value passed to the separator-collapsing step.

3) Separator-collapsing assumption in django/utils/text.py::slugify: the regex re.sub(r"[-\s]+", "-", value) collapses runs of hyphens and whitespace into a single hyphen but does not remove hyphens or underscores at the extremes of the string. Therefore a trailing run of hyphens in the input becomes a single trailing '-' in the output, and leading underscores remain unchanged.

4) Call-site propagation: django/template/defaultfilters.py::slugify delegates directly to django.utils.text.slugify (it imports slugify as _slugify and returns _slugify(value)), so the same assumptions and resulting behavior are present in the template filter.

5) Test coverage encoding this assumption: tests that exercise slugify reside in tests/utils_tests/test_text.py::test_slugify and tests/template_tests/filter_tests/test_slugify.py::FunctionTests::test_slugify. The existing test cases do not include inputs with leading underscores or trailing hyphens, so current tests do not assert the desired behavior for such inputs and therefore do not prevent regressions related to leading '_' or trailing '-'.

6) Desired final-value relationship (diagnostic expectation identified during investigation): after removing disallowed characters and collapsing separator runs, the final slug value (user-visible result) should not contain leading '_' or trailing '-' characters. Expressed as an expected relationship for the final slug: final_value should be equivalent to post-collapsing_value.strip("-_") (i.e. no leading/trailing hyphen or underscore).
StepsToReproduce: Start a Python shell with Django importable.
from django.utils import text
text.slugify("___This is a test ---")
Observe the returned string.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: slugify("___This is a test ---") returns "this-is-a-test" (no leading underscores, no trailing hyphens). The template filter defaultfilters.slugify should exhibit the same user-visible behavior.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: slugify("___This is a test ---") returns "___this-is-a-test-": leading underscores from the input are preserved and a trailing hyphen remains. The same behavior is observed via the template slugify filter because it delegates to utils.slugify.

hints_text: The current version of the patch converts all underscores to dashes which (as discussed on the PR) isn't an obviously desired change. A discussion is needed to see if there's consensus about that change.

problem_statement.Title: Query.filter raises NotSupportedError when RHS object has instance attribute 'filterable' (e.g. model field named filterable)
problem_statement.Description: When calling QuerySet.filter(...) where the RHS value is an arbitrary object that has an instance attribute named 'filterable' (for example, a Django model instance with a BooleanField named filterable=False), Django raises NotSupportedError claiming the object's type is disallowed in the filter clause. This happens during filter construction when Query.check_filterable inspects the object.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Query.check_filterable performs an instance-level lookup for the flag using getattr(expression, 'filterable', True) in django/db/models/sql/query.py::Query.check_filterable. Because getattr is called on the object instance, an object that has an instance attribute named 'filterable' (for example, a model instance whose field is named 'filterable') will be treated as non-filterable when that instance attribute is falsy, and NotSupportedError is raised. (See django/db/models/sql/query.py lines where check_filterable calls getattr(expression, 'filterable', True').)

2) The design intent in expressions is that 'filterable' is a class-level marker on expression classes: BaseExpression.filterable = True and some expression subclasses (e.g. Window) set filterable = False in django/db/models/expressions.py. These class-level attributes represent the intended source-of-truth for whether an expression type can appear in WHERE clauses.

3) build_filter and related call sites invoke Query.check_filterable for both the resolved left-hand expression and the RHS value (see django/db/models/sql/query.py where build_filter calls self.check_filterable(reffed_expression) and later self.check_filterable(value)). Because check_filterable currently reads the attribute from the instance, passing a non-expression object (such as a model instance) with an instance attribute named 'filterable' can cause a false negative and raise NotSupportedError. In other words, the instance-level lookup in a single check (Query.check_filterable) is the root cause and it affects any call site that uses that check on arbitrary objects.
StepsToReproduce: Define a model type that includes a BooleanField named 'filterable' (e.g. ProductMetaDataType with filterable = models.BooleanField(default=False)).
Define a model having a ForeignKey to that type (e.g. ProductMetaData with metadata_type = ForeignKey(ProductMetaDataType)).
Create an instance of the type with filterable=False (e.g. brand_metadata = ProductMetaDataType.objects.create(label='brand', filterable=False)).
Run a filter using that instance as the RHS: ProductMetaData.objects.filter(value='Dark Vador', metadata_type=brand_metadata).
Observe the NotSupportedError raised during filter construction.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Filtering by a model instance value (RHS) should return a queryset of matching objects (or an empty queryset) without raising NotSupportedError simply because the model instance has an attribute named 'filterable'. Only expression classes that explicitly mark themselves as non-filterable at the class level should be disallowed from use in WHERE clauses.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: A NotSupportedError is raised during filter construction with message '<ProductMetaDataType> is disallowed in the filter clause.' when the RHS object has an instance attribute 'filterable=False' (for example, a model instance whose BooleanField is named 'filterable').

problem_statement.Title: order_by("record__root_id") appends related model ordering and adds extra JOIN due to attname comparison bug in SQLCompiler.find_ordering_name
problem_statement.Description: Calling order_by() with a multi-part lookup that terminates on a related model's attname (e.g. "record__root_id") can produce an unexpected extra JOIN and cause the related model's Meta.ordering (including its ASC/DESC direction) to be included in the generated ORDER BY, instead of using only the explicit ordering requested. The symptom is visible in the emitted SQL ORDER BY and JOIN clauses.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Attname vs full-lookup comparison: In django/db/models/sql/compiler.py:SQLCompiler.find_ordering_name the code tokenizes the lookup (pieces = name.split(LOOKUP_SEP)) and sets up joins based on those pieces, but then compares the field's attname against the entire lookup string (getattr(field, 'attname', None) != name). For multi-part lookups (e.g. "record__root_id") the field.attname ("root_id") does not equal the full lookup string ("record__root_id"), so this comparison is incorrect and evaluates as if a different attribute were requested.

2) 'pk' special-case applied to full lookup: The same conditional also checks name != 'pk' where name is still the full lookup string. Because Query.names_to_path maps the literal 'pk' to the terminal primary-key field name only when the terminal token is 'pk', testing the whole lookup string against the literal 'pk' fails to recognize the 'pk' shortcut when it appears as the last token in a multi-part lookup.

3) Consequence — opts.ordering appended: When the conditional in (1)/(2) is true and the resolved field is a relation with opts.ordering, find_ordering_name appends the related model's opts.ordering (recursing into find_ordering_name for each item in opts.ordering). As a result, ordering expressions derived from the related model's Meta.ordering (and their directions) are added to the ORDER BY result and may require additional join aliases. This behavior causes the observed extra JOIN and the related model's ordering direction to appear in the final SQL.

4) Supporting behavior in query parsing: Query.names_to_path and Query.setup_joins operate on token lists and already treat the terminal token specially (including mapping the literal 'pk' to opts.pk.name). These methods demonstrate that the correct unit of comparison for attname and the 'pk' shortcut is the terminal lookup token (the last piece), not the full dashed/underscored lookup string.

5) Scope: The incorrect comparison is located in a single place — django/db/models/sql/compiler.py:SQLCompiler.find_ordering_name — and the surrounding call chain (SQLCompiler.get_order_by -> find_ordering_name -> _setup_joins -> Query.setup_joins/names_to_path) explains how the mistaken comparison leads to appending related model ordering and to the observed SQL differences. Because get_order_by delegates to find_ordering_name, all ordering generation that flows through SQLCompiler is affected by this incorrect assumption.
StepsToReproduce:
Define models:
class OneModel(models.Model):
    class Meta:
        ordering = ("-id",)
    id = models.BigAutoField(primary_key=True)
    root = models.ForeignKey("OneModel", on_delete=models.CASCADE, null=True)
    oneval = models.BigIntegerField(null=True)

class TwoModel(models.Model):
    id = models.BigAutoField(primary_key=True)
    record = models.ForeignKey(OneModel, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
    twoval = models.BigIntegerField(null=True)

Run queryset and print SQL:
qs = TwoModel.objects.filter(record__oneval__in=[1,2,3]).order_by("record__root_id")
print(qs.query)
-- Observe an extra LEFT OUTER JOIN and ORDER BY using the related model alias (e.g. ORDER BY T3."id" DESC).

Compare with explicit deep lookup:
qs = TwoModel.objects.filter(record__oneval__in=[1,2,3]).order_by("record__root__id")
print(qs.query)
-- Observe no extra JOIN and ORDER BY on the expected terminal column (e.g. ORDER BY "orion_onemodel"."root_id" ASC).

Optional workaround to show expected SQL:
qs = TwoModel.objects.filter(record__oneval__in=[1,2,3]).annotate(root_id=F("record__root_id")).order_by("root_id")
print(qs.query)
-- Observe single JOIN and ORDER BY on the annotated column.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When ordering by a related field attname using a multi-part lookup (e.g. order_by("record__root_id") or order_by("-record__root_id")): the ORM should generate SQL that orders by the terminal column (record.root_id) in the explicitly requested direction, should not append the related model's Meta.ordering, and should not introduce an extra JOIN beyond those required to reference the terminal column.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Ordering by a multi-part lookup that ends on a related field attname (e.g. order_by("record__root_id") or order_by("record__root")) can produce SQL that: 1) includes an unnecessary extra LEFT OUTER JOIN to the related table, and 2) appends the related model's Meta.ordering (including its ASC/DESC direction) to the ORDER BY. Example: order_by("record__root_id") produced ORDER BY T3."id" DESC (extra JOIN and DESC from related model Meta.ordering) while order_by("record__root__id") produced the expected single-JOIN ORDER BY "orion_onemodel"."root_id" ASC.

This is with a postgres backend. Fairly vanilla Django. Some generic middleware installed (cors, csrf, auth, session). Apps are: INSTALLED_APPS = ( "django.contrib.contenttypes", "django.contrib.auth", "django.contrib.admin", "django.contrib.sessions", "django.contrib.messages", "django.contrib.staticfiles", "corsheaders", "<ours>" )
Something is definitely wrong here. order_by('record__root_id') should result in ORDER BY root_id order_by('record__root') should use OneModel.Meta.ordering because that's what the root foreign key points to ​as documented. That should result in ORDER BY root_join.id DESC order_by('record__root__id') should result in ORDER BY root_join.id
Thanks for this report. A potential fix could be: diff --git a/django/db/models/sql/compiler.py b/django/db/models/sql/compiler.py index abbb1e37cb..a8f5b61fbe 100644 --- a/django/db/models/sql/compiler.py +++ b/django/db/models/sql/compiler.py @@ -727,7 +727,7 @@ class SQLCompiler: # If we get to this point and the field is a relation to another model, # append the default ordering for that model unless it is the pk # shortcut or the attribute name of the field that is specified. - if field.is_relation and opts.ordering and getattr(field, 'attname', None) != name and name != 'pk': + if field.is_relation and opts.ordering and getattr(field, 'attname', None) != pieces[-1] and name != 'pk': # Firstly, avoid infinite loops. already_seen = already_seen or set() join_tuple = tuple(getattr(self.query.alias_map[j], 'join_cols', None) for j in joins) but I didn't check this in details.
FWIW, I did apply the suggested fix to my local version and I do now get what looks like correct behavior and results that match what Simon suggested should be the sort results. Also my annotate "workaround" continues to behave correctly. Looks promising.
Jack, would you like to prepare a patch?

I did some additional work on this to verify the scope of the change. I added the following test code on master and ran the entire test suite: +++ b/django/db/models/sql/compiler.py @@ -727,6 +727,11 @@ class SQLCompiler: # If we get to this point and the field is a relation to another model, # append the default ordering for that model unless it is the pk # shortcut or the attribute name of the field that is specified. + if (field.is_relation and opts.ordering and name != 'pk' and + ((getattr(field, 'attname', None) != name) != + (getattr(field, 'attname', None) != pieces[-1]))): + print(f"JJD <{getattr(field, 'attname', '')}> <{name}> <{pieces[-1]}>") + breakpoint() if field.is_relation and opts.ordering and getattr(field, 'attname', None) != name and name != 'pk': # Firstly, avoid infinite loops. already_seen = already_seen or set() The idea being to display every time that the change from name to pieces[-1] in the code would make a difference in the execution. Of course verified that when running the reproducer one does go into the test block and outputs: JJD <root_id> <record__root_id> <root_id>. The code is not triggered for any other test across the entire test suite, so the scope of the change is not causing unexpected changes in other places. This seems reassuring.

diff --git a/django/db/models/sql/query.py b/django/db/models/sql/query.py
--- a/django/db/models/sql/query.py
+++ b/django/db/models/sql/query.py
@@ -305,6 +305,7 @@ def clone(self):
             obj.annotation_select_mask = None
         else:
             obj.annotation_select_mask = self.annotation_select_mask.copy()
+        obj.combined_queries = tuple(query.clone() for query in self.combined_queries)
         # _annotation_select_cache cannot be copied, as doing so breaks the
         # (necessary) state in which both annotations and
         # _annotation_select_cache point to the same underlying objects.
@@ -1777,6 +1778,8 @@ def split_exclude(self, filter_expr, can_reuse, names_with_path):
 
     def set_empty(self):
         self.where.add(NothingNode(), AND)
+        for query in self.combined_queries:
+            query.set_empty()
 
     def is_empty(self):
         return any(isinstance(c, NothingNode) for c in self.where.children)

problem_statement.Title: QuerySet.none() on combined QuerySets (union/intersection/difference) is not truly empty; empty form submission adds all matching objects
problem_statement.Description: When a QuerySet is constructed using combinator set operations (union(), intersection(), difference()) and then QuerySet.none() is called, the resulting QuerySet is marked empty only at the outer Query.where level. The combinator metadata (query.combinator and query.combined_queries) is left on the Query object and the SQL compiler still consults the original subqueries. In a form using such a queryset (for example, ModelMultipleChoiceField returning queryset.none() on empty submission), submitting the form empty can cause all objects matching the original combined query to be added instead of none.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) django/db/models/query.py::QuerySet.none — incorrect assumption: none() calls clone = self._chain() and then clone.query.set_empty(). It assumes setting the outer query.where to a NothingNode is sufficient to make a previously-combinator QuerySet truly empty. The implementation is:
   def none(self):
       clone = self._chain()
       clone.query.set_empty()
       return clone
   (django/db/models/query.py:919-923)

2) django/db/models/query.py::Query._combinator_query and combinator setters — combinator metadata is placed on the Query by the combinator logic (union()/intersection()/difference()). These methods set query.combined_queries and query.combinator on the Query instance; e.g.:
   clone.query.combined_queries = (self.query,) + tuple(qs.query for qs in other_qs)
   clone.query.combinator = combinator
   clone.query.combinator_all = all
   (django/db/models/query.py:988-997)
   This is the origin of the combinator/composed-subquery data that none() leaves behind.

3) django/db/models/sql/query.py::Query.clone / Query.chain and django/db/models/query.py::QuerySet._clone/_chain — cloning semantics preserve combined_queries on cloned Query objects. Query.clone performs a shallow copy of __dict__ (while cloning where) so attributes like combinator and combined_queries remain on the cloned Query unless explicitly cleared. Example excerpts:
   obj.__dict__ = self.__dict__.copy()
   obj.where = self.where.clone()
   (django/db/models/sql/query.py:288-336)
   QuerySet._clone constructs a new QuerySet by calling query=self.query.chain(), which calls Query.clone(). (django/db/models/query.py:1289-1296)
   As a result, none() operating on a cloned Query does not remove combined_queries unless additional steps are taken.

4) django/db/models/sql/query.py::Query.set_empty and ::Query.is_empty — set_empty inserts a NothingNode into self.where and is_empty reports emptiness by checking for a NothingNode in where.children. These methods operate solely on the where clause and do not clear combinator/composed-query attributes:
   def set_empty(self):
       self.where.add(NothingNode(), AND)
   def is_empty(self):
       return any(isinstance(c, NothingNode) for c in self.where.children)
   (django/db/models/sql/query.py:1778-1782)

5) django/db/models/sql/compiler.py::SQLCompiler.get_combinator_sql — compiler behavior: when compiling combined queries it iterates over self.query.combined_queries and includes SQL for any subquery that is not query.is_empty():
   compilers = [query.get_compiler(...) for query in self.query.combined_queries if not query.is_empty()]
   ...
   part_sql, part_args = compiler.as_sql()
   (django/db/models/sql/compiler.py:433-460)
   Because combined_queries entries remain referencing the original non-empty subqueries, the compiler will compile them even though the outer query.where contains a NothingNode.

6) django/forms/models.py::ModelMultipleChoiceField.clean — form behavior that exposes the bug: ModelMultipleChoiceField.clean returns self.queryset.none() when the field is empty and required=False:
   elif not self.required and not value:
       return self.queryset.none()
   (django/forms/models.py:1321-1326)
   The returned queryset (produced by none()) is later consumed by model-relations code.

7) django/db/models/fields/related_descriptors.py::ManyRelatedManager.set — consumer behavior: ManyRelatedManager.set forces evaluation of the provided iterable via objs = tuple(objs), which causes a queryset returned by clean() to be iterated and compiled. Example:
   def set(self, objs, *, bulk=True, clear=False):
       # Force evaluation of `objs` in case it's a queryset whose value
       # could be affected by `manager.clear()`.
       objs = tuple(objs)
   (django/db/models/fields/related_descriptors.py:732-736)
   Combined, the above items explain why a QuerySet produced by none() on a previously-combinator Query can still result in SQL that includes the original combined subqueries and thus produce results when iterated.
StepsToReproduce: Define models: Publication and Article with publications = ManyToManyField(Publication, blank=True, null=True).

Create a ModelForm (or admin form) with a ModelMultipleChoiceField using a combined queryset built by union(), for example: Publication.objects.filter(id__lt=2).union(Publication.objects.filter(id__gt=5)).
Open the form and submit it without selecting any publications (empty submission, required=False).
Save the form (or save the Article instance).
Observe that all Publication objects matching the combined queryset have been added to the Article's publications relation.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Submitting the form without selecting any publications should result in no publications being associated with the Article (no objects added).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Submitting the form empty results in all Publication objects that match the original combined queryset (the union/intersection/difference) being added to the Article's publications relation.

hints_text: Thanks for the report. QuerySet.none() doesn't work properly on combined querysets, it returns all results instead of an empty queryset.

diff --git a/django/core/exceptions.py b/django/core/exceptions.py
--- a/django/core/exceptions.py
+++ b/django/core/exceptions.py
@@ -1,6 +1,9 @@
 """
 Global Django exception and warning classes.
 """
+import operator
+
+from django.utils.hashable import make_hashable
 
 
 class FieldDoesNotExist(Exception):
@@ -182,6 +185,23 @@ def __str__(self):
     def __repr__(self):
         return 'ValidationError(%s)' % self
 
+    def __eq__(self, other):
+        if not isinstance(other, ValidationError):
+            return NotImplemented
+        return hash(self) == hash(other)
+
+    def __hash__(self):
+        # Ignore params and messages ordering.
+        if hasattr(self, 'message'):
+            return hash((
+                self.message,
+                self.code,
+                tuple(sorted(make_hashable(self.params))) if self.params else None,
+            ))
+        if hasattr(self, 'error_dict'):
+            return hash(tuple(sorted(make_hashable(self.error_dict))))
+        return hash(tuple(sorted(self.error_list, key=operator.attrgetter('message'))))
+
 
 class EmptyResultSet(Exception):
     """A database query predicate is impossible."""

problem_statement.Title: ValidationError instances with identical contents do not compare equal
problem_statement.Description: ValidationError objects (django.core.exceptions.ValidationError) created with identical messages/codes/params do not compare equal (==), even when their visible messages are the same. The comparison is currently identity-based rather than value-based and does not account for the order-independence requested for field/non-field errors.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Missing value-based equality on the exception class: django/core/exceptions.py::ValidationError defines __init__, message_dict, messages, update_error_dict, __iter__, __str__, and __repr__ but does not implement __eq__. Because __eq__ is absent, Python falls back to identity equality for ValidationError instances, so two separately created instances with identical contents compare unequal.

2) Internal normalization but no canonical equality: ValidationError.__init__ normalizes inputs into either self.error_dict (a mapping field -> list of atomic ValidationError instances) or self.error_list (a flat list of atomic ValidationError). Atomic entries (when created as single errors) have attributes message, code, and params. These internal representations provide the canonical data needed for value-based equality, but no equality method uses them today.

3) Formatting behavior and order exposure: The class's __iter__ and messages/message_dict properties expose formatted messages and lists in stored insertion order. In particular, __iter__ formats atomic errors by taking error.message and, when error.params is truthy, applying message %= error.params before yielding the string. Because existing APIs expose ordered lists, a naive equality of messages (e.g., simple list equality) would remain order-dependent.

4) Equality semantics that must be considered (diagnostic goals): For value-based equality to be meaningful and to satisfy the reported expectation of order-independence, equality should compare the semantic content of atomic errors rather than object identity or raw list order. Concretely, equality should be based on a canonical representation of each atomic error that includes at least:
   - the error's visible message (the formatted message string as presented by the public APIs),
   - the error's code (the .code attribute), and
   - a stable representation of the error's params (a normalization that makes equivalent param values compare equal regardless of mutable containers or dict key ordering).
   The current code exposes the raw pieces (message, code, params) but does not define how to canonicalize or aggregate them for equality.

5) Order-independence requirement: Where ValidationError instances represent multiple errors (either via error_list for non-field errors or via error_dict for field-specific errors), equality should treat the collections as multisets of atomic error content per field (i.e., ignore ordering within a field and ignore the ordering of non-field errors). The current class stores and exposes lists in insertion order, so no such multiset semantics exist today.

6) Compatibility and hashing implication (audit gap): Adding a value-based __eq__ would change object equality semantics and (unless __hash__ is also provided) would make ValidationError instances unhashable in Python (Python sets __hash__ = None when __eq__ is defined but __hash__ is not). The codebase raises ValidationError widely; an audit is required to find call sites or tests that rely on identity-based equality or on hashing of ValidationError instances. The present investigation did not exhaustively search for uses of ValidationError as dict keys or set members, so this remains an outstanding compatibility concern.
StepsToReproduce: In a Django shell or test, import ValidationError: from django.core.exceptions import ValidationError

Create two atomic errors with identical message/code/params: e1 = ValidationError('error', code='c', params={'x':1}); e2 = ValidationError('error', code='c', params={'x':1}); compare e1 == e2 (currently False)
Create two non-field (list) errors with same entries but different order: e3 = ValidationError(['a', 'b']); e4 = ValidationError(['b', 'a']); compare e3 == e4 (currently False)

Create two field-mapped errors with same field contents but different ordering of the field lists: e5 = ValidationError({'field': ['x', 'y']}); e6 = ValidationError({'field': ['y', 'x']}); compare e5 == e6 (currently False)
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Two ValidationError instances that contain equivalent information (same set of fields, and for each atomic error the same formatted message, same code, and equivalent params) should compare equal (==). Equality should be independent of the ordering of errors within a field and of non-field (top-level) errors; different codes or non-equivalent params should make errors unequal.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: ValidationError equality falls back to object identity. Two instances with identical messages (and identical codes/params) are not equal unless they are the same object. Comparisons are order-dependent because no multiset-based equality is defined.

hints_text:
I probably wouldn't want to limit the comparison to an error's message but rather to its full set of attributes (message, code, params). While params is always pushed into message when iterating over the errors in an ValidationError, I believe it can be beneficial to know if the params that were put inside are the same.
​PR

problem_statement.Title: django.contrib.syndication.views.Feed.get_feed does not forward item_comments to feed.add_item
problem_statement.Description: django.contrib.syndication.views.Feed.get_feed does not pass an item-level comments value to feed.add_item(), preventing Feed subclasses from defining item_comments(self, item) to set per-item comments. As a result callers must supply a 'comments' key via item_extra_kwargs to include comments in generated RSS items, despite the feed generator already supporting a comments field.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Missing forwarding of item-level comments in views.Feed.get_feed — location: django/contrib/syndication/views.py, class Feed, method get_feed (feed.add_item(...) invocation at lines 202–218). The method constructs explicit standard item arguments but never obtains or forwards the per-item item_comments value via _get_dynamic_attr('item_comments', item); only **self.item_extra_kwargs(item) is expanded.

2) views.Feed.get_feed treats item-level comments as not a first-class forwarded field — location: same as (1). The implementation makes an implicit assumption that callers will provide non-standard keys (including 'comments') through item_extra_kwargs rather than forwarding item_comments directly.

3) The feed generator already defines and emits a comments field — location: django/utils/feedgenerator.py, class SyndicationFeed.add_item (signature includes comments=None and stores item['comments'], lines 85–88 and 106) and class Rss201rev2Feed.add_item_elements (emits <comments> when item['comments'] is not None, lines 262–265). Therefore the missing behavior is an omission in the views adapter layer, not in the generation layer.

4) Duplicate-key and precedence risk when merging item_extra_kwargs with explicit fields — location: django/contrib/syndication/views.py, get_feed (add_item call expands **self.item_extra_kwargs(item) at line 217). If a future change adds an explicit comments=... argument while still expanding item_extra_kwargs, a 'comments' key present in item_extra_kwargs(item) could cause a duplicate-key TypeError or ambiguous overriding semantics. The diagnostic requirement is that a deterministic precedence be defined: the per-item item_comments hook should be considered a standard source of the comments value, with a clear rule for how it relates to any 'comments' key present in item_extra_kwargs (for example: prefer a non-None item_comments value, otherwise fall back to item_extra_kwargs['comments']).

5) Other arbitrary extra keys (e.g., geometry) are currently passed via item_extra_kwargs and must be preserved — location: django/contrib/gis/feeds.py, class Feed (subclassing views.Feed), methods feed_extra_kwargs and item_extra_kwargs return {'geometry': ...} (lines 136–140). Any change to how extra kwargs are merged must maintain forwarding of such non-standard keys to feed.add_item so existing consumers (Geo feeds) continue to receive them.

6) Missing tests and documentation coverage for item_comments — evidence: no occurrences of 'item_comments' found in the codebase search. There are no existing tests exercising the item_comments hook; this omission likely allowed the regression to go undetected.
StepsToReproduce: Create a Feed subclass and implement item_comments(self, item) to return a comments URL or text for each item.
Wire the Feed to a URL in a Django project and request the RSS feed from that URL.
Inspect the generated feed output for the item <comments> element — observe that it is absent.
As a control, implement item_extra_kwargs(self, item) to return {'comments': '<url-or-text>'}, request the feed again, and observe that the <comments> element is present.

Optionally, implement both item_comments and item_extra_kwargs returning different values and observe which value is used (current behavior: item_comments is not forwarded by get_feed and thus has no effect unless comments is supplied via item_extra_kwargs).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When a Feed subclass defines item_comments(self, item), the returned value should be used for the per-item comments field passed to the feed generator so that RSS output includes an <comments> element for that item. If both item_comments and item_extra_kwargs provide a comments value, a deterministic precedence should apply (diagnostically: item_comments should be considered a first-class per-item source and take precedence when it returns a non-None value; otherwise fall back to any 'comments' in item_extra_kwargs). Other arbitrary keys returned by item_extra_kwargs (for example, 'geometry' used by Geo feeds) should continue to be forwarded unchanged to feed.add_item.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Defining item_comments(self, item) in a Feed subclass has no effect: views.Feed.get_feed does not forward item_comments to feed.add_item, so per-item comments are not emitted via the item_comments hook. Only providing 'comments' via item_extra_kwargs results in comments being present in RSS output. The codebase contains no tests referencing item_comments and no documentation examples exercising the hook.

problem_statement.Title: AddIndex(fields=['look','_order']) generated/applied before AlterOrderWithRespectTo — migrations fail because implicit _order column does not exist
problem_statement.Description: When a model declares order_with_respect_to and an Index references the implicit '_order' field (for example, Index(fields=['look', '_order'])), makemigrations can emit an AddIndex operation that references '_order' before it emits an AlterOrderWithRespectTo operation that creates the implicit column. Applying the resulting migration fails because the AddIndex operation runs while the '_order' column does not yet exist in the database.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) django/db/migrations/autodetector.py:MigrationAutodetector._detect_changes (generate_added_indexes / generate_altered_order_with_respect_to)
   - _detect_changes calls generate_added_indexes() before generate_altered_order_with_respect_to(), so AddIndex operations can be appended into self.generated_operations earlier than the AlterOrderWithRespectTo operation that creates the implicit '_order' column. (See the generate_added_indexes()/generate_altered_order_with_respect_to call sequence in _detect_changes.)

2) django/db/migrations/autodetector.py:add_operation and _build_migration_list / _sort_migrations / _optimize_migrations
   - add_operation sets operation._auto_deps and appends (or inserts) operations into generated_operations in emission order. The generated_operations list is later chopped and sorted into migrations by _build_migration_list and _sort_migrations and then optimized in _optimize_migrations. These pieces preserve the relative ordering that can leave an earlier-emitted AddIndex before a later-emitted AlterOrderWithRespectTo in the final migration operations list.

3) django/db/migrations/operations/models.py:CreateModel.reduce
   - CreateModel.reduce contains a reduction that folds an AlterOrderWithRespectTo into a CreateModel by adding 'order_with_respect_to' to the CreateModel.options. That folding is performed when the optimizer attempts to reduce a CreateModel with a later AlterOrderWithRespectTo, producing a CreateModel that implies the implicit '_order' column exists as part of the CreateModel's effect.

4) django/db/migrations/optimizer.py:MigrationOptimizer.optimize
   - The optimizer performs right-side reductions and, when a reduction is applied on the right, it preserves the slice of operations that were in-between the pair (the in_between slice is copied before the reduction result). As a result, an AddIndex that was emitted between a CreateModel and its corresponding AlterOrderWithRespectTo can remain earlier than the folded CreateModel in the optimized operations list.

5) django/db/migrations/operations/models.py:AlterOrderWithRespectTo.database_forwards
   - AlterOrderWithRespectTo.database_forwards is the operation that actually creates (or removes) the implicit '_order' database column: it obtains to_model._meta.get_field('_order') and calls schema_editor.add_field(...) when adding order_with_respect_to. Therefore the database column '_order' comes into existence only once this operation (or an equivalent CreateModel that includes the option) has been applied.

6) django/db/migrations/operations/models.py:AddIndex.database_forwards (and state_forwards)
   - AddIndex.state_forwards appends the index to the model state's 'indexes' option, and AddIndex.database_forwards calls schema_editor.add_index(model, self.index). There is no implicit creation of the '_order' column by AddIndex; if the index references a non-existent column (like '_order' before AlterOrderWithRespectTo has run), schema_editor.add_index will fail. Any operation that produces DDL referencing the implicit '_order' column (AddIndex, AddConstraint, etc.) is subject to this ordering requirement.

Combined effect: the observed failure arises from the combination of (a) autodetector emitting AddIndex before emitting AlterOrderWithRespectTo, (b) add_operation/appending and migration chopping/sorting/optimizing preserving that relative emission order, and (c) CreateModel.reduce + MigrationOptimizer.optimize folding behavior that can produce a CreateModel that implies creation of '_order' while leaving AddIndex positioned earlier in the final operations list. The code paths above are the locations where this ordering behavior is encoded and where the mismatch between "when an index is emitted" and "when the implicit column is created" manifests.
StepsToReproduce: Define a new model with Meta.order_with_respect_to = 'look' and an Index that includes the implicit field, e.g. Index(fields=['look', '_order']).
Run python manage.py makemigrations to generate migrations for the new model.

Inspect the generated migration file: confirm the sequence contains CreateModel(...), AddIndex(model='lookimage', fields=['look','_order']), ..., AlterOrderWithRespectTo(name='lookimage', order_with_respect_to='look').
Run python manage.py migrate to apply the migration.
Observe the migration failing when applying AddIndex due to a missing column '_order'.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The generated migration sequence ensures the implicit '_order' column (created by AlterOrderWithRespectTo or by a CreateModel that includes order_with_respect_to) is created before any migration operation that performs database DDL referencing that column (such as AddIndex or AddConstraint). Migrations should apply successfully and an index on ['look', '_order'] should be created only after the '_order' column exists.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Makemigrations (autodetector) generates a migration where AddIndex(fields=['look','_order']) appears before the AlterOrderWithRespectTo that creates the implicit '_order' column; running migrate fails when the AddIndex operation attempts to create an index on the non-existent '_order' column.

 def apply_limit_choices_to_to_formfield(formfield):
     """Apply limit_choices_to to the formfield's queryset if needed."""
+    from django.db.models import Exists, OuterRef, Q
     if hasattr(formfield, 'queryset') and hasattr(formfield, 'get_limit_choices_to'):
         limit_choices_to = formfield.get_limit_choices_to()
-        if limit_choices_to is not None:

+        if limit_choices_to:
+            complex_filter = limit_choices_to
+            if not isinstance(complex_filter, Q):
+                complex_filter = Q(**limit_choices_to)
+            complex_filter &= Q(pk=OuterRef('pk'))
+            # Use Exists() to avoid potential duplicates.
+            formfield.queryset = formfield.queryset.filter(
+                Exists(formfield.queryset.model._base_manager.filter(complex_filter)),
+            )

problem_statement.Title: limit_choices_to using a Q() that forces JOINs can produce duplicate options in ForeignKey/form choices
problem_statement.Description: When a ForeignKey (or reverse relation) has limit_choices_to supplied as a Q() that causes SQL JOINs, the choice lists built for forms and the admin can contain duplicate options for the same related instance.
problem_statement.RootCause:
The code that builds choice lists or assigns filtered querysets makes an incorrect assumption that the QuerySet returned by rel_model._default_manager.complex_filter(limit_choices_to) (or equivalent calls) yields each related model instance exactly once. When limit_choices_to is a Q() that references related tables, the SQL produced can include JOINs that cause the underlying query to return multiple rows representing the same related model instance. The following locations exhibit this incorrect assumption (each finding given equal weight):

1) django/db/models/fields/__init__.py::Field.get_choices
- Behavior: calls rel_model._default_manager.complex_filter(limit_choices_to), optionally applies order_by, then iterates the queryset to produce choices: [(choice_func(x), str(x)) for x in qs].
- Incorrect assumption: the queryset yields unique model instances; no deduplication is performed before iteration.

2) django/db/models/fields/reverse_related.py::ForeignObjectRel.get_choices
- Behavior: calls self.related_model._default_manager.complex_filter(limit_choices_to), optionally applies order_by, then iterates the queryset to produce choices: [(x.pk, str(x)) for x in qs].
- Incorrect assumption: the queryset yields unique model instances; no deduplication is performed before iteration.

3) django/forms/models.py::apply_limit_choices_to_to_formfield
- Behavior: when a form field has a queryset and a limit_choices_to, the function assigns formfield.queryset = formfield.queryset.complex_filter(limit_choices_to).
- Incorrect assumption: the resulting queryset will not contain duplicate model instances; no deduplication is applied at assignment time.

4) django/forms/models.py::ModelChoiceIterator.__iter__
- Behavior: iterates over self.queryset (or queryset.iterator()) and yields each object's choice without performing any deduplication.
- Incorrect assumption: the iterator assumes the queryset it receives has been prepared to contain unique model instances.

5) Admin callers that rely on Field.get_choices / remote_field.get_choices
- Behavior: admin components (e.g., RelatedFieldListFilter and formfield_for_choice_field) call field.get_choices and use its returned choices in the UI.
- Incorrect assumption: these callers rely on get_choices to supply unique choice tuples; when get_choices iterates a non-deduplicated queryset, duplicate options are surfaced in the admin UI. Note: other admin code (e.g., AllValuesFieldListFilter) uses distinct() in its lookup path, which illustrates an inconsistency.

Additional context: complex_filter accepts Q objects and adds them to the query, which can introduce JOINs that cause duplicate rows for the same instance. Other uses of complex_filter exist (e.g., validation paths), but the primary user-visible duplication arises where querysets are converted into choice lists or iterated to produce choices.
StepsToReproduce: Define two or more models such that filtering the related model with a Q() causes SQL JOINs (for example, ModelA has a ForeignKey to ModelB, and ModelB has another relation that is referenced by the Q object so the filter touches an additional table).

Create database rows so that the complex_filter(limit_choices_to) condition matches the same related instance via multiple joined rows (i.e., the underlying SQL would return multiple rows with the same related PK).
Set limit_choices_to on the ForeignKey (or use a reverse relation) using a Q() that references the related table (the Q() used in step 1).
Render a ModelChoiceField for that ForeignKey (or open the admin form/filter that uses Field.get_choices / remote_field.get_choices) so that choices are built from the filtered queryset.
Observe that the rendered choices list contains duplicate entries for the same related instance.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Form fields and admin choice lists for the ForeignKey/reverse relation show each related instance exactly once (no duplicate options), regardless of whether limit_choices_to is supplied as a plain dict or as a Q() that requires JOINs.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: When limit_choices_to is supplied as a Q() that causes SQL JOINs, the code iterates or presents the raw queryset returned by complex_filter(limit_choices_to) without deduplication, producing duplicate options for the same related instance in forms and admin choice lists.

Copying here for reference: class PointField(models.Field): description = _("A geometric point") __metaclass__ = models.SubfieldBase pattern = re.compile('^\(([\d\.]+),([\d\.]+)\)$') def db_type(self, connection): if connection.settings_dict['ENGINE'] is not 'django.db.backends.postgresql_psycopg2': return None return 'point' def to_python(self, value): if isinstance(value, tuple): return (float(value[0]), float(value[1])) if not value: return (0, 0) match = self.pattern.findall(value)[0] return (float(match[0]), float(match[1])) def get_prep_value(self, value): return self.to_python(value) def get_db_prep_value(self, value, connection, prepared=False): # Casts dates into the format expected by the backend if not prepared: value = self.get_prep_value(value) return '({0}, {1})'.format(value[0], value[1]) def get_prep_lookup(self, lookup_type, value): raise TypeError('Lookup type %r not supported.' % lookup_type) def value_to_string(self, obj): value = self._get_val_from_obj(obj) return self.get_db_prep_value(value)
This is nasty because it not only renders duplicates but also blows up when .get() is called on the queryset if you select one of the duplicates (MultipleObjectsReturned).

diff --git a/django/contrib/sessions/backends/base.py b/django/contrib/sessions/backends/base.py
--- a/django/contrib/sessions/backends/base.py
+++ b/django/contrib/sessions/backends/base.py
@@ -121,6 +121,15 @@ def decode(self, session_data):
             return signing.loads(session_data, salt=self.key_salt, serializer=self.serializer)
         # RemovedInDjango40Warning: when the deprecation ends, handle here
         # exceptions similar to what _legacy_decode() does now.
+        except signing.BadSignature:
+            try:
+                # Return an empty session if data is not in the pre-Django 3.1
+                # format.
+                return self._legacy_decode(session_data)
+            except Exception:
+                logger = logging.getLogger('django.security.SuspiciousSession')
+                logger.warning('Session data corrupted')
+                return {}
         except Exception:
             return self._legacy_decode(session_data)

diff --git a/tests/sessions_tests/tests.py b/tests/sessions_tests/tests.py
--- a/tests/sessions_tests/tests.py

     def test_decode_failure_logged_to_security(self):
-        bad_encode = base64.b64encode(b'flaskdj:alkdjf').decode('ascii')
-        with self.assertLogs('django.security.SuspiciousSession', 'WARNING') as cm:
-            self.assertEqual({}, self.session.decode(bad_encode))
-        # The failed decode is logged.
-        self.assertIn('corrupted', cm.output[0])
+        tests = [
+            base64.b64encode(b'flaskdj:alkdjf').decode('ascii'),
+            'bad:encoded:value',
+        ]
+        for encoded in tests:
+            with self.subTest(encoded=encoded):
+                with self.assertLogs('django.security.SuspiciousSession', 'WARNING') as cm:
+                    self.assertEqual(self.session.decode(encoded), {})
+                # The failed decode is logged.
+                self.assertIn('Session data corrupted', cm.output[0])

problem_statement.Title: Malformed/legacy session_data can raise binascii.Error and cause 500 from db session backend
problem_statement.Description: Requests can crash with an Internal Server Error (500) when the session store encounters malformed or legacy session_data that is not valid ASCII base64. The crash is observed when a browser sends an old/invalid session cookie and the database-backed session loader attempts to decode it.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) django/contrib/sessions/backends/base.py :: SessionBase._legacy_decode — The method performs base64.b64decode(session_data.encode('ascii')) before entering its existing try/except block (see base.py line where encoded_data = base64.b64decode(session_data.encode('ascii'))). This assumes session_data is an ASCII-compatible, correctly-padded base64 string. If session_data is not ASCII or is not valid base64 (for example, incorrectly padded), base64.b64decode or .encode('ascii') will raise binascii.Error or UnicodeEncodeError which are not caught by _legacy_decode and therefore propagate out of decode().

2) django/contrib/sessions/backends/base.py :: SessionBase._legacy_encode/_legacy_decode — The legacy format encodes data as base64.b64encode(hash.encode() + b':' + serialized).decode('ascii'), so the decoder expects decoded bytes to have the form b'<hash>:<serialized>' and verifies expected_hash = self._hash(serialized) (then compares hash.decode() vs expected_hash). The split and subsequent verification are inside _legacy_decode's try/except (so ValueError from a missing ':' or serializer errors are handled and result in an empty session). The uncovered problem is that base64 decoding (and ASCII encoding of the input) is performed before that try/except, so base64-related or encoding errors escape handling.

3) django/contrib/sessions/backends/db.py :: SessionStore.load — The DB session loader calls self.decode(s.session_data) if a non-expired session row is found (return self.decode(s.session_data) if s else {}). There is no try/except around decode() here, so any exception propagated from SessionBase.decode/_legacy_decode (including binascii.Error from base64 decoding) will bubble up into request handling and can trigger a 500.

4) django/contrib/sessions/backends/signed_cookies.py :: SessionStore.load (contrast) — The signed_cookies backend wraps signing.loads(...) in a try/except and resets the session on error (calls create() and returns {}), so malformed input there does not produce an uncaught binascii.Error. Additionally, django/core/signing.py uses urlsafe base64 decoding with explicit padding handling, so signing.loads raises BadSignature rather than raw binascii.Error for signature failures. This contrast shows the exposure is specific to backends that rely on the legacy decode path (i.e., persist raw legacy base64 strings and call SessionBase.decode on them).

5) Scope: Any backend that loads persisted session_data and calls SessionBase.decode (and therefore may fall back to _legacy_decode) is potentially affected; the db backend is a confirmed example because it directly returns decode(s.session_data) without guarding decode() exceptions.
StepsToReproduce: Run a Django site configured to use the database session backend.

Insert or modify a session row in the django_session table so that session_data is an invalid or malformed base64 string (for example, a truncated or incorrectly-padded string that will cause base64.b64decode(...).a2b_base64 to raise binascii.Error).
Send a request to the site with the cookie corresponding to that session (visit any page that triggers session access, e.g. a protected page).

Observe an Internal Server Error (500) and a traceback showing binascii.Error: Incorrect padding originating from base64.b64decode(session_data.encode('ascii')) during SessionBase._legacy_decode, bubbling through SessionStore.load.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When a request includes malformed or legacy session data, the server should not crash. The session should be treated as empty or invalid (the user appears anonymous or is asked to re-authenticate) and the page should render normally without a 500 caused by session decoding.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Requests that include certain malformed or legacy session cookies cause a 500 Internal Server Error. The server traceback shows binascii.Error: Incorrect padding raised by base64.b64decode(session_data.encode('ascii')) inside SessionBase._legacy_decode; this exception propagates through db.SessionStore.load into request handling and aborts the request (e.g. login attempts from the affected browser fail).

hints_text:
I tried to run clearsessions, but that didn't help. The only workaround was to delete all rows in the django_session table.
Thanks for this report, however I cannot reproduce this issue. Can you provide a sample project? Support for user sessions created by older versions of Django remains until Django 4.0. See similar tickets #31864, #31592, and #31274, this can be a duplicate of one of them.
Thanks for the response. It does look similar to the other issues you posted. I don't have a reproducible instance at present. The only way I can think to reproduce would be to start up a 3.0 site, login, wait for the session to expire, then upgrade to 3.1. These are the steps that would have happened on the environment where I encountered the issue.
Thanks I was able to reproduce this issue with an invalid session data. Regression in d4fff711d4c97356bd6ba1273d2a5e349326eb5f.

problem_statement.Title: Abstract model fields compare equal across different concrete models (creation_counter-only comparison)
problem_statement.Description: When a Field is declared on an abstract model and copied into multiple concrete subclasses, the resulting Field instances can compare equal and hash-equal, causing deduplication/collisions (for example, putting the two fields into a set yields length 1). This is visible when using sets, dicts, or other consumers that rely on equality, hashing, or deterministic ordering of Field instances.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Field equality uses creation_counter only (django/db/models/fields/__init__.py:Field.__eq__). The implementation returns self.creation_counter == other.creation_counter when comparing two Field instances (see lines where __eq__ returns creation_counter equality). This encodes the incorrect assumption that creation_counter alone is a unique identity for a Field instance.

2) Field ordering uses creation_counter only (django/db/models/fields/__init__.py:Field.__lt__). The implementation compares self.creation_counter < other.creation_counter. This encodes the incorrect assumption that creation_counter defines a total order with no ties; ties can occur when a field is deep-copied into multiple concrete models.

3) Field hashing uses creation_counter only (django/db/models/fields/__init__.py:Field.__hash__). The implementation returns hash(self.creation_counter), so two distinct Field instances with the same creation_counter will hash-equal and collide in sets/dicts.

4) Abstract-base fields are deep-copied into concrete subclasses (django/db/models/base.py:ModelBase.__new__). During class creation, fields from abstract bases are copied via copy.deepcopy(field) and added to the concrete class (new_field = copy.deepcopy(field); new_class.add_to_class(field.name, new_field)). Because Field.__copy__/__deepcopy__ copies the instance __dict__, the creation_counter value is preserved on the copy. As a result, separate Field instances attached to different models can carry identical creation_counter values.

5) Field instances acquire per-model identity when attached (django/db/models/fields/__init__.py:Field.contribute_to_class). contribute_to_class sets self.model = cls before registering the field with cls._meta, so each copied field instance has a distinct attached-model reference after being added to its concrete class.

6) Consumers rely on Field ordering and equality semantics (django/db/models/options.py:Options.add_field). Options.add_field uses bisect.insort(self.local_fields, field) to insert fields in creation order, so ordering depends on Field.__lt__ being deterministic and consistent with equality/hash semantics.

7) Correct relationship (diagnostic): for distinct Field instances attached to different concrete models, a correct identity relation must distinguish them even when creation_counter values are equal. In relational terms (as found during investigation): equality should require creation_counter equality AND identical attached-model identity (i.e., the model referenced by the field instance). Hashing should incorporate both creation_counter and the attached-model identity to avoid collisions for deep-copied fields. Ordering semantics should preserve creation_counter as the primary key for order but provide a deterministic tie-breaker based on model identity when creation_counters are equal (for example comparing model labels or a stable model identity). These relationship formulas describe what must hold for equality, hashing, and ordering to avoid the observed collisions; they are reported as diagnostics, not as prescriptive implementation code.

8) Outstanding verification gap: the code inspection confirms deepcopy is used and that deepcopy preserves creation_counter, closing that particular investigation gap. However, the potential for ordering-sensitive regressions (tests that rely on previous equality/ordering semantics) was not validated by executing the project's test-suite in this review. A test-suite run is required to determine whether changing equality/hashing/ordering semantics would cause regressions in consumers that implicitly relied on the prior behavior.
StepsToReproduce:
Obtain the Field instances and check equality and set membership:
field_b = B._meta.get_field('myfield')
field_c = C._meta.get_field('myfield')
Evaluate: len({field_b, field_c}) and (field_b == field_c)
Observe that len({field_b, field_c}) == 1 and field_b == field_c (unexpected deduplication) when the two fields have identical creation_counter values.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Field instances attached to different concrete models (even if copied from the same abstract base) should be treated as distinct by equality and hashing so they are not deduplicated in sets/dicts. Deterministic ordering used for insertion should preserve creation order as the primary key but must not treat distinct per-model Field instances as equal simply because they share the same creation_counter.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Distinct Field instances that originate from the same abstract base may retain identical creation_counter values; because Field.__eq__ and Field.__hash__ rely only on creation_counter, those distinct instances compare equal and hash-equal. As a result, sets/dicts can deduplicate them (set length becomes 1) and ordering/insertions that rely on Field.__lt__ (which uses creation_counter only) can encounter tie situations.

problem_statement.Title: AdminSite app_list per-model dicts omit Model class (no public build_app_dict API)
problem_statement.Description: In the Django admin, the app_list context (produced by AdminSite._build_app_dict and returned by get_app_list / used by app_index) constructs per-model dictionaries that do not include the actual Model class object. The method that builds this structure is private (_build_app_dict) and there is no public build_app_dict API.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) AdminSite._build_app_dict (django/contrib/admin/sites.py::AdminSite._build_app_dict) constructs per-model dictionaries containing only serialized fields: 'name', 'object_name', 'perms', 'admin_url', and 'add_url' (and optionally 'view_only'). The code creating each model_dict does not include or expose the Model class object (the 'model' key is absent). Evidence: model_dict is constructed as a dict with those keys and no 'model' key (django/contrib/admin/sites.py lines 462–469, 472–480).

2) There is no public AdminSite.build_app_dict API in the codebase; the only builder present is the private _build_app_dict. Call sites within the admin use this private method (get_app_list and app_index call _build_app_dict). Location: django/contrib/admin/sites.py (definition at line 433; calls at lines 506 and 537). Search shows no public build_app_dict function defined in the repository.

3) Internal callers and templates encode the assumption that model dicts only need human-readable/URL/permission keys. The admin templates and AdminSite consumers reference only fields such as model.name, model.object_name, model.admin_url, model.add_url, and model.view_only (django/contrib/admin/templates/admin/app_list.html lines 10–32), and admin methods sort and return the produced dicts without expecting a 'model' key (django/contrib/admin/sites.py lines 506–515, 537–546).

4) Permission-related methods are used to determine which models appear in the app list; these methods operate on ModelAdmin internals and do not require a 'model' key in the produced dict. _build_app_dict calls model_admin.has_module_permission(request) and model_admin.get_model_perms(request) to decide inclusion and to populate the 'perms' dict (django/contrib/admin/sites.py lines 451 and 455; ModelAdmin.get_model_perms implementation in django/contrib/admin/options.py lines 653–664).

5) The admin producers do not perform or enforce serialization of the app_list or its model entries. _build_app_dict returns Python dicts which are placed into template contexts (each_context, index, and app_index pass the result into TemplateResponse), and there are no checks in these methods preventing non-JSON-serializable values from being present in the context (django/contrib/admin/sites.py lines 312–318, 524–531, 542–549). Consequently, if a Model class object were added directly into each model dict, external consumers that attempt to JSON-serialize or otherwise export the app_list could encounter failures — this is a compatibility risk for external code rather than an observed core error.
StepsToReproduce: Run a Django project with the admin enabled and log in as a staff/superuser.
Open the admin index (site root at /admin/) or the app-specific index.
Inspect the app list data produced by the admin (for example, call admin.site.get_app_list(request) from a view or shell with a RequestFactory request) and examine each app['models'] entry.
Observe that each model dictionary contains keys like 'name', 'object_name', 'perms', 'admin_url', 'add_url' but does not include a 'model' key referencing the Model class.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: For each model entry in the admin app list (the app_list used by index and app_index), the context returned to views/templates should provide a way for consumers to access the actual Model class when desired (i.e., each model entry should expose the model object or otherwise make the class available through a supported API), so custom admin views and template-level consumers can use the class directly.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Per-model dictionaries produced for the admin app list omit the Model class (no 'model' key). The builder method that creates these dicts is private (_build_app_dict) and there is no public AdminSite.build_app_dict API.

PASS_TO_PASS: ["test_add_action (admin_views.test_adminsite.SiteActionsTests)", "test_disable_action (admin_views.test_adminsite.SiteActionsTests)", "AdminSite.get_action() returns an action even if it's disabled.", "test_each_context (admin_views.test_adminsite.SiteEachContextTest)", "test_each_context_site_url_with_script_name (admin_views.test_adminsite.SiteEachContextTest)"]

+            # Migrations run.
+            mocked_migrate.assert_called()
+            args, kwargs = mocked_migrate.call_args
+            self.assertEqual(args, ([('app_unmigrated', '0001_initial')],))
+            self.assertEqual(len(kwargs['plan']), 1)
+            # App is not synced.
+            mocked_sync_apps.assert_not_called()
         finally:
             with mock.patch.object(creation, '_destroy_test_db'):
                 creation.destroy_test_db(old_database_name, verbosity=0)

problem_statement.Title: Test runner setup_databases crashes when TEST.MIGRATE is False (serialization queries missing tables)
problem_statement.Description: Running ./manage.py test with DATABASES[...]['TEST']['MIGRATE'] = False causes the test setup to crash during database serialization. The test database can be created without running migrations, but the test-DB serialization step selects apps based on migrations present on disk and then queries model tables that do not exist in the database, producing database errors during test setup.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) django/db/backends/base/creation.py::BaseDatabaseCreation.create_test_db — create_test_db will call self.serialize_db_to_string() whenever the caller requests serialization (serialize=True), even when tests were configured with TEST.MIGRATE = False and migrations were skipped. (See create_test_db: it checks TEST.MIGRATE to decide whether to run migrate, then separately executes the serialize block if serialize is truthy.)

2) django/db/backends/base/creation.py::BaseDatabaseCreation.serialize_db_to_string — serialize_db_to_string selects apps to serialize by checking MigrationLoader(self.connection).migrated_apps (which reflects migrations present on disk) and then iterates each model's default manager (queryset.iterator()) to read rows from the database without checking that the model's DB table exists. Because migrated_apps comes from disk, serialize_db_to_string can attempt to query tables that were not created when migrations were skipped.

3) django/db/migrations/loader.py::MigrationLoader.load_disk — MigrationLoader.migrated_apps is populated from migrations found on disk (presence of a migrations module/package), and does not represent which migrations are applied to the current database. This disk-vs-applied mismatch is part of the root cause.

4) django/db/migrations/recorder.py::MigrationRecorder.applied_migrations — applied_migrations() returns an empty mapping when the django_migrations table does not exist (i.e., when migrations haven't been applied). This shows that applied-migration state can legitimately be empty while migrated_apps is non-empty.

5) django/test/utils.py::setup_databases — the test runner sets serialize from connection.settings_dict['TEST'].get('SERIALIZE', True) and passes that value into create_test_db. Because SERIALIZE defaults to True, the default behavior is to attempt serialization even when TEST.MIGRATE is False, exposing the mismatch described above.

6) tests/backends/base/test_creation.py — existing unit tests that exercise TEST.MIGRATE = False call create_test_db with serialize=False explicitly; no test in that file exercises the default SERIALIZE=True + MIGRATE=False combination. This coverage gap allowed the regression to reach users.
StepsToReproduce: In settings.DATABASES for your database alias, add a TEST setting with: {'MIGRATE': False} (do not set SERIALIZE).
Ensure the project has installed apps that include migrations on disk (for example, django.contrib.admin which references the django_admin_log table).
Run the test suite: ./manage.py test
Observe the traceback during test setup/create_test_db showing a ProgrammingError for a missing table (e.g., relation "django_admin_log" does not exist) and a subsequent InvalidCursorName.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When DATABASES[...]['TEST']['MIGRATE'] is False (migrations skipped for the test database), test database setup should not attempt to read model tables that were not created. The test runner should not crash with missing-table errors during setup for the default SERIALIZE setting.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The test run aborts during setup_databases/create_test_db: serialization code queries tables for apps that have migrations on disk but whose tables were not created (because TEST.MIGRATE is False), producing a ProgrammingError (e.g., relation "django_admin_log" does not exist) and a follow-on InvalidCursorName, causing the test run to crash.

hints_text:
Thanks for this report, now I see that we need to synchronize all apps when MIGRATE is False, see comment. I've totally missed this when reviewing f5ebdfce5c417f9844e86bccc2f12577064d4bad. We can remove the feature from 3.1 if fix is not trivial.
Mocking settings.MIGRATION_MODULES to None for all apps sounds like an easier fix, see draft below: diff --git a/django/db/backends/base/creation.py b/django/db/backends/base/creation.py index 503f7f56fd..3c0338d359 100644 --- a/django/db/backends/base/creation.py +++ b/django/db/backends/base/creation.py @@ -69,6 +69,22 @@ class BaseDatabaseCreation: database=self.connection.alias, run_syncdb=True, ) + else: + try: + old = settings.MIGRATION_MODULES + settings.MIGRATION_MODULES = { + app.label: None + for app in apps.get_app_configs() + } + call_command( + 'migrate', + verbosity=max(verbosity - 1, 0), + interactive=False, + database=self.connection.alias, + run_syncdb=True, + ) + finally: + settings.MIGRATION_MODULES = old # We then serialize the current state of the database into a string # and store it on the connection. This slightly horrific process is so people but I'm not convinced.
That seems similar to the solution I've been using for a while: class NoMigrations: """Disable migrations for all apps""" def __getitem__(self, item): return None def __contains__(self, item): return True MIGRATION_MODULES = NoMigrations() (Which I also suggested it as a temporary solution in the original ticket https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/25388#comment:20) I hadn't actually tried this MIGRATION_MODULES override on this project before. I just did a test run with the override and or some reason I had to add a fixtures = ['myapp/initial_data.json'] line to some of the TestCase classes that worked fine without it before. It seems that these test cases really needed this fixture, but for some reason worked fine when migrations are enabled. Is (test) fixture loading somehow tied to migrations? Anyway, the tests work fine (the same 3 failures) with the MIGRATION_MODULES override, so it seems like it would be a reasonable alternative solution.
Is (test) fixture loading somehow tied to migrations? I don't think so, you've probably have these data somewhere is migrations.
