problem_statement.Title: Password reset tokens remain valid after user's email is changed
problem_statement.Description: Password reset tokens issued for a user are still accepted after that user's email address is changed, allowing a token sent to a previous email to reset the account password.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Token HMAC input omits user email (django/contrib/auth/tokens.py:PasswordResetTokenGenerator._make_hash_value). The method builds the string used as HMAC input from the user's primary key, the password field, a truncated last_login value, and the timestamp (i.e., str(user.pk) + user.password + str(login_timestamp) + str(timestamp)). There is no email or email-derived canonical value included in that input, so changing user.email does not change the data used to generate/verify tokens.

2) Token creation and verification are symmetric (django/contrib/auth/tokens.py: make_token, check_token, _make_token_with_timestamp). Both token generation and token validation call into _make_hash_value, so the token semantics are determined solely by that method's inputs. Any data omitted from _make_hash_value is effectively ignored for token validity.

3) Password reset recipient lookup uses a canonicalized comparison that is independent of the token representation (django/contrib/auth/forms.py: PasswordResetForm.get_users and _unicode_ci_compare). The form locates users by case-insensitive/Unicode-normalized comparison of the provided lookup email to stored emails; the token HMAC input does not currently reflect that canonicalized email representation. This mismatch means the identity used to find the recipient can be normalized differently than any value included in token generation.

4) No model-level hook currently exists to supply a canonical email-for-token value (django/contrib/auth/base_user.py: AbstractBaseUser). AbstractBaseUser exposes get_email_field_name(), but there is no get_email_for_token() or equivalent hook in the codebase to let user models normalize or supply the precise email string that should be bound into token generation.

5) Multiple related locations share the same root-assumption issue. The omission of email in token input and the separation between lookup normalization and token representation are encoded across tokens.py (token construction), forms.py (recipient lookup and email delivery), and the user model API (base_user.py). All these locations are equally implicated in the root-cause pattern: tokens are not bound to the email identity used to select the recipient.

6) Test coverage gap and operational impact (tests/auth_tests). Existing tests exercise token generation, timeouts, and legacy algorithm compatibility but do not assert that changing a user's email invalidates previously issued tokens. As a result, there is no existing test asserting the expected security property that tokens issued to a prior email address cannot be used after the account email is changed. Also, tokens already issued (under the current logic) remain valid until their current inputs change (password, last_login or timeout) — changing email alone does not affect them.
StepsToReproduce: Create a user account with email address foo@example.com.
Request a password reset for foo@example.com; receive the reset email and note the reset link (do not use it yet).
Sign in to the account or otherwise change the account's email from foo@example.com to bar@example.com.
Use the previously received password reset link (issued to foo@example.com).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: A password reset token issued to a previous/old email address should be rejected after the user's email has been changed; a password may only be reset using a token that is tied to the currently valid, canonical identity used for password reset delivery.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The password reset token issued before the email change is accepted and can be used to reset the account password despite the email having been changed.

problem_statement.Title: Passing namedtuple to lookup (e.g. __range) raises TypeError after upgrade 2.2->3.0
problem_statement.Description: After upgrading Django from 2.2 to 3.0, passing a collections.namedtuple (a tuple subclass) as a lookup value (for example Model.objects.filter(field__range=Pair(1,2))) raises TypeError: "__new__() missing 1 required positional argument: 'far'". The failure occurs during lookup-value normalization inside the ORM and prevents range-style lookups that use named tuples.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect constructor assumption in Query.resolve_lookup_value (django/db/models/sql/query.py::Query.resolve_lookup_value): the method reconstructs iterable lookup values by calling type(value)(generator expression). The code path is:
   elif isinstance(value, (list, tuple)):
       return type(value)(
           self.resolve_lookup_value(sub_value, can_reuse, allow_joins)
           for sub_value in value
       )
   This assumes the target type's constructor accepts a single iterable argument and reconstructs the container from that iterable.

2) Mismatch with tuple-subclass constructors (e.g. collections.namedtuple): many tuple subclasses (including classes produced by collections.namedtuple) have constructors (__new__) whose signature expects one positional argument per field (or otherwise do not accept a single iterable argument in the same way builtin tuple/list do). Passing a single generator/iterable to such a constructor can trigger TypeError messages like "__new__() missing 1 required positional argument: 'far'". The TypeError reported in the bug is consistent with this mismatch.

3) Downstream caller expectation in Query.build_filter (django/db/models/sql/query.py::Query.build_filter): callers call resolve_lookup_value early and then rely on the returned value's semantics (for example, the value is later passed to check_related_objects and to build_lookup). Because resolve_lookup_value attempts to reconstruct tuple subclasses with a single iterable argument, it can raise an exception before downstream code runs, breaking lookups that previously accepted tuple subclasses.

4) Location and scope: a codebase search for the specific reconstruction pattern (type(value)(...)) in this repository snapshot returned a single occurrence at django/db/models/sql/query.py (the code quoted above). This indicates the incorrect constructor assumption is encoded at this single site and the behavioral mismatch is localized to Query.resolve_lookup_value in this codebase.

5) Secondary investigative finding (detection heuristics): the investigation noted two ways to distinguish builtin tuple from tuple subclasses at runtime: (a) check whether the value is a tuple subclass (for example, isinstance(value, tuple) and type(value) is not tuple) or (b) detect namedtuple-specific classes (for example, classes with a '_fields' attribute). This is an observed diagnostic distinction relevant to why tuple subclasses differ from builtin tuple/list; it is not a prescription for a particular code change.
StepsToReproduce: In a Django 3.0 project, define a model with a numeric field (e.g. class M(Model): field = IntegerField()).
In Python shell or a test, create a namedtuple: Pair = collections.namedtuple('Pair', ('near','far')).
Attempt a range lookup using a Pair instance: M.objects.filter(field__range=Pair(1, 2)).
Observe that the query raises TypeError: "__new__() missing 1 required positional argument: 'far'" instead of returning results.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Using a namedtuple (or other tuple subclass) as a lookup value (for example in __range) should not raise an exception. The ORM should accept such a container as a lookup argument, normalize any expression values inside it, and proceed to build the lookup and return a queryset as it did in Django 2.2.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: At runtime the lookup raises TypeError: "__new__() missing 1 required positional argument: 'far'" when a namedtuple is passed as the lookup value (e.g. field__range=Pair(1,2)). The query fails before it can be executed and does not return results.

patch:
diff --git a/django/core/management/__init__.py b/django/core/management/__init__.py
--- a/django/core/management/__init__.py
+++ b/django/core/management/__init__.py
@@ -344,7 +344,12 @@ def execute(self):
         # Preprocess options to extract --settings and --pythonpath.
         # These options could affect the commands that are available, so they
         # must be processed early.
-        parser = CommandParser(usage='%(prog)s subcommand [options] [args]', add_help=False, allow_abbrev=False)
+        parser = CommandParser(
+            prog=self.prog_name,
+            usage='%(prog)s subcommand [options] [args]',
+            add_help=False,
+            allow_abbrev=False,
+        )
         parser.add_argument('--settings')
         parser.add_argument('--pythonpath')
         parser.add_argument('args', nargs='*')  # catch-all
test_patch:
diff --git a/tests/admin_scripts/tests.py b/tests/admin_scripts/tests.py
--- a/tests/admin_scripts/tests.py

@@ -17,7 +17,7 @@

 )

 from django.core.management.commands.runserver import (
@@ -31,6 +31,7 @@
 from django.test import (
     LiveServerTestCase, SimpleTestCase, TestCase, override_settings,
 )
+from django.test.utils import captured_stderr, captured_stdout
 
 custom_templates_dir = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), 'custom_templates')
 
@@ -1867,6 +1868,20 @@ def _test(self, args, option_b="'2'"):
         )
 
 
+class ExecuteFromCommandLine(SimpleTestCase):
+    def test_program_name_from_argv(self):
+        """
+        Program name is computed from the execute_from_command_line()'s argv
+        argument, not sys.argv.
+        """
+        args = ['help', 'shell']
+        with captured_stdout() as out, captured_stderr() as err:
+            with mock.patch('sys.argv', [None] + args):
+                execute_from_command_line(['django-admin'] + args)
+        self.assertIn('usage: django-admin shell', out.getvalue())
+        self.assertEqual(err.getvalue(), '')
+
+
 @override_settings(ROOT_URLCONF='admin_scripts.urls')
 class StartProject(LiveServerTestCase, AdminScriptTestCase):

problem_statement.Title: ManagementUtility/utility parser instantiates CommandParser without prog, causing '%(prog)s' to use sys.argv[0] instead of supplied argv[0]
problem_statement.Description: ManagementUtility computes a canonical program name (self.prog_name) from the argv passed to it, but when it builds a temporary CommandParser in ManagementUtility.execute it does not pass that computed name as prog. As a result, usage/help text that contains '%(prog)s' falls back to argparse's default (derived from sys.argv[0]) rather than using the already-computed canonical name. The same omission is present in django/core/management/utils.py:get_command_line_option when that function parses a supplied argv list.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) ManagementUtility.execute instantiates a temporary CommandParser without an explicit prog while using a usage string containing '%(prog)s'. Location: django/core/management/__init__.py:ManagementUtility.execute (parser = CommandParser(usage='%(prog)s subcommand [options] [args]', add_help=False, allow_abbrev=False) at line 347). Effect: ArgumentParser will use its default .prog (derived from sys.argv[0]) instead of ManagementUtility.prog_name.

2) ManagementUtility.__init__ computes and stores an authoritative canonical program name (self.prog_name) based on the supplied argv, but that value is not used when constructing the temporary parser in execute. Location: django/core/management/__init__.py:ManagementUtility.__init__ (self.argv = argv or sys.argv[:]; self.prog_name = os.path.basename(self.argv[0]); special-case '__main__.py' -> 'python -m django' at lines 188-192). Relationship: self.prog_name is defined as os.path.basename(argv[0]) with a special-case mapping when it equals '__main__.py'.

3) django/core/management/utils.py:get_command_line_option constructs a CommandParser to parse a provided argv list but does not set parser.prog based on that argv. Location: django/core/management/utils.py:get_command_line_option (parser = CommandParser(add_help=False, allow_abbrev=False) and parser.parse_known_args(argv[2:]) at lines 118-121). Effect: When parsing a supplied argv list, usage/help or behavior that depends on parser.prog will reflect argparse's default (sys.argv[0]) instead of the supplied argv[0].

4) BaseCommand.create_parser demonstrates the intended/established pattern where callers pass an explicit prog derived from a supplied prog_name. Location: django/core/management/base.py:BaseCommand.create_parser (parser = CommandParser(prog='%s %s' % (os.path.basename(prog_name), subcommand), ...) at lines 280-282). Significance: This shows that parsers used when a caller controls the program name are expected to receive that name via the prog argument; the two problematic locations deviate from this established pattern.

Each of the above four points is an independent but related incorrect assumption/location: parsers that are created while owning or parsing a specific argv should not rely on argparse's default .prog (sys.argv[0]) but instead use the program name derived from the supplied argv; the code contains two concrete places (ManagementUtility.execute and utils.get_command_line_option) where this is not followed.
StepsToReproduce: Arrange a process environment where argparse's default program name (derived from sys.argv[0]) will be incorrect or None (for example, set sys.argv[0] = None or run in an embedded environment where sys.argv is not the expected value).
Construct an argv list where argv[0] is the canonical name you want used and include a management subcommand (e.g. [None, 'help'] or [None, 'runserver']).
Instantiate django.core.management.ManagementUtility with that argv: ManagementUtility(argv=argv_list).
Call ManagementUtility.execute() (or use execute_from_command_line(argv) which calls ManagementUtility(argv).execute()).

Observe the help/usage text and any parsing output; '%(prog)s' in usage/help will reflect argparse's default (sys.argv[0]) rather than the canonical name computed from the supplied argv, and parsing-related errors or confusing usage text may appear in environments where sys.argv[0] is incorrect.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When ManagementUtility (or utility functions that parse a supplied argv list) renders usage/help or formats '%(prog)s', it should use the canonical program name derived from the argv supplied to the function (i.e., os.path.basename(argv[0]) with the '__main__.py' special-case yielding 'python -m django'), so usage/help text reflects the program name the caller provided rather than an unrelated sys.argv[0]. No user-visible incorrect program name should appear when a valid argv is supplied.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: ManagementUtility.execute and get_command_line_option create CommandParser instances without passing an explicit prog. As a result, '%(prog)s' falls back to argparse's default (derived from sys.argv[0]) instead of the canonical name computed from the supplied argv. In environments where sys.argv[0] is incorrect or None, this produces incorrect usage/help text and can lead to parsing-related errors or confusing output.

problem_statement.Title: python -m django shell -c: functions defined in -c code raise NameError because exec is called without explicit globals
problem_statement.Description: When running `python -m django shell -c` with code that defines a function which references an import (for example, importing django and then defining a function that reads django.__version__), calling that function can raise NameError because the management command executes the supplied code using exec() without providing an explicit globals mapping. The symptom surfaces from the management command shell's Command.handle() method.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption and location: Command.handle calls exec(options['command']) without providing an explicit globals mapping, which means functions defined in the executed string may not bind the executed code's imports into their globals and thus can raise NameError when referencing those names. Location: django/core/management/commands/shell.py, class Command, method handle (lines ~86–88: exec(options['command'])).

2) Incorrect assumption and location: Command.handle calls exec(sys.stdin.read()) without providing an explicit globals mapping, which results in the same scoping problem for code read from stdin. Location: django/core/management/commands/shell.py, class Command, method handle (lines ~92–94: exec(sys.stdin.read())).

3) Related observation (equal-weight evidence): Command.python in the same file uses exec(compile(...), imported_objects) (line ~78), i.e. it already provides an explicit globals mapping for pythonrc execution. This demonstrates the intended pattern exists elsewhere in the file and highlights the mismatch with the two exec callsites in handle().

4) Required relationship (diagnostic conclusion): Code executed via exec() that defines functions expects a module-like globals mapping so functions bind to that mapping and see imports performed in the executed code. To emulate typical module/`python -c` semantics, the globals should present a module identity (for example, by setting __name__ and __package__ appropriately); the investigation identified {'__name__': '__main__', '__package__': None} as the module-like mapping that would emulate python -c semantics. This mapping is an investigative conclusion (not present in the file) describing the relationship needed for correct behavior.
StepsToReproduce:
Using an environment where Django is importable, run: python -c "import django; def f():
    print(django.__version__)

f()" — this prints the Django version.

Run: python -m django shell -c "import django; def f():
    print(django.__version__)

f()" — this reproduces the failure (NameError when calling f).
Observe the traceback points to django/core/management/commands/shell.py in Command.handle where exec(...) is invoked.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Executing code with `python -m django shell -c '<code>'` should behave like `python -c '<code>'`: imports performed in the supplied code should be visible to functions defined in that code (for example, the sample should print the Django version when the function is called).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Calling a function defined in the code passed to `python -m django shell -c` raises NameError (e.g., NameError: name 'django' is not defined). The traceback shows the error originates at the exec call(s) in django/core/management/commands/shell.py:Command.handle.

problem_statement.Title: Admin Inline verbose_name_plural not derived from Inline.verbose_name
problem_statement.Description: In Django admin, an InlineModelAdmin that sets verbose_name but not verbose_name_plural still uses the model's Meta.verbose_name_plural (or the model's name) for the plural. The inline-level verbose_name is ignored when computing the default plural, which causes admin headings and 'Add another ...' strings to show the model's plural instead of a plural derived from the Inline.verbose_name.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) InlineModelAdmin.__init__ (django/contrib/admin/options.py) unconditionally defaults verbose_name_plural from the model when verbose_name_plural is None. The method contains the direct assignments:
   - if self.verbose_name is None:
       self.verbose_name = self.model._meta.verbose_name
   - if self.verbose_name_plural is None:
       self.verbose_name_plural = self.model._meta.verbose_name_plural
   This means an Inline class that defines verbose_name but not verbose_name_plural will receive the model's plural rather than a plural derived from the Inline.verbose_name.

2) Model Options (django/db/models/options.py) demonstrates the canonical derivation used for models: when a model's verbose_name_plural is None it is computed from verbose_name using the formula format_lazy('{}s', self.verbose_name). The InlineModelAdmin behavior diverges from this model-level relationship: InlineModelAdmin does not derive a default verbose_name_plural from an inline-level verbose_name.

3) Consumer locations (templates/helpers) read the InlineModelAdmin attributes directly. Examples include django/contrib/admin/templates/admin/edit_inline/tabular.html which uses inline_admin_formset.opts.verbose_name_plural for the inline heading, and django/contrib/admin/helpers.py which uses gettext('Add another %(verbose_name)s') with opts.verbose_name for the add-text. Because InlineModelAdmin.__init__ sets the plural from the model, those user-visible strings reflect the model plural instead of an inline-derived plural.

4) No other component contains naming logic that would override this initialization. For example, django/forms/models.py::BaseInlineFormSet focuses on formset/foreign-key behavior and does not derive or alter verbose_name/verbose_name_plural; therefore the incorrect assumption is encoded in InlineModelAdmin.__init__.

5) Tests indicate the intended outcomes. The admin inlines tests (tests/admin_inlines/tests.py) include assertions that verify headings and the 'Add another ...' label for various combinations of inline/model verbose_name and verbose_name_plural, demonstrating the expected semantics that an inline's names should control the displayed strings in those cases.

6) Verification gap (inheritance / declaration origin): it is not verified whether the intended behavior should treat a verbose_name defined on a base inline class differently from one defined on the concrete inline subclass, or whether any detection of a "declared" inline.verbose_name needs special handling beyond normal attribute lookup. The current code uses attribute values available on the instance (which reflect Python's normal class attribute resolution). The specific proposed detection mechanism (scanning the inline class MRO or class __dict__ to determine where an attribute was declared) is an implementation detail and has not been validated by runtime traces or tests.
StepsToReproduce: Define an InlineModelAdmin (e.g. TabularInline) subclass that sets verbose_name but does NOT set verbose_name_plural.
Register that inline on a ModelAdmin and register the ModelAdmin with the admin site.
Open the admin add/change page for the parent model that displays the inline.
Observe the inline heading and the 'Add another ...' button/label.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: If an Inline defines verbose_name but not verbose_name_plural, the admin should display a plural derived from the Inline.verbose_name (the same derivation used by model Options: format_lazy('{}s', verbose_name)) for inline headings. The 'Add another ...' label should continue to use the singular Inline.verbose_name.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The admin displays the model's plural (model._meta.verbose_name_plural or the model name) for inline headings and related labels even when the Inline class defines verbose_name but not verbose_name_plural, requiring both singular and plural forms to be specified on the Inline to override the model's names.

problem_statement.Title: KeyTransform __isnull=True matches JSON null on SQLite and Oracle
problem_statement.Description: Using the KeyTransform isnull lookup (for example value__key__isnull=True) on SQLite and Oracle returns rows where the key exists with JSON null as well as rows where the key is absent. The lookup should only match objects where the key is absent.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) KeyTransformIsNull.as_oracle and KeyTransformIsNull.as_sqlite (django/db/models/fields/json.py): for isnull=True these methods call the generic IsNull implementation (super().as_sql), which compiles the transformed LHS and returns "<compiled_lhs> IS NULL". On Oracle the KeyTransform compiles to COALESCE(JSON_QUERY(...), JSON_VALUE(...)); on SQLite it compiles to JSON_EXTRACT(...). Those compiled expressions can evaluate to SQL NULL both when the JSON path is absent and when the JSON path exists but its value is JSON null. Therefore the "%s IS NULL" test produced by the generic IsNull lookup matches both absent keys and existing keys whose value is JSON null on these backends.

2) lookups.IsNull.as_sql (django/db/models/lookups.py): the generic IsNull lookup compiles its LHS and returns "%s IS NULL" (for rhs True). That generic behavior assumes the compiled LHS will distinguish the semantic cases of interest; this assumption is not true for KeyTransform extraction SQL on Oracle and SQLite because the extraction expressions conflate path-absence and JSON null into SQL NULL.

3) HasKey / HasKeyLookup (django/db/models/fields/json.py): HasKeyLookup implements backend-specific existence checks that correctly report whether a JSON path exists (including when the value is JSON null). For example, HasKeyLookup.as_oracle uses JSON_EXISTS(%s, '...') and HasKeyLookup.as_sqlite uses JSON_TYPE(%s, ...) IS NOT NULL. These expressions correctly indicate presence of a key/path; their logical negation corresponds to "key is absent." The incorrect use of the generic IsNull expression for KeyTransform.isnull=True on Oracle/SQLite is what causes the lookup to conflate absence and JSON null.

4) KeyTransformExact.as_oracle (django/db/models/fields/json.py): this method implements the exact-None (JSON null) semantics for a KeyTransform by composing a HasKey(...) condition with the result of self.lhs.get_lookup('isnull'). Because it calls get_lookup('isnull') on the same KeyTransform LHS, it relies on KeyTransformIsNull producing a "value IS NULL" expression. If KeyTransformIsNull is interpreted differently (e.g. to mean "key does not exist") the composition in KeyTransformExact.as_oracle would no longer reflect the intended logical relationship for equality-to-JSON-null. The correct logical relationship required for "value equals JSON null" is: (key exists) AND (the extracted value is SQL NULL), i.e. HasKey(...) AND (compiled_extracted_value IS NULL).

5) Scope/impact: the incorrect assumption and its effects are encoded in the KeyTransformIsNull.as_oracle and KeyTransformIsNull.as_sqlite branches and in the consumer KeyTransformExact.as_oracle (which depends on the isnull lookup on KeyTransform). The generic IsNull implementation is unchanged and continues to be correct for ordinary fields; the problem is specific to applying the generic "%s IS NULL" test to KeyTransform extraction SQL that does not distinguish path-absence from JSON null on Oracle and SQLite. Additional call sites that obtain an isnull lookup via lhs.get_lookup('isnull') (for example, the generic build_lookup rewrite of exact None to isnull) can be affected when the lhs is a KeyTransform, so consumers that rely on the prior KeyTransformIsNull semantics must be considered when changing behavior.
StepsToReproduce:
On a SQLite or Oracle test database, run the JSON-field isnull test with a key that can be JSON null. For example, edit tests.model_fields.test_jsonfield.TestQuerying.test_isnull_key and change the first assertion to:
    self.assertSequenceEqual(
        NullableJSONModel.objects.filter(value__j__isnull=True),
        self.objs[:4] + self.objs[5:],
    )
Then run that test on SQLite/Oracle and observe the failure (the query also matches the row where 'j' exists and contains JSON null).

Alternatively, in a Django shell connected to SQLite/Oracle, create the same test fixtures used by TestQuerying and run:
    NullableJSONModel.objects.filter(value__j__isnull=True)
Observe that the result includes objects where key 'j' exists with JSON null, not only objects where 'j' is absent.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: A filter using value__<key>__isnull=True should return only objects where the JSON key is absent. A filter using value__<key>=None (Exact None) should return only objects where the key exists and its JSON value is null.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: On SQLite and Oracle, value__<key>__isnull=True matches both objects where the key is absent and objects where the key exists with JSON null (rows with a JSON null value for that key are returned as if the key were absent).

problem_statement.Title: Signal.send_robust() does not log exceptions raised by receivers
problem_statement.Description: Exceptions raised by signal receivers are caught and returned by Signal.send_robust() but are not recorded in the process logs. As a result, operator-facing logging/error-aggregation tools (e.g. Sentry) do not receive traceback information for these exceptions unless receivers or callers log or re-raise them.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Signal.send_robust catches and returns Exception instances without emitting any log record (django/dispatch/dispatcher.py::Signal.send_robust). Evidence: the except body is:
    except Exception as err:
        responses.append((receiver, err))
   Wrong assumption encoded here: returning exception objects in responses alone is sufficient for observability.

2) The dispatch module contains no module-level logger (django/dispatch/dispatcher.py module top-level). Evidence: the module imports threading, warnings, weakref and Django utilities but does not import logging or define a logger variable. Wrong assumption encoded here: the dispatch module does not need a dedicated logger to surface receiver exceptions.

3) Callers rely on the current return semantics (send_robust returning Exception instances) and may re-raise those exceptions (django/test/utils.py::override_settings.disable and tests/dispatch/tests.py). Evidence: override_settings.disable collects responses from setting_changed.send_robust(...) and later raises any Exception responses; tests/dispatch/tests.py asserts that failing receivers produce Exception instances with tracebacks. Wrong assumption encoded here: observability changes must not alter the returned responses or control flow consumers depend on.

4) Signal subclasses inherit the same behavior (django/db/models/signals.py::ModelSignal). Evidence: ModelSignal subclasses Signal and does not override send_robust, so the lack of logging applies to ModelSignal-based signals as well. Wrong assumption encoded here: that fixes must be applied to multiple subclasses rather than centrally.

5) Testing and representation-safety gaps (investigation gap). Evidence: there is no existing unit test asserting that a raising receiver produces an ERROR-level log from the dispatch module, and the investigation did not verify whether formatting receivers or the Signal instance (e.g. via repr()/%r) in log messages is always safe (repr() can raise or expose sensitive data). Wrong assumption encoded here: that adding logging will have no test or data-exposure implications without further verification.
StepsToReproduce: Define a Django Signal instance and connect a receiver function that raises an Exception when called.
Call signal.send_robust(sender=...) (or trigger the signal in context).
Observe that the returned responses list contains a tuple (receiver, Exception) for the failing receiver.
Check application logs / error-aggregation tool (e.g. Sentry) and observe that no log or traceback for the receiver exception is present.
Alternate: run code path that uses setting_changed.send_robust (e.g. override_settings.disable) and observe it re-raises the Exception from the responses but still produces no logged traceback.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When a signal receiver raises an exception, operators can observe the exception and its traceback in the process logs/error-aggregation system (so operational visibility is available), while send_robust continues to return responses in which failing receivers are represented by Exception objects.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Signal.send_robust catches exceptions raised by receivers and returns them in the responses list but emits no log records or traceback information for those exceptions; operators and external error-aggregation tools do not receive an ERROR-level log for these receiver exceptions.

problem_statement.Title: models.W042 warning emitted for concrete child models that inherit primary key via multi-table inheritance
problem_statement.Description: When a concrete model inherits a primary key from an ancestor via multi-table inheritance, Django emits models.W042 (auto-created primary key) for the child model even though the child uses the inherited parent-link primary key. This occurs when running system checks or makemigrations under Django 3.2 alpha.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Model check uses pk.auto_created alone as the discriminator for DEFAULT_AUTO_FIELD: django/db/models/base.py:Model._check_default_pk checks cls._meta.pk.auto_created (combined with DEFAULT_AUTO_FIELD override checks) and emits models.W042 when pk.auto_created is True. (Location: django/db/models/base.py:Model._check_default_pk, lines ~1299–1304.)

2) Parent-link OneToOneField is created with auto_created=True and parent_link=True: django/db/models/base.py:ModelBase.__new__ creates the parent-link OneToOneField for multi-table inheritance with the arguments auto_created=True and parent_link=True. (Location: django/db/models/base.py:ModelBase.__new__, lines ~242–249.)

3) Promotion of parent-link to primary key retains auto_created flag: django/db/models/options.py:Options._prepare promotes the parent-link field to be the model's primary key by setting field.primary_key = True and calling setup_pk(field), but it does not clear field.auto_created. As a result, an inherited parent-link primary key on the child model has pk.auto_created == True. (Location: django/db/models/options.py:Options._prepare, lines ~271–284 and setup_pk at ~323–327.)

4) The combination of (2) and (3) makes inherited PKs indistinguishable from locally auto-created id fields to the check in (1): an inherited parent-link primary key is observed as pk.auto_created == True (and pk.remote_field.parent_link == True), but Model._check_default_pk only considers pk.auto_created, so it treats inherited parent-link PKs the same as model-local auto-created IDs and emits models.W042 for concrete child models that inherited their PK.

5) Tests do not include an explicit case asserting that a concrete child inheriting a parent's explicit PK does not generate models.W042: tests/check_framework/test_model_checks.py contains DEFAULT_AUTO_FIELD checks (e.g. simple auto-created pk and explicit pk tests) but lacks a test that defines a Parent with an explicit PK and a concrete Child inheriting that PK and asserts the Child does not produce models.W042. (Location: tests/check_framework/test_model_checks.py.)
StepsToReproduce: Using Django 3.2 alpha, define a parent model (for example, class Entity) with an explicit primary key field.
Define a concrete child model (for example, class User) that inherits from that parent using multi-table inheritance (do not declare a new primary key on the child).
Run python manage.py makemigrations or run system checks (checks.run_checks()).
Observe models.W042 warnings reported for the child model(s) claiming an auto-created primary key was used.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Concrete child models that inherit a primary key from an ancestor via multi-table inheritance should not trigger models.W042; checks should recognize the inherited parent-link primary key and not report an auto-created local primary key warning for the child.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: models.W042 is reported for child models that inherit a primary key: the check treats the inherited parent-link (which ends up with auto_created==True and remote_field.parent_link==True) as if the child had a Django auto-created local 'id' field and emits the DEFAULT_AUTO_FIELD / AutoField warning.

problem_statement.Title: ModelChoiceField does not provide value of invalid choice when raising ValidationError
problem_statement.Description: When ModelChoiceField rejects an invalid input it raises a ValidationError whose message does not include the invalid value. Unlike ChoiceField and ModelMultipleChoiceField, the user-visible error text for ModelChoiceField never shows which value was invalid.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) django/forms/models.py: ModelChoiceField.default_error_messages['invalid_choice'] is a literal message that omits the '%(value)s' placeholder. This encodes the incorrect assumption that the invalid value need not be displayed; as a result, even if a ValidationError carried params, the template has no placeholder to interpolate the value.

2) django/forms/models.py: ModelChoiceField.to_python(...) raises ValidationError(self.error_messages['invalid_choice'], code='invalid_choice') without supplying any params. This encodes the incorrect assumption that the ValidationError need not carry params for the invalid value, so no value is available for interpolation.

3) django/forms/models.py: InlineForeignKeyField.default_error_messages['invalid_choice'] and InlineForeignKeyField.clean() use the same pattern: the default message lacks a '%(value)s' placeholder and the ValidationError is raised without params. This is a separate location in the codebase that shares the same root cause pattern as ModelChoiceField.

4) django/forms/models.py: ModelMultipleChoiceField demonstrates the intended message/params contract: its default_error_messages['invalid_choice'] contains '%(value)s' and its _check_values method raises ValidationError(..., params={'value': pk}). This shows the expected relationship between message templates and ValidationError.params for choice-like fields.

5) django/forms/fields.py: ChoiceField and related multiple-choice fields use the same canonical pattern (message contains '%(value)s' and ValidationError is raised with params={'value': value}), confirming the consistent contract expected for choice validation messages.

6) django/forms/forms.py (BaseModelForm._update_errors): the form error normalization code replaces ValidationError.message from form-level mappings but does not clear ValidationError.params. Therefore, when a field supplies params and the message template contains the corresponding placeholder, the params will be available for interpolation by the consumers.
StepsToReproduce: Construct a ModelChoiceField bound to a queryset (or use a ModelForm with a ModelChoiceField).
Provide an invalid primary-key value for that field (a value not present in the queryset) — e.g., submit form data with the field set to an invalid PK.
Validate the field or form (call field.to_python(value) or form.is_valid()) and inspect the resulting ValidationError / form.errors for that field.
Observe that the error message does not include the invalid value that was supplied.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When the user supplies an invalid choice for a ModelChoiceField, the displayed validation error should include the invalid value (interpolated into the error message), e.g. the message should contain the supplied value alongside the text that it is not one of the available choices.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The validation error for ModelChoiceField reads a generic message (e.g. 'Select a valid choice. That choice is not one of the available choices.') and does not show the invalid value supplied by the user.

problem_statement.Title: Parent FK left as empty string when related unsaved instance with non-auto CharField PK is later saved
problem_statement.Description: When a parent model is assigned an unsaved related instance whose primary key defaults to the empty string (for example, Product() where sku defaults to ''), and the related instance's PK is later set and saved, the parent's foreign-key column can remain the empty string instead of being updated to the child's PK. This results in incorrect queries and a deferred foreign-key violation on transaction commit.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Model._prepare_related_fields_for_save treats a parent's local foreign-key attribute as "missing" only when the attribute value is exactly None (django/db/models/base.py :: Model._prepare_related_fields_for_save). The method checks obj.pk is None and raises if so; otherwise it only copies obj.pk into the parent's attname when getattr(self, field.attname) is None. It does not consider the empty string ('') to be missing, so a parent's attname that is '' is treated as a present value and not updated.

2) Assignment copies the related object's raw attribute values into the parent's FK attname without normalizing empty strings (django/db/models/fields/related_descriptors.py :: ForwardManyToOneDescriptor.__set__). The descriptor assigns each lh_field.attname from getattr(value, rh_field.attname) directly, so an unsaved related instance whose PK attribute is '' will make the parent's foreign-key attname equal to '' at assignment time.

3) The code that prepares values for the database already defines a different notion of "missing" for foreign keys (django/db/models/fields/related.py :: ForeignKey.get_db_prep_save). That method treats a value as missing for DB semantics when it is None or when it is an empty string in a context where empty strings are not allowed by the target field or where the backend interprets empty strings as NULLs. Concretely, the canonical DB-prep rule is:

    value is missing if value is None or (value == '' and (not target_field.empty_strings_allowed or connection.features.interprets_empty_strings_as_nulls)).

4) The bug is caused by the inconsistency between (1) and (3): Model._prepare_related_fields_for_save's "is None" only test is not aligned with ForeignKey.get_db_prep_save's policy for treating empty strings as missing. As a result, when assignment has earlier set the parent's FK attname to '' (via the descriptor), Model._prepare_related_fields_for_save will not copy the related object's later-saved PK into the parent's attname, leaving the parent's foreign-key column as ''. This mismatch produces the observed incorrect queries and deferred foreign-key violation on transaction commit.

5) Multi-column/ForeignObject cases: ForeignObject supports multi-field relations via related_fields tuples and the descriptor assignment loop applies per related_fields pair. These code paths and abstractions (django/db/models/fields/related.py and django/db/models/fields/related_descriptors.py) were not exhaustively validated in the investigation. They share the same root-cause pattern (copying mapped attributes at assignment and later testing for "missing"), so analogous issues may exist for multi-column relations and should be reviewed.
StepsToReproduce: Define Product with non-auto CharField primary key: sku = models.CharField(primary_key=True, max_length=50)
Define Order with product = models.ForeignKey(Product, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
Start a transaction (transaction.atomic())
Create a parent without saving: order = Order()
Assign an unsaved related instance that defaults to empty PK: order.product = Product()
Set the related instance's PK and save it: order.product.sku = 'foo'; order.product.save()
Save the parent: order.save()
Observe queries: Order.objects.filter(product_id='').exists() returns True and Order.objects.filter(product=order.product).exists() returns False; commit will raise a deferred ForeignKeyViolation
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: After assigning an unsaved related instance, then setting and saving that instance's primary key, the parent record's foreign-key column should reflect the related instance's primary key (for example, Order.product_id == 'foo'). Queries that filter by the related object (Order.objects.filter(product=order.product)) should return the parent, and no deferred foreign-key violation should occur on commit.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The parent's foreign-key column is left as the empty string (product_id == ''), so filters by the related object fail (Order.objects.filter(product=order.product) returns no rows) and the transaction later fails on commit with a deferred ForeignKeyViolation due to the absent '' primary-key row on the related table.

hints_text: Thanks for this report. product_id is an empty string in ​_prepare_related_fields_for_save() that's why pk from a related object is not used. We could use empty_values: diff --git a/django/db/models/base.py b/django/db/models/base.py index 822aad080d..8e7a8e3ae7 100644 --- a/django/db/models/base.py +++ b/django/db/models/base.py @@ -933,7 +933,7 @@ class Model(metaclass=ModelBase): "%s() prohibited to prevent data loss due to unsaved " "related object '%s'." % (operation_name, field.name) ) - elif getattr(self, field.attname) is None: + elif getattr(self, field.attname) in field.empty_values: # Use pk from related object if it has been saved after # an assignment. setattr(self, field.attname, obj.pk) but I'm not sure. Related with #28147.

problem_statement.Title: TypeError: cannot pickle 'dict_keys' when combining Q objects with | operator
problem_statement.Description: Applying the | operator to a Q object that contains a non-deepcopyable leaf value (for example, a dict_keys object) raises TypeError: "cannot pickle 'dict_keys' object". The exception happens when the Q instance is duplicated via copy.deepcopy (called from Q._combine), which triggers the Node.__deepcopy__ implementation that deep-copies the Q.children.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Q._combine (django/db/models/query_utils.py: Q._combine) returns copy.deepcopy(self) when other is empty (and copy.deepcopy(other) when self is empty). This encodes the assumption that duplicating a Q by delegating to copy.deepcopy is always safe. (See Q._combine: "if not other: return copy.deepcopy(self)" / "elif not self: return copy.deepcopy(other)").

2) Node.__deepcopy__ (django/utils/tree.py: Node.__deepcopy__) unconditionally deep-copies the node children via copy.deepcopy(self.children, memodict). This assumes every child (and every object nested inside children, e.g. values in tuples) is deepcopyable/pickleable. That assumption is violated when a child contains a value such as dict_keys, causing copy.deepcopy to raise TypeError: "cannot pickle 'dict_keys' object".

3) The Q class stores keyword arguments as (key, value) tuples in its children (django/db/models/query_utils.py: Q.__init__), so non-deepcopyable objects nested within those tuples (e.g. dict_keys returned by {}.keys()) become reachable to Node.__deepcopy__ and trigger the error during deepcopy.

4) A contrasting implementation is WhereNode.clone (django/db/models/sql/where.py: WhereNode.clone), which shows the safer pattern used elsewhere: recursively clone child nodes that provide clone()/clone-like semantics and append raw leaf children unchanged. Q does not follow this pattern and instead relies on Node.__deepcopy__ deep-copying raw children.

5) Related/risk: FilteredRelation.__init__ uses a default condition=Q() (django/db/models/query_utils.py: FilteredRelation.__init__), so Q instances may be created at import time and propagate to other code paths. There are other copy.deepcopy usage sites across the codebase; the set of call-sites that might deep-copy Q instances was not exhaustively audited, so there is a risk that callers elsewhere rely on the current deepcopy semantics. These facts are relevant to assessing the full impact of the incorrect assumption identified above.
StepsToReproduce: Open a Python shell with Django available.
>>> from django.db.models import Q
Q: (AND: ('x__in', dict_keys([])))
Traceback (most recent call last): ...
TypeError: cannot pickle 'dict_keys' object
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Combining Q objects with the | operator should succeed even when a Q contains non-deepcopyable leaf values (such as dict_keys). The result should be a Q object representing the combined condition rather than raising a TypeError.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Q() | Q(x__in={}.keys()) raises TypeError: cannot pickle 'dict_keys' object. The exception originates from copy.deepcopy being invoked on a Q instance (via Q._combine when one operand is empty), which triggers Node.__deepcopy__ to deep-copy the children and fail on the non-deepcopyable leaf.

hints_text: Thanks for this report. Regression in bb0b6e526340e638522e093765e534df4e4393d2.

problem_statement.Title: Q() & Exists(...) raises TypeError; Q/Exists boolean combine is not commutative
problem_statement.Description: Combining a Q() object on the left with an Exists(...) expression on the right (Q() & Exists(...)) raises a TypeError, while the reverse order (Exists(...) & Q()) succeeds. The boolean/bitwise combinators (& and |) are not behaving commutatively for Q versus conditional Expression operands.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Q._combine enforces that the right-hand operand be a Q instance — django/db/models/query_utils.py::Q._combine contains an explicit check: if not isinstance(other, Q): raise TypeError(other). This encodes the assumption that the RHS must already be a Q.

2) Combinable.__and__/__or__ perform left-side wrapping for conditional operands — django/db/models/expressions.py::Combinable.__and__ (and __or__) check if both operands are conditional and return Q(self) & Q(other) (or Q(self) | Q(other)). This means a conditional Expression on the left is converted into Q(expression) before Q._combine runs.

3) Exists is a conditional/boolean Expression — django/db/models/expressions.py::Exists sets output_field = fields.BooleanField(), so BaseExpression.conditional evaluates True for Exists. Exists therefore meets the criteria Combinable uses to consider an operand conditional.

4) Q instances are conditional and can be formed from Expressions — django/db/models/query_utils.py::Q has conditional = True and Q.__init__ accepts arbitrary args (including Expression instances), producing Q nodes that may contain Expression children.

5) Node internals accept Expression children — django/utils/tree.py::Node.add and the Node implementation accept and store arbitrary child data (including Expression instances) and provide the squashing/merging semantics used by Q nodes. Consequently, wrapping an Expression into Q(expression) produces a Q node acceptable to the tree internals.

Each of the above is supported by the code locations named; together they explain the observed asymmetry: Combinable wraps a left-hand conditional Expression into Q before combining, while Q._combine rejects a non-Q RHS, so Q() & Exists(...) triggers the TypeError originating in Q._combine.
StepsToReproduce: Open Django shell / interactive environment with the project models loaded.
From django.db.models.expressions import Exists; from django.db.models import Q; from myapp.models import Product (or equivalent model).
Evaluate: Exists(Product.objects.all()) & Q()  # succeeds and returns a combined Q.
Evaluate: Q() & Exists(Product.objects.all())  # raises TypeError: <django.db.models.expressions.Exists object at ...>.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Boolean combination operators (& and |) between Q objects and conditional Expression operands (such as Exists) should be commutative: both Q() & Exists(...) and Exists(...) & Q() should produce equivalent combined Q/where representations rather than one order raising TypeError.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Exists(...) & Q() returns a combined Q object, but Q() & Exists(...) raises TypeError because Q._combine rejects the Exists instance (TypeError is raised with the Exists instance as the argument).

patch:
diff --git a/django/urls/resolvers.py b/django/urls/resolvers.py
--- a/django/urls/resolvers.py
+++ b/django/urls/resolvers.py
@@ -59,9 +59,16 @@ def __getitem__(self, index):
         return (self.func, self.args, self.kwargs)[index]
 
     def __repr__(self):
-        return "ResolverMatch(func=%s, args=%s, kwargs=%s, url_name=%s, app_names=%s, namespaces=%s, route=%s)" % (
-            self._func_path, self.args, self.kwargs, self.url_name,
-            self.app_names, self.namespaces, self.route,
+        if isinstance(self.func, functools.partial):
+            func = repr(self.func)
+        else:
+            func = self._func_path
+        return (
+            'ResolverMatch(func=%s, args=%r, kwargs=%r, url_name=%r, '
+            'app_names=%r, namespaces=%r, route=%r)' % (
+                func, self.args, self.kwargs, self.url_name,
+                self.app_names, self.namespaces, self.route,
+            )
         )

+++ b/tests/urlpatterns_reverse/tests.py
@@ -1141,10 +1141,30 @@ def test_repr(self):
         self.assertEqual(
             repr(resolve('/no_kwargs/42/37/')),
             "ResolverMatch(func=urlpatterns_reverse.views.empty_view, "
-            "args=('42', '37'), kwargs={}, url_name=no-kwargs, app_names=[], "
-            "namespaces=[], route=^no_kwargs/([0-9]+)/([0-9]+)/$)",
+            "args=('42', '37'), kwargs={}, url_name='no-kwargs', app_names=[], "
+            "namespaces=[], route='^no_kwargs/([0-9]+)/([0-9]+)/$')",
         )
 
+    @override_settings(ROOT_URLCONF='urlpatterns_reverse.urls')
+    def test_repr_functools_partial(self):
+        tests = [
+            ('partial', 'template.html'),
+            ('partial_nested', 'nested_partial.html'),
+            ('partial_wrapped', 'template.html'),
+        ]
+        for name, template_name in tests:
+            with self.subTest(name=name):
+                func = (
+                    f"functools.partial({views.empty_view!r}, "
+                    f"template_name='{template_name}')"
+                )
+                self.assertEqual(
+                    repr(resolve(f'/{name}/')),
+                    f"ResolverMatch(func={func}, args=(), kwargs={{}}, "
+                    f"url_name='{name}', app_names=[], namespaces=[], "
+                    f"route='{name}/')",
+                )
+
 
 @override_settings(ROOT_URLCONF='urlpatterns_reverse.erroneous_urls')
 class ErroneousViewTests(SimpleTestCase):

problem_statement.Title: ResolverMatch.__repr__ shows functools.partial as 'functools.partial' and omits partial pre-bound args/kwargs
problem_statement.Description: When a functools.partial object is used as the resolved view, ResolverMatch reports the function path as 'functools.partial' and __repr__ does not include the partial's pre-bound args/kwargs. This makes debugging output (repr(resolve(...)), URL display, and some debug pages unhelpful.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) ResolverMatch.__init__ mis-classifies functools.partial when computing the display function path (django/urls/resolvers.py:ResolverMatch.__init__). The constructor sets self._func_path using a hasattr(func, '__name__') check and, if absent, uses func.__class__.__module__ + '.' + func.__class__.__name__ (resolvers.py lines 48–53). functools.partial instances do not expose __name__, so this produces the dotted path 'functools.partial' instead of the underlying callable's dotted path.

2) ResolverMatch.__repr__ omits functools.partial pre-bound args/keywords (django/urls/resolvers.py:ResolverMatch.__repr__). The representation formats self._func_path, self.args, and self.kwargs only (resolvers.py lines 61–65); there is no inclusion of any pre-bound partial.args or partial.keywords in the displayed args/kwargs.

3) The runtime invocation contract must be preserved (django/core/handlers/base.py:BaseHandler._get_response; django/urls/resolvers.py:ResolverMatch.__getitem__; django/urls/base.py:translate_url/reverse). Call sites unpack the ResolverMatch into (func, args, kwargs) and use those values to invoke the view (BaseHandler._get_response unpacks the resolved object and calls wrapped_callback(request, *callback_args, **callback_kwargs); see base.py lines 166–182). ResolverMatch.__getitem__ returns (self.func, self.args, self.kwargs) (resolvers.py lines 58–59). Therefore any handling of partial for display must not change the runtime semantics of the callable or the argument lists used by invocation and reverse/translate_url (base.py lines 171–175 use match.args/match.kwargs when reversing).

4) Inconsistent precedent: URLPattern.lookup_str already unwraps functools.partial for display (django/urls/resolvers.py:URLPattern.lookup_str). lookup_str contains an explicit isinstance(callback, functools.partial) check and uses callback.func for building the dotted path (resolvers.py lines 366–376). ResolverMatch behavior is inconsistent with this existing display logic.

5) Debug/error consumers inspect resolver_match.func directly and will continue to show 'functools.partial' (django/views/debug.py:technical_404_response). The debug view composes a caller string from resolver_match.func by checking __name__/__class__.__name__ and __module__ (views/debug.py lines 512–527); this will show 'functools.partial' for partials unless those consumers use ResolverMatch-provided display metadata.

6) Test coverage gap: The test suite contains partial-wrapped example views (tests/urlpatterns_reverse/views.py: empty_view_partial, empty_view_nested_partial — tests/views.py lines 59–60) but there is no explicit test asserting repr(resolve(...)) or ResolverMatch.__repr__ includes the unwrapped dotted path and pre-bound partial args/kwargs. The lack of a test for the partial case allows the behavior to remain unverified by automated tests.

7) Observed relationships important for correct display (diagnostic/formula-level description):
   - For display purposes, when the resolved callable is a functools.partial object, the underlying callable is partial.func and the partial may carry pre-bound values in partial.args and partial.keywords.
   - The displayed function path should reflect the underlying callable's module and name/qualname when present, i.e. use the wrapped callable's __module__ + '.' + (its __name__ or __class__.__name__ / __qualname__) rather than the partial object's class name.
   - The displayed arguments for human-readable repr should reflect both the partial's pre-bound arguments and the URL-resolved arguments; conceptually: display_args = partial.args + tuple(self.args), and display_kwargs = {**(partial.keywords or {}), **self.kwargs}. These are diagnostics of the relationship that should hold for a complete human-readable representation; they describe what information is currently missing from __repr__, not a specific implementation prescription.
StepsToReproduce: In a Django project, define a normal view function, e.g. def view(request, a, b): pass
Create a functools.partial wrapping the view with pre-bound args/kwargs, e.g. partial_view = functools.partial(view, 1, b=2)
Add a URL pattern that uses partial_view as the view and resolve that URL, e.g. match = resolve('/path/to/that/url/')
Print or inspect repr(match) or print(match) and observe the function display and shown args/kwargs
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When a functools.partial is used as the resolved view, debug/display output should show the underlying function's dotted path (module.function or module.ClassName for class-based callables) rather than 'functools.partial', and a human-readable representation should include both the partial's pre-bound args/kwargs and the URL-resolved args/kwargs merged for inspection.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The function path is shown as 'functools.partial' (the partial object's class name) and the __repr__ output shows only the URL-resolved args/kwargs, omitting any pre-bound arguments/keywords from the functools.partial.

problem_statement.Title: DEFAULT_AUTO_FIELD rejects user subclasses of BigAutoField/SmallAutoField
problem_statement.Description: When DEFAULT_AUTO_FIELD is set to a user-defined field class that subclasses models.BigAutoField or models.SmallAutoField, Django raises a ValueError stating the class must subclass AutoField during app/model import.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) django/db/models/fields/__init__.py :: AutoFieldMeta.__subclasscheck__ uses an identity membership test against the _subclasses tuple: it evaluates `subclass in self._subclasses or super().__subclasscheck__(subclass)`. Because `_subclasses` is the tuple (BigAutoField, SmallAutoField), this test returns True only when the checked class object is exactly one of those two built-in classes, not when it is a user-defined subclass of them.

2) django/db/models/options.py :: Options._get_default_pk_class imports the class referenced by DEFAULT_AUTO_FIELD and validates it with `issubclass(pk_class, AutoField)`. That validation delegates to AutoFieldMeta.__subclasscheck__ (AutoField's metaclass). As a result of the identity check in __subclasscheck__, `issubclass(MyBigAutoField, AutoField)` can evaluate to False for a user-defined `class MyBigAutoField(models.BigAutoField): ...`, and Options._get_default_pk_class raises the ValueError observed by users.

3) django/db/models/fields/__init__.py :: AutoFieldMeta._subclasses returns the tuple `(BigAutoField, SmallAutoField)`. The combination of this tuple with the identity test in __subclasscheck__ effectively treats the tuple as an exact set of allowed classes rather than as base classes whose subclasses should also be considered AutoField subclasses.

4) django/db/models/fields/__init__.py :: AutoFieldMeta.__instancecheck__ uses `isinstance(instance, self._subclasses)` (a tuple), which accepts instances of subclasses. This differs from the class-level behavior, producing an inconsistency between instance-level checks (which accept subclass instances) and subclass-level checks (which currently require exact identity to the built-in classes).
StepsToReproduce: In Django settings, set DEFAULT_AUTO_FIELD = "example.core.models.MyBigAutoField".
Run a Django command that imports apps (for example, `./manage.py runserver` or `./manage.py check`).
Observe the import-time crash with a ValueError about DEFAULT_AUTO_FIELD not subclassing AutoField.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Django accepts a DEFAULT_AUTO_FIELD value that points to a user-defined class which subclasses models.BigAutoField or models.SmallAutoField; app/model import completes normally and models are prepared without raising a ValueError.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: During app/model import Django raises: "ValueError: Primary key 'example.core.models.MyBigAutoField' referred by DEFAULT_AUTO_FIELD must subclass AutoField." Models fail to load and Django crashes at startup.

--- a/django/core/management/templates.py
+++ b/django/core/management/templates.py
@@ -73,9 +73,9 @@ def handle(self, app_or_project, name, target=None, **options):
             except OSError as e:
                 raise CommandError(e)
         else:
-            if app_or_project == 'app':
-                self.validate_name(os.path.basename(target), 'directory')
             top_dir = os.path.abspath(os.path.expanduser(target))
+            if app_or_project == 'app':
+                self.validate_name(os.path.basename(top_dir), 'directory')
             if not os.path.exists(top_dir):
                 raise CommandError("Destination directory '%s' does not "
                                    "exist, please create it first." % top_dir)

problem_statement.Title: django-admin startapp fails when target directory ends with trailing slash
problem_statement.Description: When a user supplies a target path that ends with a trailing path separator (for example, "directory/" as produced by shell tab-completion), django-admin startapp raises CommandError because the code extracts an empty basename and treats it as the app directory name. The error arises in TemplateCommand.handle where the directory name is derived with os.path.basename(target) without normalizing or stripping trailing separators. Note: startproject delegates to TemplateCommand but does not perform the same directory-name validation and therefore is not affected by this specific empty-basename validation check.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption about basename extraction: TemplateCommand.handle assumes os.path.basename(target) always yields the intended directory name and is safe to validate. Location: django/core/management/templates.py, TemplateCommand.handle (call at lines ~75-77: self.validate_name(os.path.basename(target), 'directory')). Evidence: If target ends with a trailing separator, os.path.basename('directory/') -> '' and that empty string is passed to validate_name.

2) validate_name rejects empty names: TemplateCommand.validate_name assumes it receives a non-empty identifier and enforces name.isidentifier(), rejecting the empty string produced by basename on a trailing-separator path. Location: django/core/management/templates.py, TemplateCommand.validate_name (lines ~211-219: if not name.isidentifier(): raise CommandError(...)). Evidence: Passing name == '' triggers the observed CommandError: "'' is not a valid app directory. Please make sure the directory is a valid identifier."

3) Scope of impact (commands): The immediate failure site is in TemplateCommand.handle and it affects startapp because startapp invokes TemplateCommand.handle with app_or_project=='app'. Location: django/core/management/commands/startapp.py (super().handle('app', app_name, target, **options')). Evidence: TemplateCommand.handle runs the directory validation only when app_or_project == 'app', so startapp is affected. startproject delegates to TemplateCommand but calls super().handle('project', ...), and the directory-name validation branch is not executed for projects; therefore startproject is not affected by this specific validate_name-on-empty-basename failure.

4) Inconsistent defensive practices elsewhere: Other modules guard against empty basenames produced by trailing separators (e.g., django/core/files/utils.py checks if os.path.basename(name) in {'', '.', '..'} and raises SuspiciousFileOperation). Location: django/core/files/utils.py (validate_file_name). Evidence: The existence of explicit defensive checks in other code shows TemplateCommand.handle lacks similar normalization or guarding.

5) Cross-platform normalization caveat: A minimal rstrip(os.sep) approach is fragile on systems or inputs with mixed separators (for example, user-supplied '/' on Windows where os.sep == '\\'). The robust relationship for extracting the intended name is to normalize the path first and then take the basename (e.g., os.path.basename(os.path.normpath(target))). Location: analysis of basename usage in django/core/management/templates.py and general path semantics across platforms.

6) Testing gap: There is no regression test that asserts that providing an existing directory path with a trailing separator is accepted. Location: tests/admin_scripts/tests.py (StartApp tests cover invalid target names but do not include a valid existing directory passed with a trailing slash), so regressions can go undetected.
StepsToReproduce: Create an existing directory named 'myapp' inside a project (e.g., mkdir myapp).
Run the startapp command with a trailing slash: django-admin startapp myapp/  (or python manage.py startapp myapp/).
Observe the command fail with a validation error.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The startapp command accepts a valid existing directory path whether or not it ends with a trailing path separator and proceeds to create files for the app without raising a validation error about the directory name.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: CommandError is raised with the message: "'' is not a valid app directory. Please make sure the directory is a valid identifier." — caused by os.path.basename(target) returning the empty string when target ends with a trailing separator and that empty string being passed to validate_name.

 class AdminPasswordChangeFormTest(TestDataMixin, TestCase):

problem_statement.Title: Label for ReadOnlyPasswordHashWidget points to non-labelable element
problem_statement.Description: In the admin / user-change form, the <label> for the ReadOnlyPasswordHashWidget includes a for="id_..." attribute that points at a non-labelable element. The widget renders static summary markup (a <div> in tests) rather than a labelable form control, so the label's for attribute references an element that cannot be labelled.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) ReadOnlyPasswordHashWidget (django/contrib/auth/forms.py::ReadOnlyPasswordHashWidget) renders static summary markup (tests show a <div id="id_password">) and sets read_only = True, but the class does not define an id_for_label method. Incorrect assumption encoded: the widget is treated as if it maps to a single labelable control (location: django/contrib/auth/forms.py: class ReadOnlyPasswordHashWidget).

2) Base Widget.id_for_label (django/forms/widgets.py::Widget.id_for_label) returns the supplied id_ by default (implementation: def id_for_label(self, id_): return id_). Incorrect assumption encoded: returning id_ signals that associating a <label for="..."> is appropriate for every widget (location: django/forms/widgets.py: Widget.id_for_label).

3) BoundField.label_tag (django/forms/boundfield.py::BoundField.label_tag) computes an id for the field and calls widget.id_for_label(id_); if that call returns a truthy value, label_tag emits a for="..." attribute. Relationship used: id_ = widget.attrs.get('id') or self.auto_id; id_for_label = widget.id_for_label(id_); if id_for_label: include for=id_for_label. Because the base id_for_label returns id_ by default, label_tag will produce a for attribute even when the widget's rendered markup is non-labelable (location: django/forms/boundfield.py: BoundField.label_tag).

4) AdminReadonlyField (django/contrib/admin/helpers.py::AdminReadonlyField) contains a path that treats read-only widgets specially (it renders widget.render(...) directly and emits a label without a for attribute). However, UserChangeForm places the password field in ordinary form fieldsets (UserAdmin uses UserChangeForm as its form), so the BoundField.label_tag path can be used for the password field in the admin UI. Incorrect assumption exposed: having an admin-specific readonly rendering path does not guarantee that read_only widgets will always be rendered via that path (location: django/contrib/admin/helpers.py: AdminReadonlyField and django/contrib/auth/admin.py: UserAdmin setup).

5) Investigation gap: the widget template auth/widgets/read_only_password_hash.html was not present in the checked-out repository tree used for this investigation; tests assert the widget renders a <div id="id_password">, but the template source was not located here to independently confirm its literal markup (template reference: django/contrib/auth/forms.py: template_name = 'auth/widgets/read_only_password_hash.html').
StepsToReproduce: In a Django project with contrib.auth enabled, open the admin User change page for a user and inspect the HTML for the password field label (or instantiate a Form using ReadOnlyPasswordHashField and call f['password'].label_tag()).
Observe the generated <label> element for the password field and note whether it includes a for="id_password" attribute.
Inspect the element with that id and confirm it is a non-labelable element (for example, <div id="id_password">...).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When a field's widget does not render a labelable control, the rendered <label> for that field should not include a for="..." attribute that references a non-labelable element; the label should be rendered without an associated for attribute.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The label includes for="id_password" (or similar), referencing the element produced by ReadOnlyPasswordHashWidget (tests show a <div id="id_password">), resulting in a label for attribute that points at a non-labelable element.

problem_statement.Title: BoundWidget.id_for_label ignores per-option id from ChoiceWidget.create_option, causing label 'for' mismatch for choice subwidgets
problem_statement.Description: When rendering choice-based subwidgets (ChoiceWidget subclasses such as CheckboxSelectMultiple and RadioSelect), labels' for attributes can point to a different id than the input elements. The option dicts produced for subwidgets include per-option ids (derived from a base id when provided), but BoundWidget.id_for_label reconstructs an id from the widget name and index instead of using the per-option id stored in the option dict's attrs.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) BoundWidget.id_for_label unconditionally reconstructs an id using the literal pattern 'id_%s_%s' % (self.data['name'], self.data['index']) and does not consult the option dict's attrs (where ChoiceWidget.create_option stores per-option ids). File/class/method: django/forms/boundfield.py: BoundWidget.id_for_label (lines 279-281: return 'id_%s_%s' % (self.data['name'], self.data['index'])).

2) BoundField.subwidgets computes a base id and passes it into choice subwidgets via attrs so per-option ids will be produced downstream when a base id exists. File/class/method: django/forms/boundfield.py: BoundField.subwidgets (lines 45-51: id_ = self.field.widget.attrs.get('id') or self.auto_id; attrs = {'id': id_} if id_ else {}; ... for widget in self.field.widget.subwidgets(..., attrs=attrs)).

3) ChoiceWidget.create_option is the authoritative producer of per-option ids: it builds option_attrs = self.build_attrs(self.attrs, attrs) and, if 'id' is present in option_attrs, sets option_attrs['id'] = self.id_for_label(option_attrs['id'], index) (i.e. base-id + '_' + index when add_id_index is True) and stores that id in the returned option dict's 'attrs'. File/class/method: django/forms/widgets.py: ChoiceWidget.create_option and ChoiceWidget.id_for_label (lines 621-628 and 645-652).

4) Templates expect the per-option id to be available at widget.attrs.id when rendering labels (input_option.html uses {% if widget.attrs.id %} for="{{ widget.attrs.id }}" {% endif %}). File: django/forms/templates/django/forms/widgets/input_option.html.

5) The consumer (BoundWidget.id_for_label) therefore violates the producer/consumer expectation: the authoritative id for an option lives in the option dict's attrs['id'] (created by ChoiceWidget.create_option), but BoundWidget.id_for_label ignores that value and reconstructs a legacy id string. The current hardcoded reconstruction ('id_%s_%s') is only correct under the particular assumption that the auto-generated base id uses the exact 'id_%s' pattern; when the base id differs (for example when widget.attrs['id'] or form.auto_id are set to a different format), label for attributes will not match input ids. File/class/method: django/forms/boundfield.py: BoundWidget.id_for_label (lines 279-281) and django/forms/widgets.py: ChoiceWidget.create_option (lines 621-628).
StepsToReproduce: Create a Form with a ChoiceField or MultipleChoiceField rendered with a choice widget that produces subwidgets (e.g. CheckboxSelectMultiple or RadioSelect).

Provide a base id so ChoiceWidget.create_option will produce per-option ids: either set form.auto_id to a non-default value that includes the field name or pass attrs={'id': '<base-id>'} to the widget.
Render the form or the specific field and inspect the generated HTML for the input elements and their labels.

Compare each input element's id attribute (from the rendered input element or widget.attrs.id in the template context) with the corresponding label's for attribute. Labels will use a reconstructed id ('id_<name>_<index>') while inputs use the per-option id produced by ChoiceWidget.create_option (base-id + '_' + index) when a base id was provided.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: For choice-based subwidgets, each label's for attribute matches the input element's id. When a base id is provided (via form.auto_id or widget.attrs['id']), per-option ids should be derived from that base id (e.g. base-id + '_' + index) and labels should reference those per-option ids.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Labels for choice subwidgets use an id reconstructed as 'id_<name>_<index>' (from BoundWidget.id_for_label) instead of the per-option id stored in widget.attrs.id by ChoiceWidget.create_option, causing the label for attribute to point to a different id than the corresponding input element when a non-default base id is present.

@@ -658,6 +658,13 @@ def test_serialize_functools_partialmethod(self):
     def test_serialize_type_none(self):

+        self.assertSerializedResultEqual(
+            MigrationWriter.serialize(models.Model),

     def test_simple_migration(self):
