problem_statement.Title: makemigrations generates migration that references models.Model but omits 'from django.db import models' causing NameError
problem_statement.Description: makemigrations can produce a migration file whose bases tuple contains models.Model (for example: bases=(app.models.MyMixin, models.Model)) but the generated file does not include the import 'from django.db import models', causing a NameError when the migration module is imported.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) TypeSerializer special-case omits required import for the type object models.Model: In django/db/migrations/serializer.py, TypeSerializer.serialize special_cases contains the tuple (models.Model, "models.Model", []) so serializing the type object models.Model returns ("models.Model", set()) — i.e. the textual name is emitted but no "from django.db import models" import is reported. (See django/db/migrations/serializer.py:275-281.)

2) MigrationWriter relies on serializer-reported imports and will not synthesize the models import: In django/db/migrations/writer.py, MigrationWriter.as_string only merges a models import into the migrations import when the literal "from django.db import models" is present in the collected imports; otherwise it emits only "from django.db import migrations". Because the serializer did not report the models import, the writer omits it. (See django/db/migrations/writer.py:165-171.)

3) The bug is exposed when CreateModel serializes non-default bases: CreateModel sets self.bases = bases or (models.Model,) and only includes bases in its deconstructed kwargs when bases != (models.Model,), so a model inheriting a mixin plus models.Model will trigger serialization of the type object models.Model and therefore invoke TypeSerializer. That serialization path is where the missing import surfaces. (See django/db/migrations/operations/models.py:46-50 and 62-71.)

4) Inconsistent serialization patterns: DeconstructableSerializer._serialize_path does include the import for django.db.models (it returns name "models.<ClassName>" together with imports {"from django.db import models"} when path.module == "django.db.models"), demonstrating that some serializers add the models import while TypeSerializer's special-case does not. This inconsistency explains why some serialized values produce the models import while others (the type object models.Model via TypeSerializer) do not. (See django/db/migrations/serializer.py:94-102.)
StepsToReproduce: Create a Django app with models.py containing: from django.db import models; class MyField(models.TextField): pass; class MyBaseModel(models.Model): class Meta: abstract = True; class MyMixin: pass; class MyModel(MyMixin, MyBaseModel): name = MyField(primary_key=True)
Run python manage.py makemigrations for that app
Open the generated migration (e.g. app/migrations/0001_initial.py) and observe that bases contains (app.models.MyMixin, models.Model) but the file imports lack 'from django.db import models'
Import the generated migration module or run Django; observe NameError: name 'models' is not defined at import time
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: makemigrations generates a syntactically valid Python migration file that includes any imports required by identifiers used in the generated code (for example, if a generated file references models.Model it should include the appropriate import such that importing the migration module does not raise NameError).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The generated migration file references models.Model in its bases tuple but does not include 'from django.db import models' (it only contains 'from django.db import migrations' and 'import app.models'), causing a NameError: name 'models' is not defined when the migration module is imported.

hints_text:
I could reproduce the issue with 3.2.4, 2.2.24 and the main branch. For what it's worth, the issue doesn't occur if the class MyModel does inherit from MyMixin.
MyBaseModel is not necessary to reproduce this issue, it's due to the fact that MyModel doesn't have fields from django.db.models and has custom bases. It looks like an issue with special casing of models.Model in TypeSerializer. Proposed patch diff --git a/django/db/migrations/serializer.py b/django/db/migrations/serializer.py index e19c881cda..6e78462e95 100644 --- a/django/db/migrations/serializer.py +++ b/django/db/migrations/serializer.py @@ -273,7 +273,7 @@ class TupleSerializer(BaseSequenceSerializer): class TypeSerializer(BaseSerializer): def serialize(self): special_cases = [ - (models.Model, "models.Model", []), + (models.Model, "models.Model", ['from django.db import models']), (type(None), 'type(None)', []), ] for case, string, imports in special_cases:

     def clean(self):

problem_statement.Title: FormSet non-form errors are not tagged with 'nonform' CSS class
problem_statement.Description: FormSet-level (non-form) errors are rendered without a distinguishing CSS class. Forms add 'nonfield' to non-field ErrorList instances, but FormSets construct their non-form ErrorList without an equivalent 'nonform' marker, preventing renderers from distinguishing form field errors, form non-field errors, and formset non-form errors.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1) django/forms/formsets.py: BaseFormSet.full_clean constructs the formset-level non-form errors using self.error_class() (initialization) and self.error_class(e.error_list) (ValidationError except path) with no error_class keyword. These two sites create the ErrorList instance(s) used for formset.non_form_errors() and therefore determine the CSS class used when rendering those errors (locations: BaseFormSet.full_clean at the initial assignment and in the except ValidationError handler). 2) django/forms/utils.py: ErrorList.__init__ and ErrorList.as_ul define the exact composition used for the rendered <ul> class attribute. ErrorList stores either 'errorlist' or 'errorlist <error_class>' (code: if error_class is None: self.error_class = 'errorlist' else: self.error_class = 'errorlist {}'.format(error_class)) and uses self.error_class as the class attribute in as_ul(), so passing error_class='nonform' would produce '<ul class="errorlist nonform">'. 3) django/forms/forms.py: BaseForm.non_field_errors and BaseForm.add_error show the established pattern that non-field/form-level error lists are constructed with an error_class kw (forms call self.error_class(error_class='nonfield')), i.e., the convention for forms is to tag non-field errors as 'nonfield'. FormSets do not currently follow that convention for formset-level non-form errors. 4) django/contrib/admin/helpers.py: AdminErrorList collects/merges errors from a form and its inline formsets by creating an ErrorList via super().__init__() (no extra marker) and then extending itself with the values of form.errors and inline formset errors (self.extend(form.errors.values()); self.extend(inline_formset.non_form_errors()); self.extend(errors_in_inline_form.values())). Because extend() iterates the supplied containers, this flattens inner ErrorList contents into the AdminErrorList. The merged AdminErrorList therefore renders as a single <ul> using the AdminErrorList instance's own error_class (default 'errorlist' here), and per-source UL wrappers (and their classes) are not preserved in that merged output. 5) Compatibility note (existing pattern): BaseForm already calls self.error_class(error_class='nonfield') when constructing non-field errors, so callers that set custom error_class types are already required to accept being constructed with an error_class kw. Nevertheless, third-party/custom ErrorList subclasses that override __init__ with incompatible signatures remain a general compatibility risk if used as an error_class. (This is a pre-existing constraint established by BaseForm usage.)
StepsToReproduce: Create a FormSet instance that produces a formset-level (non-form) error (for example, raise ValidationError in BaseFormSet.clean() or otherwise cause non_form_errors to be populated).
Render the formset non-form errors (e.g., call formset.non_form_errors().as_ul() or include {{ formset.non_form_errors }} in a template).
Inspect the resulting HTML for the <ul> element that contains the non-form errors.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: FormSet-level non-form errors are rendered with a distinguishing CSS class in addition to 'errorlist' (user-visible example: <ul class="errorlist nonform">...), so renderers can differentiate form field errors, form non-field errors, and formset non-form errors.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: FormSet non-form errors render as <ul class="errorlist"> (no 'nonform' class). Forms already render non-field errors as <ul class="errorlist nonfield">, so FormSets lack the symmetric 'nonform' marker.

problem_statement.Title: QuerySet.defer() doesn't clear deferred field when chaining with only()
problem_statement.Description: Chaining QuerySet.only(...).defer(...) can result in SQL that selects all model columns instead of only the expected minimal columns (for example, only the primary key). Example: Company.objects.only("name").defer("name") yields SELECT id, name, trade_number, country instead of SELECT id.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Query.deferred_loading representation and state transition (django/db/models/sql/query.py: Query.add_deferred_loading): the Query.deferred_loading tuple holds (field_names_set, defer_bool). Query.add_deferred_loading removes names from the immediate-only set when defer_bool is False by assigning existing.difference(field_names), False. This allows the state to become (frozenset(), False) when the last immediately-loaded name is removed, i.e. an empty immediate set with defer==False. (See add_deferred_loading implementation.)

2) Empty field set semantics in deferred_to_data / get_loaded_field_names (django/db/models/sql/query.py: Query.deferred_to_data and Query.get_loaded_field_names): deferred_to_data unpacks field_names, defer = self.deferred_loading and immediately returns if not field_names ("if not field_names: return"), so an empty field_names set produces no entries in the mapping returned to the caller. get_loaded_field_names caches that mapping. Therefore the representation (frozenset(), False) is decoded as "no restrictions" (i.e. do not suppress any columns), which is semantically incorrect when it resulted from removing the last name from a prior immediate-only set.

3) Mutations to deferred_loading are not accompanied by loaded-field cache invalidation (django/db/models/sql/query.py: Query.add_deferred_loading, Query.add_immediate_loading, Query.clear_deferred_loading): get_loaded_field_names caches results in self._loaded_field_names_cache, but the methods that change self.deferred_loading do not clear or update that cache. As a result, a previously computed loaded-field mapping can remain in use after deferred_loading changes, producing stale/incorrect behavior.

4) Compiler dependency (django/db/models/sql/compiler.py: SQLCompiler.deferred_to_columns and SQLCompiler.get_default_columns): the SQL compiler asks the Query to convert deferred_loading into a mapping of models/tables to loaded field names via query.deferred_to_data / query.get_loaded_field_names. Any incorrect encoding of deferred_loading (including an empty immediate set being interpreted as "no restrictions") or stale cache values propagates directly to the set of selected SQL columns.

5) Semantic relationship that is violated by the current encoding: when an "immediate-only" set becomes empty because all previously-immediate field names were removed, that logical situation should not be encoded as an empty immediate set interpreted as "no restrictions." Concretely, the intended effect of only("name").defer("name") is that the resulting selection is the minimal allowed set (e.g. primary key only for the base model). The code currently produces an empty immediate-set representation which deferred_to_data treats as including all fields. In the conceptual domain, the empty immediate-only result should instead be represented (or decoded) as an explicit deferred set describing the fields to exclude (for a simple base model, the set of non-pk concrete fields) so that the compiler will select only the remaining allowed fields. Note: deferred_to_data already contains logic to expand related-path names (it splits names on LOOKUP_SEP and walks related opts), which is relevant to how any representation should be interpreted for prefixed related-field names.
StepsToReproduce: Define a Company model with fields: id (primary key), name, trade_number, country.
Run Company.objects.only("name").defer("name") and inspect the generated SQL (e.g., print(query) or str(query.query)).
Observe that the SQL is: SELECT "company"."id", "company"."name", "company"."trade_number", "company"."country" instead of SELECT "company"."id".
Run Company.objects.only("name").defer("name").defer("country") and inspect the SQL; it also selects all fields.
Run Company.objects.only("name", "country").defer("name") and inspect the SQL; this produces SELECT "company"."id", "company"."country" (expected behavior) and contrasts the failing cases.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When fields are first restricted by only(...) and then the same fields are deferred via defer(...), the resulting query should load only the remaining explicitly permitted fields. For the base example, Company.objects.only("name").defer("name") should produce SQL selecting only the model primary key: SELECT "company"."id".
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Chaining only(...).defer(...) (for example, Company.objects.only("name").defer("name")) produces SQL that selects all model columns (SELECT id, name, trade_number, country) rather than only the remaining permitted columns (e.g., primary key).

hints_text:
Replying to Manuel Baclet: Considering a simple Company model with four fields: id, name, trade_number and country. If we evaluate a queryset containing a .defer() following a .only(), the generated sql query selects unexpected fields. For example: Company.objects.only("name").defer("name") loads all the fields with the following query: SELECT "company"."id", "company"."name", "company"."trade_number", "company"."country" FROM "company" This is an expected behavior, defer() removes fields from the list of fields specified by the only() method (i.e. list of fields that should not be deferred). In this example only() adds name to the list, defer() removes name from the list, so you have empty lists and all fields will be loaded. It is also ​documented: # Final result is that everything except "headline" is deferred. Entry.objects.only("headline", "body").defer("body") Company.objects.only("name").defer("name").defer("country") also loads all the fields with the same query: SELECT "company"."id", "company"."name", "company"."trade_number", "company"."country" FROM "company" I agree you shouldn't get all field, but only pk, name, and trade_number: SELECT "ticket_32704_company"."id", "ticket_32704_company"."name", "ticket_32704_company"."trade_number" FROM "ticket_32704_company" this is due to the fact that defer() doesn't clear the list of deferred field when chaining with only(). I attached a proposed patch.
Draft.
After reading the documentation carefully, i cannot say that it is clearly stated that deferring all the fields used in a previous .only() call performs a reset of the deferred set. Moreover, in the .defer() ​section, we have: You can make multiple calls to defer(). Each call adds new fields to the deferred set: and this seems to suggest that: calls to .defer() cannot remove items from the deferred set and evaluating qs.defer("some_field") should never fetch the column "some_field" (since this should add "some_field" to the deferred set) the querysets qs.defer("field1").defer("field2") and qs.defer("field1", "field2") should be equivalent IMHO, there is a mismatch between the doc and the actual implementation.
calls to .defer() cannot remove items from the deferred set and evaluating qs.defer("some_field") should never fetch the column "some_field" (since this should add "some_field" to the deferred set) Feel-free to propose a docs clarification (see also #24048). the querysets qs.defer("field1").defer("field2") and qs.defer("field1", "field2") should be equivalent That's why I accepted Company.objects.only("name").defer("name").defer("country") as a bug.
I think that what is described in the documentation is what users are expected and it is the implementation that should be fixed! With your patch proposal, i do not think that: Company.objects.only("name").defer("name").defer("country") is equivalent to Company.objects.only("name").defer("name", "country")
Replying to Manuel Baclet: I think that what is described in the documentation is what users are expected and it is the implementation that should be fixed! I don't agree, and there is no need to shout. As documented: "The only() method is more or less the opposite of defer(). You call it with the fields that should not be deferred ...", so .only('name').defer('name') should return all fields. You can start a discussion on DevelopersMailingList if you don't agree. With your patch proposal, i do not think that: Company.objects.only("name").defer("name").defer("country") is equivalent to Company.objects.only("name").defer("name", "country") Did you check this? with proposed patch country is the only deferred fields in both cases. As far as I'm aware that's an intended behavior.
With the proposed patch, I think that: Company.objects.only("name").defer("name", "country") loads all fields whereas: Company.objects.only("name").defer("name").defer("country") loads all fields except "country". Why is that? In the first case: existing.difference(field_names) == {"name"}.difference(["name", "country"]) == empty_set and we go into the if branch clearing all the deferred fields and we are done. In the second case: existing.difference(field_names) == {"name"}.difference(["name"]) == empty_set and we go into the if branch clearing all the deferred fields. Then we add "country" to the set of deferred fields.
Hey all, Replying to Mariusz Felisiak: Replying to Manuel Baclet: With your patch proposal, i do not think that: Company.objects.only("name").defer("name").defer("country") is equivalent to Company.objects.only("name").defer("name", "country") Did you check this? with proposed patch country is the only deferred fields in both cases. As far as I'm aware that's an intended behavior. I believe Manuel is right. This happens because the set difference in one direction gives you the empty set that will clear out the deferred fields - but it is missing the fact that we might also be adding more defer fields than we had only fields in the first place, so that we actually switch from an .only() to a .defer() mode. See the corresponding PR that should fix this behaviour ​https://github.com/django/django/pull/14667

problem_statement.Title: ManyToManyRel.identity inserts raw through_fields (list) into identity causing TypeError when hashed
problem_statement.Description: ManyToManyRel.identity places self.through_fields directly into the relation identity tuple. When through_fields is a list, the identity tuple contains an unhashable element, causing a TypeError during operations that hash relations (for example, model checks).
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) django/db/models/fields/reverse_related.py::ManyToManyRel.identity encodes an incorrect assumption: it appends self.through_fields directly into the identity tuple (return super().identity + (self.through, self.through_fields, self.db_constraint,)). This assumes through_fields is already hashable. When through_fields is a list, that assumption is false and the identity tuple contains an unhashable element.

2) django/db/models/fields/reverse_related.py::ForeignObjectRel.__hash__ and identity establish the requirement that all identity elements be hashable: ForeignObjectRel.__hash__ returns hash(self.identity), and ForeignObjectRel.identity already canonicalizes limit_choices_to with make_hashable(self.limit_choices_to). This demonstrates the intended pattern that unhashable or mutable components used in identity should be converted to a stable, hashable representation before inclusion.

3) django/utils/hashable.py::make_hashable is the canonical helper in the codebase for producing hashable, canonical representations of potentially-unhashable values (it converts dicts to sorted tuples and converts non-hashable iterables to tuples of their make_hashable'd elements). The omission in ManyToManyRel.identity of analogous canonicalization for through_fields violates the relationship required by ForeignObjectRel.__hash__.

4) django/db/models/base.py::_check_m2m_through_same_relationship encodes the same incorrect assumption in model-check signatures: it builds signature = (f.remote_field.model, cls, f.remote_field.through, f.remote_field.through_fields) using through_fields directly. If through_fields can be provided as a list, that signature tuple may contain a list and therefore be type-inconsistent or unhashable in contexts that expect canonical/compare-safe signature elements.

5) django/db/migrations/utils.py::field_references (field_references) returns references_through = (remote_field, through_fields) using through_fields unchanged. This is another location where through_fields is propagated inside tuples without canonicalization and therefore shares the same root-cause pattern that can lead to inconsistent comparisons or hashing failures when through_fields is a non-hashable type (e.g., list).
StepsToReproduce: Create models exactly as in the repro: define Parent (with a CharField), ProxyParent (Meta: proxy = True) inheriting Parent, Child with a ForeignKey to Parent and a ManyToManyField to Parent using through='ManyToManyModel' and through_fields=['child', 'parent'], and define ManyToManyModel with parent = ForeignKey(Parent, related_name='+'), child = ForeignKey(Child, related_name='+'), plus an extra FK field.
Run Django management command that triggers system checks (for example: python manage.py check or any manage.py command that runs app checks).
Observe the process raising TypeError: unhashable type: 'list' from django/db/models/fields/reverse_related.py when hashing a relation identity.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Model checks and other operations that compare or hash relation objects complete without error. Many-to-many relationship objects are comparable and hashable regardless of whether through_fields was supplied as a list or tuple; logically equivalent through_fields representations do not cause TypeError or inconsistent equality results.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Django raises TypeError: unhashable type: 'list' during model checks (stack trace points to django/db/models/fields/reverse_related.py.__hash__). The error occurs because ManyToManyRel.identity contains a list in its identity tuple (self.through_fields), which cannot be hashed when hash(self.identity) is called.

problem_statement.Title: Developer-supplied related_name is silently ignored for symmetrical self ManyToManyField (no system check)
problem_statement.Description: When a ManyToManyField is symmetrical and points to 'self' (self-referential), Django does not create a reverse accessor for the relation, but user-supplied related_name values are accepted and effectively ignored/overwritten without any error or warning. This can mislead developers who expect the related_name to produce a reverse accessor or otherwise be meaningful.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) ManyToManyRel.get_accessor_name (django/db/models/fields/reverse_related.py) intentionally suppresses reverse accessors for symmetrical self relations: when the relation is many-to-many, self.symmetrical is True and the resolved model equals the relation's own model, get_accessor_name returns None. Concretely: in get_accessor_name the branch "if self.symmetrical and model == self.model: return None" prevents any reverse accessor name from being installed for that relation.

2) ManyToManyField.contribute_to_class (django/db/models/fields/related.py) unconditionally assigns an internal related_name for symmetrical/self relationships (e.g. "%s_rel_+" % name) when the remote_field is symmetrical and remote_field.model is the recursive constant ('self') or matches the host class name. This assignment does not check whether the developer explicitly supplied a related_name, so a user-provided related_name can be overwritten or masked by the internal name generation.

3) RelatedField.deconstruct (django/db/models/fields/related.py) preserves a developer-supplied related_name via self._related_name in the serialized field kwargs (deconstruct will include related_name if self._related_name is not None). This means that, although the runtime reverse accessor is suppressed/overwritten, the original developer argument can still appear in deconstructed/migration state — increasing the potential for confusion.

4) There is no existing field-system validation that rejects or warns about an explicit related_name on a symmetrical self relation. ManyToManyField.check (django/db/models/fields/related.py) calls a set of existing helpers (e.g. _check_unique, _check_relationship_model, _check_ignored_options, _check_table_uniqueness) but contains no check that surfaces the mismatch between a user-supplied related_name and the fact that a reverse accessor will not be created in the symmetrical self case.

5) The codebase relies on the RECURSIVE_RELATIONSHIP_CONSTANT ('self') and the trailing '+' sentinel in related_name strings to indicate internal/hidden related names. RECURSIVE_RELATIONSHIP_CONSTANT is defined as 'self' (django/db/models/fields/related.py) and the is_hidden() check inspects whether related_name ends with '+' (django/db/models/fields/reverse_related.py). These conventions are used by contribute_to_class and get_accessor_name and are part of why a developer-supplied related_name can be present yet have no effect.

6) The precise condition under which a developer-supplied related_name will be ignored (diagnostic formulation) is:
   - Let to_model = resolve_relation(from_model, self.remote_field.model) (the repository provides resolve_relation for resolving relation strings relative to a scope model).
   - If the relation is symmetrical (remote_field.symmetrical is True) and to_model == from_model, then no reverse accessor will be created and any developer-supplied related_name will not result in a usable reverse accessor. Note: resolving this condition reliably requires a concrete from_model (i.e. checks that depend on from_model cannot run when from_model is None such as during some abstract/unresolved contexts).
StepsToReproduce: Run the project checks: python manage.py check — observe no errors or warnings about the supplied related_name.

Start a Django shell (python manage.py shell), import the model and inspect the target model for the reverse accessor (e.g. hasattr(Person, 'buddies') or dir(Person)) — observe that the 'buddies' reverse accessor is not present.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: User-visible expectation: If a developer passes related_name='X' on a ManyToManyField they reasonably expect a reverse accessor named 'X' to be available on the related model, or for the system to clearly indicate that the argument is inapplicable. Concretely, user-visible behavior should not allow a developer to specify a related_name and then silently have that argument be ignored with no warning or error; either the provided related_name should produce the corresponding reverse accessor or the framework should surface that the argument is not applicable for the relation.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Developers can pass related_name to a symmetrical self ManyToManyField and no error or warning is raised. Internally, contribute_to_class generates/overwrites the related_name (using patterns like "%s_rel_+" or names ending with '+'), get_accessor_name returns None for symmetrical self relations, and the user-supplied related_name has no effect on the runtime reverse accessor (while remaining present in deconstructed/migration representations).

--- a/tests/admin_views/test_autocomplete_view.py
+++ b/tests/admin_views/test_autocomplete_view.py
@@ -1,3 +1,4 @@
+import datetime
 import json
 from contextlib import contextmanager
 
@@ -293,6 +294,29 @@ class PKOrderingQuestionAdmin(QuestionAdmin):
             'pagination': {'more': False},
         })
 
+    def test_serialize_result(self):
+        class AutocompleteJsonSerializeResultView(AutocompleteJsonView):
+            def serialize_result(self, obj, to_field_name):
+                return {
+                    **super().serialize_result(obj, to_field_name),
+                    'posted': str(obj.posted),
+                }
+
+        Question.objects.create(question='Question 1', posted=datetime.date(2021, 8, 9))
+        Question.objects.create(question='Question 2', posted=datetime.date(2021, 8, 7))
+        request = self.factory.get(self.url, {'term': 'question', **self.opts})
+        request.user = self.superuser
+        response = AutocompleteJsonSerializeResultView.as_view(**self.as_view_args)(request)
+        self.assertEqual(response.status_code, 200)
+        data = json.loads(response.content.decode('utf-8'))
+        self.assertEqual(data, {
+            'results': [
+                {'id': str(q.pk), 'text': q.question, 'posted': str(q.posted)}
+                for q in Question.objects.order_by('-posted')
+            ],
+            'pagination': {'more': False},
+        })
+
 
 @override_settings(ROOT_URLCONF='admin_views.urls')
 class SeleniumTests(AdminSeleniumTestCase):

problem_statement.Title: AutocompleteJsonView requires full get() override to add extra per-item fields
problem_statement.Description: Developers cannot add extra fields to each item in the admin autocomplete JSON response without overriding AutocompleteJsonView.get(), forcing copy/paste of the entire get() implementation and creating maintenance overhead as get() evolves.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Per-item JSON serialization is implemented inline inside AutocompleteJsonView.get(): the method constructs the response with a list comprehension that builds dictionaries with only 'id' and 'text'. Location: django/contrib/admin/views/autocomplete.py :: AutocompleteJsonView.get() (see the JsonResponse returned at the end of get()). This encodes the incorrect assumption that per-item JSON cannot be customized without replacing the whole get() implementation.

2) There is no dedicated per-item serialization extension point in the AutocompleteJsonView API. Location: django/contrib/admin/views/autocomplete.py :: AutocompleteJsonView (the class exposes get(), process_request(), etc., but contains no method intended specifically for per-item result serialization). The absence of such a hook forces subclasses to override get() to change per-item fields.

3) The view uses a fixed server-side page size that tests and consumers rely on: AutocompleteJsonView.paginate_by is defined at class level (value 20). Location: django/contrib/admin/views/autocomplete.py :: AutocompleteJsonView.paginate_by. Tests reference this value (tests/admin_views/test_autocomplete_view.py) and therefore any change to response shape should preserve pagination behavior.

4) The AutocompleteJsonView is instantiated via AdminSite.autocomplete_view, which calls AutocompleteJsonView.as_view(admin_site=self)(request). Location: django/contrib/admin/sites.py :: AdminSite.autocomplete_view. This central registration means changes to AutocompleteJsonView's public surface or expected behavior affect AdminSite consumers and third-party custom AdminSite code.

5) Client-side usage in the admin relies on select2 and the JSON shape containing 'id' and 'text'. Location: django/contrib/admin/widgets.py :: AutocompleteMixin (the widget wires select2 via media and sets AJAX/data attributes), and tests in tests/admin_views/test_autocomplete_view.py assert the response includes 'id' and 'text'. This establishes the client-side contract that 'id' and 'text' must remain present; extra keys are not required by the upstream server-side code but may be consumed by custom client code.

6) Test coverage does not include a unit test demonstrating a consumer subclassing the view to customize only per-item fields using a dedicated extension point (because that extension point does not exist). Location: tests/admin_views/test_autocomplete_view.py — the existing tests assert the default JSON shape and pagination but do not exercise a per-item-serialization override. This is a coverage gap that contributes to the maintenance risk if the view's interface is changed.
StepsToReproduce: Configure a Django admin site with a model that uses an autocomplete foreign-key field.

Create a CustomAutocompleteJsonView subclass of AutocompleteJsonView and override get() to return JsonResponse with additional per-item keys (for example, copying the implementation and changing the list comprehension to {'id': str(getattr(obj, to_field_name)), 'text': str(obj), 'notes': obj.notes}).
Register that view (or replace the autocomplete URL) and trigger the admin autocomplete endpoint in a browser or HTTP client.
Inspect the JSON response; observe that adding extra fields required copying the entire get() implementation.
Confirm that the overridden get() duplicates permission checks, pagination, and other logic from the upstream AutocompleteJsonView (demonstrating maintenance burden when upstream get() changes).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: It should be possible to add custom fields to each item in the admin autocomplete JSON response without overriding the entire AutocompleteJsonView.get(). The default response must continue to include at least 'id' and 'text' and preserve pagination behavior (server-side page size and pagination.more flag).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: AutocompleteJsonView.get() constructs per-item dictionaries inline with only 'id' and 'text' (using {'id': str(getattr(obj, to_field_name)), 'text': str(obj)} in a list comprehension) so developers who need extra per-item fields must override the entire get() method and duplicate its logic, causing maintenance overhead as upstream get() changes.

hints_text: Makes sense to me.

diff --git a/django/utils/decorators.py b/django/utils/decorators.py
--- a/django/utils/decorators.py
+++ b/django/utils/decorators.py
@@ -37,7 +37,7 @@ def _wrapper(self, *args, **kwargs):
         # 'self' argument, but it's a closure over self so it can call
         # 'func'. Also, wrap method.__get__() in a function because new
         # attributes can't be set on bound method objects, only on functions.
-        bound_method = partial(method.__get__(self, type(self)))
+        bound_method = wraps(method)(partial(method.__get__(self, type(self))))
         for dec in decorators:
             bound_method = dec(bound_method)
         return bound_method(*args, **kwargs)
test_patch:
diff --git a/tests/decorators/tests.py b/tests/decorators/tests.py
--- a/tests/decorators/tests.py
+++ b/tests/decorators/tests.py
@@ -425,6 +425,29 @@ class Test:
                 def __module__(cls):
                     return "tests"
 
+    def test_wrapper_assignments(self):
+        """@method_decorator preserves wrapper assignments."""
+        func_name = None
+        func_module = None
+
+        def decorator(func):
+            @wraps(func)
+            def inner(*args, **kwargs):
+                nonlocal func_name, func_module
+                func_name = getattr(func, '__name__', None)
+                func_module = getattr(func, '__module__', None)
+                return func(*args, **kwargs)
+            return inner
+
+        class Test:
+            @method_decorator(decorator)
+            def method(self):
+                return 'tests'
+
+        Test().method()
+        self.assertEqual(func_name, 'method')
+        self.assertIsNotNone(func_module)
+
 
 class XFrameOptionsDecoratorsTests(TestCase):
     """

problem_statement.Title: method_decorator() passes functools.partial to decorators, causing AttributeError when decorators expect function metadata
problem_statement.Description: Decorating an instance method with method_decorator results in a functools.partial being passed into the function decorator. Because functools.partial does not expose normal function metadata (for example __name__ and __module__), decorators that inspect the callable they are given can raise AttributeError when applied to methods via method_decorator.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. django/utils/decorators.py::_multi_decorate constructs and passes a functools.partial as the bound callable into decorators. The problematic code is: bound_method = partial(method.__get__(self, type(self))) (django/utils/decorators.py, line 40). This assumes a functools.partial is an acceptable stand-in for a function when a decorator inspects the callable.

2. django/utils/decorators.py::_update_method_wrapper copies decorator-added attributes onto the class-level wrapper after the decorators are applied (it creates a dummy decorated function and then calls update_wrapper on the class-level wrapper — lines 13-19 and 45-49). This assumes copying attributes later is sufficient to satisfy decorators, but it does not prevent decorators from failing when they inspect the object they receive at decoration/call time.

3. django/utils/decorators.py::method_decorator delegates to _multi_decorate for both function- and class-level decoration (lines 61-76). As a result, the partial-based approach in _multi_decorate is used by method_decorator in all its call paths, spreading the same incorrect assumption throughout all uses of method_decorator.

4. Tests do not cover the specific case where a decorator inspects the callable passed into it (for example, decorators that directly access func.__name__ inside their wrapper). tests/decorators/tests.py defines decorators that preserve attributes and uses functools.wraps in some test helpers (e.g. simple_dec at lines 157-163), but the test suite does not include a decorator that directly reads attributes like __name__ from the callable it receives in the method_decorator path. This gap means the failure mode reported (AttributeError from a functools.partial missing __name__) is not exercised by existing tests.
StepsToReproduce: Define a decorator that inspects the wrapped callable's metadata (for example, references func.__name__ inside its wrapper).
Apply that decorator to an instance method using @method_decorator(your_decorator).
Instantiate the class and call the decorated method.
Observe an AttributeError such as: AttributeError: 'functools.partial' object has no attribute '__name__'.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Decorating an instance method via method_decorator should not raise exceptions when the supplied decorator inspects the wrapped callable. Decorators that access or rely on function metadata (for example __name__, __module__, __doc__) should be able to do so when used on methods via method_decorator.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Calling a method decorated via method_decorator causes an exception such as AttributeError: 'functools.partial' object has no attribute '__name__' because the decorator received a functools.partial that lacks usual function metadata.

problem_statement.Title: Wrong admin URL namespace for readonly related-object links and Recent actions in custom AdminSite
problem_statement.Description: When using a custom AdminSite, readonly related-object links rendered in the admin (and links in the "Recent actions" list) are generated using the default 'admin' namespace instead of the custom AdminSite namespace, so links point to /admin/... rather than the custom site's URL prefix (for example, /custom-admin/...).
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) django/contrib/admin/helpers.py :: AdminReadonlyField.get_admin_url — This method builds the URL name as 'admin:<app_label>_<model_name>_change' but calls reverse(url_name, args=[quote(remote_obj.pk)]) without supplying a current_app (AdminSite) namespace. The code path has access to self.model_admin on AdminReadonlyField instances, but omitting current_app means URL reversing uses the resolver's default/registered namespaces (commonly the 'admin' app namespace) rather than the AdminSite instance that is rendering the page. The incorrect assumption is that reverse(url_name, ...) (by itself) will resolve to the AdminSite currently serving the page.

2) django/contrib/admin/models.py :: LogEntry.get_admin_url — This method similarly constructs url_name = 'admin:<app_label>_<model_name>_change' and returns reverse(url_name, args=(quote(self.object_id),)) without a current_app. Because this method does not accept a request or context, it cannot rely on request.current_app and yet assumes reversing by name alone will yield an AdminSite-specific URL. The incorrect assumption is that reverse(...) without a site namespace will produce a link appropriate for the AdminSite in which the log entry is being displayed.

3) Inconsistency evidence across the admin package — Other admin code that has access to the AdminSite or request consistently supplies the AdminSite namespace when reversing admin URLs (for example, options.py and sites.py set request.current_app or call reverse(..., current_app=self.admin_site.name); widgets.py and sites.py use the admin_site.name when constructing namespaced URLs). The two methods above deviate from this established pattern, causing per-site links to be generated in the default 'admin' namespace instead of the custom AdminSite namespace.

Notes and gaps: the code paths for AdminReadonlyField do make model_admin available on instances in normal ModelAdmin rendering, but model_admin could be None in other usage contexts — this is an edge case that was identified but not exhaustively tested. The report's diagnosis is limited to the incorrect assumption about URL namespace resolution in the two locations named above.
StepsToReproduce: Create and register a custom AdminSite with a non-default name (for example, name='custom-admin') and mount it at a custom URL prefix (for example, /custom-admin/).
Register a model that has a ForeignKey to another model; include that ForeignKey field in readonly_fields of the model's ModelAdmin.

Visit the change (or view) page for an instance of the model in the custom AdminSite and inspect the rendered readonly related-object link — it will point to /admin/<app>/<model>/<pk>/change/ instead of /custom-admin/<app>/<model>/<pk>/change/.
Open the AdminSite index page and inspect links in the "Recent actions" section — those links will also point to the default admin namespace (e.g. /admin/...), not the custom AdminSite prefix.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Readonly related-object links and "Recent actions" links generated by the admin should resolve using the AdminSite namespace currently serving the page (the custom AdminSite), producing URLs under the custom admin prefix (for example, /custom-admin/<app>/<model>/<pk>/change/).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Readonly related-object links and "Recent actions" links are generated under the default admin namespace (URLs beginning with /admin/...) even when viewing pages served by a custom AdminSite (for example, links point to /admin/<app>/<model>/<pk>/change/ instead of /custom-admin/<app>/<model>/<pk>/change/).

Hey Ken, yes seems right. Good spot. Looks like this should have been part of b79088306513d5ed76d31ac40ab3c15f858946ea for #31181 (which was Django 3.2) ​here. However, I don't know if there are any side effects and I have not yet run the full suite of tests on this. Mostly looking for feedback whether I'm on the right track. I ran your suggestion against most of the usual suspects admin_* tests without issue so... Would you like to prepare a patch? Looks like setting up the test case is the most of it... Thanks!
I'll be happy to try - but I'm not likely to be able to get to it before the weekend. (I don't know how "urgent" you consider it.) If it can sit that long, I'll see what I can do. (First "real patch" and all that - want to make sure I do it reasonably right.)
Hey Ken. Super thanks! Since it's a bug in a new feature it's marked as release blocker and will be backported to Django 3.2. We'll target ​3.2.8, which is slated for the beginning of October. If it gets close to that and you've not had time we can pick it up. Reach out on the Forum if you'd like input at all. 🙂 Thanks! (And Welcome Aboard! ⛵️)
Heyy folks, I wanted to assign the ticket to myself and fix the issue, instead it assigned the ownership to me. Apologies
Changes ownership again.
I found out that changes got accepted, sorry for the inconvenience caused.

problem_statement.Title: ModelChoiceIteratorValue is unhashable causing TypeError in widget create_option / dict membership after Django 3.1
problem_statement.Description: After upgrading to Django 3.1, ModelChoiceField option values are ModelChoiceIteratorValue instances that are unhashable; code that performs membership tests or uses option values as dict/set keys (for example, in widget create_option overrides) raises TypeError: "unhashable type: 'ModelChoiceIteratorValue'".
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) django/forms/models.py: ModelChoiceIteratorValue — The class defines __eq__ (equality compares to the wrapped .value) but does not define __hash__. In Python, defining __eq__ without defining __hash__ results in instances being unhashable (__hash__ = None), which raises TypeError when such objects are used in hash-based operations (dict membership, set membership, dict keys). (See ModelChoiceIteratorValue.__eq__ and absence of __hash__.)

2) django/forms/models.py: ModelChoiceIterator.choice — The iterator wraps the prepared value into a ModelChoiceIteratorValue instance using ModelChoiceIteratorValue(self.field.prepare_value(obj), obj). Because equality is implemented to compare against the wrapped .value, consumers treat the ModelChoiceIteratorValue as a proxy for the underlying prepared value; any hash-related semantics must therefore be consistent with that equality relationship (i.e., objects considered equal to their wrapped .value must have consistent hash behavior: hash(instance) == hash(instance.value)).

3) django/forms/models.py: ModelChoiceField.prepare_value — The wrapped .value is produced by prepare_value (typically returning the model instance PK or a serializable field value). In common cases this prepared value is a hashable primitive (int or str), so consumers commonly expect to be able to use the value in dict/set membership or as dict keys. ModelChoiceIteratorValue wrapping of the prepared value changes the runtime object type presented to consumers while not preserving hashability.

4) django/forms/widgets.py: ChoiceWidget.create_option and user create_option overrides — Widgets and user overrides receive the choice "value" argument (the first element of each choice tuple) unchanged. For ModelChoiceField choices this is the ModelChoiceIteratorValue instance. Consumer code commonly assumes a primitive hashable prepared value (for example, "if value in self.show_fields" when show_fields is a dict keyed by ints); because ModelChoiceIteratorValue instances are unhashable, such code raises TypeError.

5) docs/releases/3.1.txt and docs/ref/forms/fields.txt (informational) — The 3.1 release introduced ModelChoiceIteratorValue and the documentation demonstrates and encourages using value.instance in widgets. The docs state the iterator "proxies transparently in most cases," which aligns with a compatibility expectation; however, the implementation's lack of a hash that is consistent with __eq__ breaks common usage patterns that rely on hashability. This is a behavioral incompatibility that affects code performing hash-based operations on received option values.

6) Edge-case assumption — If a custom prepare_value implementation were to return an inherently unhashable prepared value (for example, a list), then any consumer or any hypothetical hash derived from the wrapped .value would raise TypeError. The API does not enforce that prepare_value returns a hashable object for choice values, so this is a related constraint that callers and implementers should be aware of.
StepsToReproduce: Use Django 3.1 (or a version where ModelChoiceIteratorValue is used for ModelChoiceField choices).
Create a ModelChoiceField on a form and attach the default select widget (or a custom widget).

Override the widget's create_option method and perform a membership check using the provided value against a dict keyed by primary-key ints, e.g.:
  def create_option(...):
    context = super().create_option(...)
    if not value:
      return context
    if value in self.show_fields:  # self.show_fields is a dict keyed by ints
      context['attrs']['data-fields'] = json.dumps(self.show_fields[value])
Render the form or otherwise invoke create_option for the ModelChoiceField choices. Observe the TypeError.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Option values delivered to widgets and user overrides behave like the prepared primitive values (hashable when the prepared value is hashable). Membership tests ("if value in dict"), use as dict keys, and inclusion in sets should not raise TypeError when the prepared value is a hashable primitive (e.g., int or str).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: When rendering choices for a ModelChoiceField, the option "value" argument is a ModelChoiceIteratorValue instance that is unhashable; operations that require hashing (membership tests, dict/set insertion or lookup) raise TypeError: "unhashable type: 'ModelChoiceIteratorValue'".

hints_text:
Thanks for the ticket. Agreed, we could make ModelChoiceIteratorValue hashable by adding: def __hash__(self): return hash(self.value) For now you can use value.value as ​documented in the "Backwards incompatible changes in 3.1" section. Would you like to prepare a patch?
Replying to Mariusz Felisiak: Thanks for the ticket. Agreed, we could make ModelChoiceIteratorValue hashable by adding: def __hash__(self): return hash(self.value) For now you can use value.value as ​documented in the "Backwards incompatible changes in 3.1" section. Would you like to prepare a patch? Yes, sure.
Patch: ​https://github.com/django/django/pull/14915

@@ -212,11 +212,7 @@ def __init__(self, table, expressions, compiler, quote_value):

         if self.table != old_table:
             return
-        expressions = deepcopy(self.expressions)
-        self.columns = []
-        for col in self.compiler.query._gen_cols([expressions]):
-            col.alias = new_table
-        self.expressions = expressions
+        self.expressions = self.expressions.relabeled_clone({old_table: new_table})
         super().rename_table_references(old_table, new_table)

+    def test_rename_table_references_without_alias(self):
+        compiler = Query(Person, alias_cols=False).get_compiler(connection=connection)
+        table = Person._meta.db_table
+        expressions = Expressions(
+            table=table,
+            expressions=ExpressionList(
+                IndexExpression(Upper('last_name')),
+                IndexExpression(F('first_name')),
+            ).resolve_expression(compiler.query),
+            compiler=compiler,
+            quote_value=self.editor.quote_value,
+        )
+        expressions.rename_table_references(table, 'other')
+        self.assertIs(expressions.references_table(table), False)
+        self.assertIs(expressions.references_table('other'), True)
+        expected_str = '(UPPER(%s)), %s' % (
+            self.editor.quote_name('last_name'),
+            self.editor.quote_name('first_name'),
+        )
+        self.assertEqual(str(expressions), expected_str)
+
     def test_rename_column_references(self):
         table = Person._meta.db_table
         self.expressions.rename_column_references(table, 'first_name', 'other')

problem_statement.Title: Remaking table with UniqueConstraint crashes on SQLite due to table-qualified column in deferred index expression
problem_statement.Description: When applying a migration that remakes a table which has a UniqueConstraint (or other expression-based index/constraint), Django generates deferred CREATE INDEX / CREATE UNIQUE INDEX SQL whose index expression contains a table-qualified column identifier (a dot operator). SQLite prohibits the '.' operator inside index expressions; the qualified identifier appears because expression references were renamed to include the table name prior to executing deferred DDL, causing the migration to fail during the SQLite table-remake flow.
problem_statement.RootCause:
Multiple pieces of code that generate expression-based index/constraint DDL rely on compilers constructed with Query(..., alias_cols=False) and therefore expect expression SQL to use unqualified column names. However, Expressions.rename_table_references unconditionally assigns an alias to column expressions during rename, producing table-qualified column identifiers in compiled expressions. The incorrect assumptions and affected locations (all given equal weight):

1) Unconditional aliasing in rename of expression parts
   - Location: django/db/backends/ddl_references.py :: Expressions.rename_table_references
   - Problem: The method iterates over columns generated from the expressions and assigns col.alias = new_table for each, without consulting the compiler/query intent. This forces expression columns to be rendered as qualified identifiers (alias.column) when compiled.
   - Why this is wrong: Some expression compilers are created with alias_cols=False to indicate that compiled expressions should use unqualified column names; unconditional aliasing overrides that intent and introduces a '.' operator into index expressions.

2) Expression-to-SQL renders alias-qualified identifiers when alias is present
   - Location: django/db/models/expressions.py :: Col.as_sql
   - Problem: When a Col has a non-None alias, Col.as_sql builds an identifier sequence that includes the alias and joins identifiers with a '.' (yielding alias.column). Thus any alias set by the rename step materializes as a '.' in the generated SQL for that expression.

3) Index/unique/constraint DDL compilers are intentionally created to produce unqualified column names
   - Locations (all construct compilers or resolve expressions with alias_cols=False and therefore expect unqualified column rendering):
     - django/db/backends/base/schema.py :: _create_index_sql (compiler = Query(model, alias_cols=False).get_compiler(...))
     - django/db/backends/base/schema.py :: _create_unique_sql (compiler = Query(model, alias_cols=False).get_compiler(...))
     - django/db/models/constraints.py :: UniqueConstraint._get_index_expressions and other UniqueConstraint paths (use Query(model, alias_cols=False) when preparing index expressions)
     - django/db/models/indexes.py :: Index (create_sql and condition/expression compilation via Query(model, alias_cols=False))
     - django/db/models/constraints.py :: CheckConstraint._get_check_sql (Query(model, alias_cols=False))
     - django/contrib/postgres/constraints.py :: ExclusionConstraint.constraint_sql (Query(model, alias_cols=False))
   - Problem: These locations construct a compiler/query with alias_cols=False, signalling expressions used for index/constraint DDL should compile to unqualified column names. Because Expressions.rename_table_references assigns aliases unconditionally, the compiled DDL ends up qualified contrary to the compiler/query intent.

4) SQLite table-remake flow applies renames before executing deferred DDL
   - Location: django/db/backends/sqlite3/schema.py :: _remake_table (and BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor.alter_db_table)
   - Problem: The SQLite table-remake sequence renames the temporary/new table to the target table and calls rename_table_references on deferred Statement objects before executing deferred SQL. Because rename_table_references has previously assigned aliases unconditionally, the deferred CREATE INDEX / CREATE UNIQUE INDEX SQL contains qualified column names and triggers SQLite's "the '.' operator prohibited in index expressions" OperationalError.

Summary of the incorrect assumption pattern: rename_table_references assumes it is always correct to set a table alias on Col objects during a table rename. In contrast, the index/unique/constraint DDL generation explicitly constructs compilers/queries with alias_cols=False to indicate expressions should compile without table qualification. The mismatch between unconditional aliasing and the alias_cols=False intent leads to generation of SQL incompatible with SQLite's index-expression rules.
StepsToReproduce: Create an initial migration that creates the model and adds the UniqueConstraint (value max_length=200 in initial migration).
Create a second migration that alters the field (e.g. change value max_length from 200 to 150).
Using the sqlite backend, run: python manage.py migrate
Observe the migration fails during the table-remake step for the alter_field migration with an OperationalError from SQLite.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The migration completes successfully: the field alteration is applied, the table is remade if necessary, and any unique/index/constraint DDL is emitted and executed in a form compatible with SQLite (i.e. index expressions do not contain a '.' operator).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: During migrate, the alter_field migration fails with sqlite OperationalError: "the '.' operator prohibited in index expressions". The failure is caused by deferred CREATE INDEX / CREATE UNIQUE INDEX SQL that includes table-qualified column identifiers (a dot operator) produced after expressions were renamed/aliased.

Thanks for the report! Looks like we don't check if an alias is set on the Col before we update it to new_table in Expressions.rename_table_references when running _remake_table.

problem_statement.Title: RenameModel with db_table should be a noop
problem_statement.Description: A RenameModel migration that only renames the model class but the model Meta.db_table is explicitly set (so the effective DB table name does not change) should not perform any database schema changes. Currently RenameModel triggers schema edits: on Postgres it drops and recreates foreign key constraints; on SQLite it recreates the table.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) django/db/migrations/operations/models.py::RenameModel.database_forwards — the operation unconditionally performs database schema calls for a model rename. It calls schema_editor.alter_db_table(...) and then proceeds to iterate related objects and local many-to-many fields, invoking schema_editor.alter_field(...) and schema_editor.alter_db_table(...) for M2M through models, regardless of whether the effective DB table name actually changes. (See models.py: database_forwards flow around lines ~319–369.)

2) django/db/backends/base/schema.py::BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor.alter_db_table — alter_db_table itself already treats identical table names as a no-op: it returns early when old_db_table == new_db_table or when case-insensitive backends have equal lowercased names. However, RenameModel relies on alter_db_table but continues to perform related-field alterations even when alter_db_table would be a no-op. (See base/schema.py: alter_db_table early-return around lines ~468–473.)

3) django/db/backends/base/schema.py::BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor._field_should_be_altered and _alter_field/alter_field — the decision to alter a field is based on comparing old_field.deconstruct() and new_field.deconstruct() (with some non-database attributes removed). When alter_field runs it may drop and later recreate foreign-key constraints as part of the alteration (the implementation explicitly drops FK constraints and incoming FK constraints in _alter_field). Field instance differences that arise from using different ModelState/App registries can make deconstruct() results differ even when the database-level table name is effectively unchanged, causing destructive schema work. (See base/schema.py: _field_should_be_altered lines ~1122–1149 and _alter_field FK-dropping logic around lines ~624–674.)

4) django/db/migrations/operations/models.py::AlterModelTable — AlterModelTable is the explicit migration operation that changes a model's db_table and maps to schema_editor.alter_db_table(...) and M2M table renames. This shows the intended separation of responsibilities: AlterModelTable for db_table changes, and RenameModel should be a state-only rename when the effective db_table value does not change. (See models.py: AlterModelTable.database_forwards around lines ~436–455.)

5) Effective-noop decision formula and semantics — whether a RenameModel should be a no-op must be determined by comparing the effective database table names from state: old_db_table = old_model._meta.db_table and new_db_table = new_model._meta.db_table, using the same case-insensitivity semantics as BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor.alter_db_table: treat as identical when old_db_table == new_db_table or (schema_editor.connection.features.ignores_table_name_case and old_db_table.lower() == new_db_table.lower()). The relationship between these values is the correct basis for deciding that no database schema work is necessary.

6) Investigation gaps remaining to validate behavior across all cases before any code change: tests and the migration autodetector behavior that might create RenameModel with explicit db_table were not reviewed; concrete tracing of which specific Field.deconstruct() attribute differences (between from_state and to_state Field instances) trigger alter_field's destructive paths was not exhaustively performed; and migration/test coverage for case-insensitive backends or other backend-specific corner cases should be reviewed to ensure the noop decision and semantics hold in all supported backends.
StepsToReproduce: Create a Django app with a model that sets Meta.db_table explicitly (e.g. class MyModel(Model): class Meta: db_table = 'custom_table').
Create a migration that renames the model class (RenameModel) but leaves Meta.db_table unchanged (old_name -> new_name).
Run the migration against a Postgres database and observe schema activity (or run against SQLite and observe schema activity).
Observe that on Postgres foreign key constraints are dropped and recreated; on SQLite the table is recreated.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Applying a RenameModel migration where the model's Meta.db_table is unchanged should be a no-op at the database level: no tables should be recreated or renamed, and no foreign-key constraints should be dropped or recreated. The migration should only update migration/state metadata.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: RenameModel triggers database schema changes despite db_table being unchanged: on Postgres foreign key constraints are dropped and recreated; on SQLite the table is recreated.

problem_statement.Title: MultiWidget.id_for_label appends '_0' causing parent <label> to point at first subwidget
problem_statement.Description: MultiWidget.id_for_label appends the suffix '_0' to the base id and returns it, so a form field whose widget is a MultiWidget causes the top-level <label for="..."> to reference the first subwidget instead of omitting the for attribute.
StepsToReproduce: Create a Django Form with a field whose widget subclasses MultiWidget (i.e., a multi-part widget) and ensure the widget receives an id (use auto_id or set attrs['id']).
Render the field's label (e.g., call field.label_tag() or render the form in a template).
Inspect the rendered <label> element: observe the for attribute value.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: For a MultiWidget-based field, the top-level <label> should not include a for attribute (the label should omit for="...") while each subwidget input should keep unique indexed ids like '<baseid>_0', '<baseid>_1', etc.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The top-level <label> contains a for attribute whose value is the base id suffixed with '_0' (e.g., for="id_field_0"), so the label points to the first subwidget instead of omitting the for attribute.

hints_text:
I agree that we should remove for from MultiWidget's <label> but not because "It has not sense" but to improve accessibility when using a screen reader, see also #32338. It should be enough to return an empty string: def id_for_label(self, id_): return ''

problem_statement.Title: forms.URLField.clean raises ValueError("Invalid IPv6 URL") instead of ValidationError
problem_statement.Description: A ValueError("Invalid IPv6 URL") raised by urllib.parse.urlsplit can propagate out of django.core.validators.URLValidator.__call__ instead of being converted to a django.core.exceptions.ValidationError. URLValidator.__call__ calls urlsplit(value) in multiple places; some calls are guarded, but others (used for IPv6/netloc detection and hostname-length checks) are unguarded and may let urllib.parse.urlsplit.ValueError escape into callers (including form or model validation paths that invoke the validator). Note: django/forms/fields.URLField.to_python already wraps urlsplit in try/except and converts ValueError into ValidationError, so whether forms.URLField.clean triggers an escaping ValueError depends on the validation flow and which urlsplit call raises the error.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Inconsistent exception-safety assumption in django/core/validators.py :: URLValidator.__call__: the method assumes urlsplit(value) will not raise ValueError in later validation checks (IPv6 detection and hostname-length checks). Evidence: URLValidator.__call__ contains a guarded urlsplit inside the IDN-handling branch (it catches ValueError there) but also contains unguarded calls that directly use urlsplit(value).netloc and urlsplit(value).hostname (see code locations below). These unguarded calls allow urllib.parse.urlsplit(ValueError("Invalid IPv6 URL")) to escape as a ValueError instead of being translated into a ValidationError.

2) Repeated, un-reused urlsplit invocations in URLValidator.__call__: the method invokes urlsplit(value) multiple times for different checks rather than obtaining a single SplitResult and reusing it. Examples in the validator: host_match = re.search(..., urlsplit(value).netloc) and if len(urlsplit(value).hostname) > 253:. Making multiple independent urlsplit calls increases the surface where urlsplit may raise ValueError and demonstrates the inconsistent handling of parsing errors.

3) Inconsistency between validator and field-level parsing: django/forms/fields.py :: URLField.to_python (split_url) already wraps urlsplit(url) in try/except and converts ValueError into ValidationError; the validator does not replicate that defensive behavior in all places. This inconsistency means that some call paths (e.g., code that directly invokes the validator, or flows that reach the validator without earlier urlsplit-triggered errors) can observe a ValueError escaping, while the field's own parsing will convert such parse errors to ValidationError.

4) API surface affected: URLValidator is used directly as a default validator for models.URLField (django/db/models/fields/__init__.py :: URLField.default_validators = [validators.URLValidator()]) and is also used by form fields during validators execution. Therefore, an uncaught ValueError in the validator can affect both model and form validation paths depending on execution order and which urlsplit invocation fails.

5) Required relationships identified by the investigation (diagnostic observations, not a code patch): the validator's checks rely on attributes of a parsed URL (e.g., netloc, hostname). The investigation shows these relationships should be treated as follows: obtain a single SplitResult from urlsplit(value) and reuse its attributes (split_result.netloc, split_result.hostname) for subsequent checks; when an IDN/punycode normalization branch reconstructs a normalized URL, parsing should operate on the normalized URL; any ValueError raised by urlsplit represents a malformed input that the validator should report as a ValidationError (i.e., an error in the validator's public API contract), not as an uncaught ValueError. The investigation did not produce a single-line trace identifying which urlsplit call raises ValueError for the sample input, so the exact failing invocation in this input remains to be confirmed.
StepsToReproduce: In a Python environment with this Django codebase, reproduce by calling the URL validator directly or via a model/form validator path that reaches the validator's unguarded parsing checks.

Example direct invocation: from django.core.validators import URLValidator; URLValidator()('////]@N.AN') — observe whether a ValueError('Invalid IPv6 URL') from urllib.parse.urlsplit is raised instead of django.core.exceptions.ValidationError.

Example form path: from django import forms; forms.URLField().clean('////]@N.AN') — depending on the code path and whether URLField.to_python or URLValidator.__call__'s unguarded urlsplit is reached first, a ValueError may propagate. (Note: URLField.to_python itself catches urlsplit.ValueError; therefore an escaping ValueError depends on execution reaching the validator's unguarded calls.)

Observe the traceback: if ValueError escapes, stack will include urllib.parse.urlsplit -> django.core.validators.URLValidator.__call__ -> django.forms.fields.Field.run_validators -> Field.clean (or direct validator invocation).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Validator and field validation paths should surface malformed URLs to callers as django.core.exceptions.ValidationError (using the validator's message and code), not as a raw ValueError from urllib.parse. Form and model validation should consistently present validation errors via ValidationError.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Under some execution flows, urllib.parse.urlsplit raises ValueError('Invalid IPv6 URL') during URLValidator.__call__ and that ValueError propagates out of the validator into caller code (instead of being translated to ValidationError). The stack trace shows urllib.parse.urlsplit as the origin of the uncaught exception and URLValidator.__call__ as the Django call site that does not convert it.

problem_statement.Title: ExpressionWrapper(~Q(pk__in=[])) produces malformed SELECT (empty left-hand) leading to invalid SQL
problem_statement.Description: Annotating a queryset with ExpressionWrapper(~Q(pk__in=[]), output_field=BooleanField()) generates invalid SQL: the SELECT list contains an empty expression (e.g. "SELECT AS \"foo\" ...") instead of a valid boolean expression or literal, causing the query to be syntactically invalid.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) ExpressionWrapper.as_sql forwards the inner expression's compiled SQL unchanged. In django/db/models/expressions.py, ExpressionWrapper.as_sql is implemented as: return compiler.compile(self.expression). This assumes the compiled result is always a valid SQL fragment for use in a SELECT clause; that assumption is incorrect when the inner expression is a WhereNode that uses sentinel return values.

2) WhereNode.as_sql documents and implements sentinel semantics intended for WHERE context. The WhereNode.as_sql docstring and implementation treat certain outcomes specially: the docstring states that a node that "matches everything" yields '', [], an empty node may be represented as None, [], and a node that "can't match anything" raises EmptyResultSet. The method's logic returns '' when a negated node's children lead to the "matches everything" condition, and raises EmptyResultSet when the node "can't match anything." These sentinel values are meaningful for WHERE clauses but are not universally valid SQL fragments for SELECT clauses.

3) SQLCompiler.get_select expects compile(...) to either raise EmptyResultSet or return a usable SQL fragment; it special-cases the exception case but does not special-case a returned empty-string SQL. In django/db/models/sql/compiler.py:get_select, when compiler.compile(col) raises EmptyResultSet the compiler substitutes a fallback (for example the hardcoded '0' when empty_result_set_value is NotImplemented). However, if compile(col) returns ('', params) (the WhereNode "matches everything" sentinel), get_select does not treat that as a special case and forwards the empty sql through col.select_format into the SELECT list, which can produce an empty left-hand expression ("SELECT AS alias").

4) The triggering chain for this user-observable failure begins in the In lookup for empty RHS. In django/db/models/lookups.py:In.process_rhs, an empty RHS (e.g. pk__in=[]) causes the lookup to raise EmptyResultSet. For an unnegated Q(pk__in=[]), that EmptyResultSet is observed by SQLCompiler.get_select and results in the fallback '0' being used in the SELECT (producing SELECT 0 AS "foo"). For the negated ~Q(pk__in=[]), WhereNode.as_sql converts the child EmptyResultSet condition into the "matches everything" sentinel ('' , []), and ExpressionWrapper.as_sql forwards that '' into the SELECT context unchanged, producing a malformed SELECT column (empty left-hand expression).

5) In short: a set of context-dependent sentinel conventions (EmptyResultSet exception vs. special returned SQL strings like '') used by WhereNode.as_sql are not translated when a WhereNode is used as a wrapped expression in a SELECT context. The compiler has a fallback for the exception case (EmptyResultSet -> '0') but no corresponding handling for the '' sentinel returned by WhereNode in negated/match-everything cases; as a result, an ExpressionWrapper around such a WhereNode can cause invalid SQL to be generated in SELECT clauses.
StepsToReproduce: Create a queryset for a model (for example, Model.objects.all()).
Annotate with a boolean ExpressionWrapper of a negated empty-IN condition, for example: queryset.annotate(foo=ExpressionWrapper(~Q(pk__in=[]), output_field=BooleanField())).values('foo').query
Print or execute the generated SQL (e.g. print(queryset.annotate(...).values('foo').query)) and observe the generated SQL.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Annotating with ExpressionWrapper(~Q(pk__in=[]), output_field=BooleanField()) should produce a valid SQL SELECT list for the annotation column, i.e. a syntactically valid SQL expression or boolean literal (for example, SELECT 1 AS "foo" or an equivalent DB-specific TRUE literal) instead of an empty expression.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The generated SQL is malformed with an empty left-hand expression for the SELECT column, for example: SELECT AS "foo" FROM "table", which is syntactically invalid.

+                return self._create_project_state(with_applied_migrations=False)

+            self.recorder.ensure_schema()

             plan = self.migration_plan(targets)
