problem_statement.Title: MigrationRecorder ignores DB router and creates/updates django_migrations on disallowed DBs
problem_statement.Description: MigrationRecorder (django/db/migrations/recorder.py) creates the django_migrations table and reads/writes/deletes migration rows on any database connection it is instantiated with, ignoring the project's DB router (router.allow_migrate). This causes migration recording and table creation or mutation on databases the router intends to exclude.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) MigrationRecorder.ensure_schema() assumes it may create the django_migrations table on any connection and calls schema_editor.create_model(self.Migration) without consulting the DB router. (Location: django/db/migrations/recorder.py::MigrationRecorder.ensure_schema — lines 63-69 show the unconditional create_model call.)

2) MigrationRecorder.applied_migrations() uses the existence of the django_migrations table (self.has_table()) as the sole condition for returning applied migration rows and does not consult router.allow_migrate, so it can report migrations as present/read from DBs the router forbids. (Location: django/db/migrations/recorder.py::MigrationRecorder.applied_migrations — lines 72-82.)

3) MigrationRecorder.record_applied() and record_unapplied() call ensure_schema() and then perform create/delete operations on the migration table unconditionally, without consulting the DB router, causing table creation and mutation on disallowed DBs. (Location: django/db/migrations/recorder.py::MigrationRecorder.record_applied / MigrationRecorder.record_unapplied — lines 84-93.)

4) MigrationRecorder.flush() performs an unconditional delete of all migration records via self.migration_qs.all().delete(), and does not consult the DB router (note: flush() does not call ensure_schema()). This means flush can mutate the migration table on any connection the recorder uses. (Location: django/db/migrations/recorder.py::MigrationRecorder.flush — lines 94-96.)

5) The nested Migration model inside MigrationRecorder hardcodes Meta.app_label = 'migrations' and db_table = 'django_migrations'. Any router decision regarding whether the 'migrations' app may be migrated on a connection must use this app_label/model. (Location: django/db/migrations/recorder.py::MigrationRecorder.Migration — lines 35-39.)

6) Callers construct and use MigrationRecorder (self.recorder = MigrationRecorder(self.connection)) and call recorder.applied_migrations(), recorder.record_applied(...), recorder.record_unapplied(...), and recorder.ensure_schema() without additional router checks, so the recorder's omission causes migration bookkeeping and schema actions on disallowed DBs. (Locations: django/db/migrations/executor.py::MigrationExecutor (recorder construction and calls, e.g. lines 19, 98-101, 247-254, 255-266, 283) and django/db/migrations/loader.py::MigrationLoader (build_graph() uses recorder.applied_migrations() at lines 222-224).)

7) The exact router call identified by the investigation that is expected to be consulted when deciding to create/read/write/delete migration state is: router.allow_migrate(self.connection.alias, self.Migration._meta.app_label, model_name=self.Migration._meta.model_name, model=self.Migration). The recorder currently does not perform this check before creating the table, reading applied rows, writing rows, or deleting rows. (Related example of canonical router usage found in django/db/migrations/executor.py::MigrationExecutor.detect_soft_applied at lines 300-306.)

8) Investigation gaps: no runtime test was executed to confirm behavior differences when router.allow_migrate returns None vs False, and no unit test was added to verify that no django_migrations table is created or mutated when allow_migrate returns False for the 'migrations' app. (General test/runtime verification missing.)
StepsToReproduce: Configure a Django project with multiple DATABASES (one 'default' and at least one secondary connection).
Create a DB router that returns True only for 'default', e.g. Router.allow_migrate(self, db, model): return db == 'default'.
Add standard managed migrations for the project (normal migrations that would create tables).
Run the test runner (python manage.py test) or otherwise trigger test DB creation so Django calls create_test_db for each connection.
Observe that the django_migrations table is created and migration records are written (or that deletes occur via flush) on the secondary (router-disallowed) databases.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Django should only create the django_migrations table and record applied/unapplied migrations (or perform test-specific flushes) on database connections for which the project's DB router allows migrations. On connections where router.allow_migrate returns False (or otherwise disallows the 'migrations' app), MigrationRecorder should not create the table, should report no applied migrations, and should not write or delete migration rows.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: During test DB creation (and when migrations run against non-default connections), MigrationRecorder creates the django_migrations table and reads/writes/deletes migration records on every connection it is instantiated with, regardless of DB router decisions. This results in migration tables and records (or test cleanup deletes) appearing on databases the router intended to exclude.

hints_text:
I don't think you've implemented your router correctly, but I'd need to check the router code to see if it's called multiple times (num_dbs*num_models) to be sure. This is how we implement our routers for allow_migrate: def allow_migrate(self, db, model): if db == 'other': return model._meta.app_label == 'legacy_app' # allow migration for new django models implemented in legacy db elif model._meta.app_label == 'legacy_app': # do not allow migration for legacy on any other db return False return None # this router not responsible So, I'm not sure if there is a bug or not (I'll let someone more familiar answer that), but this is what works for us.
#22583 is somewhat related. It deals with the inability to skip RunSQL/RunPython operations on given database.
@jarshwah: I don't think it is the router. Mainly because the router is not called at this point. Which is what I believe is the bug. @akaariai: I agree that there are similarities. Surely I should be able to manage which connections I actually run migrations against. That seems to be what the db_router is trying to do. I thought that something like the following would solve our problem: from django.db import router . . . def ensure_schema(self): """ Ensures the table exists and has the correct schema. """ # If the table's there, that's fine - we've never changed its schema # in the codebase. if self.Migration._meta.db_table in self.connection.introspection.get_table_list(self.connection.cursor()): return # Make the table # Change below, similar to how allowed_to_migrate in django/db/migrations/operations/base.py works if router.allow_migrate(self.connection, self.Migration): with self.connection.schema_editor() as editor: editor.create_model(self.Migration) But this means that changes to applied_migrations, record_applied, and record_unapplied need to be made, so that it doesn't try to write to a none existent table. For us this is not an urgent issue, as we have appropriate permissions to those connections that are not our core connection. But I do have a concern for any time that we are using a true read-only connection, where we do not have the CREATE TABLE permission. Because this code, as it exists, will blow up in this situation. I tested that with a read-only connection in our db setup, and got an insufficient permissions error. Thanks, Dylan
This is an issue, but bear in mind that migrate must be separately executed for each database alias, so this isn't going to happen unless you specifically run migrate on a database that you know isn't supposed to have migrations on it. I think the best fix would be to have migrations refuse to run entirely and just exit if the migration model is not allowed to be created on the target database.
I see what you mean about the needing to run migarte for each database. I noticed this with the django test runner, where it is trying to run a migration against every connection alias that we have. So that might be an issue with the test runner as much as with the migrations stuff. Thanks, Dylan
Just stumbled on this issue. With a properly written router, I expected the migrations of each app to be executed in the right database by just using : manage.py migrate Could this be the behavior ? Instead it's assuming I'm using the default database, OK, no, but ok :-)... and it doesn't follow the allow_migrate rule and creates in the default database the tables that are supposed to live exclusively in the another one (NOT OK !). So for now the workaround for me is to use a shell script where the app and the database are specified.: ./manage.py migrate inapppurchase --database=iap ./manage.py migrate myapp --database=default
dperetti: Just like syncdb, migrate only runs on one database at a time, so you must execute it individually for each database, as you suggest. This is by design. froomzy: Yes, this is likely a test runner issue, that would be doing this kind of migrate-on-everything. I think the suggested fix of refusing to migrate databases where allow_migrate on the migration model returns False will still work, as long as it errors in a way we can catch in the test runner.
Replying to andrewgodwin: dperetti: Just like syncdb, migrate only runs on one database at a time, so you must execute it individually for each database, as you suggest. This is by design. The question is : is it the best design ? When I run migrate, I don't want to know about how the router is configured. I just want to migrate the app. If the router dispatches the tables of an app to different databases, then I want migrate to operate on those. In other words, I think it would make sense to make migrate database agnostic.
Another side issue : we cannot just manage.py migrate someapp if the someapp is a "django <1.7" app without migration : the old syncdb behavior is not applied when an application is specified. So if want the old apps to sync, I have to just run manage.py migrate, without argument... which will create unwanted tables when we have multiple databases.
Hi guys, Just wondering if there is any chance this will make it into 1.8? Or soon there after? Thanks, Dylan
It's not clear to me what fixing this issue involves exactly. Anyway, it doesn't appear anyone is working on it so there's no timetable for its resolution.
I wanted to chime in here to broaden the scope of the bug, as I was affected by it recently in a different context. The bigger issue is not just that the framework tries to create the migrations table where it's not needed, but that it marks as applied migrations that in fact were not. That puts the database in an inconsistent state, at least as far as migrations are concerned. It's a harmless inconsistency for the specific settings file used at that specific moment in time, but it lays the seed for big problems down the line. For example, if you later decide to change your routing scheme, or (as happened in my case) if you have multiple projects with different settings using the same app on the same database. In terms of a solution, what seems straightforward to my untrained eye is for the migration framework to simply not record migrations as applied that it didn't apply (and as a corollary, it shouldn't try to create the migration table on a database if it doesn't need to record any migrations there). The fix suggested above ("to have migrations refuse to run entirely and just exit if the migration model is not allowed to be created on the target database") doesn't address the broader issue.
Let me amend my last comment: I think that having migrate blow up in this situation would in fact solve the problem with having an inconsistent migrations table, which is the most important thing. My question is, since allow_migrate() operates at the model level and the migrate command operates at the app level, wouldn't this make it impossible to migrate some models of an app but not others?
I can confirm marfire's findings. 1/ For example even with this router applied: class testerouter(object): def allow_migrate(self, db, app_label, model_name=None, **hints): return False And then executing: migrate --database=replica All apps and models migrations get applied to this replica database, while according to the router nothing should happen at all. 2/ From the documentation it is not clear whether it is possible to isolate certain models from certain databases, or whether isolation can only be done on the app-level. From the description in the documentation, it seems possible to use the model_name argument for this, but by the way django stores its migrations, I don't see how that could work.
Just to further confirm: I've been wrestling with this problem for a couple days. I have multiple databases and multiple apps. When I'm running tests, the router allow_migrate works properly to control which migrations can be applied to which database/app. The actual migration portion works fine, but when it comes time to actually write the migration history, it seems to only have a concept of the default database. When it runs ensure_schema, it's expecting that tables should exist on the default DB when they really only exist for certain other apps/databases. This leads to a ProgrammingError where it can't find tables that it shouldn't be checking for in the first place.
Can you please have a look at ​http://stackoverflow.com/questions/40893868/using-redshift-as-an-additional-django-database?noredirect=1#comment69004673_40893868, which provides a decent use case for this bug.
Since 3.1 you can set 'TEST': {'MIGRATE': False} to avoid running migrations on a given db connection, so that solves the test runner issue. Still, even if you do that, apps are still synced (see fix to #32012), Django ends up calling migrate to do the syncing, and this will cause queries in MigrationRecorder.ensure_schema() that might create tables (or fail with permission errors, see #27141). I plan to open a PR to do roughly this from comment:13: it shouldn't try to create the migration table on a database if it doesn't need to record any migrations there

patch:
diff --git a/django/db/models/expressions.py b/django/db/models/expressions.py
--- a/django/db/models/expressions.py
+++ b/django/db/models/expressions.py
@@ -1149,7 +1149,8 @@ class Subquery(BaseExpression, Combinable):
 
     def __init__(self, queryset, output_field=None, **extra):
         # Allow the usage of both QuerySet and sql.Query objects.
-        self.query = getattr(queryset, 'query', queryset)
+        self.query = getattr(queryset, 'query', queryset).clone()
+        self.query.subquery = True
         self.extra = extra
         super().__init__(output_field)

problem_statement.Title: Subquery.as_sql unconditionally strips first/last characters of inner SQL, producing invalid SQL
problem_statement.Description: Subquery.as_sql in django/db/models/expressions.py assumes the inner query SQL is parenthesized and unconditionally removes the first and last characters of the inner SQL string. When the inner Query.as_sql does not return a parenthesized string, this slicing removes real SQL characters (e.g. the leading 'S' of 'SELECT'), producing malformed SQL fragments.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. Unconditional slicing in Subquery.as_sql (django/db/models/expressions.py :: Subquery.as_sql): the method calls query.as_sql(...) and then sets template_params['subquery'] = subquery_sql[1:-1], removing the first and last characters of the returned SQL string. This code assumes the returned SQL is surrounded by parentheses and will strip them, but if the inner SQL is not parenthesized the slice removes real SQL characters.

2. Subquery.__init__ stores the incoming Query/QuerySet.query without enforcing the subquery parenthesization contract (django/db/models/expressions.py :: Subquery.__init__): the constructor does self.query = getattr(queryset, 'query', queryset) and does not set any subquery flag on that Query nor clone it. Because Query.as_sql only wraps SQL in parentheses when Query.subquery is True, leaving self.query unflagged means query.as_sql may return unparenthesized SQL which the Subquery.as_sql slicing then corrupts.

3. Subquery.set_source_expressions reassigns the inner query without establishing the subquery invariant (django/db/models/expressions.py :: Subquery.set_source_expressions): set_source_expressions assigns self.query = exprs[0] with no clone or subquery flag, allowing an unflagged Query to reach Subquery.as_sql and be subject to the unsafe slice.

4. Subquery.as_sql relies on the Query.as_sql parenthesization contract (django/db/models/sql/query.py :: Query.as_sql): Query.as_sql only wraps SQL with '(%s)' when Query.subquery is True (if self.subquery: sql = '(%s)' % sql). The Subquery implementation assumes that contract is already satisfied by the Query it calls, but Subquery.__init__ and set_source_expressions do not ensure it.

5. Call sites can provide unparenthesized queries (e.g. Exists.as_sql) and thus trigger the problem (django/db/models/expressions.py :: Exists.as_sql): Exists.as_sql passes query = self.query.exists(...) into Subquery.as_sql. Query.exists constructs a Query modified for EXISTS but does not set subquery=True, so the Query passed to Subquery.as_sql may not be parenthesized before the unconditional slice is applied.
StepsToReproduce: from django.db import connection
from apps.models import App
from django.db.models.expressions import Subquery
q = Subquery(App.objects.all())
print(str(q.query))
print(q.as_sql(q.query.get_compiler('default'), connection))
q.query.subquery = True
print(q.as_sql(q.query.get_compiler('default'), connection))
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Subquery.as_sql(...) returns a valid, parenthesized SQL fragment for the inner query, e.g. ('(SELECT "apps_app"."id", "apps_app"."name" FROM "apps_app")', ()).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Subquery.as_sql(...) can return malformed SQL with the first and/or last characters removed from the inner query, e.g. ('(ELECT "apps_app"."id", "apps_app"."name" FROM "apps_app)', ()), where the leading 'S' from 'SELECT' and the final '"' are missing. Explicitly setting q.query.subquery = True before calling as_sql makes the inner Query.as_sql return a parenthesized SQL and produces the correct result.

patch:
diff --git a/django/contrib/messages/storage/cookie.py b/django/contrib/messages/storage/cookie.py
--- a/django/contrib/messages/storage/cookie.py
+++ b/django/contrib/messages/storage/cookie.py
@@ -19,7 +19,7 @@ def default(self, obj):
             # Using 0/1 here instead of False/True to produce more compact json
             is_safedata = 1 if isinstance(obj.message, SafeData) else 0
             message = [self.message_key, is_safedata, obj.level, obj.message]
-            if obj.extra_tags:
+            if obj.extra_tags is not None:
                 message.append(obj.extra_tags)
             return message

problem_statement.Title: Messages: extra_tags empty string lost during serialization ('' -> None on decode)
problem_statement.Description: When a Message with extra_tags == "" is serialized and then deserialized using the built-in storage backends, extra_tags is converted to None. The encoder omits empty-string extra_tags from the serialized payload (by testing its truthiness) and the decoder reconstructs the Message without an extra_tags argument, causing the value to become None.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) django/contrib/messages/storage/cookie.py:MessageEncoder.default — The encoder builds the serialized list as [message_key, is_safedata, level, message] and uses a truthiness test (if obj.extra_tags) to decide whether to append extra_tags (cookie.py lines 21-23). This treats the empty string ('') as falsy and results in extra_tags being omitted from the serialized payload (incorrect assumption encoded here: falsy == absent).

2) django/contrib/messages/storage/cookie.py:MessageDecoder.process_messages — The decoder recognizes the serialized shape and reconstructs Message instances with positional unpacking via Message(*obj[2:]) (cookie.py line 38). When the serialized form omits the fifth element, Message is called without an extra_tags argument and Message.__init__'s default extra_tags=None is applied; combined with the encoder omission, this produces the observed '' -> None conversion.

3) django/contrib/messages/storage/base.py:Message.__init__ and Message._prepare — Message.__init__ sets extra_tags default to None (base.py line 14). Message._prepare converts extra_tags to str only if it is not None (base.py line 25), indicating that the codebase treats None (absent) and the empty string ('') as distinct and that an empty-string extra_tags is a valid value that should be preserved by serialization.

4) Storage backend scope: django/contrib/messages/storage/session.py, cookie.py and fallback.py — Both SessionStorage and CookieStorage rely on the same encoder/decoder logic (session.py lines 44-49; cookie.py MessageSerializer uses MessageEncoder/MessageDecoder on lines 51-59), and FallbackStorage composes CookieStorage and SessionStorage (fallback.py line 11). Therefore the encoder's truthiness-based omission impacts all standard built-in storage backends and the common API paths for messages.

5) Test coverage observation — The repository's message tests reference extra_tags values (e.g., non-empty strings and None) but do not include an explicit assertion that a Message constructed with extra_tags == '' serializes and deserializes back to extra_tags == '' (tests/messages_tests/*). This absence is a test-coverage gap relevant to the behavioral contract between Message, the encoder, and the decoder.
StepsToReproduce: from django.conf import settings
settings.configure()
from django.contrib.messages.storage.base import Message
from django.contrib.messages.storage.cookie import MessageEncoder, MessageDecoder
original_message = Message(10, 'Here is a message', extra_tags='')
encoded_message = MessageEncoder().encode(original_message)
decoded_message = MessageDecoder().decode(encoded_message)
assert original_message.extra_tags == ''
assert decoded_message.extra_tags is None
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: After serializing and deserializing a Message whose extra_tags == "", the decoded Message.extra_tags remains the empty string (''), preserving the original value.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: A Message with extra_tags == "" is serialized without an extra_tags entry and, after deserialization, decoded_message.extra_tags is None instead of an empty string.

problem_statement.Title: Dev server autoreloader stops restarting when BASE_DIR added to TEMPLATES[0]['DIRS']
problem_statement.Description: Adding the project root (BASE_DIR) to TEMPLATES[0]['DIRS'] can cause the Django development autoreloader to stop restarting on file saves anywhere in the project. The template-change handler treats many non-template files as handled template changes, which prevents the autoreloader from performing a full restart.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) django/template/autoreload.py::template_changed assumes that any file whose path is inside a configured template directory can be handled as a template change. Location: django/template/autoreload.py::template_changed (lines 49–54). Manifestation: for each template_dir, if template_dir in file_path.parents it calls reset_loaders() and returns True.

2) django/template/autoreload.py::get_template_directories normalizes configured template directories relative to the current working directory using cwd = Path.cwd() and items.update(cwd / to_path(dir) for dir in backend.engine.dirs). Location: django/template/autoreload.py::get_template_directories (lines 16–23). Manifestation: when an absolute project root (e.g., BASE_DIR) or the working directory is present in TEMPLATES['DIRS'], that directory can be an ancestor of most project files and therefore included in the returned set.

3) django/template/autoreload.py::watch_for_template_changes registers watchers using the glob '**/*' for each template directory. Location: django/template/autoreload.py::watch_for_template_changes (lines 43–46). Manifestation: the reloader watches all files under configured template directories, not just typical template file name patterns.

4) django/utils/autoreload.py::BaseReloader.notify_file_changed treats any truthy return value from file_changed receivers as "handled" and suppresses trigger_reload(path). Location: django/utils/autoreload.py::BaseReloader.notify_file_changed (lines 360–365). Manifestation: if any receiver (including template_changed) returns a truthy value, the reloader will not perform the full restart for that file change.

5) Combined effect: when BASE_DIR (or Path.cwd()) is included in TEMPLATES['DIRS'], files such as Python source files become children of a configured template directory. template_changed's membership check therefore matches these non-template files, calls reset_loaders(), and returns True — which, per notify_file_changed's semantics, prevents a full autoreload. Location: interaction between django/template/autoreload.py::get_template_directories, django/template/autoreload.py::template_changed, django/template/autoreload.py::watch_for_template_changes, and django/utils/autoreload.py::BaseReloader.notify_file_changed.

6) Test coverage gap: tests/template_tests/test_autoreloader.py does not include a test asserting that a .py file inside a configured TEMPLATES['DIRS'] is not classified as a template change. Existing tests cover templates and .py files outside template dirs but not .py files inside a configured template dir (see tests/template_tests/test_autoreloader.py lines 37–40 and normalization tests lines 67–85).

7) Investigation omissions / environment notes: the Watchman-based reloader paths and different file extensions (e.g., .pyc, .pyo, other custom template extensions) were not exhaustively exercised. The file_changed signal semantics are the same across reloader implementations, but behavior under different watchers or unusual file types may need separate review. Locations to review if relevant: django/utils/autoreload.py (WatchmanReloader code paths) and any template backends/loaders that expose nonstandard directories or file extensions.
StepsToReproduce: pip install -U django
django-admin startproject <name>
Open settings.py. Copy the BASE_DIR variable (project root Path) and add it into TEMPLATES[0]['DIRS'] (for example, add BASE_DIR to the DIRS list).
./manage.py runserver
In your editor/IDE, save any project file (for example, edit and save a Python source file).
Observe that the development server does NOT restart automatically.
(Optional) Remove BASE_DIR from TEMPLATES[0]['DIRS'], restart the dev server, then re-save a file to confirm autoreload resumes.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The development server autoreloader should detect saves to project files (including Python source files) and restart automatically. Adding the project root or other directories to TEMPLATES['DIRS'] should not prevent code changes from triggering a full autoreload.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: When BASE_DIR (the project root) is added to TEMPLATES[0]['DIRS'], saving files anywhere in the project does not cause the dev server to restart automatically. The server only restarts after a manual restart or after removing BASE_DIR from the template DIRS.

hints_text:
I don't think this is a bug, really. Adding BASE_DIR to the list of template directories causes the entire project directory to be marked as a template directory, and Django does not watch for changes in template directories by design.
I think I encountered this recently while making examples for #33461, though I didn't get fully to the bottom of what was going on. Django does not watch for changes in template directories by design. It does, via the template_changed signal listener, which from my brief poking around when I saw it, is I believe the one which prevented trigger_reload from executing. But that mostly led to my realising I don't know what function is responsible for reloading for python files, rather than template/i18n files, so I moved on. I would tentatively accept this, personally.
Replying to Keryn Knight: Django does not watch for changes in template directories by design. It does, via the template_changed signal listener My bad, I meant that Django does not watch for changes in template directories to reload the server. The template_changed signal listener returns True if the change occurs in a file located in a designated template directory, which causes notify_file_changed to not trigger the reload. AFAIK from browsing the code, for a python file (or actually any file not in a template directory), the template_changed signal listener returns None, which causes notify_file_changed to trigger the reload, right? So could we fix this by checking if the changed file is a python file inside the template_changed signal listener, regardless of whether it is in a template directory? def template_changed(sender, file_path, **kwargs): if file_path.suffix == '.py': return # Now check if the file was a template file This seems to work on a test project, but I have not checked for side effects, although I don't think there should be any.
I would tentatively accept this, personally. 😀 I was thinking I'd tentatively wontfix, as not worth the complication — but let's accept for review and see what the consensus is. Hrushikesh, would you like to prepare a PR based on your suggestion? Thanks!

problem_statement.Title: LazyObject/SimpleLazyObject missing reflected operator methods (e.g. __radd__)
problem_statement.Description: LazyObject (and therefore SimpleLazyObject and its subclasses) in django/utils/functional.py defines a small, hardcoded set of proxied special methods via new_method_proxy but omits reflected binary/bitwise/unary operator special methods (notably __radd__). As a result, expressions where a SimpleLazyObject appears on the right-hand side (for example other + SimpleLazyObject(...)) can fail when the left operand's __add__ returns NotImplemented because the proxy does not provide the reflected operator dispatch.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) LazyObject's design encodes the assumption that only a small, explicit set of magic methods must be proxied at the class level. The class-level proxied methods in django/utils/functional.py are limited (examples: __bytes__, __str__, __bool__, __dir__, __class__ property, __eq__, __lt__, __gt__, __ne__, __hash__, __getitem__, __setitem__, __delitem__, __iter__, __len__, __contains__). These declarations are implemented with new_method_proxy and do not include arithmetic/reflected operator dunders (no __add__, __radd__, __sub__, __rsub__, etc.). Location: django/utils/functional.py (LazyObject class, class-level attributes around lines 352–374 and full class at ~272–374).  

2) Delegation semantics required for operator methods are encoded by new_method_proxy: it ensures the wrapped object is initialized (calls self._setup() when self._wrapped is empty) and then calls func(self._wrapped, *args). Because Python operator dispatch for binary/reflected operators relies on class-level special methods, a proxy must expose those methods at the class level for reflected dispatch to occur. Location: django/utils/functional.py (new_method_proxy at lines 263–269).  

3) The lazy()/Promise proxy in the same module already provides __add__ and __radd__ (it evaluates the underlying value and implements both left and right addition). This demonstrates that Django's own proxy utilities have precedent for exposing reflected operators; LazyObject's omission is inconsistent with that precedent. Location: django/utils/functional.py (Promise/__proxy__ methods at lines 194–199).  

4) Multiple important Django components inherit the LazyObject omission: SimpleLazyObject (django/utils/functional.py), LazySettings (django/conf/__init__.py), DefaultStorage (django/core/files/storage.py), ConfiguredStorage (django/contrib/staticfiles/storage.py), DefaultAdminSite (django/contrib/admin/sites.py), and types built on SimpleLazyObject (e.g., django/utils/text.py:Truncator). Each of these inherits LazyObject's limited proxied-method set and therefore the reflected-operator omission is inherited across these components.  

5) Test-coverage gap: tests for LazyObject (tests/utils_tests/test_lazyobject.py) exercise many behaviors (attribute access, copy/pickle, comparisons, bytes/str/len/contains/getitem/setitem), but do not include tests for binary or reflected operator delegation. This gap explains why the omission is not caught by the existing test suite.  

6) Investigation caveat: the analysis did not exhaustively test runtime edge cases involving C-implemented/extension types or CPython-specific numeric coercion rules. CPython has nuances in magic-method resolution for numeric types implemented in C; those subtle behaviors were not stress-tested here and could affect some edge cases of operator dispatch.
StepsToReproduce: Define a left-hand operand whose __add__ always returns NotImplemented. For example: class L: def __add__(self, other): return NotImplemented.
Create a SimpleLazyObject wrapping a concrete value (for example an int or str) using SimpleLazyObject(lambda: wrapped_value).

Evaluate left_instance + simple_lazy_instance and observe that, when left.__add__ returns NotImplemented, the reflected method on the wrapped object is not invoked and the operation fails (returns NotImplemented or raises TypeError), demonstrating the proxy did not supply __radd__.
For control, evaluate simple_lazy_instance + other to show that the wrapped object's left-side operator is reachable when the proxy is on the left.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Binary operations where a SimpleLazyObject is the right-hand operand should behave the same as if the wrapped object were on the right-hand side. In particular, when the left operand's __add__ returns NotImplemented, Python should fall back to calling the wrapped object's reflected method so that other + SimpleLazyObject(...) yields the same result as other + wrapped.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Expressions such as other + SimpleLazyObject(...) can fail (e.g., return NotImplemented or raise an error) when the left operand's __add__ returns NotImplemented because LazyObject does not expose reflected operator methods (for example __radd__), so the wrapped object's reflected method is never invoked.

Could you please give some sample code with your use case?
In a boiled-down nutshell: def lazy_consumer(): # something more complex, obviously. return [1, 3, 5] consumer = SimpleLazyObject(lazy_consumer) # inside third party code ... def some_func(param): third_party_code = [...] # then, through parameter passing, my value is provided to be used. # param is at this point, `consumer` third_party_code_plus_mine = third_party_code + param which ultimately yields: TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for +: 'list' and 'SimpleLazyObject'

Replying to kezabelle: def lazy_consumer(): # something more complex, obviously. return [1, 3, 5] consumer = SimpleLazyObject(lazy_consumer) If you know what is the resulting type or possible resulting types of your expression, I think you better use django.utils.functional.lazy which will provide all the necessary methods.
Replying to kezabelle: As far as I can tell, the implementation can't just be __radd__ = new_method_proxy(operator.radd) because that doesn't exist, which is rubbish. __radd__ = new_method_proxy(operator.attrgetter("__radd__")) also won't work because types may not have that attr, and attrgetter doesn't supress the exception (correctly) Wouldn't the following code work? __add__ = new_method_proxy(operator.add) __radd__ = new_method_proxy(lambda a, b: operator.add(b, a)) I have tested this and it seems to work as excepted.

problem_statement.Title: Empty If-Modified-Since header raises exception in static file view
problem_statement.Description: An empty or whitespace-only HTTP_IF_MODIFIED_SINCE header ("" or all-whitespace) causes an exception when serving static files via django.views.static. Previously such headers were ignored; regression introduced since commit d6aff369ad3.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. was_modified_since assumes only header is None indicates a missing header. The function treats non-None header values (including empty or whitespace-only strings) as present and proceeds to parse them. (Location: django/views/static.py:was_modified_since)

2. was_modified_since uses a regex match and then subscripts the match object without checking for a failed match: matches = re.match(r"^([^;]+)(; length=([0-9]+))?$", header, re.IGNORECASE) followed by header_mtime = parse_http_date(matches[1]) and header_len = matches[3]. If re.match returns None (e.g. header == ""), subscripting matches raises a TypeError (NoneType not subscriptable). That TypeError is not caught by the function's except clause, so the exception propagates and the request fails. (Location: django/views/static.py:was_modified_since; exact lines show matches = re.match(...) and immediate use of matches[1]/matches[3])

3. parse_http_date is strict and raises ValueError for inputs that do not match HTTP date formats. was_modified_since calls parse_http_date(matches[1]) for matched headers; when parse_http_date raises ValueError for invalid-but-nonempty dates, the existing except (which catches ValueError) results in treating the header as invalid (the function returns True, meaning "consider modified"). This explains why malformed non-empty headers are handled gracefully while empty headers cause a different (uncaught) error. (Location: django/utils/http.py:parse_http_date and its invocation in django/views/static.py:was_modified_since)

4. Other code in the codebase already treats falsy/empty If-Modified-Since headers as missing. For example, get_conditional_response uses the idiom if_modified_since = request.META.get("HTTP_IF_MODIFIED_SINCE") followed by if_modified_since = if_modified_since and parse_http_date_safe(if_modified_since), which prevents parse_http_date from being called for empty/falsy headers. The static view does not follow this pattern. (Location: django/utils/cache.py:get_conditional_response)

5. Test coverage gap: tests/view_tests/tests/test_static.py contains tests for valid and malformed If-Modified-Since values but does not include an explicit test for an empty or whitespace-only HTTP_IF_MODIFIED_SINCE header. The missing test allowed this regression to be introduced without detection. (Location: tests/view_tests/tests/test_static.py)
StepsToReproduce: Send an HTTP request for a static file to Django's static file view (serve) with the HTTP header If-Modified-Since set to an empty string (HTTP_IF_MODIFIED_SINCE: "").
Observe the server response/traceback when the view invokes was_modified_since(request.META.get('HTTP_IF_MODIFIED_SINCE'), statobj.st_mtime, statobj.st_size).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: An empty or whitespace-only If-Modified-Since header is ignored (treated the same as a missing header) and the static file is served normally according to modification-time logic.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: An exception is raised when processing an empty If-Modified-Since header, causing the request to fail. The traceback originates from django.views.static.was_modified_since handling of the header (subscripting the regex match result when the match is None).

PASS_TO_PASS: ["A floating point mtime does not disturb was_modified_since (#18675).", "test_404 (view_tests.tests.test_static.StaticHelperTest)", "The static view should stream files in chunks to avoid large memory usage", "test_copes_with_empty_path_component (view_tests.tests.test_static.StaticHelperTest)", "No URLs are served if DEBUG=False.", "test_empty_prefix (view_tests.tests.test_static.StaticHelperTest)", "test_index (view_tests.tests.test_static.StaticHelperTest)", "test_index_custom_template (view_tests.tests.test_static.StaticHelperTest)", "test_index_subdir (view_tests.tests.test_static.StaticHelperTest)", "Handle bogus If-Modified-Since values gracefully", "Handle even more bogus If-Modified-Since values gracefully", "test_is_modified_since (view_tests.tests.test_static.StaticHelperTest)", "test_not_modified_since (view_tests.tests.test_static.StaticHelperTest)", "test_prefix (view_tests.tests.test_static.StaticHelperTest)", "The static view can serve static media", "No URLs are served if prefix contains a netloc part.", "test_unknown_mime_type (view_tests.tests.test_static.StaticHelperTest)", "test_404 (view_tests.tests.test_static.StaticTests)", "test_copes_with_empty_path_component (view_tests.tests.test_static.StaticTests)", "test_index (view_tests.tests.test_static.StaticTests)", "test_index_custom_template (view_tests.tests.test_static.StaticTests)", "test_index_subdir (view_tests.tests.test_static.StaticTests)", "test_is_modified_since (view_tests.tests.test_static.StaticTests)", "test_not_modified_since (view_tests.tests.test_static.StaticTests)", "test_unknown_mime_type (view_tests.tests.test_static.StaticTests)"]

problem_statement.Title: RenameIndex() crashes when re-applying after backward for auto-named (unnamed) index on PostgreSQL
problem_statement.Description: Using RenameIndex to rename an auto-generated (unnamed) index (old_fields=...) leads to a crash when the migration is run backwards and then re-applied. The reverse implementation is a no-op for unnamed-index renames, and re-applying the forward RenameIndex can result in a PostgreSQL ProgrammingError indicating the target index name already exists.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) django/db/migrations/operations/models.py :: RenameIndex.database_backwards encodes that reversing a RenameIndex that targeted an unnamed (auto-generated) index is a no-op when old_fields is set. The method returns immediately in that case, leaving the database index named with the explicit new_name instead of restoring an old auto-generated name. (See database_backwards early return for self.old_fields.)

2) django/db/migrations/operations/models.py :: RenameIndex.database_forwards unconditionally performs a rename: it resolves the current index by columns (via schema_editor._constraint_names) when old_fields is used or by old_name when provided, computes the desired new_index from the target state, and calls schema_editor.rename_index(model, old_index, new_index) without checking whether old_index.name == new_index.name. There is no guard against the discovered index already having the requested new name.

3) Deterministic auto-name generation is used in multiple places but with differing composition rules: django/db/backends/base/schema.py::_create_index_name builds a deterministic name (names_digest(..., length=8) in that helper) and django/db/models/indexes.py::Index.set_name_with_model also generates deterministic index names (using names_digest(..., length=6) and Index.suffix == 'idx' and Index.max_name_length == 30). Because index names can be recomputed deterministically from the table/columns, the code-paths that resolve indexes by columns can discover an index whose computed/auto-generated name equals the requested new_name.

4) Backend rename semantics: the schema editor executes a rename SQL statement when the backend supports it. BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor.rename_index uses _rename_index_sql (ALTER INDEX %(old_name)s RENAME TO %(new_name)s) when the connection supports renaming; the PostgreSQL DatabaseSchemaEditor uses that base behavior. Attempting to run ALTER INDEX to rename to a name that already exists in the database triggers a database error on PostgreSQL (the observed ProgrammingError: relation "new_pony_test_idx" already exists).
StepsToReproduce: Create a migration using RenameIndex with old_fields set to an unnamed/index-by-fields index and new_name set to a concrete name (for example, old_fields=['pony'], new_name='new_pony_test_idx').
Apply the migration (database_forwards) so the database index is created/renamed to new_pony_test_idx.
Run the migration backwards (database_backwards). For the old_fields case this is a no-op and the DB index remains named new_pony_test_idx.
Re-apply the migration forward (database_forwards). On PostgreSQL this raises: django.db.utils.ProgrammingError: relation "new_pony_test_idx" already exists.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Re-applying the forward RenameIndex after running its backward should be safe and idempotent: if the database already has the target index name, applying the forward operation should not error or attempt to create/rename to a duplicate name; the migration operation should complete successfully with the index present and named new_pony_test_idx.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Re-applying the forward RenameIndex after its backward run raises a PostgreSQL ProgrammingError: relation "new_pony_test_idx" already exists. The error occurs when database_forwards calls schema_editor.rename_index (which issues ALTER INDEX ... RENAME TO or falls back to remove+add) while the target name is already present in the DB.

I understand the issue that arises when one reverses a RenameIndex, but it was made "on purpose" somehow. In https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/27064, For backwards operations with unnamed old indexes, RenameIndex is a noop. From my understanding, when an unnamed index becomes "named", the idea was that it remained "named" even when reversing the operation. I guess the implementation is not entirely correct, since it doesn't allow idempotency of the operation when applying/un-applying it. I'll try to find a fix

Yes, sorry, I should predict that this is going to cause naming issues. problem_statement.Title: Django migration fails with ValueError when removing unique_together and changing ForeignKey → ManyToMany in a single migration
problem_statement.Description: Running makemigrations/migrate that both removes a unique_together and alters a model field from ForeignKey to ManyToMany raises a ValueError about the wrong number of constraints and the migration aborts.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) django/db/migrations/operations/models.py:AlterTogetherOptionOperation.database_forwards invokes the schema editor via a single call that passes the "new" model object while supplying both the old and new option values. Concretely the code executes:
    alter_together = getattr(schema_editor, "alter_%s" % self.option_name)
    alter_together(
        new_model,
        getattr(old_model._meta, self.option_name, set()),
        getattr(new_model._meta, self.option_name, set()),
    )
   This causes the deletion phase (the old option values passed as the second argument) to be resolved against new_model instead of against the old model instance.

2) django/db/backends/base/schema.py:_delete_composed_index (called from alter_unique_together/alter_index_together) resolves field → column names using the model object it is passed (columns = [model._meta.get_field(field).column for field in fields]) and then looks up constraint names for those columns. If the passed model does not have matching column-backed fields for those names (for example because the field was changed to a ManyToMany and is no longer column-backed), the constraint-name lookup can return zero matches and the method raises:
    raise ValueError("Found wrong number (%s) of constraints for %s(%s)" % ...)
   The observed ValueError message originates from this location.

3) Both AlterUniqueTogether and AlterIndexTogether inherit this behavior because they are subclasses of AlterTogetherOptionOperation (they only set option_name = "unique_together" or "index_together" and rely on the same database_forwards implementation). This makes both unique_together and index_together migrations vulnerable to the same issue.

4) ManyToManyField is not represented by a single column (ManyToManyField.get_attname_column returns (attname, None) and db_type returns None), so when a migration changes a field from a column-backed field (e.g., ForeignKey) to a ManyToMany, resolving the old unique/index constraint by querying column names against the new model can fail. In short: deletion of old composed constraints must resolve columns using the old (from_state) model, and creation of new composed constraints must resolve columns using the new (to_state) model; resolving deletions against the new model breaks transitions where a field stops being column-backed.

5) Backend overrides: some backends (e.g., django/db/backends/mysql/schema.py) override _delete_composed_index but call through to the base implementation; they therefore remain affected by the same root cause pattern because the base deletion logic is still used.
StepsToReproduce: Start from models where Authors.project_data_set is a ForeignKey and Authors.Meta.unique_together includes ('project_data_set', 'state', 'start_date').
Modify models in a single change: remove the unique_together entry and change Authors.project_data_set from ForeignKey to ManyToMany.
Run: python3 manage.py makemigrations
Run: python3 manage.py migrate
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The migration completes successfully in one migration: the unique_together constraint is removed and the Authors.project_data_set field is converted from a ForeignKey to a ManyToMany without raising a ValueError, and the database reflects the new model state.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Migration fails and aborts with ValueError: "Found wrong number (0) of constraints for app_authors(project_data_set, state, start_date)" (raised from the schema editor's composed-index-deletion path), preventing the single-step migration that both removes unique_together and alters the field.

Thanks for the report. Tentatively accepting, however I'm not sure if we can sort these operations properly, we should probably alter unique_together first migrations.AlterUniqueTogether( name='authors', unique_together=set(), ), migrations.RemoveField( model_name='authors', name='project_data_set', ), migrations.AddField( model_name='authors', name='project_data_set', field=models.ManyToManyField(to='dict.ProjectDataSet'), ), You should take into account that you'll lose all data because ForeignKey cannot be altered to ManyToManyField.
I agree that you'll loose data but Alter(Index|Unique)Together should always be sorted before RemoveField ​https://github.com/django/django/blob/b502061027b90499f2e20210f944292cecd74d24/django/db/migrations/autodetector.py#L910 ​https://github.com/django/django/blob/b502061027b90499f2e20210f944292cecd74d24/django/db/migrations/autodetector.py#L424-L430 So something's broken here in a few different ways and I suspect it's due to the fact the same field name project_data_set is reused for the many-to-many field. If you start from class Authors(models.Model): project_data_set = models.ForeignKey( ProjectDataSet, on_delete=models.PROTECT ) state = models.IntegerField() start_date = models.DateField() class Meta: unique_together = (('project_data_set', 'state', 'start_date'),) And generate makemigrations for class Authors(models.Model): project_data_set = models.ManyToManyField(ProjectDataSet) state = models.IntegerField() start_date = models.DateField() You'll get two migrations with the following operations # 0002 operations = [ migrations.AddField( model_name='authors', name='project_data_set', field=models.ManyToManyField(to='ticket_31788.ProjectDataSet'), ), migrations.AlterUniqueTogether( name='authors', unique_together=set(), ), migrations.RemoveField( model_name='authors', name='project_data_set', ), ] # 0003 operations = [ migrations.AddField( model_name='authors', name='project_data_set', field=models.ManyToManyField(to='ticket_31788.ProjectDataSet'), ), ] If you change the name of the field to something else like project_data_sets every work as expected operations = [ migrations.AddField( model_name='authors', name='project_data_sets', field=models.ManyToManyField(to='ticket_31788.ProjectDataSet'), ), migrations.AlterUniqueTogether( name='authors', unique_together=set(), ), migrations.RemoveField( model_name='authors', name='project_data_set', ), ] It seems like there's some bad interactions between generate_removed_fields and generate_added_fields when a field with the same name is added.

problem_statement.Title: Triple-quoted BaseCommand.help loses newlines/indentation in manage.py help output
problem_statement.Description: Multi-line (triple-quoted) BaseCommand.help text is shown with collapsed paragraphs and lost indentation when running manage.py help <command>; example blocks and intended newlines are flattened in the help output.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) BaseCommand.create_parser hardcodes the argparse formatter_class — File: django/core/management/base.py, Method: BaseCommand.create_parser. In create_parser the CommandParser is constructed with formatter_class=DjangoHelpFormatter and description=self.help; because the formatter is hardcoded here, command help rendering always uses DjangoHelpFormatter. (See create_parser: parser = CommandParser(... description=self.help or None, formatter_class=DjangoHelpFormatter, ...)).

2) DjangoHelpFormatter implements only option reordering and delegates description formatting to argparse.HelpFormatter — File: django/core/management/base.py, Class: DjangoHelpFormatter. DjangoHelpFormatter defines a show_last set and reorders actions via _reordered_actions, add_usage and add_arguments, but it does not override the HelpFormatter behavior for descriptions. The show_last set is hardcoded in this class ("--version", "--verbosity", "--traceback", "--settings", "--pythonpath", "--no-color", "--force-color", "--skip-checks"). Because description formatting is handled by the underlying HelpFormatter, multi-line/triple-quoted help strings are subject to the stdlib formatter's paragraph-wrapping/normalization rather than preserved verbatim.

3) No per-command formatter configuration is provided on BaseCommand — File: django/core/management/base.py, Class: BaseCommand. BaseCommand has no formatter_class attribute or mechanism to select a different formatter at the command class level; the hardcoded formatter in create_parser enforces a single formatter for all commands. This encodes the assumption that formatter selection need not be per-command, which prevents commands from selecting a formatter that preserves raw description formatting.

4) Reordering behavior is coupled to the formatter implementation — Files: django/core/management/base.py (DjangoHelpFormatter) and BaseCommand.create_parser. Because show_last and the reordering logic live inside DjangoHelpFormatter, replacing the formatter with an alternate argparse formatter (one that preserves raw description semantics) would omit DjangoHelpFormatter's reordering behavior unless that behavior is reproduced in the alternate formatter. The code therefore implicitly couples option-reordering semantics to the chosen formatter implementation.
StepsToReproduce: Create a management command class inheriting BaseCommand with a multi-line/triple-quoted help string containing blank lines and indented example usage (for example the code in the original report).
Run: ./manage.py help <your_command> (example: ./manage.py help tzkt_import).
Observe the formatting of the help text and example block in the output.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The help output preserves the multi-line formatting, blank lines, and indentation from BaseCommand.help. Example usage blocks and indented lines appear on their own lines with original indentation, matching the command's help string.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The help output collapses paragraphs and removes intended indentation from the multi-line help string: the example block is flattened into the preceding paragraph (or placed without original indentation), so example usage appears merged into the description instead of on separate indented lines.

hints_text:
This seems no fault of Django but is rather ​the default behavior of ArgumentParser ("By default, ArgumentParser objects line-wrap the description and epilog texts in command-line help messages"). This can be changed by using a custom ​formatter_class, though Django already specifies a custom one (​DjangoHelpFormatter).
It seems reasonable, to make it customizable by passing via kwargs to the ​BaseCommand.create_parser() (as documented): django/core/management/base.py diff --git a/django/core/management/base.py b/django/core/management/base.py index f0e711ac76..52407807d8 100644 a b class BaseCommand: 286286 Create and return the ``ArgumentParser`` which will be used to 287287 parse the arguments to this command. 288288 """ 289 kwargs.setdefault("formatter_class", DjangoHelpFormatter) 289290 parser = CommandParser( 290291 prog="%s %s" % (os.path.basename(prog_name), subcommand), 291292 description=self.help or None, 292 formatter_class=DjangoHelpFormatter, 293293 missing_args_message=getattr(self, "missing_args_message", None), 294294 called_from_command_line=getattr(self, "_called_from_command_line", None), 295295 **kwargs, What do you think?
Looks good but I don't see a reason for keeping a default that swallows newlines because PEP257 forbids having a multiline sentence on the first line anyway: Multi-line docstrings consist of a summary line just like a one-line docstring, followed by a blank line, followed by a more elaborate description. As such, the default formater which purpose is to unwrap the first sentence encourages breaking PEP 257. And users who are naturally complying with PEP257 will have to override the formatter, it should be the other way around.
Also, the not-unwraping formater will also look fine with existing docstrings, it will work for both use cases, while the current one only works for one use case and breaks the other. The default formater should work for both
Replying to James Pic: Also, the not-unwraping formater will also look fine with existing docstrings, it will work for both use cases, while the current one only works for one use case and breaks the other. The default formater should work for both It seems you think that Python's (not Django's) default behavior should be changed according to PEP 257. I'd recommend to start a discussion in Python's bugtracker. As far as I'm aware the proposed solution will allow users to freely change a formatter, which should be enough from the Django point of view.
No, I think that Django's default behavior should match Python's PEP 257, and at the same time, have a default that works in all use cases. I think my report and comments are pretty clear, I fail to understand how you could get my comment completely backward, so, unless you have any specific question about this statement, I'm going to give up on this.
So as part of this issue, do we make changes to allow a user to override the formatter through kwargs and also keep DjangoHelpFormatter as the default?
Replying to Subhankar Hotta: So as part of this issue, do we make changes to allow a user to override the formatter through kwargs and also keep DjangoHelpFormatter as the default? Yes, see comment.

problem_statement.Title: django.utils.html.json_script hardcodes DjangoJSONEncoder and does not accept a custom encoder
problem_statement.Description: The utility function json_script in django.utils.html always uses DjangoJSONEncoder when dumping JSON, preventing programmatic callers from supplying a custom JSONEncoder class. The template filter wrapper also accepts only (value, element_id) and therefore cannot forward an encoder parameter.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Hardcoded encoder in django/utils/html.py::json_script — json_script imports DjangoJSONEncoder and calls json.dumps(value, cls=DjangoJSONEncoder). Wrong assumption: the encoder must always be DjangoJSONEncoder. Exact code: json_str = json.dumps(value, cls=DjangoJSONEncoder).translate(_json_script_escapes) (django/utils/html.py). Correct relationship (diagnosis of defect): the cls argument supplied to json.dumps is currently a hardcoded DjangoJSONEncoder; to support custom encoding the cls supplied to json.dumps must be derived from a caller-supplied encoder parameter (e.g. cls=encoder) with a documented fallback to DjangoJSONEncoder when encoder is unspecified.

2) Template-filter wrapper missing encoder parameter in django/template/defaultfilters.py::json_script — the template filter is defined as def json_script(value, element_id=None): return _json_script(value, element_id). Wrong assumption: only value and element_id need to be accepted/forwarded. As implemented, the filter cannot accept or forward an encoder argument to the underlying utility.

3) Project API pattern and validation differences (contextual, equal weight): other Django APIs accept an encoder parameter and pass it to json.dumps as cls=encoder or validate it. For example, django/http/response.py::JsonResponse accepts encoder=DjangoJSONEncoder and calls json.dumps(data, cls=encoder, ...); django/db/models/fields/json.py::JSONField accepts encoder=None, validates that it is callable if provided, and uses json.dumps(..., cls=self.encoder). The json_script utility currently diverges from these patterns.

4) Tests do not exercise custom-encoder usage — existing tests (tests/utils_tests/test_html.py and tests/template_tests/filter_tests/test_json_script.py) exercise json_script output using the default behavior but contain no cases that pass or verify a custom encoder parameter, so the absence of an encoder parameter was not covered by tests.
StepsToReproduce: Open a Django Python shell (manage.py shell) within a project using this codebase.
From Python, import the utility: from django.utils.html import json_script

Attempt to call json_script with an encoder keyword: json_script(value, 'test_id', encoder=MyCustomEncoder) — observe that the function signature does not accept encoder (TypeError or unexpected keyword argument).

Alternatively, call json_script with a value that requires non-default encoding (e.g. a datetime/Decimal/UUID) and observe the output is encoded using DjangoJSONEncoder's behavior (evidence: source shows json.dumps(..., cls=DjangoJSONEncoder)).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When called programmatically, json_script should allow callers to provide a custom JSONEncoder class so the JSON output can be encoded according to that encoder. Template usage should continue to produce safe, application/json script tags; note that template syntax cannot pass Python class objects directly, so an encoder parameter would primarily benefit programmatic callers.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: json_script always uses DjangoJSONEncoder for json.dumps and does not accept or forward a custom encoder class. The template filter wrapper accepts only (value, element_id) and cannot forward an encoder parameter to the underlying implementation.

problem_statement.Title: False-positive templates.E003 when same template tag module is present in TEMPLATES['OPTIONS']['libraries'] and autodiscovered
problem_statement.Description: The system check check_for_template_tags_with_the_same_name reports templates.E003 when the same full module path for a template tag (for example 'someapp.templatetags.my_tags') appears both in TEMPLATES['OPTIONS']['libraries'] and among autodiscovered installed-app template tag modules. The error message can list the identical full module path multiple times, producing a false-positive conflict.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) In django/core/checks/templates.py::check_for_template_tags_with_the_same_name the code aggregates template tag module paths into libraries = defaultdict(list) and unconditionally appends module_path values from two sources: TEMPLATES['OPTIONS']['libraries'] and get_template_tag_modules(). (See templates.py lines where libraries = defaultdict(list), custom_libraries entries are appended, and get_template_tag_modules() entries are appended.) This encodes the incorrect assumption that each appended entry represents a distinct module; identical full module_path strings appended from different sources are treated as multiple distinct modules.

2) The detection and message logic in the same check treats len(items) > 1 as evidence of multiple modules for the same short module name and reports E003 using the items list joined into the message. Because identical full module_path strings are not deduplicated before this length check and message formatting, identical paths can appear multiple times in the reported message (the check uses if len(items) > 1 and ", ".join(repr(item) for item in items)).

3) One source of these module_path strings is autodiscovery: django/template/backends/django.py::get_template_tag_modules yields (module_name, full_module_path) pairs and get_installed_libraries returns {module_name: full_path} built from those pairs. Separately, user-provided TEMPLATES['OPTIONS']['libraries'] can contain the same full_module_path. Both sources therefore can supply the same full module path string that the check later appends to its per-name list.

4) DjangoTemplates.get_templatetag_libraries (django/template/backends/django.py) merges installed libraries and provided custom_libraries via libraries = get_installed_libraries(); libraries.update(custom_libraries). That merging behavior is relevant context for how a full module path may appear in settings and via autodiscovery, but the false-positive arises because the check collects both sources independently and appends both entries into the per-name list without de-duplicating identical strings.

5) Tests in tests/check_framework/test_templates.py assert a specific, deterministic ordering of reported module paths in the E003 message. This demonstrates that the check's output is expected to be deterministic and that any resolution of duplicate reporting must preserve deterministic ordering of reported module paths. (The tests construct an expected message containing two module paths in a fixed order and compare equality against the check's output.)

Locations encoding the incorrect assumptions (each stated with equal weight):
- django/core/checks/templates.py::check_for_template_tags_with_the_same_name — builds libraries as defaultdict(list), appends both custom and autodiscovered module_path strings, and flags len(items) > 1 as E003.
- django/template/backends/django.py::get_template_tag_modules and get_installed_libraries — supply autodiscovered (module_name, full_module_path) pairs and a dict mapping module_name to full_path; these are a source of module_path strings that the check subsequently aggregates.
- django/template/backends/django.py::DjangoTemplates.get_templatetag_libraries — merges installed libraries and custom libraries with dict.update; relevant as context for how the same full module path may appear in both settings and autodiscovery.
StepsToReproduce: Create a template tag module, e.g. someapp/templatetags/my_tags.py, and ensure someapp is in INSTALLED_APPS.
In settings.TEMPLATES, add an OPTIONS entry 'libraries' mapping the short name to the same full module path, e.g. {'my_tags': 'someapp.templatetags.my_tags'}.
Run Django's system checks (e.g. python manage.py check) or run the checks that include check_for_template_tags_with_the_same_name.
Observe the reported error for 'my_tags'.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: No templates.E003 error should be reported when the same full module path for a template tag is present both in TEMPLATES['OPTIONS']['libraries'] and discovered via installed-app autodiscovery. The check should report templates.E003 only when multiple distinct full module paths exist for the same short module name.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: templates.E003 is reported and its message lists the identical full module path more than once (for example: "'my_tags' is used for multiple template tag modules: 'someapp.templatetags.my_tags', 'someapp.templatetags.my_tags'"), indicating a false-positive duplicate detection.

problem_statement.Title: QuerySet.only() after select_related() crashes on proxy models (ValueError: 'id' is not in list)
problem_statement.Description: When evaluating a QuerySet that uses select_related() to follow a ForeignKey to a proxy model combined with only() to restrict selected fields, queryset evaluation can raise ValueError: 'id' is not in list. The exception is raised during object instantiation when Django attempts to initialize related objects from the query result row.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Inconsistent use of proxy vs concrete model metadata in Query.deferred_to_data (django/db/models/sql/query.py). The method initializes must_include keyed by the concrete model class (orig_opts.concrete_model) but, while traversing multi-part lookup paths, it assigns opts = cur_model._meta where cur_model may be a proxy class. Because add_to_dict and the must_include/seen mappings use the model class objects as dict keys, using a proxy class _meta during traversal produces mapping entries that do not match the concrete_model keys. (See deferred_to_data: must_include = {orig_opts.concrete_model: {orig_opts.pk}} and later opts = cur_model._meta.)

2) As a result of (1), the set of DB columns that deferred_to_data and the compiler decide must be selected can omit the concrete model's primary key column. The pipeline relies on model identity being consistent (DB-relevant decisions are keyed by the DB-owning model class), but the traversal can populate must_include/seen with proxy-class keys instead of the concrete model class, so required concrete-model columns (notably the PK) are not always added to the final select column list.

3) RelatedPopulator assumes the SELECT columns contain the concrete model's attnames (django/db/models/query.py). It builds model_init_attnames from klass_info['model']._meta.concrete_fields and then computes self.pk_idx by finding self.model_cls._meta.pk.attname in the init_list. If the concrete-model PK attname is missing from the init_list because the SELECT omitted that column (due to the mismatch described above), index() raises ValueError: 'id' is not in list. (See RelatedPopulator: model_init_attnames = (f.attname for f in klass_info['model']._meta.concrete_fields) ... self.pk_idx = self.init_list.index(self.model_cls._meta.pk.attname).)

4) The SQLCompiler stores klass_info entries using the Query model and select_fields built from get_default_columns (django/db/models/sql/compiler.py). klass_info = {'model': self.query.model, 'select_fields': select_list} and related_klass_infos refer to relation targets (f.remote_field.model). The overall pipeline therefore expects concrete-model column identities (concrete_fields / concrete_model) to be consistently used when deciding which DB columns are selected and when instantiating models. The inconsistency in deferred_to_data between concrete_model keys and proxy _meta during traversal is the mismatch that leads to the omitted PK and the subsequent crash.
StepsToReproduce: Define models: CustomModel (normal model with name CharField), ProxyCustomModel (proxy=True subclass of CustomModel), and AnotherModel with custom = ForeignKey(ProxyCustomModel, on_delete=..., null=True, blank=True).
In the project shell or a management command, evaluate: list(AnotherModel.objects.select_related('custom').only('custom__name').all()).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The queryset evaluates without raising an exception. Returned AnotherModel instances include their related custom object (ProxyCustomModel) with the requested field(s) (custom__name) available; no ValueError is raised during queryset evaluation.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Query evaluation raises ValueError: 'id' is not in list during RelatedPopulator initialization (traceback shows the exception originates from django/db/models/query.py when computing the pk index).

problem_statement.Title: inspectdb omits related_name causing reverse-accessor clashes (fields.E304)
problem_statement.Description: When inspectdb generates Django model code for tables that contain multiple foreign keys to the same target (including self-references), it does not emit explicit related_name arguments on the generated relation fields. Because Django falls back to default reverse-accessor naming when related_name is absent, this omission can produce duplicate reverse-accessor names and trigger Django system check fields.E304.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) inspectdb's generator does not set related_name on generated relation fields. Evidence location: django/core/management/commands/inspectdb.py::Command.handle_inspection. At the point where relation fields are emitted the code computes att_name (via normalize_col_name), builds extra_params, and writes extra_params into the field constructor text, but there is no assignment of a "related_name" key in extra_params. The relevant variables exist (relations, att_name, extra_params, table2model) but inspectdb does not populate related_name.

2) The ORM uses related_name if present, otherwise it computes a default accessor name (model_name + "_set"). Evidence location: django/db/models/fields/reverse_related.py::ForeignObjectRel.get_accessor_name which returns self.related_name if set; otherwise opts.model_name + '_set'. Because inspectdb does not provide related_name, the default accessor name is used for each generated relation.

3) Duplicate default accessor names are detected and reported as checks.Error fields.E304. Evidence location: django/db/models/fields/related.py::_check_clashes. _check_clashes obtains the accessor name via get_accessor_name() and compares it to existing accessors/field names; when two relations produce the same accessor the code emits an Error with id "fields.E304" and a hint recommending adding or changing a related_name.

4) The concrete diagnostic relationship discovered from the generator context: when a single table has multiple foreign-key columns referencing the same target table (or when a table self-references), the generator must produce distinct reverse-accessor identifiers for each emitted relation or else the ORM will detect duplicates and raise E304. The values available in inspectdb at generation time that determine uniqueness are the source table name (table2model(table_name)) and the generated attribute name for the field (att_name, produced by normalize_col_name). From the investigation, a deterministic, unique related_name can be formed from the source model name and the att_name (for example, a pattern of source_model_lower + '_' + att_name was proposed as the distinguishing relationship). This statement is a description of the naming relationship required to avoid duplicate accessors, not a prescription for code changes.

5) Related-field lifecycle notes that are relevant diagnostic facts: RelatedField.contribute_to_class may apply a default_related_name pattern if remote_field.related_name is absent (see django/db/models/fields/related.py::RelatedField.contribute_to_class), and RelatedField.deconstruct includes an explicitly set related_name in the deconstruction kwargs (see RelatedField.deconstruct). These code locations show that an explicit related_name on the field will be materialized and preserved by Django's field machinery and deconstruction, and therefore setting related_name in generated source is the expected way to avoid E304 reported by the ORM.
StepsToReproduce: Create a database schema with a table that has two foreign-key columns to the same target table (or a self-referential table with multiple FK columns).
Run python manage.py inspectdb and capture the generated models (for example: python manage.py inspectdb > models.py).
Include the generated models in a Django project and run system checks or makemigrations (python manage.py check or python manage.py makemigrations).
Observe a fields.E304 error reporting that reverse accessors for the two generated relation fields clash and the hint suggesting to add or change a related_name.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: inspectdb-generated model code for a fresh project should not produce reverse-accessor name collisions. For tables with multiple FKs to the same target (including self-references), the emitted model code should result in distinct reverse-accessor names (so Django checks do not raise fields.E304) when the generated models are loaded.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: inspectdb-generated models omit related_name on relation fields; when those models are used Django reports fields.E304: Reverse accessor for 'module.Model.field1' clashes with reverse accessor for 'module.Model.field2' and hints to add or change a related_name argument.

problem_statement.Title: PostgreSQL dbshell: parameters passed after DBNAME are ignored/warned by psql
problem_statement.Description: When using manage.py dbshell with extra psql options (for example: ./manage.py dbshell -- -c "SQL"), the PostgreSQL client implementation in the codebase constructs the psql command-line with the database name before the user-supplied parameters. psql treats arguments after the database name as positional arguments and emits warnings or ignores them.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) django/db/backends/postgresql/client.py::DatabaseClient.settings_to_cmd_args_env constructs the final argument list by inserting the database name into args and then extending args with the provided parameters. In code: if dbname: args += [dbname]; args.extend(parameters). This encodes the assumption that parameters may follow DBNAME, which does not match psql's expectation that options/flags precede the DBNAME.

2) tests/dbshell/test_postgresql.py::test_parameters encodes and asserts the same DBNAME-first ordering (it expects ['psql', 'dbname', '--help'] for parameters=['--help']). The test mirrors the current implementation's ordering assumption.

3) django/core/management/commands/dbshell.py::Command.handle forwards CLI parameters verbatim to connection.client.runshell (it calls connection.client.runshell(options['parameters'])). django/db/backends/base/client.py::BaseDatabaseClient.runshell calls the backend's settings_to_cmd_args_env and executes the returned args. Because the management command and BaseDatabaseClient do not reorder parameters, the DatabaseClient implementation is the component that determines the final ordering passed to psql.
StepsToReproduce: From a Django project configured to use the PostgreSQL backend, run: ./manage.py dbshell -- -c "select * from some_table;"
Observe psql's output/warnings and whether the provided -c command is executed.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Options and flags provided after the -- in manage.py dbshell (for example -c "SQL") are passed to psql as options (i.e., before the database name) so that psql interprets and executes them (or exits) rather than treating them as positional arguments.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior:
psql emits warnings that extra command-line arguments (for example "-c" and the SQL string) are ignored and then opens an interactive prompt for the database. Example observed output:
psql: warning: extra command-line argument "-c" ignored
psql: warning: extra command-line argument "select * from some_table;" ignored
psql (10.21)
