problem_statement.Title: ManagementForm rendering in FormSet triggers RemovedInDjango50Warning for "django/forms/default.html"
problem_statement.Description: Including a FormSet (or its management_form) causes a RemovedInDjango50Warning about the default form templates even when the management form only produces hidden inputs. The warning is emitted during ManagementForm rendering and appears noisy because the management form's output is only hidden fields.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1) RenderableMixin.render (used by RenderableFormMixin) emits DEFAULT_TEMPLATE_DEPRECATION_MSG when the resolved template equals the exact strings "django/forms/default.html" or "django/forms/formsets/default.html"; location: django/forms/utils.py:RenderableMixin.render (lines 64–75). 2) BaseRenderer sets its default template-name attributes to those exact strings: BaseRenderer.form_template_name = "django/forms/default.html" and BaseRenderer.formset_template_name = "django/forms/formsets/default.html"; location: django/forms/renderers.py:BaseRenderer (lines 17–23). 3) ManagementForm is defined as a Form subclass with no render override and declares the management fields as IntegerField(widget=HiddenInput); therefore, when rendered without an explicit template_name it inherits the form rendering path and the renderer's default form_template_name (the deprecated "django/forms/default.html"); location: django/forms/formsets.py:ManagementForm (lines 28–41) and django/forms/forms.py:Form/BaseForm (inheritance via RenderableFormMixin). 4) Formset templates include the management form via "{{ formset.management_form }}", which triggers ManagementForm rendering (and thus the RenderableMixin deprecation check) when a FormSet is rendered; location: django/forms/templates/django/forms/formsets/default.html (line 1) and related formset templates. 5) The management form's declared fields (TOTAL_FORMS, INITIAL_FORMS, MIN_NUM_FORMS, MAX_NUM_FORMS) use HiddenInput widgets, so the management form's intended visible output is only hidden inputs; routing that output through the generic form-level template selection causes the deprecation check to run even though the visible output is hidden fields; location: django/forms/formsets.py:ManagementForm (lines 35–41). 6) The test-suite contains tests that assert DEFAULT_TEMPLATE_DEPRECATION_MSG is raised in certain default-template rendering contexts (e.g., str(form) and str(formset)), so the present behavior is relied upon by tests; location: tests/forms_tests/tests/test_formsets.py::DeprecationTests (lines ~1901–1912) and tests/forms_tests/tests/test_forms.py. 7) Note: external callers that explicitly pass a template_name to ManagementForm.render are not enumerated in core code; the current behavior applies when no explicit template_name is provided and the renderer defaults are used (observation based on BaseFormSet.management_form construction passing renderer=self.renderer; location: django/forms/formsets.py:management_form property lines 145–166).
StepsToReproduce: In a Django shell or test, define a simple form (e.g., class Choice(forms.Form): choice = forms.CharField()) and create a formset: ChoiceFormSet = formset_factory(Choice).
Instantiate the formset: formset = ChoiceFormSet().
Render the formset (for example, call str(formset) or render a template that uses {{ formset.management_form }} or {{ formset }}).
Observe that a RemovedInDjango50Warning is emitted mentioning "django/forms/default.html" (DEFAULT_TEMPLATE_DEPRECATION_MSG).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Rendering a FormSet that only emits hidden management inputs should not emit the RemovedInDjango50Warning about the default form templates; users should not see a deprecation warning when the visible output contains only hidden inputs produced by the management form.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Rendering a FormSet (or including {{ formset.management_form }} in a template) emits a RemovedInDjango50Warning complaining about "django/forms/default.html" or "django/forms/formsets/default.html", even though the management form's visible output is only hidden inputs.

problem_statement.Title: Combined Enum Flag values serialized as module.EnumClass[None] in migrations
problem_statement.Description: When a Flag/IntFlag value is a combination of members (for example re.UNICODE | re.IGNORECASE) the migrations serializer emits expressions like re.RegexFlag[None] instead of a valid expression that reconstructs the combined flags. This appears in generated migrations (field defaults and RegexValidator flags).
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) EnumSerializer assumes every enum value has a .name and unconditionally serializes using that name. Location: django/db/migrations/serializer.py: EnumSerializer.serialize (returns "%s.%s[%r]" % (module, enum_class.__qualname__, self.value.name)). For combined Flag/IntFlag values self.value.name is None, causing expressions such as module.EnumClass[None].

2) The serializer registry routes all enum.Enum instances (including Flag/IntFlag subclasses) to EnumSerializer, so the behavior affects every enum value serialized via serializer_factory. Location: django/db/migrations/serializer.py: Serializer._registry (enum.Enum: EnumSerializer) and serializer_factory matching logic.

3) RegexSerializer and other call sites that pass Flag/IntFlag values to serializer_factory rely on the serializer producing a valid Python expression. RegexSerializer computes flags and calls serializer_factory(flags).serialize(), so EnumSerializer's behavior is reflected in compiled regex/validator serialization. Location: django/db/migrations/serializer.py: RegexSerializer.serialize.

4) To correctly reconstruct a combined Flag/IntFlag value at runtime, the serialized expression must represent the named members whose combination equals the value. The investigation derived the decomposition rule applicable to Flag-like enums: include a named member when either (member.value == 0 and self.value == member) or (member.value != 0 and (self.value & member) == member). Each included part should reference the member as module.EnumClass['MEMBER'] and the parts joined with the bitwise OR operator (" | ") so that evaluating the expression yields the same Flag combination. This decomposition requirement is a diagnostic statement about what a correct serializer must achieve; the current code does not implement decomposition.

5) The test suite does not include a test covering combined Flag/IntFlag serialization (e.g., re.UNICODE | re.IGNORECASE). Existing tests cover single named members (e.g., re.RegexFlag['DOTALL']) but not combined values, which explains why the issue was not detected. Location: tests/migrations/test_writer.py (enum/flag tests and RegexValidator flag test show single-flag expectations).
StepsToReproduce: Create a model field or validator that uses a combined RegexFlag value, for example: regex_flags = models.IntegerField(default=re.UNICODE | re.IGNORECASE)
Run python manage.py makemigrations
Open the generated migration file and inspect the default/validator flags expression (the migration contains default=re.RegexFlag[None] or flags=re.RegexFlag[None])

Alternative: Create a RegexValidator with flags=re.UNICODE | re.IGNORECASE, serialize it with MigrationWriter.serialize (or observe its serialized form in a migration) and inspect the flags part of the generated string
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Generated migration code should contain a valid Python expression that reconstructs the combined Flag value using named members joined by bitwise OR, for example: re.RegexFlag['UNICODE'] | re.RegexFlag['IGNORECASE']. For single named members, the serializer should keep the single-name form (module.Class['NAME']). If no named members decompose to the value, the serializer should emit a literal representation that round-trips (for example, a repr(value) that the migration system can evaluate).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The serializer emits module.EnumClass[None] (for example re.RegexFlag[None]) for combined Flag/IntFlag values. This produces an invalid or meaningless flags expression in the generated migration (e.g., re.compile(..., flags=re.RegexFlag[None])).

problem_statement.Title: Rendering formset.empty_form crashes when form_kwargs contains explicit form keywords (e.g. empty_permitted)
problem_statement.Description: When a formset is constructed with form_kwargs that include keys that BaseFormSet.empty_form also passes explicitly (notably 'empty_permitted'), rendering formset.empty_form in a template raises a duplicate-key keyword error and crashes template rendering.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) django/forms/formsets.py::BaseFormSet.empty_form constructs the empty form by supplying explicit keyword arguments (auto_id, prefix, empty_permitted, use_required_attribute, renderer) and then expanding **self.get_form_kwargs(None) in the same call. The code is:
   form = self.form(
       auto_id=self.auto_id,
       prefix=self.add_prefix("__prefix__"),
       empty_permitted=True,
       use_required_attribute=False,
       **self.get_form_kwargs(None),
       renderer=self.renderer,
   )
   If the mapping returned by get_form_kwargs(None) contains any of the explicit keys (for example 'empty_permitted'), Python raises a duplicate-key TypeError at call time. (Location: django/forms/formsets.py: empty_form property)

2) django/forms/forms.py::BaseForm.__init__ accepts the 'empty_permitted' keyword and sets self.empty_permitted. Because empty_permitted is a valid Form constructor argument, users reasonably pass {'empty_permitted': True/False} via form_kwargs; this makes the collision in (1) observable in real user code. (Location: django/forms/forms.py: BaseForm.__init__)

3) django/forms/formsets.py::BaseFormSet._construct_form uses a different, safe pattern: it builds a defaults dict (including empty_permitted when appropriate), calls defaults.update(kwargs), and then calls self.form(**defaults). That merge pattern allows user-supplied kwargs to override defaults without causing duplicate-key exceptions. empty_form deviates from this pattern by expanding get_form_kwargs(None) directly into the constructor call. (Location: django/forms/formsets.py: _construct_form)

4) BaseFormSet.get_form_kwargs by default returns a shallow copy of self.form_kwargs (return self.form_kwargs.copy()). Therefore, in the default BaseFormSet implementation, empty_form receives a copy of the stored form_kwargs and does not mutate the formset's stored mapping. However, user-defined overrides of get_form_kwargs may return arbitrary mappings (including the original dict or shared mutable objects), and in those cases empty_form's expansion of the returned mapping could cause mutation of user-owned objects. This nuance explains the investigation's concern about mutation risk but shows the default implementation already returns a copy. (Location: django/forms/formsets.py: get_form_kwargs)

5) Tests (tests/forms_tests/tests/test_formsets.py::test_form_kwargs_empty_form and related assertions) specify the intended user-visible properties: non-conflicting custom kwargs passed via form_kwargs should be present on empty_form, and empty_form.empty_permitted should be True. The behavior observed when get_form_kwargs(None) contains conflicting keys (e.g. 'empty_permitted') violates these test expectations and causes the reported runtime failure. (Location: tests/forms_tests/tests/test_formsets.py: test_form_kwargs_empty_form and assertions around empty_form.empty_permitted)
StepsToReproduce: Define a ModelFormSet factory for a model, e.g. ff = modelformset_factory(MyModel, fields=['a_field']).
Instantiate the formset with queryset=MyModel.objects.none() and form_kwargs={'empty_permitted': True} (or False): ff(queryset=MyModel.objects.none(), form_kwargs={'empty_permitted': True}).
Render a template that outputs the formset.empty_form (for example include {{ formset }} and {{ formset.empty_form }} in the template).
Load the view/URL that renders that template; template rendering will raise a duplicate-key keyword error (TypeError) and fail.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Rendering a template that includes {{ formset.empty_form }} should not crash. The empty_form should render successfully, should preserve user-supplied form_kwargs that do not conflict with required empty_form arguments, and empty_form.empty_permitted should be True.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Rendering formset.empty_form raises a duplicate-key keyword error (TypeError) when form_kwargs passed to the formset contain keys that empty_form also supplies explicitly (notably 'empty_permitted'), causing template rendering to fail.

problem_statement.Title: IndexError in django.utils.numberformat.format() when given empty string
problem_statement.Description: Calling django.utils.numberformat.format() with an empty-string value causes an IndexError: "string index out of range" because the function converts the input to a string and then indexes the first character without checking for emptiness. This surfaces when formatting fields (for example in admin list_display) that can pass an empty string to the formatter.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) django/utils/numberformat.py : format() — incorrect assumption that str(number) is non-empty. The function assigns str_number = str(number) (or a formatted Decimal string) and then immediately does if str_number[0] == "-": which raises IndexError when str_number == '' (evidence: numberformat.py lines where str_number = str(number) and if str_number[0] == "-").

2) django/utils/numberformat.py : fast-path early return — the function returns mark_safe(number) for ints when grouping and decimal_pos conditions are not met (evidence: numberformat.py fast-path branch). This passes a non-string (an int) into mark_safe. mark_safe accepts and coerces non-string inputs (it returns SafeString(s)), so this is an inconsistency in input types passed to the safety wrapper rather than the root cause of the IndexError, but it is a secondary observation in the same area of code.

3) tests/utils_tests/test_numberformat.py : test coverage gap — the test suite contains no explicit case asserting behavior for empty-string input to the formatter. The absence of a test for nformat("", decimal_sep) means this regression was not covered by existing tests (evidence: test file contains many numeric and string cases but no empty-string assertion).

4) django/contrib/admin/utils.py : caller assumptions — display_for_field calls formats.number_format(value, ...) for numeric fields after checking only for None; there is no explicit check for empty string. Therefore, an empty string value can propagate from model attributes or callables into formats.number_format and thence into numberformat.format, triggering the IndexError (evidence: display_for_field returns formats.number_format(value, ...) for DecimalField/IntegerField/FloatField and checks only for value is None).
StepsToReproduce: Open a Django project shell with the project environment loaded.
Import the formatter: from django.utils import numberformat
Call the formatter with an empty string: numberformat.format(number='')
Observe the IndexError: "string index out of range".

Alternate reproduction: In the Django admin, have a model/display value that evaluates to an empty string for a numeric column and open the list_display page; the admin rendering path that calls formats.number_format may raise the same IndexError.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Formatting an empty-string value should not raise an exception; from a user perspective the formatter should handle empty or missing numeric content gracefully (for example returning an empty string or the configured empty_value_display in the admin) rather than crashing with an IndexError.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Calling numberformat.format() with an empty string raises IndexError: "string index out of range" (trace originates from the unguarded access of str_number[0] in django/utils/numberformat.py).

problem_statement.Title: Password change link on UserChangeForm is broken (404) when UserAdmin is accessed via a related lookup using to_field
problem_statement.Description: When the User change page is reached via another model's admin related-link that uses _to_field (e.g. ?_to_field=uuid), the "Password" help-text link in the User change form is constructed as a relative "../password/" URL. Resolved against the current URL path segment (which may be a non-PK value), this produces a link that does not match the admin password-change endpoint and results in a 404.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. django/contrib/auth/forms.py:UserChangeForm.__init__ formats the password field help_text using a hardcoded relative path: password.help_text = password.help_text.format("../password/") (forms.py, line 166). This embeds the assumption that resolving "../password/" relative to the current change-form URL will target the password-change endpoint for the current user.

2. django/contrib/auth/admin.py:UserAdmin.get_urls registers the password-change endpoint with a path pattern of "<id>/password/" (admin.py, lines 102-106), and UserAdmin.user_change_password looks up the user using self.get_object(request, unquote(id)) (admin.py, lines 150-152). The password-change view therefore expects the URL path segment used as id to be the object's primary key (PK).

3. django/contrib/admin/options.py:ModelAdmin.changeform_view reads TO_FIELD_VAR ("_to_field") from the request and passes it to get_object when resolving the object for the change form (options.py, lines 1748-1766). ModelAdmin.get_object implements the lookup as field = (model._meta.pk if from_field is None else model._meta.get_field(from_field)) and then queries by that field (options.py, lines 861-866). As a result, the change form may be rendered for an object identified by a non-PK field when _to_field is present.

4. django/contrib/admin/widgets.py and django/contrib/admin/templatetags/admin_urls.py: RelatedFieldWidgetWrapper, ForeignKeyRawIdWidget, and related URL builders add or preserve TO_FIELD_VAR in related-links and popup lookups (admin/widgets.py, e.g. url_parameters and get_context usage of TO_FIELD_VAR and view_related_url_params). This is how change-form links can be opened with a non-PK identifier (a to_field value) in the URL querystring.

5. Mismatch between assumptions: the change form's help-text link is a static relative reference ("../password/") which the browser resolves relative to the current path segment (which may be a to_field value). The password-change endpoint and its view expect the object's primary key in the path segment (the URL pattern and view use the PK for lookup). This mismatch between (a) a change form that can be reached using a non-PK identifier via _to_field and (b) a password-change endpoint that requires the PK is the root cause of the broken (404) password link.
StepsToReproduce: Register a second model in the admin that has a relation to User using to_field (for example, ForeignKey(User, to_field='uuid')).

From the related model's admin, use the related lookup to open the User via the provided admin link. Observe the change-page URL, which will include the related identifier and a query parameter like ?_to_field=uuid.
On the User change page, inspect or click the "this form" link in the Password field help text.
Observe that the link resolves to a path like /.../<to_field_value>/password/ and that the request returns 404 because the password-change view treats the path segment as a PK.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: On the User change page (whether reached directly or via a related lookup using _to_field), the "Password" help-text link should navigate to the admin password-change page for that specific user and return the password-change page (not a 404).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: When the change page is reached via a to_field lookup (URL contains ?_to_field=...), the password help-text link is formatted as a relative "../password/" URL resolved against the non-PK path segment, producing a URL such as /.../<to_field_value>/password/ that does not match the PK-based password-change endpoint and results in a 404.

problem_statement.Title: Hidden "initial-" inputs for fields with callable defaults are rendered with bound/submitted value, causing admin inline to be dismissed on second submit
problem_statement.Description: When a ModelForm field's model default is a callable (for example ArrayField(default=list)) and show_hidden_initial is enabled, the hidden "initial-..." input can end up containing the bound/submitted value when the form is re-displayed after a validation error. On the next submit that value is treated as the original initial value by change-detection, so an inline that previously errored may be considered unchanged and be dismissed, losing the user's entered value.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. django/db/models/fields/__init__.py::Field.formfield — When a model field has a callable default, Field.formfield sets the form field's initial to the callable and enables show_hidden_initial (defaults['initial'] = self.default; defaults['show_hidden_initial'] = True). This causes the forms machinery to render a hidden "initial-..." input for such fields and establishes that a hidden initial will be used as the baseline for change detection.

2. django/forms/boundfield.py::BoundField.as_widget and BoundField.__str__ — BoundField.__str__ emits a hidden initial when field.show_hidden_initial is True (as_hidden(only_initial=True)). BoundField.as_widget always passes value=self.value() into widget.render (name is chosen according to only_initial), i.e. widget.render(name=..., value=self.value(), ...). For bound forms self.value() reflects the submitted/bound data (BoundField.value applies field.bound_data(self.data, self.initial) and then field.prepare_value). As a result, the hidden "initial-..." input can be rendered using the submitted value rather than a widget-formatted representation of the original initial.

3. django/forms/boundfield.py::BoundField._has_changed — The change-detection path reads the posted hidden initial via form._widget_data_value(hidden_widget, html_initial_name) and then calls field.to_python(initial_value) before invoking field.has_changed(initial_value, self.data). This encoding requires that the hidden initial contain a widget-formatted original initial that field.to_python can parse back into the original python value; otherwise the baseline used by has_changed will be incorrect.

4. django/contrib/postgres/forms/array.py::SimpleArrayField.prepare_value and SimpleArrayField.to_python (concrete example) — prepare_value serializes a Python list into a delimiter-joined string and to_python parses that string back into a Python list. This shows the expected complementary relationship: if the hidden initial contains field.prepare_value(self.initial) (a widget-formatted string), then later field.to_python(posted_value) reproduces the original python initial. The current write-side (BoundField.as_widget) does not guarantee this widget-formatted original initial is written into the hidden input for bound forms.

5. Interaction/impact scope — Field.formfield's callable-default behavior applies to all model fields that set defaults['initial'] to a callable and show_hidden_initial True; ArrayField is a concrete, common instance because ArrayField.formfield uses SimpleArrayField as its form_class. Any model field using a callable default and show_hidden_initial participates in the same write/read mismatch pattern described above.
StepsToReproduce: Define models and admin as in the original report: Thing model and RelatedModel with plop = ArrayField(models.CharField(max_length=42), default=list). Create RelatedModelForm.clean to raise ValidationError('whatever'), register RelatedModelInline (extra=1) on ThingAdmin.
Start Django dev server and open /admin/my_app/thing/add/.
In the inline row's plop field, type any value (e.g. 'test') and submit the form.
Observe the form re-displays with the inline showing the validation error from RelatedModelForm.clean.
Submit the form again without changing the inline.
Observe: no inline error is shown and the inline is dismissed/treated as unchanged; the plop value becomes empty/unfilled.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: After a validation error and form re-render, the hidden "initial-..." inputs should preserve a widget-formatted representation of the original model initial (the evaluated callable default) so that change-detection correctly determines whether the inline was modified. Subsequent submits should not silently dismiss an inline that previously errored unless the user actually cleared or reverted the inline data.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: On re-render after a validation error, the hidden "initial-..." input contains the submitted/bound value. On the next submit that submitted value is treated as the original initial, change-detection considers the inline unchanged and dismisses it, causing the entered value to be lost.

problem_statement.Title: Sitemap.get_latest_lastmod raises ValueError on callable lastmod when items() is empty
problem_statement.Description: When a Sitemap defines lastmod as a callable but items() yields no items, Sitemap.get_latest_lastmod calls max([...]) on an empty sequence which raises ValueError and causes the sitemap index view to return a 500 error.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. django/contrib/sitemaps/__init__.py: Sitemap.get_latest_lastmod: The method, when lastmod is callable, calls max([self.lastmod(item) for item in self.items()]) and only catches TypeError (see lines 165-174). This encodes the incorrect assumption that self.items() is non-empty. In the empty-items case max([]) raises ValueError ("max() arg is an empty sequence") which is not handled and therefore propagates out of get_latest_lastmod.

2. django/contrib/sitemaps/views.py: index: The view calls site.get_latest_lastmod() directly and does not protect against exceptions from that call (see lines 68-82). As a result, any exception raised by get_latest_lastmod (including the ValueError from max([])) will bubble up and produce a 500 Internal Server Error.

3. django/contrib/sitemaps/__init__.py: Sitemap._urls: The _urls method computes latest_lastmod and uses an all_items_lastmod flag to set self.latest_lastmod only when every item has a non-None lastmod (see lines 176-227). The behavior expected by other parts of the codebase and tests is that the sitemap-level latest_lastmod is present only if every item has a lastmod; get_latest_lastmod's callable-lastmod path currently does not preserve this invariant in the empty-items case because it can raise ValueError instead of returning None.

4. django/contrib/sitemaps/__init__.py: GenericSitemap.get_latest_lastmod: GenericSitemap overrides get_latest_lastmod and uses queryset ordering/first() semantics to return None safely for empty sets (see lines 251-258). This demonstrates the intended safe behavior for empty data sources, highlighting that the base Sitemap implementation is the location of the defect.

5. tests/sitemaps_tests: Tests and test fixtures encode the expected behavior: test_callable_sitemod_partial and related tests assert that when not all items have lastmod (partial) or when items() is empty, the index and sitemap responses must not include Last-Modified (i.e., get_latest_lastmod should be None in those cases). The current base Sitemap.get_latest_lastmod implementation allows a ValueError in the empty-callable-lastmod case, which contradicts these expectations (see tests/sitemaps_tests/test_http.py and tests/sitemaps_tests/urls/http.py).
StepsToReproduce: Create a Sitemap subclass where lastmod is a callable and items() returns an empty iterable (e.g. the EmptySitemap test fixture).
Include that sitemap in the sitemap index (urls pointing to django.contrib.sitemaps.views.index).
Request /sitemap.xml (the index view).
Observe a 500 Internal Server Error with traceback showing ValueError: "max() arg is an empty sequence" originating from django/contrib/sitemaps/__init__.py in get_latest_lastmod.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Requesting the sitemap index does not raise an exception. For sitemaps with callable lastmod, when items() is empty or not all items provide lastmod, the sitemap index should omit lastmod entries for that sitemap (no Last-Modified header for that sitemap/index entry). When every item has a valid lastmod, the index should include the latest lastmod datetime.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: A 500 Internal Server Error is raised. The traceback shows ValueError: "max() arg is an empty sequence" from django/contrib/sitemaps/__init__.py:get_latest_lastmod when lastmod is callable and items() is empty; the exception propagates into views.index and causes the response to fail.

hints_text:
Thanks for the report.
The default argument of max() can be used.

problem_statement.Title: FileBasedCache.has_key race condition causes FileNotFoundError under concurrent access
problem_statement.Description: When multiple threads/processes concurrently access an expired file-based cache entry, FileBasedCache.has_key can raise FileNotFoundError. has_key checks os.path.exists(fname) and then opens the file; if the file is removed between those operations (for example by _is_expired), open() raises FileNotFoundError which can propagate up through add() and get_or_set().
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. Incorrect assumption in django/core/cache/backends/filebased.py:FileBasedCache.has_key: the method assumes os.path.exists(fname) guarantees the file can be opened immediately. The code performs os.path.exists(fname) and then open(fname, 'rb') (django/core/cache/backends/filebased.py lines 91-96), which is racy when another thread/process deletes the file between those calls.

2. Side-effect in django/core/cache/backends/filebased.py:FileBasedCache._is_expired contributes to the window: _is_expired reads expiry from the open file and, if expired, closes the file and calls self._delete(f.name) (django/core/cache/backends/filebased.py lines 147-159). That deletion can occur while another caller has observed the file via exists() but has not yet opened it.

3. Inconsistent error-handling across the file-based backend: FileBasedCache.get and FileBasedCache.touch treat a missing cache file as a benign cache miss by catching FileNotFoundError (django/core/cache/backends/filebased.py lines 31-39 and 61-76), and _delete handles FileNotFoundError from os.remove (lines 81-89). has_key does not mirror this safe pattern, leading to inconsistent behavior across the backend.

4. Absence of a concurrency guard for the check-then-act pattern in has_key: LocMemCache uses a threading.Lock around add/get/has_key to make those operations thread-safe (django/core/cache/backends/locmem.py lines 21-24 and 25-33, 34-42, 79-85). FileBasedCache.has_key has no equivalent locking or exception handling around the exists()->open sequence, making it vulnerable to races under concurrent access.
StepsToReproduce: Configure Django to use the FileBasedCache backend and point its cache directory to a writable location.
Create or ensure a cache entry whose on-disk cache file is expired (so that _is_expired will delete it when inspected).
From multiple threads or processes simultaneously call cache.get_or_set(key, default) or cache.add(key, value) targeting the same key.
Observe that one or more callers raise FileNotFoundError with a stack trace showing django/core/cache/backends/filebased.py:FileBasedCache.has_key -> open(...) raising.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Concurrent cache access should not cause unhandled FileNotFoundError. When multiple callers race to read an expired or concurrently-removed cache file, the cache API should treat a missing file as a benign cache miss (return default/False as appropriate) rather than propagating a low-level filesystem exception to the caller.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Under concurrent access to an expired cache entry, a FileNotFoundError is raised from FileBasedCache.has_key (open on the cache file), propagating through FileBasedCache.add and BaseCache.get_or_set and causing callers to see an unhandled exception.

patch:
diff --git a/django/contrib/auth/management/__init__.py b/django/contrib/auth/management/__init__.py
--- a/django/contrib/auth/management/__init__.py
+++ b/django/contrib/auth/management/__init__.py
@@ -95,11 +95,16 @@ def create_permissions(
         .values_list("content_type", "codename")
     )
 
-    perms = [
-        Permission(codename=codename, name=name, content_type=ct)
-        for ct, (codename, name) in searched_perms
-        if (ct.pk, codename) not in all_perms
-    ]
+    perms = []
+    for ct, (codename, name) in searched_perms:
+        if (ct.pk, codename) not in all_perms:
+            permission = Permission()
+            permission._state.db = using
+            permission.codename = codename
+            permission.name = name
+            permission.content_type = ct
+            perms.append(permission)
+
     Permission.objects.using(using).bulk_create(perms)
     if verbosity >= 2:
         for perm in perms:
test_patch:
diff --git a/tests/auth_tests/test_management.py b/tests/auth_tests/test_management.py
--- a/tests/auth_tests/test_management.py
+++ b/tests/auth_tests/test_management.py
@@ -1485,3 +1485,22 @@ def test_permission_with_proxy_content_type_created(self):
                 codename=codename,
             ).exists()
         )
+
+
+class DefaultDBRouter:
+    """Route all writes to default."""
+
+    def db_for_write(self, model, **hints):
+        return "default"
+
+
+@override_settings(DATABASE_ROUTERS=[DefaultDBRouter()])
+class CreatePermissionsMultipleDatabasesTests(TestCase):
+    databases = {"default", "other"}
+
+    def test_set_permissions_fk_to_using_parameter(self):
+        Permission.objects.using("other").delete()
+        with self.assertNumQueries(6, using="other") as captured_queries:
+            create_permissions(apps.get_app_config("auth"), verbosity=0, using="other")
+        self.assertIn("INSERT INTO", captured_queries[-1]["sql"].upper())
+        self.assertGreater(Permission.objects.using("other").count(), 0)

problem_statement.Title: migrate management command ignores --database for some post-migrate DB reads (router consulted with empty hints)
problem_statement.Description: Running manage.py migrate --database <alias> completes migrations but a subsequent DB read is performed that consults the DB router with empty hints (observed as the router printing "read {}"). This occurs during post-migrate work (e.g., create_permissions/create_contenttypes) where a Manager/QuerySet without an explicit _db falls back to calling router.db_for_read/write(...) with self._hints == {}.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) BaseManager.db unconditionally falls back to router.db_for_read when self._db is falsy. Location and code pattern: django/db/models/manager.py:BaseManager.db: "return self._db or router.db_for_read(self.model, **self._hints)". This means a manager instance with no explicit _db will call the router even if _hints is empty.

2) QuerySet.db and RawQuerySet.db implement the same unconditional fallback for reads/writes. Location and code patterns: django/db/models/query.py:QuerySet.db: "return self._db or router.db_for_read(self.model, **self._hints)" (and the _for_write branch that calls router.db_for_write); django/db/models/query.py:RawQuerySet.db: "return self._db or router.db_for_read(self.model, **self._hints)". These properties can therefore call the router with empty hints when _db is falsy.

3) ContentTypeManager cache lookups access self.db as a key into the instance cache. Location and code patterns: django/contrib/contenttypes/models.py:ContentTypeManager.__init__ sets "self._cache = {}" and methods use "self._cache[self.db]" (e.g., get_by_natural_key and _get_from_cache). Because self.db may trigger the router fallback above, cache lookups can cause router.db_for_read(...) to be invoked.

4) BaseManager.db_manager uses a shallow copy (copy.copy(self)) and sets obj._db = using or self._db and obj._hints = hints or self._hints. Location and code pattern: django/db/models/manager.py:BaseManager.db_manager: "obj = copy.copy(self)"; "obj._db = using or self._db"; "obj._hints = hints or self._hints". Because ContentTypeManager._cache is an instance attribute created in __init__, a shallow copy will share the same _cache reference. Shared cache plus manager instances/clones with differing _db values can cause cache accesses performed on an instance with no _db to evaluate its db property (and thus consult the router).

5) The post-migrate signal is emitted with using=db, but the presence of using in the signal kwargs does not prevent receivers (or code they call) from accessing managers without an explicit using. Location and code pattern: django/core/management/sql.py:emit_post_migrate_signal sends post_migrate with "using=db". Core contrib handlers invoked in post-migrate (e.g., django.contrib.auth.management.create_permissions and django.contrib.contenttypes.management.create_contenttypes) call ContentType/Permission managers at various points, some with explicit using (e.g., ContentType.objects.db_manager(using).get_for_model) and other manager APIs in the same flow may be used without explicit using. These code patterns make it possible for a manager access without explicit _db to trigger router lookups with empty hints during post-migrate handling.
StepsToReproduce: Add a request-local or thread-local dependent DB router that prints from db_for_read/db_for_write (and raises if the routing context is absent).

Add the TenantSubdomainMiddleware and TenantDatabaseRouter (router prints in db_for_read/db_for_write and raises when no thread-local subdomain) into a Django project, and set TENANT_MAP and DATABASE_ROUTERS accordingly.
Run a migration with an explicit database alias: python manage.py migrate --database default
Observe that migrations complete but a line like "read {}" is printed and a router-related exception (e.g., InvalidTenantException) is raised during post-migrate handling.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When manage.py migrate is invoked with --database <alias>, post-migrate handlers and any manager/QuerySet access performed as part of the migrate command should use the specified database alias and not trigger DB router lookups with empty hints that depend on request-local/thread-local state.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Migrations complete, but post-migrate steps (create_permissions/create_contenttypes or other post-migrate receivers) trigger a DB router call observed as "read {}". The router is consulted with empty hints and routers that depend on request-local/thread-local state can raise errors even though --database was supplied.

Thanks for this report, it's related with adding missing permissions. I was able to fix this by setting _state.db, however I'm not convinced that it's the best solution: django/contrib/auth/management/__init__.py diff --git a/django/contrib/auth/management/__init__.py b/django/contrib/auth/management/__init__.py index 0b5a982617..27fe0df1d7 100644 a b def create_permissions( 9494 ) 9595 .values_list("content_type", "codename") 9696 ) 97 98 perms = [ 99 Permission(codename=codename, name=name, content_type=ct) 100 for ct, (codename, name) in searched_perms 101 if (ct.pk, codename) not in all_perms 102 ] 97 perms = [] 98 for ct, (codename, name) in searched_perms: 99 if (ct.pk, codename) not in all_perms: 100 permission = Permission() 101 permission._state.db = using 102 permission.codename = codename 103 permission.name = name 104 permission.content_type = ct 105 perms.append(permission) 103106 Permission.objects.using(using).bulk_create(perms) 104107 if verbosity >= 2: 105108 for perm in perms: Partly related to #29843.
This patch resolves the problem at my end. I hope it can be added in the 4.1.4 since #29843 seems to be not actively worked on at the moment.
After diving a bit deeper it turned out that the issue was with one of the libraries in my project which was not adapted for multi-DB. I've made a PR with changes on the django-admin-interface which resolved my issue.
Aryan, this ticket doesn't have submitted PR.
Replying to Mariusz Felisiak: Thanks for this report, it's related with adding missing permissions. I was able to fix this by setting _state.db, however I'm not convinced that it's the best solution: django/contrib/auth/management/__init__.py diff --git a/django/contrib/auth/management/__init__.py b/django/contrib/auth/management/__init__.py index 0b5a982617..27fe0df1d7 100644 a b def create_permissions( 9494 ) 9595 .values_list("content_type", "codename") 9696 ) 97 98 perms = [ 99 Permission(codename=codename, name=name, content_type=ct) 100 for ct, (codename, name) in searched_perms 101 if (ct.pk, codename) not in all_perms 102 ] 97 perms = [] 98 for ct, (codename, name) in searched_perms: 99 if (ct.pk, codename) not in all_perms: 100 permission = Permission() 101 permission._state.db = using 102 permission.codename = codename 103 permission.name = name 104 permission.content_type = ct 105 perms.append(permission) 103106 Permission.objects.using(using).bulk_create(perms) 104107 if verbosity >= 2: 105108 for perm in perms: Partly related to #29843. I think bulk_create already sets the _state.db to the value passed in .using(), right? Or is it in bulk_create that we require _state.db to be set earlier? In which case, we could perhaps change something inside of this method. Replying to Vasanth: After diving a bit deeper it turned out that the issue was with one of the libraries in my project which was not adapted for multi-DB. I've made a PR with changes on the django-admin-interface which resolved my issue. So would it be relevant to close the issue or is the bug really related to Django itself?
Replying to David Wobrock: I think bulk_create already sets the _state.db to the value passed in .using(), right? Yes, but it's a different issue, strictly related with Permission and its content_type. get_content_type() is trying to find a content type using obj._state.db so when we create a Permission() without ._state.db it will first try to find a content type in the default db. So would it be relevant to close the issue or is the bug really related to Django itself? IMO we should fix this for permissions.
Replying to Mariusz Felisiak: Replying to David Wobrock: I think bulk_create already sets the _state.db to the value passed in .using(), right? Yes, but it's a different issue, strictly related with Permission and its content_type. get_content_type() is trying to find a content type using obj._state.db so when we create a Permission() without ._state.db it will first try to find a content type in the default db. Okay, I understand the issue now, thanks for the details!! First thing, it makes me wonder why we require to have a DB attribute set, at a moment where we are not (yet) interacting with the DB. So we are currently checking, when setting the content_type FK, that the router allows this relation. I guess one option is to not do that for not-saved model instances. Would it make sense to defer this to when we start interacting with the DB? But it brings a whole other lot of changes and challenges, like changing a deep behaviour of FKs and multi-tenancy :/ Apart from that, if we don't want to set directly the internal attribute _state.db, I guess we would need a proper way to pass the db/using to the model instantiation. What would be the most Django-y way? Passing it through the model constructor => this has quite a large impact, as a keyword argument would possibly shadow existing field names: Permission(..., db=using). Quite risky in terms of backward compatibility I guess. Adding a method to Model? Something like: Permission(...).using(db), which could perhaps then be re-used in other places also. (EDIT: which wouldn't work, as the setting the FK happens before setting the DB alias.) What do you think ? :) Or am I missing other solutions?
Apart from that, if we don't want to set directly the internal attribute _state.db, I guess we would need a proper way to pass the db/using to the model instantiation. _state is ​documented so using it is not so bad. What would be the most Django-y way? Passing it through the model constructor => this has quite a large impact, as a keyword argument would possibly shadow existing field names: Permission(..., db=using). Quite risky in terms of backward compatibility I guess. Adding a method to Model? Something like: Permission(...).using(db), which could perhaps then be re-used in other places also. What do you think ? :) Or am I missing other solutions? Django doesn't support cross-db relationships and users were always responsible for assigning related objects from the same db. I don't think that we should add more logic to do this. The Permission-content_type issue is really an edge case in managing relations, as for me we don't need a generic solution for it.

problem_statement.Title: Multi-level FilteredRelation with select_related() can return wrong related object (mis-wired aliases)
problem_statement.Description: When an annotation uses a multi-level FilteredRelation (relation_name containing LOOKUP_SEP) together with select_related(), the related-object mapping can be mis-wired: a related attribute resolved via the annotated FilteredRelation can point to the wrong model instance. Example: p[0].tournament_pool.tournament ends up referencing a PoolStyle instance instead of the Tournament instance.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Query.names_to_path truncates an inner FilteredRelation path (django/db/models/sql/query.py::Query.names_to_path): when a top-level filtered relation's relation_name contains LOOKUP_SEP, names_to_path calls names_to_path() recursively for the inner parts and then extends the outer path with filtered_relation_path[:-1], dropping the final PathInfo element (the literal slice '[:-1]' is present in the code).

2) Query.setup_joins records the path it is given in filtered_relation.path (django/db/models/sql/query.py::Query.setup_joins): setup_joins sets filtered_relation.path = joins[:] when creating the filtered relation join, so it expects names_to_path to supply the full join chain.

3) FilteredRelation.as_sql / Query.build_filtered_relation_q use filtered_relation.path as the reuse set (django/db/models/query_utils.py::FilteredRelation.as_sql and django/db/models/sql/query.py::Query.build_filtered_relation_q): FilteredRelation.as_sql calls query.build_filtered_relation_q(self.condition, reuse=set(self.path)), therefore an incomplete filtered_relation.path produces an incorrect reuse set used by build_filter/join-reuse logic.

4) SQLCompiler.get_related_selections relies on the alias returned by setup_joins to collect model columns and build klass_info['select_fields'] (django/db/models/sql/compiler.py::SQLCompiler.get_related_selections): if setup_joins was given a truncated path and thus returned an alias that does not correspond to the intended related table, the klass_info mapping for select_related will map the wrong table's columns into the related model slots.

5) Join identity vs equals behavior makes the reuse set semantics critical (django/db/models/sql/datastructures.py::Join.identity, Join.__eq__, Join.equals): Join.identity includes filtered_relation so __eq__ compares filtered_relation while equals() ignores it; the join-reuse decision uses these comparison semantics depending on reuse flags, so an incorrect reuse set (from a truncated path) can change whether a join is considered identical or reusable, contributing to alias mis-wiring.

6) Investigation gap: a concrete alias-by-alias trace for the failing PoolStyle/Pool/Tournament case was not produced in the investigation (no before/after alias lists mapping alias -> table were recorded). This trace is needed to demonstrate the exact alias mis-wiring for that specific test case.
StepsToReproduce:
Add this test method to known_related_objects.tests.ExistingRelatedInstancesTests:

def test_wrong_select_related(self):
    with self.assertNumQueries(3):
        p = list(PoolStyle.objects.annotate(
            tournament_pool=FilteredRelation('pool__tournament__pool'),
        ).select_related('tournament_pool'))
    self.assertEqual(p[0].pool.tournament, p[0].tournament_pool.tournament)

Run the Django test suite (or the containing test module) and observe the failing assertion where the related object returned via the annotated FilteredRelation does not match the expected related instance.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: select_related() used with an annotated multi-level FilteredRelation should map related columns to the correct related model instances. In the example, p[0].tournament_pool.tournament should resolve to the same Tournament instance as p[0].pool.tournament.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The select_related mapping is mis-wired: the attribute resolved via the annotated FilteredRelation (p[0].tournament_pool.tournament) is not the Tournament instance but instead corresponds to a PoolStyle instance (the wrong model/instance is placed in the related slot).

problem_statement.Title: Admin 'Save as new' button shown to users without add permission
problem_statement.Description: The admin change-form submit-line can show the "Save as new" button to users who do not have add permission. The UI flag controlling this is computed in the submit_row template tag and currently omits an add-permission check.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) django/contrib/admin/templatetags/admin_modify.py :: submit_row(context): submit_row computes the template context key "show_save_as_new" using the expression: not is_popup AND has_change_permission AND change AND save_as. Although has_add_permission is present in the function's local variables (has_add_permission = context["has_add_permission"]), it is not included in this expression. This is an incorrect assumption that has_change_permission (plus change and save_as and not is_popup) is sufficient to show the "Save as new" option.

2) django/contrib/admin/options.py :: ModelAdmin.render_change_form: render_change_form supplies the template context keys needed by submit_row (including "has_add_permission" and "save_as"). Because these keys are available in the context, the omission of has_add_permission is localized to submit_row and is not caused by missing context plumbing.

3) django/contrib/admin/options.py :: ModelAdmin._changeform_view: server-side handling treats a POST with "_saveasnew" as an add (object_id set to None / add = True) and enforces has_add_permission(request), raising PermissionDenied when missing. This creates a mismatch: the UI may expose an action that the server will subsequently reject for users without add permission.

4) Test coverage / verification gap: existing admin tests that assert show_save_as_new are executed while logged in as a superuser (tests/admin_views/tests.py uses a superuser via force_login). There is no explicit test asserting that a user who has change permission but not add permission does not receive show_save_as_new in the template context, which allows the UI inconsistency to be untested.

5) Logical relationship found in investigation: the UI flag for the "Save as new" option is expected to reflect the permissions required for the operation. In diagnostic terms, the intended relationship is that the button should only be shown when (not is_popup) AND has_add_permission AND has_change_permission AND change AND save_as. The current code omits the has_add_permission conjunct from show_save_as_new, causing the mismatch between UI and server-side permission enforcement.
StepsToReproduce: Enable ModelAdmin.save_as = True for a model.
Create a staff user who has change permission on the model but explicitly does NOT have add permission.
Log in to the admin as that staff user.
Open the change form for an existing instance of the model (not a popup).
Observe whether the "Save as new" button/option is present in the submit-line.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Users who do not have add permission should not see a "Save as new" button or option in the admin change-form submit-line.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: A user who has change permission but lacks add permission may see the "Save as new" button in the submit-line when editing an object (when save_as is enabled and not in a popup). Submitting the action is prevented server-side, but the UI presents an option the user cannot perform.

problem_statement.Title: Migration optimizer fails to collapse consecutive AlterField operations
problem_statement.Description: When multiple consecutive AlterField operations target the same model.field and there is no preceding AddField in the same optimization pass, the migration optimizer leaves all AlterField operations uncollapsed. This occurs during MigrationOptimizer.optimize and produces redundant AlterField operations in the optimized output.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Missing case in django/db/migrations/operations/fields.py: AlterField.reduce does not handle the case where the incoming operation is another AlterField on the same model and field name. AlterField.reduce only special-cases RemoveField and RenameField (the RenameField special-case only applies when self.field.db_column is None), and otherwise delegates to the FieldOperation / Operation fallback. (See AlterField.reduce in django/db/migrations/operations/fields.py.)

2) Fallback behavior prevents collapsing: Operation.reduce (base implementation) returns False by default unless either operation is elidable. FieldOperation.reduce returns super().reduce(...) or not operation.references_field(...). For a later AlterField that references the same model.field, operation.references_field(...) is True, so FieldOperation.reduce evaluates to False. That means AlterField.reduce currently returns False for AlterField-vs-AlterField, which the optimizer interprets as a boundary rather than a merge. (See Operation.reduce in django/db/migrations/operations/base.py and FieldOperation.reduce in django/db/migrations/operations/fields.py.)

3) Optimizer contract: MigrationOptimizer.optimize (optimize_inner) treats a list returned by operation.reduce(other, app_label) as the replacement sequence for the earlier operation. Therefore, to collapse an earlier AlterField into a later AlterField the reduce implementation must return a list (e.g., a list containing the later AlterField). Because AlterField.reduce never returns such a list for incoming AlterField operations, the optimizer never collapses them. (See MigrationOptimizer.optimize_inner in django/db/migrations/optimizer.py.)

4) Symmetry / related behavior: AddField.reduce already implements the pattern of consuming a following AlterField (Add + Alter -> single Add with the final field). The absence of an equivalent path in AlterField.reduce encodes an assumption asymmetry: AddField.merge is handled but AlterField-vs-AlterField is treated as a non-merge boundary. (See AddField.reduce in django/db/migrations/operations/fields.py.)

5) Secondary/test gaps: The investigation did not validate edge cases where collapsing AlterField operations might affect preserve_default, serialization, or interactions with RenameField when field.db_column is not None. Those are noted as gaps in verification rather than confirmed regressions; they share the same root-cause pattern (omitted reduce logic and potential untested consequences).
StepsToReproduce: Construct the following operations list in a Django migration context:
operations = [
migrations.AddField(model_name='book', name='title', field=models.CharField(max_length=256, null=True)),
migrations.AlterField(model_name='book', name='title', field=models.CharField(max_length=128, null=True)),
migrations.AlterField(model_name='book', name='title', field=models.CharField(max_length=128, null=True, help_text='help')),
migrations.AlterField(model_name='book', name='title', field=models.CharField(max_length=128, null=True, help_text='help', default=None)),
Run the migration optimizer across only the AlterField operations (i.e. without the AddField in the same pass):
optimizer = MigrationOptimizer(); optimizer.optimize(operations[1:], 'books')
Inspect the returned list of operations.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When optimizing a sequence of consecutive AlterField operations on the same model.field, the optimizer should collapse them so that only a single operation representing the final field state remains (i.e., only the last AlterField in the sequence). If an AddField and then AlterField(s) occur in the same optimization pass, the optimizer should produce a single AddField reflecting the final field state.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: When optimize is run on a sequence containing only the AlterField operations (no preceding AddField in the same pass), the optimizer returns all AlterField operations unchanged. Example: calling optimizer.optimize(operations[1:], 'books') returns a list containing three separate AlterField operations instead of a single final AlterField.

hints_text:
Your analysis is correct Laurent, the reduction of multiple AlterField against the same model is simply not implemented today hence why you're running into this behaviour. Given you're already half way there ​I would encourage you to submit a PR that adds these changes and ​an optimizer regression test to cover them if you'd like to see this issue fixed in future versions of Django.
Thanks Simon, I submitted a PR.

problem_statement.Title: admin.E108 check misses list_display items that runtime rejects, causing 500 on admin list view
problem_statement.Description: A ModelAdmin list_display entry (for example, "choice") can pass the static system check (admin.E108) but cause a 500 error when rendering the admin changelist because the runtime label resolution (label_for_field) rejects the same name. The root cause is a semantic mismatch between the static check-time field-resolution logic and the runtime helper used when rendering the admin.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) django/contrib/admin/checks.py -> ModelAdminChecks._check_list_display_item (and other check methods) assume that obj.model._meta.get_field(name) exhibits the same semantics as the admin's runtime field-resolution. In _check_list_display_item, the code calls obj.model._meta.get_field(item) (checks.py:899) and then applies local heuristics such as isinstance(field, models.ManyToManyField) or (getattr(field, 'rel', None) and field.rel.field.many_to_one) to decide whether to emit admin.E109 (checks.py:919-921). This local heuristic is not the authoritative runtime rule and therefore can disagree with runtime behavior.

2) The authoritative runtime semantics are implemented in django/contrib/admin/utils.py:_get_non_gfk_field and label_for_field. _get_non_gfk_field calls opts.get_field(name) and then explicitly excludes GenericForeignKey, reverse relations (one_to_many), and FK attname cases by raising FieldDoesNotExist or FieldIsAForeignKeyColumnName (utils.py:319-336). label_for_field relies on that helper and, if _get_non_gfk_field raises FieldDoesNotExist, falls back to attribute/callable checks and ultimately raises AttributeError("Unable to lookup '%s' on %s or %s") when nothing matches (utils.py:350-378). The runtime traceback in the original report is produced by this path.

3) The checks module (django/contrib/admin/checks.py) does not import or call the runtime helper(s) that enforce these semantics: its import from django.contrib.admin.utils includes NotRelationField, flatten, and get_fields_from_path but does not import _get_non_gfk_field or FieldIsAForeignKeyColumnName (checks.py:6). Because the checks reimplement relation-detection heuristics locally rather than reusing the canonical helper, there is a semantic mismatch between check-time and run-time validation.

4) Multiple check methods in django/contrib/admin/checks.py share the same root-cause pattern (calling model._meta.get_field(...) and applying local heuristics), and therefore could be affected by the same mismatch. Representative locations include: _check_list_display_item (checks.py:893-931), _check_raw_id_fields_item (checks.py:284-313), _check_field_spec_item (checks.py:430-459), _check_filter_item (checks.py:521-538), _check_radio_fields_key (checks.py:560-579), _check_prepopulated_fields_key (checks.py:630-655), _check_prepopulated_fields_value_item (checks.py:673-684), _check_ordering_item (checks.py:704-740), and _check_list_editable_item (checks.py:1093-1146). Each of these locations calls obj.model._meta.get_field(...) and applies local validation logic that may not match utils._get_non_gfk_field's runtime exclusions.

5) Test coverage gap: tests/modeladmin/test_checks.py contains tests for admin.E108 and admin.E109 (e.g., missing field and reverse/M2M cases at tests/modeladmin/test_checks.py:520-555), but there is no explicit regression test asserting the exact scenario that passed checks yet caused the runtime AttributeError seen in the report. As a result, the check-time vs run-time semantic mismatch for that precise case was not captured by the test suite.
StepsToReproduce: Define two models where Choice is related to Question (for example, Choice has a ForeignKey to Question, or a related_name that exposes a reverse relation attribute).
Register QuestionAdmin with list_display = ['choice'].
Start the Django development server and log into the admin.
Visit the admin changelist for Question, e.g. GET /admin/polls/question/.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The admin system check should flag invalid list_display entries that the admin runtime would reject (so that invalid configuration is reported at check time), and visiting the Question changelist should not raise an unhandled 500 due to a failed label lookup. The admin changelist should render normally or otherwise present a handled diagnostic instead of an internal server error.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Visiting /admin/polls/question/ raises a 500 Internal Server Error. The traceback shows django/contrib/admin/utils.py:label_for_field raising AttributeError: "Unable to lookup 'choice' on Question or QuestionAdmin" (the error message and the call path are produced when _get_non_gfk_field excludes the name and the fallback attribute/callable checks do not find a match). The static system check did not emit admin.E108 for this configuration.

diff --git a/django/db/migrations/operations/models.py b/django/db/migrations/operations/models.py
--- a/django/db/migrations/operations/models.py
+++ b/django/db/migrations/operations/models.py
@@ -303,6 +303,71 @@ def reduce(self, operation, app_label):
                         managers=self.managers,
                     ),
                 ]
+        elif (
+            isinstance(operation, IndexOperation)
+            and self.name_lower == operation.model_name_lower
+        ):
+            if isinstance(operation, AddIndex):
+                return [
+                    CreateModel(
+                        self.name,
+                        fields=self.fields,
+                        options={
+                            **self.options,
+                            "indexes": [
+                                *self.options.get("indexes", []),
+                                operation.index,
+                            ],
+                        },
+                        bases=self.bases,
+                        managers=self.managers,
+                    ),
+                ]
+            elif isinstance(operation, RemoveIndex):
+                options_indexes = [
+                    index
+                    for index in self.options.get("indexes", [])
+                    if index.name != operation.name
+                ]
+                return [
+                    CreateModel(
+                        self.name,
+                        fields=self.fields,
+                        options={
+                            **self.options,
+                            "indexes": options_indexes,
+                        },
+                        bases=self.bases,
+                        managers=self.managers,
+                    ),
+                ]
+            elif isinstance(operation, RenameIndex) and operation.old_fields:
+                options_index_together = {
+                    fields
+                    for fields in self.options.get("index_together", [])
+                    if fields != operation.old_fields
+                }
+                if options_index_together:
+                    self.options["index_together"] = options_index_together
+                else:
+                    self.options.pop("index_together", None)
+                return [
+                    CreateModel(
+                        self.name,
+                        fields=self.fields,
+                        options={
+                            **self.options,
+                            "indexes": [
+                                *self.options.get("indexes", []),
+                                models.Index(
+                                    fields=operation.old_fields, name=operation.new_name
+                                ),
+                            ],
+                        },
+                        bases=self.bases,
+                        managers=self.managers,
+                    ),
+                ]
         return super().reduce(operation, app_label)

problem_statement.Title: Squashed migrations preserve Meta.index_together and trigger RemovedInDjango51Warning on import
problem_statement.Description: When migrations are squashed, Meta.index_together entries are preserved in the generated squashed migration file (in CreateModel/AlterModelOptions and/or AlterIndexTogether operations). Importing the squashed migration emits RemovedInDjango51Warning for "index_together", so squashing does not eliminate the deprecation warnings.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) The squash command constructs a new Migration using the in-memory operations list and writes it out with MigrationWriter without normalizing deprecated model option forms (django/core/management/commands/squashmigrations.py:Command.handle). Evidence: Command.handle builds a Migration subclass with 'operations': new_operations and passes it to MigrationWriter.as_string() for file output (see the operations=new_operations assignment and writer.as_string() call).

2) The migration writer serializes each Operation using operation.deconstruct() and emits the returned args/kwargs directly (django/db/migrations/writer.py:OperationWriter.serialize and MigrationWriter.as_string). Evidence: OperationWriter calls name, args, kwargs = self.operation.deconstruct() and then writes those kwargs (respecting operation.serialization_expand_args) into the migration file; there is no general normalization step that would rewrite deprecated option keys.

3) The migration Operation classes can and do include index_together in their deconstructed kwargs (django/db/migrations/operations/models.py:CreateModel.deconstruct and AlterTogetherOptionOperation/AlterIndexTogether.deconstruct). Evidence: CreateModel lists 'options' in serialization_expand_args and deconstruct() includes self.options when present; AlterTogetherOptionOperation.deconstruct() places its option_name (for AlterIndexTogether that option_name == 'index_together') into the kwargs that the writer will serialize.

4) Options.__init__ treats 'index_together' as a legacy option and emits RemovedInDjango51Warning at import/initialization time (django/db/models/options.py:Options.__init__). Evidence: DEFAULT_NAMES retains 'index_together' for backward compatibility, Options.__init__ normalizes index_together and issues warnings.warn("'index_together' is deprecated...") when index_together is present.

5) The canonical representation used elsewhere in Django (makemigrations/autodetector) for the same semantics is Index instances in options['indexes'], not an 'index_together' option. Concretely, index_together values (iterables/tuples of field names) correspond to django.db.models.Index instances whose fields equal the tuple of names (an Index constructed as models.Index(fields=list(fields), name=None) is the canonical representation that autodetector recognizes). Evidence: django/db/migrations/autodetector.py implements detection/diffing logic that treats Index objects as the canonical form and recognises when index_together values have been replaced by Index instances (create_altered_indexes and related routines examine Index.deconstruct() and Index.fields to match former index_together tuples).

6) Because the squasher writes operations as-is and the writer serializes operation.deconstruct() output verbatim, any 'index_together' that remains in CreateModel.options, AlterModelOptions.options, or explicit AlterIndexTogether operations will be written into the squashed migration file and will trigger the import-time deprecation warning described above. This includes a remaining gap: explicit AlterIndexTogether operations in the operation stream are not converted by a minimal canonicalization pass and will still serialize 'index_together' (django/db/migrations/operations/models.py:AlterIndexTogether). Autodetector contains the more complex state-diffing logic required to convert AlterIndexTogether into equivalent AddIndex/RemoveIndex/RenameIndex operations; that conversion is not performed by the squasher and therefore remains a source of emitted warnings.
StepsToReproduce: Create a model with Meta.index_together (for example, class MyModel: class Meta: index_together = [("a", "b")] ) and run makemigrations to create initial migrations.
Make additional migrations for the app (so there are multiple migrations to squash).
Run ./manage.py squashmigrations <app_label> <migration_name> to produce a squashed migration file.
Import the squashed migration (for example, run ./manage.py migrate or run tests that import migrations) and observe RemovedInDjango51Warning mentioning "index_together".
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Squashing migrations should produce a squashed migration file that does not contain deprecated Meta.index_together usage; importing the squashed migration should not emit RemovedInDjango51Warning for "index_together".
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The generated squashed migration file still contains 'index_together' in CreateModel/AlterModelOptions and/or AlterIndexTogether operations, and importing the squashed migration emits RemovedInDjango51Warning for 'index_together'. Users must currently manually edit squashed migrations to remove the deprecation warnings.

diff --git a/django/template/defaultfilters.py b/django/template/defaultfilters.py
--- a/django/template/defaultfilters.py
+++ b/django/template/defaultfilters.py
@@ -586,8 +586,9 @@ def join(value, arg, autoescape=True):
     """Join a list with a string, like Python's ``str.join(list)``."""
     try:
         if autoescape:
-            value = [conditional_escape(v) for v in value]
-        data = conditional_escape(arg).join(value)
+            data = conditional_escape(arg).join([conditional_escape(v) for v in value])
+        else:
+            data = arg.join(value)
     except TypeError:  # Fail silently if arg isn't iterable.
         return value
     return mark_safe(data)

problem_statement.Title: join filter escapes separator even when {% autoescape off %} is active
problem_statement.Description: The template filter `join` HTML-escapes the separator argument (the joining string) even when template autoescaping is disabled. For example, rendering a template with `{% autoescape off %}{{ some_list|join:some_var }}{% endautoescape %}` leaves list items unescaped (as expected) but produces an escaped separator (e.g. '<br/>').
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Unconditional escaping of the separator in join: django/template/defaultfilters.py::join(value, arg, autoescape=True) calls conditional_escape(arg).join(value) regardless of the autoescape flag. Location: django/template/defaultfilters.py, lines ~584–593 (def join). 2) Partial use of the autoescape flag in join: join uses the autoescape parameter to decide whether to escape list items (it does value = [conditional_escape(v) for v in value] only when autoescape is True) but it does not use that flag to decide whether to escape the separator argument. Location: django/template/defaultfilters.py, lines ~584–593 (def join). 3) conditional_escape semantics cause the observed effect: django/utils/html.py::conditional_escape(text) returns text.__html__() if present, otherwise escape(text). Thus calling conditional_escape(arg) on an ordinary (non-safe) separator yields an escaped string. Location: django/utils/html.py, lines ~82–95 (def conditional_escape). 4) Established filter pattern demonstrates the intended relationship: other filters that accept an autoescape flag select an escaper based on that flag and apply it uniformly (e.g. unordered_list chooses escaper = conditional_escape if autoescape else identity, then uses escaper for list items). This highlights that join's behavior (escaping arg unconditionally while only conditionally escaping items) is inconsistent with that pattern. Location: django/template/defaultfilters.py, lines ~655–683 (def unordered_list). 5) Tests encode the current (inconsistent) semantics for the separator: the repository's tests directly call join(..., autoescape=False) and expect the separator to be escaped. Example: tests/template_tests/filter_tests/test_join.py::FunctionTests.test_autoescape_off asserts that join(["", "", ""], "
", autoescape=False) returns the separator-escaped string '<br><br>'. Location: tests/template_tests/filter_tests/test_join.py, lines ~69–73. (Note: a regression test shown in the original bug report was provided by the reporter in their local environment but is not present in this repository.)
StepsToReproduce:
Create a template 'join01' with content: '{{ some_list|join:some_var }}' and render with context some_list = ['

Hello World!

', 'beta & me', ''] and some_var = '
' — observe escaped output when autoescape is on.

Create a template 'join02' with content: '{% autoescape off %}{{ some_list|join:some_var }}{% endautoescape %}' and render with the same context — observe that the separator appears escaped (e.g. '<br/>') even though autoescape is off.

Run the repository test suite tests/template_tests/filter_tests/test_join.py::FunctionTests.test_autoescape_off to see the current function-level expectation calling join(..., autoescape=False) (this test currently asserts that the separator is escaped). Note: the failing regression test from the original bug report was provided externally by the reporter and is not present in this repository.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When template autoescaping is disabled (for example inside `{% autoescape off %}`), the separator argument passed to the `join` filter should not be HTML-escaped. The rendered output should show the raw separator between items (i.e. some_var.join(some_list) when some_var is a plain string and autoescape is off).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The `join` filter HTML-escapes the separator argument even when autoescape is off, producing escaped separators like '<br/>' between items while list items themselves remain unescaped under autoescape off.

diff --git a/django/db/models/sql/query.py b/django/db/models/sql/query.py
--- a/django/db/models/sql/query.py
+++ b/django/db/models/sql/query.py
@@ -779,7 +779,13 @@ def _get_only_select_mask(self, opts, mask, select_mask=None):
         # Only include fields mentioned in the mask.
         for field_name, field_mask in mask.items():
             field = opts.get_field(field_name)
-            field_select_mask = select_mask.setdefault(field, {})
+            # Retrieve the actual field associated with reverse relationships
+            # as that's what is expected in the select mask.
+            if field in opts.related_objects:
+                field_key = field.field
+            else:
+                field_key = field
+            field_select_mask = select_mask.setdefault(field_key, {})
             if field_mask:
                 if not field.is_relation:
                     raise FieldError(next(iter(field_mask)))

+        request = Request.objects.create(location=location)
+        with self.assertNumQueries(1):

+        item = Item.objects.create(value=1)
+        request.items.add(item)
+        with self.assertNumQueries(1):

+
+    def test_only_reverse_many_to_many_ignored(self):
