problem_statement.Title: QuerySet.only() ignores select_related() column restrictions for reverse OneToOne relations
problem_statement.Description: On Django 4.2, calling QuerySet.only() together with select_related() using a reverse OneToOne relation (reverse lookup name, e.g. related_name='secondary') fails to restrict the selected columns of the related model; all related-model fields are still included in the generated SQL.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. _get_only_select_mask uses opts.get_field(field_name) and uses that returned object directly as the select_mask key: in django/db/models/sql/query.py::Query._get_only_select_mask the code does field = opts.get_field(field_name) and then field_select_mask = select_mask.setdefault(field, {}). For reverse relation names, opts.get_field(...) returns a reverse-relation descriptor object (not the forward Field object on the related model), so the select_mask is keyed by that Reverse-object rather than by the forward Field.

2. The SQL compiler expects select_mask keys to be forward Field objects (and (name, relation) tuples for filtered relations): in django/db/models/sql/compiler.py::SQLCompiler.get_related_selections the code looks up masks with select_mask.get(f) for forward relations and select_mask.get(related_field) for reverse relations; for filtered relations it looks up select_mask.get((name, final_field)). Because these lookups use forward Field objects (or (name, field) tuples), any mask entries keyed by Reverse-objects are ignored.

3. The defer-masking path already produces keys in the expected shape: in django/db/models/sql/query.py::Query._get_defer_select_mask reverse relation names are translated to the forward Field via reverse_rel.field and filtered relations are keyed as (field_name, relation). Example code paths: for filtered relations it does select_mask.setdefault((field_name, relation), {}), and for reverse relations it does reverse_rel = opts.get_field(field_name); field = reverse_rel.field; field_select_mask = select_mask.setdefault(field, {}). This demonstrates the key-shape the compiler expects and that the only()-path diverges from.

4. Net effect: because Query._get_only_select_mask stores reverse relation entries under the Reverse-object key while SQLCompiler.get_related_selections looks up masks using the forward Field object (or (name, field) tuple for filtered relations), the only() mask for reverse OneToOne relations (and filtered_relation entries) is not applied. As a result, the compiler falls back to selecting all columns from the related model for those relations. Relevant locations: django/db/models/sql/query.py::Query._get_only_select_mask, django/db/models/sql/query.py::Query._get_defer_select_mask, and django/db/models/sql/compiler.py::SQLCompiler.get_related_selections.
StepsToReproduce: Define models: Main and Secondary where Secondary.main = OneToOneField(Main, primary_key=True, related_name='secondary', ...), and each model has several CharField columns.
Run the queryset: Main.objects.select_related('secondary').only('main_field_1', 'secondary__secondary_field_1')
Inspect the generated SQL (e.g. print(str(queryset.query)) or run the query and capture SQL).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The generated SQL should include only the Main fields specified plus only the named field(s) from the related Secondary model (e.g. secondary.secondary_field_1). Columns from Secondary not named in only() should be omitted from the SELECT.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The generated SQL includes all columns from the related Secondary model (secondary_field_1, secondary_field_2, secondary_field_3) despite only() specifying only secondary__secondary_field_1.

problem_statement.Title: bulk_create does not return primary keys when update_conflicts=True (PostgreSQL RETURNING suppressed)
problem_statement.Description: When calling QuerySet.bulk_create(..., update_conflicts=True, ...) on backends that support RETURNING (PostgreSQL), primary keys are not populated on returned model instances because QuerySet-level logic suppresses RETURNING-based bulk-return behavior whenever any on_conflict handling is present.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) QuerySet._batched_insert encodes an incorrect assumption that any ON CONFLICT disables returning_fields: it only requests returning_fields when on_conflict is None. Concretely, the code uses the conditional

    if bulk_return and on_conflict is None:
        ... call _insert(..., returning_fields=...)
    else:
        ... call _insert(..., on_conflict=...)

(File: django/db/models/query.py, QuerySet._batched_insert)

This makes the branch that requests returning_fields and the branch that passes on_conflict mutually exclusive, so the compiler never receives both returning_fields and on_conflict for the same InsertQuery.

2) QuerySet.bulk_create encodes a related incorrect assumption that only the no-conflict case can return rows: it only performs the returned-rows length assertion when on_conflict is None, i.e.:

    if connection.features.can_return_rows_from_bulk_insert and on_conflict is None:
        assert len(returned_columns) == len(objs_without_pk)

(File: django/db/models/query.py, QuerySet.bulk_create)

This assumes UPDATE-on-conflict will not produce returned rows and therefore excludes it from the 1:1 returned-rows expectation.

3) The SQL compiler and PostgreSQL backend already support ON CONFLICT ... DO UPDATE ... RETURNING and returning/fetching rows when the backend advertises those features (so the blocking decisions are at the QuerySet level):
- SQLInsertCompiler will append an on_conflict suffix and a RETURNING clause when returning_fields is set (django/db/models/sql/compiler.py).
- Postgres DatabaseOperations provides on_conflict_suffix_sql (DO UPDATE / DO NOTHING), return_insert_columns (RETURNING ...), and fetch_returned_insert_rows, and DatabaseFeatures sets can_return_columns_from_insert and can_return_rows_from_bulk_insert to True (django/db/backends/postgresql/operations.py and features.py).

Intended invariant (diagnostic): on backends where connection.features.can_return_rows_from_bulk_insert is True, the code should expect returned rows for INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE ... RETURNING and treat them as mapping 1:1 to the input VALUES rows for the purpose of populating model PKs/returning fields — i.e. when can_return_rows_from_bulk_insert is True and on_conflict != OnConflict.IGNORE, returned_rows_count should match inserted_rows_count for objs without PK.
StepsToReproduce: Use the PostgreSQL backend and define a model with an auto-increment primary key.
Create instances without PKs: objs = [MyModel(...), ...].
Call MyModel.objects.bulk_create(objs, update_conflicts=True, update_fields=[...], unique_fields=[...])
Observe that returned model instances do not have pk attributes set, and inspect the generated SQL to verify the INSERT lacks a RETURNING <id> clause.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: On a backend that supports RETURNING (e.g. PostgreSQL), bulk_create(..., update_conflicts=True, ...) that results in INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE ... should return the updated/inserted rows (RETURNING), and the returned model instances should have their primary keys populated (returned rows map 1:1 to the input rows without PK).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: bulk_create(..., update_conflicts=True, ...) does not populate primary keys on returned model instances. At the QuerySet level, RETURNING-based behavior is suppressed when any on_conflict option is present and the generated INSERT SQL lacks a RETURNING clause.

problem_statement.Title: Nested-class classmethod serialized with wrong dotted path in migrations (outer class omitted)
problem_statement.Description: A classmethod defined on a nested (inner) class and used as a Field.default is serialized into migration files with an incorrect dotted path that omits the outer class name, causing import/runtime errors when applying migrations.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) In django/db/migrations/serializer.py, FunctionTypeSerializer.serialize serializes a bound method whose __self__ is a type by constructing the dotted path using the class's __name__ and the method's __name__. The implementation returns "%s.%s.%s" % (module, klass.__name__, self.value.__name__) (see FunctionTypeSerializer bound-method branch). Using klass.__name__ drops outer-class qualification for nested (inner) classes.

2) In the same file, TypeSerializer.serialize serializes classes using __qualname__ (returning "%s.%s" % (module, self.value.__qualname__)) which preserves nested qualification such as "Profile.Capability". The FunctionTypeSerializer behavior is inconsistent with this established serializer behavior for types.

3) The practical effect is that a classmethod bound to a nested class is emitted in migrations as module + inner_class_name + method_name (for example, "appname.models.Capability.default") instead of module + outer_class.qualname + method_name (for example, "appname.models.Profile.Capability.default").

4) Edge-case: classes defined in local scopes have __qualname__ values containing "<locals>". The free-function branch of FunctionTypeSerializer explicitly checks for "<" in value.__qualname__ and rejects local functions; the bound-method branch does not perform a similar check and also uses klass.__name__ (so local-class cases are not handled the same way). This discrepancy is an additional gap in how local / non-top-level definitions are treated by the serializer.
StepsToReproduce: Run python manage.py makemigrations for the app containing the model.
Inspect the generated migration file and observe the default argument in migrations.AddField.
Optionally, run python manage.py migrate and observe the import/runtime error caused by the incorrect dotted path.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The generated migration should contain a fully qualified dotted path for the classmethod that includes the outer class qualification (for example: default=appname.models.Profile.Capability.default), so that the migration file imports and resolves the callable correctly when applying migrations.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The generated migration contains an incorrect dotted path that omits the outer class (for example: default=appname.models.Capability.default), which leads to import/runtime errors when applying the migration.
